# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between the `LJMP`, `AJMP`, and `SJMP` instructions of the 8051. Mention the size of each jump in bytes and the maximum range.**

**Model Answer:**

| Instruction | Opcode Size | Jump Range |
|---|---|---|
| `SJMP <rel>` | 2 bytes | −128 to +127 (relative to PC + 2) |
| `AJMP <addr11>` | 2 bytes | 2 KB within the same 2-KB page (11-bit address) |
| `LJMP <addr16>` | 3 bytes | Full 64 KB (16-bit address) |

`SJMP` is *short* and *relative*; `AJMP` is *absolute within a page*; `LJMP` is *long and absolute*. For `AJMP`, the upper 5 bits of the destination address must match the upper 5 bits of the current PC.

### **Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the function of the `DA A` instruction. Why is it used only after `ADD`/`ADDC` and not after `INC`?**

**Model Answer:**
`DA A` (Decimal Adjust Accumulator) converts the binary result in the accumulator into **packed BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal)**. It works by adding `06H`, `60H`, or `66H` to A based on the lower/upper nibble values and the carry flag, so that each nibble stays in the range `0–9`.

It is used **only after `ADD` or `ADDC`** because these are the only instructions that update the **carry flag** (which `DA A` inspects). The `INC` instruction does **not** affect the carry flag; using `DA A` after `INC` would yield incorrect decimal results. This is a board-favorite 3-mark question.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 – PEMUT746]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** *Write an 8051 ALP to add two 16-bit numbers. The first number is stored in internal RAM at locations 30H (low byte) and 31H (high byte). The second number is at 32H (low) and 33H (high). Store the 32-bit result at 40H–43H (low to high).*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Calculate the total execution time of the program for f<sub>osc</sub> = 12 MHz. Assume no overflow beyond 32 bits.*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     SP, #70H
        ; A = low1, B = high1
        MOV     A, 30H         ; A = low byte of num1
        ADD     A, 32H         ; A = low1 + low2
        MOV     40H, A         ; store sum_low
        ; Now handle high bytes with carry
        MOV     A, 31H         ; A = high1
        ADDC    A, 33H         ; A = high1 + high2 + CY
        MOV     41H, A         ; store sum_high
        ; Carry out of 16-bit addition goes to byte 42H
        MOV     42H, #00H
        JNC     DONE
        MOV     42H, #01H
DONE:   SJMP    $
        END
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Cycle count (per line):

| Instruction | MC |
|---|---|
| `MOV SP, #70H` | 1 |
| `MOV A, 30H` | 1 |
| `ADD A, 32H` | 1 |
| `MOV 40H, A` | 1 |
| `MOV A, 31H` | 1 |
| `ADDC A, 33H` | 1 |
| `MOV 41H, A` | 1 |
| `MOV 42H, #00H` | 1 |
| `JNC DONE` (taken, no carry) | 2 |
| `SJMP $` | 2 |
| **Total** | **12 MC** |

For f<sub>osc</sub> = 12 MHz: $T_{MC} = 12 / 12 \times 10^6 = 1\,\mu s$.

$$T_{total} = 12 \times 1 \,\mu s = 12 \,\mu s$$

If a carry *is* generated, add 1 MC for `MOV 42H, #01H`:

$$T_{total,\,carry} = 13 \,\mu s$$

---

### **Question B-B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** *Write an 8051 ALP to find the largest number in an array of 10 bytes stored starting at internal RAM location 30H. Store the largest value at 50H.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Modify the program to also store the **index** of the largest element in 51H.*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     SP, #70H
        MOV     R2, #09H       ; remaining count (N-1 comparisons)
        MOV     R0, #30H       ; pointer to first element
        MOV     A, @R0         ; A = first element (assumed largest)
        INC     R0
LOOP:   MOV     B, @R0
        CJNE    A, B, CHECK
CHECK:  JNC     NOSWAP         ; if A >= B, A remains largest
        MOV     A, B           ; else B is new largest
NOSWAP: INC     R0
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP
        MOV     50H, A
DONE:   SJMP    $
        END
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Add Index Tracking

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     SP, #70H
        MOV     R2, #09H       ; remaining comparisons
        MOV     R0, #30H
        MOV     R1, #00H       ; R1 = index of current largest
        MOV     A, @R0         ; A = largest so far
        MOV     R3, #01H       ; R3 = current index, starts at 1
LOOP:   MOV     B, @R0
        CJNE    A, B, CHECK
CHECK:  JNC     NOSWAP
        MOV     A, B
        MOV     R1, R3         ; save new index
NOSWAP: INC     R0
        INC     R3
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP
        MOV     50H, A
        MOV     51H, R1
DONE:   SJMP    $
        END
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Cycle Count Mistakes:** Always count `DJNZ` as **2 MC** (not 1) inside a delay loop. Off-by-one errors here cost up to 2 marks.
> 2. **Stack Pointer Initialization:** A 14-mark program that omits `MOV SP, #70H` typically loses **1 mark** for failing to demonstrate a valid stack (subroutine calls would corrupt register bank 0).
> 3. **Reset Vector Omission:** A program that does not begin with `ORG 0000H` and `LJMP MAIN` is graded as incomplete in real KTU valuation — students lose a flat 2 marks.
> 4. **Carry Flag Handling in Multi-Byte Add:** Using `ADD` instead of `ADDC` for the high byte of a multi-byte addition is the most common partial-mark-losing bug.
> 5. **`CJNE` Behavior Misunderstanding:** `CJNE` always sets the carry flag (based on unsigned compare), even when the operands are equal. Board answers that treat carry as undefined after CJNE are marked wrong.
> 6. **BCD Correction Misuse:** Applying `DA A` after `INC` or `SUBB` (without first clearing C when needed) loses 1 mark in the Arithmetic/Logical unit questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Writing Assembly Language Programs)**

- **Four-field source line:** `[Label:] [Mnemonic] [Operands] [; Comment]` — column-1 labels, comments after `;`.
- **Two-pass assembler:** Pass 1 → symbol table; Pass 2 → machine code.
- **Mandatory reset vector:** Always include `ORG 0000H` then `LJMP MAIN`.
- **Five instruction families:** Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logical, Boolean (Bit), Branching.
- **Five addressing modes:** Immediate (`#`), Register (`Rn`), Direct (`addr`), Register Indirect (`@Ri`), Indexed (`@A+DPTR`).
- **Multiplication result** of `MUL AB` is in **B (high) : A (low)** — 4 MC.
- **Division result** of `DIV AB` — **A = quotient, B = remainder** — 4 MC.
- **`DA A`** is BCD correction — apply **only after** `ADD` or `ADDC`.
- **Stack grows upward** in 8051; `PUSH` increments SP first, then writes.
- **`DJNZ` consumes 2 machine cycles** — critical for delay calculations.
- **One machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods.** $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{osc}$.
- **Jump ranges:** `SJMP` ±127, `AJMP` 2 KB page, `LJMP` full 64 KB.
- **Look-up tables** use `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` (code ROM) or `MOVX A, @DPTR` (external data RAM).
- **Bit-addressable RAM** is 20H–2FH (128 bits); SFR bits are addressable as 80H–FFH with bit 0–7.
- **Subroutine call:** `ACALL` / `LCALL` push 16-bit return address; `RET` pops it.
- **Interrupt return:** Use **`RETI`** (not `RET`) — `RETI` also re-enables the interrupt logic.
- **Common automotive ALP uses:** Crankshaft de-bounce, fuel-injection LUT, RPM-to-PWM conversion, CAN message filtering, OBD-II diagnostic response generation.
- **Killer timing formula for delays:** $T_d = ( \text{outer loops} \times \text{inner cycles} + \text{overhead} ) \times T_{MC}$.
- **Pin direction:** Ports default to **FFH (input)** after reset — set to 0 for output.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming Techniques: Looping, Counting, and Indexing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Programming Techniques: Looping, Counting, and Indexing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Programming Techniques in 8051 Assembly

In the context of automotive microcontrollers (8051 family derivatives such as P89V51RD2, AT89C51, and automotive-grade variants used in ECU sub-systems), **Programming Techniques** refers to the structured use of three foundational constructs:

| Construct | Purpose | Primary 8051 Instruction(s) |
|-----------|---------|------------------------------|
| **Looping** | Repeat a block of code multiple times | `DJNZ`, `SJMP`, `CJNE`, `JZ`/`JNZ` |
| **Counting** | Track occurrences of events / bit positions | `INC`, `DJNZ`, `RLC`, `JNC`, `JC` |
| **Indexing** | Access array / lookup-table elements via an offset | `MOVC A,@A+DPTR`, `MOVC A,@A+PC`, `JMP @A+DPTR` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> *"Programming techniques – looping, counting and indexing – using 8051 instruction set for embedded automotive control tasks."*
> These three techniques together enable the microcontroller to **process arrays of sensor data, generate time delays, and implement lookup-based control laws** (e.g., fuel injection timing maps).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

### Looping — The Bucket-Filling Analogy
Imagine filling **N identical buckets of water from a tap**.
- You pick up the *N-th* bucket → fill it → decrement N → repeat until N = 0.
- In 8051, the **register (e.g., R7)** acts as the counter, and `DJNZ R7, target` is the "fill one bucket, update counter" step.

### Counting — The Coin-Tallying Analogy
A cashier counts coins in a pile: *pick the top coin → check if it is a "1" → add to tally → move to next coin → repeat 8 times for 8-bit byte*. The 8051 `RLC A` + `JNC` combination rotates each bit of the byte into the **Carry flag**, then increments the tally if the bit was `1`.

### Indexing — The Restaurant Menu Analogy
Imagine a **single-page menu** (the lookup table) where each row number corresponds to a dish. If the chef knows the **base page address** (`DPTR`) and the **row number** (`A`), the dish is found at `base + row`. `MOVC A,@A+DPTR` does exactly this in code memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Automotive Embedded Systems:**
> - Looping → generates precise **time delays** for injector pulse-width control.
> - Counting → counts **crank-shaft pulses** for RPM estimation.
> - Indexing → accesses **calibration lookup tables** (e.g., throttle-vs-fuel map).

---

## 1.3 Formal Definitions (KTU Board-Examiner Standard)

**Looping:** A control-flow construct in which a sequence of instructions is executed repeatedly until a termination condition is met. In 8051, looping is implemented via decrement-and-jump-if-not-zero (`DJNZ`) or conditional-jump (`CJNE`, `JZ`, `JNC`) instructions that branch the Program Counter (PC) backward to the loop label.

**Counting:** The process of incrementing or decrementing a register/memory location by `1` for every detected event (e.g., a `1`-bit, an external pulse, or an array element) until a defined terminal value is reached.

**Indexing:** A addressing mode in which the effective address of the operand is computed as the **sum of a 16-bit base register (DPTR or PC)** and an **8-bit offset held in the Accumulator (A)**. The computed address points to a byte in program (code) memory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Looping — Operational Breakdown

The most common 8051 looping primitive is the `DJNZ` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero) instruction. Its semantics are:

```
DJNZ  Rn , rel_addr   ; Rn ← Rn − 1 ; if (Rn ≠ 0) PC ← PC + rel_addr
```

**Operational sequence (each iteration):**

1. Fetch the byte at PC, decode as `DJNZ Rn, rel` (2-byte instruction for register form).
2. Decrement `Rn` internally.
3. Test the **Zero flag** (which is set by ALU on decrement).
4. If Zero flag is **NOT set** (i.e., result was non-zero) → update PC = PC + signed `rel` offset.
5. If Zero flag is **set** → fall through to next instruction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** The decrement happens **before** the branch test. Therefore, if you load `Rn = N`, the body of the loop executes exactly **N times** (one final DJNZ exits with no jump when `Rn` has been decremented to `0`).

### Variations of Looping in 8051

| Loop Type | Instruction Pattern | Use Case |
|-----------|---------------------|----------|
| Fixed-count loop | `MOV Rn, #N` + `DJNZ Rn, label` | Delay generation, fixed repetition |
| Conditional loop | `CJNE A, direct, label` + `SJMP exit` | Compare-and-loop |
| Sentinel loop | `JZ label` / `JNZ label` testing a flag bit | Polling-style waiting |
| Infinite loop | `HERE: SJMP HERE` | Idle / main task scheduler |

---

## 2.2 Counting — Operational Breakdown

Counting in 8051 is **event-driven**. Each event triggers an `INC` (or `DJNZ`-based decrement) on a dedicated counter register. The classic technique is **bit-counting** of a byte:

1. Load the byte into `A`.
2. Initialise a count register (e.g., `R2 = 0`) and a bit-position counter (e.g., `R1 = 8`).
3. **Rotate** `A` left through carry (`RLC A`) → MSB enters CY flag.
4. Branch on carry: `JNC skip` → if CY = 0, do not increment tally.
5. If CY = 1 → `INC R2`.
6. `DJNZ R1, step3` → repeat 8 times.

> [!NOTE]
> **Counting is the dual of Looping:** Looping *drives* iteration count; Counting *measures* events inside the loop. The two always appear together.

---

## 2.3 Indexing — Operational Breakdown

The 8051 supports **two indexed addressing modes**, both designed to fetch from **program (code) memory**:

### Mode 1: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`

```
Effective Address = (DPTR) + (A)
A ← Code[ (DPTR) + (A) ]
```

- `DPTR` is a 16-bit base address (0x0000 to 0xFFFF).
- `A` is an 8-bit offset (0x00 to 0xFF).
- Used when the table is **located far away** or when the base address is computed dynamically.

### Mode 2: `MOVC A, @A+PC`

```
Effective Address = (PC) + 1 + (A)     ; PC has been auto-incremented during fetch
A ← Code[ (PC) + 1 + (A) ]
```

- Used when the table is **near the current instruction** (PC-relative).
- Saves the DPTR register for other uses.
- Offset is *limited* to about +127 bytes forward of the next instruction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall — PC-relative indexing:**
> The PC used in the calculation is **NOT** the PC at the start of the `MOVC` instruction, but the PC of the **next instruction** (i.e., the value AFTER the 1-byte `MOVC` is fetched). Forgetting this "+1" is the #1 cause of incorrect lookup table values in board exams.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | Machine Cycle (MC) | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | seconds; **12 oscillator periods per MC** |
| 2 | Instruction Cycle | $T_{inst} = k \cdot T_{MC}$ | $k$ = # of MCs for the instruction |
| 3 | DJNZ timing (register) | $T_{DJNZ} = T_{MC}$ (no jump) or $2 \cdot T_{MC}$ (jump taken) | seconds |
| 4 | Simple-loop total MCs | $C_{loop} = 1 + N \cdot (c_{body} + 2) + 1$ | cycles; $N$ = initial counter, $c_{body}$ = body cycles per iter |
| 5 | Nested-loop total MCs | $C_{nest} = 1 + M \cdot [ 1 + N \cdot (c_{body} + 2) + 1 + 2 ] + 1$ | cycles; $M$ = outer counter |
| 6 | Total Delay | $T_{delay} = C_{total} \cdot T_{MC}$ | seconds |
| 7 | Max counter value (8-bit) | $N_{max} = 2^{8} - 1 = 255$ | unsigned register |
| 8 | Indexed address | $Addr_{eff} = (DPTR) + (A)$ | 16-bit code-memory address |
| 9 | Indexed byte fetch | $A \leftarrow \text{Code}[Addr_{eff}]$ | content of effective address |
| 10 | Max indexable offset (DPTR mode) | $A_{max} = 0xFF = 255$ | bytes |
| 11 | PC-relative effective address | $Addr_{eff} = (PC) + 1 + (A)$ | where $PC$ = address of `MOVC` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference values for 11.0592 MHz crystal (used in KTU lab boards):**
> - $T_{MC} = 12 / 11.0592 \times 10^{6} = 1.085\,\mu s$
> - One full DJNZ loop iteration (body = 1 NOP) ≈ $3 \times 1.085 = 3.255\,\mu s$
> - Maximum delay with single 8-bit counter and 1 NOP body ≈ $830\,\mu s$
> - For delays > 1 ms → **nested loops** are mandatory.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Automotive Engineering Utility

| Technique | Automotive Subsystem Example |
|-----------|------------------------------|
| Looping | Generating a **2 ms ADC-sampling window** for crank-position sensor |
| Counting | Tallying **TDC pulses per second** for engine RPM = (count × 60) / encoder-teeth |
| Indexing | Looking up the **injector pulse-width** from a throttle-position × RPM map |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Time Delay via Looping (Single DJNZ)

**Problem:** Design an 8051 assembly delay routine producing approximately **1 ms** using an 11.0592 MHz crystal and a single DJNZ loop with two NOPs in the body.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the machine-cycle period.**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

**Step 2 — Enumerate machine cycles per iteration.**

For the loop body
```
BACK: NOP          ; 1 MC
       NOP          ; 1 MC
       DJNZ R7, BACK ; 2 MC (jump taken)
```
each *executed* iteration consumes $c_{body} + 2 = 2 + 2 = 4$ MC.

The final DJNZ (when R7 becomes 0) does *not* take the jump, costing only **1 MC** for the DJNZ itself (plus the body's 2 NOPs in the last iteration — already counted in the N×c_{body} part).

**Step 3 — General formula for total MC.**

$$C_{total} = \underbrace{1}_{\text{MOV}} + N \cdot \underbrace{(c_{body} + 2)}_{\text{iter MC, jump taken}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{final DJNZ no-jump}}$$

Wait — the last iteration's body NOPs must still execute, so the more accurate formula is:

$$C_{total} = 1 + N \cdot (c_{body} + 2) + 1 \quad \text{where } N = \text{initial counter}$$

For $c_{body} = 2$ NOPs:

$$C_{total} = 2 + N \cdot 4$$

**Step 4 — Solve for N to achieve T_delay = 1 ms = 1000 µs.**

$$T_{delay} = C_{total} \cdot T_{MC}$$

$$1000\,\mu s = (2 + 4N) \cdot 1.085\,\mu s$$

$$2 + 4N = \frac{1000}{1.085} = 921.66$$

$$4N = 919.66$$

$$N = 229.9 \approx 230$$

**Step 5 — Verify by back-substitution.**

$$C_{total} = 2 + 4 \times 230 = 922 \text{ MC}$$

$$T_{delay} = 922 \times 1.085\,\mu s = 1000.37\,\mu s \approx 1.00 \text{ ms} \;\; \checkmark$$

### Final Assembly Code

```asm
        MOV  R7, #230        ; 1 MC, load counter
BACK:   NOP                  ; 1 MC
        NOP                  ; 1 MC
        DJNZ R7, BACK        ; 2 MC (jump) / 1 MC (exit)
        RET                  ; total ≈ 1 ms @ 11.0592 MHz
```

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Counting 1-Bits in a Byte (Looping + Counting Fusion)

**Problem:** The byte `10110101B` (0xB5) is stored in internal RAM location `30H`. Write an 8051 program to count the number of `1`s and store the result in `31H`.

### Step-by-Step Walkthrough

| Step | Instruction | Effect | MC |
|------|-------------|--------|----|
| 1 | `MOV A, 30H` | Load 0xB5 (= 10110101B) into A | 1 |
| 2 | `MOV R1, #08H` | Bit-position counter = 8 | 1 |
| 3 | `MOV R2, #00H` | Tally counter = 0 | 1 |
| 4 | `BACK: RLC A` | Rotate left through carry → CY ← old bit7 | 1 |
| 5 | `JNC SKIP` | If CY = 0, skip the increment | 2 |
| 6 | `INC R2` | Else, increment tally | 1 |
| 7 | `SKIP: DJNZ R1, BACK` | Loop until 8 bits processed | 2 |
| 8 | `MOV 31H, R2` | Store final count | 2 |

### Iteration Trace for Byte `0xB5 = 10110101B`

| Iteration | A before RLC | A after RLC | CY (old bit7) | Tally (R2) |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|---------------|------------|
| 1 | 10110101 | 01101010 | 1 | 1 |
| 2 | 01101010 | 11010100 | 0 | 1 |
| 3 | 11010100 | 10101001 | 1 | 2 |
| 4 | 10101001 | 01010011 | 1 | 3 |
| 5 | 01010011 | 10100110 | 0 | 3 |
| 6 | 10100110 | 01001101 | 1 | 4 |
| 7 | 01001101 | 10011010 | 0 | 4 |
| 8 | 10011010 | 00110101 | 1 | 5 |

**Result:** R2 = `5` (five 1-bits in 0xB5) → stored in `31H`. ✔ Correct.

### Final Assembly Code

```asm
        ORG  0000H
        MOV  A, 30H        ; Load byte
        MOV  R1, #08H      ; Loop counter = 8 bits
        MOV  R2, #00H      ; Tally = 0
BACK:   RLC  A             ; Rotate MSB into CY
        JNC  SKIP          ; If bit was 0, skip
        INC  R2            ; Else count it
SKIP:   DJNZ R1, BACK      ; Repeat 8 times
        MOV  31H, R2       ; Store result
        END
```

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Indexing with `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` (Lookup Table)

**Problem:** A 7-segment display code table for digits `0`–`9` is stored in code memory starting at address `0500H`. Given an input digit in `A` (range 0–9), fetch the corresponding segment code.

### Step-by-Step Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Setup DPTR with table base address.**

```asm
        MOV  DPTR, #0500H  ; DPTR = base address of table
```

**Step 2 — Verify A holds the index.**

Assume `A = 04H` (digit 4).

**Step 3 — Execute indexed fetch.**

```asm
        MOVC A, @A+DPTR    ; A ← Code[ DPTR + A ] = Code[0504H]
```

**Effective address computation:**

$$Addr_{eff} = (DPTR) + (A) = 0x0500 + 0x04 = 0x0504$$

**Step 4 — Content at `0x0504H`** (from table below) = `0x66H` → `A = 0x66`.

### Final Assembly Code

```asm
        ORG   0000H
        MOV   DPTR, #TABLE  ; Load table base
        MOV   A,   #04H     ; Index = 4
        MOVC  A,   @A+DPTR  ; Fetch segment code for '4'
HERE:   SJMP  HERE          ; Stop (idle loop)

        ORG   0500H
TABLE:  DB    0C0H, 0F9H, 0A4H, 0B0H, 99H   ; 0..4
        DB    92H,  82H,  0F8H, 80H,  90H    ; 5..9
        END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DPTR-mode, not PC-mode here?**
> The table is **far** from the `MOVC` instruction (offset > 127 bytes). The PC-relative mode `MOVC A,@A+PC` cannot reach `0500H` from `0000H`. Hence the DPTR base is mandatory.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — PC-Relative Indexing (Compact Case)

**Problem:** Read a digit `0`–`3` from Port P1 and display its 7-segment code using `MOVC A, @A+PC`.

```asm
        ORG   0000H
        MOV   A,   P1        ; A = input digit (0..3)
        ANL   A,   #03H      ; Mask to keep only bits 1:0
        MOVC  A,   @A+PC     ; A ← Code[ (PC at next instr) + A ]
TABLE:  DB    0C0H          ; '0' code
        DB    0F9H          ; '1' code
        DB    0A4H          ; '2' code
        DB    0B0H          ; '3' code
```

### Effective Address Derivation

When `MOVC A,@A+PC` executes:
- PC has been auto-incremented to point to `TABLE:` (the next instruction after `MOVC`).
- $Addr_{eff} = (PC) + (A)$
- If `A = 0` → fetches `0xC0` (first DB).
- If `A = 1` → fetches `0xF9` (second DB), and so on.

This compact form saves the DPTR register but **requires the table to immediately follow** the `MOVC` instruction.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Choosing a Technique

| Scenario | Recommended Technique | Why |
|----------|-----------------------|-----|
| Need to wait N microseconds | Single DJNZ loop | Simplest, minimal register use |
| Need delay > 1 ms | Nested DJNZ loops | 8-bit counter is too small |
| Count events from a flag | Polling + INC inside loop | Event-driven counting |
| Count bits in a byte | RLC + JNC + INC | Bit-serial processing |
| Look up constant table < 256 entries | `MOVC A,@A+DPTR` | Universal, base anywhere in 64 KB |
| Look up compact table near code | `MOVC A,@A+PC` | Saves DPTR, smaller footprint |
| Branch to one of N routines | `JMP @A+DPTR` | Indexed jump (rare, but valid) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — DJNZ Loop Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B["MOV R7, #N  (load counter)"]
    B --> C{"Loop Label: BACK"}
    C --> D["Execute loop body  (e.g. NOP, NOP)"]
    D --> E["DJNZ R7, BACK  (Rn = Rn - 1)"]
    E --> F{"R7 = 0 ?"}
    F -- "No  (R7 ≠ 0)" --> C
    F -- "Yes (R7 = 0)" --> G["Fall through to next instruction"]
    G --> H([END of loop])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Indexed Addressing (`MOVC A, @A+DPTR`)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REG_A["Accumulator A  (8-bit offset, 0x00 to 0xFF)"]
    DPTR["DPTR register  (16-bit base, 0x0000 to 0xFFFF)"]
    ADDER["16-Bit Binary Adder  inside ALU"]
    CODE["Code Memory  (Program ROM, 64 KB)"]
    REG_A_OUT["Byte fetched  (written back to A)"]

    REG_A --> ADDER
    DPTR --> ADDER
    ADDER -->|"16-bit effective address"| CODE
    CODE --> REG_A_OUT
```

**Read this block diagram as:** *(A) + (DPTR)* → effective code-memory address → byte at that address → loaded into A.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Combined Counting + Looping Architecture (Bit-Counter)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0(["Power-On / RESET"])
    P0 --> S1["Load byte from 30H into A"]
    S1 --> S2["R1 = 08H  (bit-position counter)"]
    S2 --> S3["R2 = 00H  (1-bit tally)"]
    S3 --> LOOPSTART{"Loop Begin"}

    LOOPSTART --> S4["RLC A  (shift MSB into CY)"]
    S4 --> S5{"CY flag = 1 ?"}
    S5 -- "Yes" --> S6["INC R2  (tally++)"]
    S5 -- "No"  --> S7["skip increment"]
    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S8["DJNZ R1, LoopBegin"]
    S8 --> S9{"R1 = 0 ?"}
    S9 -- "No"  --> LOOPSTART
    S9 -- "Yes" --> S10["Store R2 into 31H"]
    S10 --> P1(["HALT"])
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Nested Loop for Long Delays

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Outer_Init: MOV R7, #M
    Outer_Init --> Inner_Init: MOV R6, #N
    Inner_Init --> Inner_Body: execute body  (e.g. NOP)
    Inner_Body --> Inner_Dec: DJNZ R6, Inner_Init
    Inner_Dec --> Inner_Body: R6 ≠ 0
    Inner_Dec --> Outer_Dec: R6 = 0
    Outer_Dec --> Outer_Init: DJNZ R7, Outer_Init
    Outer_Dec --> [*]: R7 = 0  (exit)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]
**Explain the `DJNZ` instruction of 8051 with a suitable example. Show how it can be used to generate a time delay.**

**Model Answer (Board Key Pattern):**

The `DJNZ` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero) instruction is a 2-byte instruction that performs two operations atomically:

1. Decrements the contents of the specified register `Rn` by `1`.
2. If the new value is **not zero**, the Program Counter is updated with a signed relative offset (`rel`), causing a **backward jump** to a labelled loop.

**Opcode format:** `DJNZ Rn, rel` (e.g., `DJNZ R7, BACK`).

**Example — Delay loop using two NOPs:**

```asm
        MOV   R7, #230    ; Load counter
BACK:   NOP                ; 1 MC
        NOP                ; 1 MC
        DJNZ  R7, BACK     ; 2 MC if jump, 1 MC if no jump
        RET
```

**Delay calculation (11.0592 MHz crystal):**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \text{ MHz}} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

Total MC $= 1 + 230 \times 4 + 1 = 922$ → Delay $= 922 \times 1.085\,\mu s \approx 1.00$ ms.

The loop body executes **230 times**, after which `R7 = 0` and execution falls through to `RET`. *(3 marks — full marks awarded for: definition 1, opcode 1, example + delay 1.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]
**What is indexed addressing mode in 8051? Differentiate between `MOVC A,@A+DPTR` and `MOVC A,@A+PC`.**

**Model Answer (Board Key Pattern):**

Indexed addressing mode computes the effective address of an operand as the **sum of a base register (DPTR or PC) and an 8-bit offset in the Accumulator (A)**. It is primarily used to access **lookup tables in code memory**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | `MOVC A, @A+PC` |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| Base register | 16-bit DPTR | 16-bit PC (auto-incremented) |
| Effective address | $(DPTR) + (A)$ | $(PC) + 1 + (A)$ |
| Range of base | Anywhere in 64 KB code memory | Limited to next-instruction vicinity |
| Use case | Large / distant tables | Compact tables adjacent to MOVC |
| DPTR preserved | Consumes DPTR | Saves DPTR for other use |

**Key Insight:** Both instructions read from **code memory** (the `C` in `MOVC` stands for *Code*). For external **data memory**, `MOVX` is used instead. *(3 marks — definition 1, table 2.)*

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** *Answer any ONE full question from Module 2. Each carries 14 marks and has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A — (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Write an 8051 assembly program to count the number of even numbers in a block of 10 bytes stored in internal RAM starting at location `40H`. Store the count in `R3`. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

```asm
        ORG  0000H
        MOV  R0, #40H       ; R0 = pointer to data block
        MOV  R1, #0AH       ; R1 = loop counter (10 bytes)
        MOV  R3, #00H       ; R3 = even-count = 0
        MOV  R2, #01H       ; R2 = 01H for AND mask (LSB test)

BACK:   MOV  A,  @R0        ; Load current byte
        ANL  A,  R2         ; Isolate LSB
        JNZ  SKIP           ; If LSB = 1 (odd), skip
        INC  R3             ; Else (even), increment count
SKIP:   INC  R0             ; Advance pointer
        DJNZ R1, BACK       ; Repeat 10 times
        END
```

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. `R0` is used as a **data pointer** to RAM location `40H`.
2. `R1 = 10` is the **loop counter** — this is *looping*.
3. For each byte, `ANL A, R2` (where `R2 = 01H`) tests the LSB.
4. A number is **even** iff LSB = 0. The `JNZ` instruction skips the increment if odd.
5. `INC R0` advances the pointer (this is **indexing via pointer**, a cousin of indexed addressing).
6. `DJNZ R1, BACK` closes the loop — this is **looping + counting** combined.
7. After 10 iterations, `R3` holds the number of even elements.

**Execution trace for sample data {45H, 12H, 33H, 78H, 90H, 11H, 0AH, 0FFH, 02H, 55H}:**

| Iter | Byte | LSB | Even? | R3 |
|------|------|-----|-------|----|
| 1 | 45H | 1 | No | 0 |
| 2 | 12H | 0 | Yes | 1 |
| 3 | 33H | 1 | No | 1 |
| 4 | 78H | 0 | Yes | 2 |
| 5 | 90H | 0 | Yes | 3 |
| 6 | 11H | 1 | No | 3 |
| 7 | 0AH | 0 | Yes | 4 |
| 8 | FFH | 1 | No | 4 |
| 9 | 02H | 0 | Yes | 5 |
| 10 | 55H | 1 | No | 5 |

Final R3 = **5** even numbers. ✔ *(7 marks — algorithm 2, code 3, trace 2.)*

#### (b) Explain indexed addressing mode in 8051. Write a program to find the square of a number (0–9) using a lookup table stored in code memory at address `0300H`. The input number is in Accumulator A. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Theory (4 Marks):**

Indexed addressing mode in 8051 is an addressing technique where the **effective address of an operand is computed as the sum of a 16-bit base register (DPTR or PC) and an 8-bit offset held in the Accumulator**. The result of this addition is used as the address of a byte in **program (code) memory**, and the content at that address is fetched into `A`.

The instruction mnemonic `MOVC` (Move Code) signals that the read is from code memory.

**Syntax:**

```asm
MOVC  A,  @A+DPTR      ; A ← Code[ (DPTR) + (A) ]
MOVC  A,  @A+PC        ; A ← Code[ (PC) + 1 + (A) ]
```

**Effective address formula:**

$$Addr_{eff} = (DPTR) + (A)$$

Since `A` is 8-bit, the table can have at most **256 entries**.

**Program (3 Marks):**

```asm
        ORG   0000H
        MOV   DPTR, #TABLE   ; Load base address 0300H
        ; Assume A already contains the number (0..9)
        MOVC  A,  @A+DPTR    ; A ← TABLE[number]
        MOV   R0, A          ; Save square in R0
HERE:   SJMP  HERE           ; Idle

        ORG   0300H
TABLE:  DB    00H, 01H, 04H, 09H, 10H  ; 0², 1², 2², 3², 4²
        DB    19H, 24H, 31H, 40H, 51H  ; 5², 6², 7², 8², 9²
        END
```

**Example:** If `A = 05H`, then `Addr_eff = 0x0300 + 0x05 = 0x0305` → contents at `0x0305` is `0x19` (= 25 decimal). R0 = 25. ✔

---

### **Question B — (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

#### (a) Design an 8051 assembly language program to generate a delay of approximately **50 ms** using a nested loop with two DJNZ instructions. Assume an 11.0592 MHz crystal. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute machine cycle period.**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

**Step 2 — Inner-loop formula.**

Inner loop (no extra NOPs, just DJNZ):

$$C_{inner} = 1 + N \cdot (0 + 2) + 1 = 2 + 2N$$

**Step 3 — Outer-loop formula (includes inner-loop MCs + outer DJNZ).**

$$C_{outer} = 1 + M \cdot ( C_{inner} + 2 ) + 1$$

(The outer DJNZ adds 2 MC per iteration; the outer MOV adds 1 MC initially.)

**Step 4 — Total MCs formula.**

$$C_{total} = 2 + M \cdot ( 4 + 2N ) = 2 + 4M + 2MN$$

**Step 5 — Solve for M = N = 255 (max 8-bit counters).**

$$C_{total} = 2 + 4 \times 255 + 2 \times 255 \times 255 = 2 + 1020 + 130050 = 131072$$

$$T_{delay} = 131072 \times 1.085\,\mu s = 142213\,\mu s \approx 142.2 \text{ ms}$$

That's *more* than 50 ms, so we can scale down.

**Step 6 — Solve for M and N giving exactly ~50 ms.**

Target: $T_{delay} = 50000\,\mu s$ → $C_{total} = 50000 / 1.085 \approx 46083$ MCs.

Trying N = 100:
$$C_{total} = 2 + 4M + 200M = 2 + 204M$$
$$204M = 46081 \Rightarrow M \approx 225.9$$

Try **N = 100, M = 226**:

$$C_{total} = 2 + 4 \times 226 + 2 \times 226 \times 100 = 2 + 904 + 45200 = 46106$$

$$T_{delay} = 46106 \times 1.085\,\mu s = 50028\,\mu s \approx 50.03 \text{ ms} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Final Program (3 Marks):**

```asm
DELAY_50MS:
        MOV   R6, #226       ; Outer counter M
OUTER:  MOV   R7, #100       ; Inner counter N
INNER:  DJNZ  R7, INNER      ; Inner loop (2 MC taken + 1 MC final)
        DJNZ  R6, OUTER      ; Outer loop
        RET
```

#### (b) Explain the role of DPTR in indexed addressing. Write an 8051 program to convert an ASCII hex digit stored in `R2` (e.g., '0'–'F') into its 4-bit binary equivalent stored in `R3`. Use a lookup table at code memory address `0200H`. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Role of DPTR (3 Marks):**

The **Data Pointer (DPTR)** is a 16-bit register composed of the high byte **DPH** and low byte **DPL**. Its primary roles in indexed addressing are:

1. **Holds the base address** of a lookup table located anywhere in the 64 KB code memory space (0x0000–0xFFFF).
2. **Combined with the Accumulator A** (8-bit offset), it forms a 16-bit effective address:

$$Addr_{eff} = (DPH:DPL) + (A)$$

3. **Decouples the table location from the code execution point**, making programs **relocatable** — the table can be moved in memory without rewriting the offset logic.
4. The DPTR is **not modified** by `MOVC A,@A+DPTR`; only `A` is overwritten with the fetched byte. To walk through the table, the programmer must explicitly `INC DPTR` between fetches.

**Conversion Table Design:**

ASCII '0' (0x30) → 0000,  '1' (0x31) → 0001, ..., '9' (0x39) → 1001,
'A' (0x41) → 1010,  'B' (0x42) → 1011, ..., 'F' (0x46) → 1111.

A direct 1:1 table of size 7 entries (for digits 0–9 and A–F) is simplest, but a **16-entry table mapped to ASCII codes** is more elegant. We'll use the latter.

**Program (4 Marks):**

```asm
        ORG   0000H
        MOV   A,   R2        ; A = ASCII hex digit (e.g., '7')
        ANL   A,   #0FH      ; Mask lower nibble: keep only 0..15
        MOV   DPTR, #TABLE   ; DPTR = 0200H
        MOVC  A,   @A+DPTR   ; A ← TABLE[ masked value ]
        MOV   R3,  A         ; Store 4-bit binary value in R3
HERE:   SJMP  HERE           ; Done

        ORG   0200H
TABLE:  DB    00H            ; index 0 → 0000
        DB    01H            ; index 1 → 0001
        DB    02H            ; index 2 → 0010
        DB    03H            ; index 3 → 0011
        DB    04H            ; index 4 → 0100
        DB    05H            ; index 5 → 0101
        DB    06H            ; index 6 → 0110
        DB    07H            ; index 7 → 0111
        DB    08H            ; index 8 → 1000
        DB    09H            ; index 9 → 1001
        DB    0AH            ; index 10 (A) → 1010
        DB    0BH            ; index 11 (B) → 1011
        DB    0CH            ; index 12 (C) → 1100
        DB    0DH            ; index 13 (D) → 1101
        DB    0EH            ; index 14 (E) → 1110
        DB    0FH            ; index 15 (F) → 1111
        END
```

**Trace for `R2 = '7'` (0x37):**
- After `ANL A, #0FH`: A = 0x07.
- `DPTR = 0x0200`.
- $Addr_{eff} = 0x0200 + 0x07 = 0x0207$ → content `0x07`.
- R3 = `0x07` (= 0111B) ✔.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" in PC-relative indexing.** Many students write `MOVC A,@A+PC` and forget that PC has already moved to the next instruction. Result: wrong byte fetched. **Penalty: up to 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing `MOVC` with `MOVX`.** `MOVC` = Code memory (program ROM). `MOVX` = eXternal data memory. Examiners are strict about this distinction.
> 3. **Off-by-one in DJNZ loop counters.** If you want N iterations, you load `Rn = N`, *not* `N-1`. The decrement happens *before* the jump test, so loading `N-1` gives only `N-1` iterations.
> 4. **Incorrect delay calculation units.** Always state machine-cycle time ($T_{MC} = 12/f_{OSC}$) explicitly. Examiners deduct marks if you omit the oscillator frequency assumption.
> 5. **Writing `MOV DPTR, #TABLE` instead of `MOV DPTR, #0200H`.** While assemblers resolve labels, board-exam model answers frequently use the explicit address — show *both* to be safe.
> 6. **Not initialising the tally counter (R2 = 0).** Many students forget this, leading to garbage on first iteration.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **`DJNZ Rn, label`** is the primary looping primitive in 8051; body executes **N times** if counter is initialised to **N**.
- [x] Machine cycle period $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{OSC}$. At **11.0592 MHz**, $T_{MC} = 1.085\,\mu s$ (a KTU favourite).
- [x] Maximum 8-bit counter = **255**; for longer delays use **nested DJNZ loops** (2-D counters).
- [x] `DJNZ` (register form) = **1 MC if no jump**, **2 MC if jump taken**. `DJNZ direct` = 2 MC / 3 MC.
- [x] Counting typically uses **RLC + JNC + INC** to tally 1-bits in a byte; loop runs exactly **8 times** for an 8-bit byte.
- [x] Indexed addressing uses **`MOVC A,@A+DPTR`** (universal) or **`MOVC A,@A+PC`** (compact, near-call-site).
- [x] **PC-relative mode effective address** = $(PC) + 1 + (A)$, where PC points to the instruction *after* `MOVC`.
- [x] `MOVC` reads from **code memory**; `MOVX` reads from external data memory; **never confuse them**.
- [x] **DPTR is preserved** after `MOVC A,@A+DPTR` — only `A` is overwritten.
- [x] 8-bit offset in A → max **256 entries** per table. For larger tables, **walk DPTR** with `INC DPTR`.
- [x] A canonical 8051 **delay loop** combines MOV (1 MC) + body (N iterations × cycles) + final DJNZ (1 MC).
- [x] Always **comment the units** of delay (µs vs ms vs s) and **state the crystal frequency** in exam answers.
- [x] The three techniques are **interdependent**: every counting routine contains a loop; every index-based routine often needs a loop to walk the table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Additional Data Transfer and 16-bit Arithmetic Instruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Additional Data Transfer and 16-bit Arithmetic Instruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMUT746, Module 2):**
> In the context of the **Intel 8086 / 8088 microprocessor** (the historical backbone of automotive ECU development), the term **"Additional Data Transfer Instructions"** refers to the complete family of data-movement instructions that extend beyond the basic `MOV` instruction. They govern the movement of **operands, flags, stack data, strings, and I/O port data** between registers, memory locations, and the outside world. The **"16-bit Arithmetic Instructions"** are the arithmetic-logic operations performed on 16-bit operands using the 8086's Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU), including addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, and BCD/ASCII adjustment instructions.

In modern automotive electronics (CAN-bus driven ECUs, OBD-II systems, Bosch ECU firmware), the foundational data movement and arithmetic instruction set is still taught using the 8086 architecture because it forms the basis of all x86 instruction sets used in industrial controllers and onboard diagnostic processors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Factory Conveyor Belt" Analogy**

Imagine an automobile assembly plant (your **ALU + Registers**):

- **Basic `MOV`** is like a simple conveyor belt moving a single car part from Station A to Station B.
- **Additional Data Transfer Instructions** are the *specialised robotic arms*:
  - The **Stack (`PUSH/POP`)** is like a vertical storage rack — you *push* a part onto the top rack and *pop* it back when needed (Last-In-First-Out, just like car keys pushed into a pile on a workbench!).
  - **String Instructions (`MOVS, STOS, LODS`)** are *automated pipelines* that repeatedly move batches of identical car seats without you writing a loop manually.
  - **`IN` / `OUT`** is the *loading dock* where parts arrive from the outside world (sensors) and finished products leave (to actuators).
  - **`XCHG`** is the *swapping arm* that exchanges parts between two stations in one motion.
  - **`XLAT`** is the *translator* that converts one part number (e.g., a sensor value) into another (e.g., a fuel-mapping lookup table value).

- **16-bit Arithmetic Instructions** are the *machine tools* on that assembly line: they *add* fuel-injection pulses, *subtract* feedback errors, *multiply* PWM duty cycles, *compare* two RPM values, and *adjust* BCD readings on your dashboard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Memorise:**
> - **8086 Data Bus Width:** **16 bits**
> - **8086 Address Bus Width:** **20 bits**
> - **ALU Size:** **16 bits**
> - **General Purpose Registers:** **8 × 16-bit (AX, BX, CX, DX, SP, BP, SI, DI)**
> - **Flag Register Size:** **16 bits (FLAGS)**
> - **Stack Word Size:** **16 bits** (even though memory is byte-addressable)

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Automotive Engineering

In automotive microcontrollers (MPC555, MPC567, Freescale/NXP PowerPC, and modern ARM Cortex-R derived automotive MCUs), the *philosophy* of data transfer and 16-bit arithmetic remains identical to 8086 because:

| Automotive Subsystem | Instruction Used (Conceptual) |
|---|---|
| Engine RPM calculation | `MUL` (multiplication of timer ticks) |
| Throttle Position Sensor reading | `IN` (from ADC port) |
| Fuel injector pulse width | `ADD`, `SHL` (16-bit arithmetic) |
| Dashboard BCD display (old cluster) | `DAA`, `AAA` (BCD adjustments) |
| Stack-based context switching | `PUSH` / `POP` |
| ECU communication buffers | `MOVS`, `STOSB` (string transfers) |
| Lookup tables for sensor calibration | `XLAT` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Additional Data Transfer Instructions

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the complete classification tree used in the **PEMUT746 Module 2** syllabus. Each instruction is fully broken down with operation, addressing modes, flags affected, and example.

### 2.1.1 Flag-Related Transfer Instructions

| Instruction | Full Name | Operation | Flags Affected |
|---|---|---|---|
| `LAHF` | Load AH from Flags | `AH ← (FLAGS & 00FFh)` — copies SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF into AH | None |
| `SAHF` | Store AH into Flags | `FLAGS & 00FFh ← AH` — copies AH into low byte of flags | SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF |
| `PUSHF` | Push Flags onto stack | `SP ← SP – 2; [SS:SP] ← FLAGS` | None |
| `POPF` | Pop Flags from stack | `FLAGS ← [SS:SP]; SP ← SP + 2` | All |

**Mnemonic Trick:** **"L**oad **A**H **F**rom flags" and "**S**tore **A**H **into** **F**lags".

### 2.1.2 Stack Operations

| Instruction | Operation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `PUSH src` | `SP ← SP – 2; [SS:SP] ← src` | 16-bit operand only (except `PUSHA` in 80186+) |
| `POP dst` | `dst ← [SS:SP]; SP ← SP + 2` | Destination cannot be CS in `8086` |

**Allowed `PUSH` sources:** Register (16-bit), segment register, memory (16-bit word).

### 2.1.3 Exchange Instruction

| Instruction | Operation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `XCHG operand1, operand2` | `operand1 ↔ operand2` | One operand must be a register; `XCHG AX, reg` has shorter encoding |
| `XLAT` / `XLATB` | `AL ← [BX + AL]` | Used for lookup table translation |

### 2.1.4 I/O Port Transfer Instructions

| Instruction | Operation | Address Range |
|---|---|---|
| `IN AL, port8` | 8-bit I/O read into AL | `00h – FFh` (direct) |
| `IN AX, port8` | 16-bit I/O read into AX | `00h – FFh` (direct) |
| `IN AL, DX` | 8-bit I/O read via DX | `0000h – FFFFh` (indirect) |
| `IN AX, DX` | 16-bit I/O read via DX | `0000h – FFFFh` (indirect) |
| `OUT port8, AL` | 8-bit I/O write from AL | `00h – FFh` (direct) |
| `OUT port8, AX` | 16-bit I/O write from AX | `00h – FFh` (direct) |
| `OUT DX, AL` | 8-bit I/O write via DX | `0000h – FFFFh` (indirect) |
| `OUT DX, AX` | 16-bit I/O write via DX | `0000h – FFFFh` (indirect) |

### 2.1.5 String Manipulation Instructions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **String Instructions use SI, DI, and the Direction Flag (DF).**
> - **SI** = source index (points to source in DS segment by default)
> - **DI** = destination index (points to destination in ES segment by default)
> - After each operation: if `DF = 0`, SI/DI auto-increment by operand size; if `DF = 1`, auto-decrement.
> - `CLD` sets `DF = 0` (auto-increment). `STD` sets `DF = 1` (auto-decrement).

| Instruction | Operation | Bytes/Element |
|---|---|---|
| `MOVSB` | `[ES:DI] ← [DS:SI]; SI±1; DI±1` | 1 byte |
| `MOVSW` | `[ES:DI] ← [DS:SI]; SI±2; DI±2` | 1 word (16-bit) |
| `LODSB` / `LODSW` | `AL/AX ← [DS:SI]; SI ± 1/2` | Read-only |
| `STOSB` / `STOSW` | `[ES:DI] ← AL/AX; DI ± 1/2` | Write-only |
| `CMPSB` / `CMPSW` | Compares `[DS:SI]` with `[ES:DI]`; SI/DI adjust | Compare only |
| `SCASB` / `SCASW` | `AL/AX – [ES:DI]; DI adjust` | Scan compare |
| `REP` prefix | Repeat while `CX ≠ 0` | Loop control |
| `REPE` / `REPZ` | Repeat while `CX ≠ 0` AND `ZF = 1` | Equal repeat |
| `REPNE` / `REPNZ` | Repeat while `CX ≠ 0` AND `ZF = 0` | Not-equal repeat |

---

## 2.2 Detailed Classification of 16-bit Arithmetic Instructions

### 2.2.1 Addition Instructions

| Instruction | Operation | Flags Affected |
|---|---|---|
| `ADD dst, src` | `dst ← dst + src` | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF |
| `ADC dst, src` | `dst ← dst + src + CF` | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF |
| `INC dst` | `dst ← dst + 1` | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF (**CF unaffected**) |
| `AAA` | ASCII Adjust After Addition | AF, CF (others undefined) |
| `DAA` | Decimal Adjust After Addition | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF |

### 2.2.2 Subtraction Instructions

| Instruction | Operation | Flags Affected |
|---|---|---|
| `SUB dst, src` | `dst ← dst – src` | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF |
| `SBB dst, src` | `dst ← dst – src – CF` | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF |
| `DEC dst` | `dst ← dst – 1` | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF (**CF unaffected**) |
| `NEG dst` | `dst ← –dst` (two's complement) | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF |
| `CMP dst, src` | `dst – src` (result discarded) | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF |
| `AAS` | ASCII Adjust After Subtraction | AF, CF |
| `DAS` | Decimal Adjust After Subtraction | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF |

### 2.2.3 Multiplication & Division

| Instruction | Operation | Operand Sizes | Result Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| `MUL src` | `AX ← AL × src8` (unsigned 8-bit) | 8-bit | 16-bit in AX |
| `MUL src` | `DX:AX ← AX × src16` (unsigned 16-bit) | 16-bit | 32-bit in DX:AX |
| `IMUL src` | Signed multiplication | 8 or 16-bit | Same as `MUL` |
| `DIV src` | `AL ← AX/src8; AH ← AX MOD src8` (unsigned) | 8-bit divisor | 8-bit quotient + remainder |
| `DIV src` | `AX ← DX:AX/src16; DX ← DX:AX MOD src16` | 16-bit divisor | 16-bit quotient + remainder |
| `IDIV src` | Signed division | 8 or 16-bit | Same as `DIV` |

### 2.2.4 Sign Extension Instructions

| Instruction | Operation |
|---|---|
| `CBW` | Sign-extend AL into AH (`AH ← 0x00` if `AL ≥ 0`, else `0xFF`) |
| `CWD` | Sign-extend AX into DX (`DX ← 0x0000` if `AX ≥ 0`, else `0xFFFF`) |

### 2.2.5 BCD/ASCII Adjustment Instructions Summary

| Instruction | Used After | Operation Logic |
|---|---|---|
| `AAA` | `ADD` (on ASCII bytes) | If low nibble of AL > 9 OR AF=1 → AL+=6, AH+=1, AF=CF=1, AL AND 0Fh |
| `AAS` | `SUB` (on ASCII bytes) | If low nibble of AL > 9 OR AF=1 → AL-=6, AH-=1, AF=CF=1, AL AND 0Fh |
| `AAM` | `MUL` (on unpacked BCD bytes) | AH ← AL/10, AL ← AL MOD 10 |
| `AAD` | Before `DIV` (unpacked BCD) | AL ← AH*10 + AL, AH ← 0 |
| `DAA` | `ADD` (on packed BCD bytes) | Adjusts AL for BCD using CF/AF |
| `DAS` | `SUB` (on packed BCD bytes) | Adjusts AL for BCD using CF/AF |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the high-yield cheat sheet you must memorise for KTU Module 2 questions. The symbols `\vert` and `\mid` are used to avoid breaking the table syntax — they represent absolute value / division bars.**

| Formula / Rule | Equation / Statement | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| Stack Pointer Decrement | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 2$ | `PUSH` instruction |
| Stack Pointer Increment | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 2$ | `POP` instruction |
| ADC Full Equation | $Dst_{new} = Dst_{old} + Src + CF$ | Multi-precision addition |
| SBB Full Equation | $Dst_{new} = Dst_{old} - Src - CF$ | Multi-precision subtraction |
| Overflow for Signed Add | $OF = C_{n-1} \oplus C_n$ | When MSB carries differ |
| Carry for Unsigned Add | $CF = C_{out}$ (carry out of MSB) | Unsigned overflow check |
| Carry for Unsigned Sub | $CF = \overline{Borrow_{out}}$ | `SUB`, `CMP`, `SBB` |
| Parity Flag | $PF = 1$ if low 8 bits have **even** number of 1s | All arithmetic/logic |
| MUL 8-bit Result | $AX = AL_{unsigned} \times src8_{unsigned}$ | `MUL reg8` or `MUL mem8` |
| MUL 16-bit Result | $\vert DX \vert AX = AX_{unsigned} \times src16_{unsigned}$ (concatenated) | `MUL reg16` or `MUL mem16` |
| DIV 8-bit | $AL = \mid AX \div src8 \mid, \; AH = AX \bmod src8$ | `DIV reg8` |
| DIV 16-bit | $AX = \mid DX\!:\!AX \div src16 \mid, \; DX = DX\!:\!AX \bmod src16$ | `DIV reg16` |
| NEG Operation | $Dst_{new} = \overline{Dst_{old}} + 1$ (two's complement) | `NEG` |
| CBW Rule | If $AL_{bit7} = 1 \Rightarrow AH = FF_{16}$ else $AH = 00_{16}$ | `CBW` |
| CWD Rule | If $AX_{bit15} = 1 \Rightarrow DX = FFFF_{16}$ else $DX = 0000_{16}$ | `CWD` |
| String Auto-Adjust | $SI_{new} = SI_{old} \pm n$ | where $n = 1$ (byte) or $n = 2$ (word) |
| CMP → SUB | $Flags$ set as if $Dst - Src$ were performed | `CMP dst, src` |
| INC does NOT affect CF | $CF_{after} = CF_{before}$ | `INC` only |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive Applications:**

1. **Engine Control Unit (ECU) Multi-byte Calculations**: Modern vehicles use 32-bit timer counters but earlier ECUs used 16-bit arithmetic (`AX` + `DX`) for cumulative fuel injection (e.g., 16-bit + 16-bit addition with carry between engine cycles).

2. **OBD-II Diagnostic Code Processing**: P-code parsing uses ASCII arithmetic adjustments (`AAA`) when converting alphanumeric error codes.

3. **Sensor Calibration Lookup**: `XLAT` instruction is conceptually identical to the lookup tables used in factory-calibrated IAT (Intake Air Temperature) sensors.

4. **CAN-Bus Frame Buffering**: `MOVSW`/`REP MOVSW` is conceptually identical to DMA-style block transfers used in modern CAN controllers when copying mailbox buffers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Derivations

### 3.1.1 Derivative 1: Multi-Precision 32-bit Addition using ADC

> **Problem:** Add two 32-bit numbers stored at memory locations `NUM1` and `NUM2`, store the 32-bit result at `SUM`. Show how `ADD` and `ADC` combine.

**Setup:**
- `NUM1` low word → at offset `[NUM1]`
- `NUM1` high word → at offset `[NUM1 + 2]`
- `NUM2` low word → at offset `[NUM2]`
- `NUM2` high word → at offset `[NUM2 + 2]`

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

```
Step 1: Load the low words into AX and BX
        MOV  AX, [NUM1]      ; AX ← low word of NUM1
        MOV  BX, [NUM2]      ; BX ← low word of NUM2
```

$$Result_{low} = AX_{NUM1} + BX_{NUM2}$$

$$CF_1 = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if carry out from bit 15} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

```
Step 2: Add the low words and store
        ADD  AX, BX          ; AX ← AX + BX, CF updated
        MOV  [SUM], AX       ; store low word of result
```

$$SUM_{low} = (AX_{NUM1} + BX_{NUM2}) \bmod 2^{16}$$

$$CF_{saved} = \lfloor (AX_{NUM1} + BX_{NUM2}) / 2^{16} \rfloor$$

```
Step 3: Load the high words
        MOV  AX, [NUM1 + 2]  ; AX ← high word of NUM1
        MOV  BX, [NUM2 + 2]  ; BX ← high word of NUM2
```

$$Sum_{high,temp} = AX_{NUM1,high} + BX_{NUM2,high}$$

$$Sum_{high,final} = AX_{NUM1,high} + BX_{NUM2,high} + CF_{saved}$$

```
Step 4: Add with carry and store
        ADC  AX, BX          ; AX ← AX + BX + CF
        MOV  [SUM + 2], AX   ; store high word
```

$$\boxed{Final\ 32\text{-}bit\ Sum = (High\ Word_{final} \cdot 2^{16}) + Low\ Word}$$

**Why ADC?**
The 8086's ALU is only 16 bits wide, so any 32-bit value must be split. The `ADD` of low words produces a *carry flag* representing the bit that "fell off the left side." `ADC` then includes that carry in the high-word addition, producing a mathematically correct 32-bit sum.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivative 2: 16-bit Unsigned Multiplication - Full Calculation Walkthrough

> **Problem:** Compute `MUL CX` given `AX = 0005h` and `CX = 0003h`. Show the register-level result and verify the flags.

**Step 1: Identify operand widths**
- Source `CX` is 16-bit → use 16-bit `MUL`.
- Result goes into `DX:AX` (32-bit).

**Step 2: Apply the formula**
$$DX\!:\!AX = AX_{unsigned} \times CX_{unsigned}$$

$$DX\!:\!AX = 0005_{16} \times 0003_{16}$$

$$DX\!:\!AX = 5_{10} \times 3_{10} = 15_{10} = 000F_{16}$$

**Step 3: Split into register pair**
$$AX = 000F_{16} \quad (low\ 16\ bits)$$
$$DX = 0000_{16} \quad (high\ 16\ bits)$$

**Step 4: Check flags**
- `CF = OF = 0` because the high word `DX = 0` (i.e., result fits in 16 bits).
- Other flags (SF, ZF, AF, PF) are **undefined** after `MUL`.

**General Rule:**
$$\text{If } DX \neq 0 \Rightarrow CF = OF = 1 \text{ (overflow occurred)}$$

---

### 3.1.3 Derivative 3: CBW Sign Extension Example

> **Problem:** Compute `CBW` when `AL = 9Ah`.

**Step 1: Check sign bit**
$$AL = 9A_{16} = 1001\,1010_2$$
$$AL_{bit7} = 1 \Rightarrow AL \text{ is negative (signed)}$$

**Step 2: Apply sign extension**
$$AH = FF_{16} = 1111\,1111_2$$

**Step 3: Verify combined AX**
$$AX = FF9A_{16} = -102_{10}\ in\ signed\ 16\text{-}bit\ interpretation$$

**Cross-check:**
$$AL = 9A_{16} = -102_{10}\ in\ signed\ 8\text{-}bit$$
$$FF9A_{16} = -102_{10}\ in\ signed\ 16\text{-}bit\ \checkmark$$

---

### 3.1.4 Derivative 4: PUSH/POP Stack Frame Mechanics

> **Problem:** Execute the sequence and show the stack frame at each step. Assume initial `SP = 0100h`.

```
Step 1: MOV AX, 1234h       ; AX = 1234h, SP = 0100h (unchanged)
Step 2: PUSH AX              ; AX = 1234h, SP becomes 00FEh
                              ; Memory at [SS:00FEh] = 34h (low byte)
                              ; Memory at [SS:00FFh] = 12h (high byte)
                              ; Note: little-endian storage!
Step 3: MOV BX, [SS:00FEh]   ; BX ← word at [SS:00FEh]
                              ; BX = 1234h (because little-endian reconstructs the word)
Step 4: POP CX               ; CX ← word from [SS:00FEh] = 1234h
                              ; SP becomes 0100h (restored)
```

**Mathematical representation of stack pointer evolution:**

$$SP_0 = 0100_h$$

$$SP_1 = SP_0 - 2 = 00FE_h \quad \text{(after PUSH)}$$

$$SP_2 = SP_1 + 2 = 0100_h \quad \text{(after POP)}$$

---

### 3.1.5 Derivative 5: DAA - Decimal Adjust After Addition Walkthrough

> **Problem:** Add `AL = 28h` (BCD 28) and `BL = 39h` (BCD 39) using `ADD` then `DAA`.

**Step 1: Perform ADD**
$$AL_{before} = 28_{16} = 0010\,1000_2$$
$$BL = 39_{16} = 0011\,1001_2$$

$$AL_{after\ ADD} = 28_{16} + 39_{16} = 61_{16} = 0110\,0001_2$$

**Step 2: Examine lower nibble**
$$Lower\ nibble\ of\ AL = 1$$
$$1 \leq 9 \Rightarrow \text{No adjustment needed for low nibble}$$

**Step 3: Examine upper nibble**
$$Upper\ nibble\ of\ AL = 6$$
$$6 \leq 9 \Rightarrow \text{No adjustment needed for high nibble (assuming } AF = 0, CF = 0)$$

**Step 4: DAA leaves AL unchanged**
$$AL_{final} = 61_{16} = 61\ (in\ BCD,\ which\ matches\ 28 + 39 = 67 ?)$$

⚠ **Wait — correction**: The proper BCD result should be `67h`, not `61h`. Let me re-trace:

$$AL_{after\ ADD} = 61_{16}$$
$$Low\ nibble = 1 \le 9 \Rightarrow no\ low\ adjustment$$
$$High\ nibble = 6 \le 9 \Rightarrow no\ high\ adjustment$$
$$Result\ AL = 61_{16}$$

**Re-checking the arithmetic:** `0x28 + 0x39 = 0x61` ✓ (this is mathematically correct as a binary add). 

To get the proper BCD result `67`, you should add unpacked BCD values like `02h + 08h = 0Ah` followed by `03h + 09h = 0Ch`, then combine. The `DAA` instruction is for **packed BCD** (where each byte holds two BCD digits).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DDA / DAA reminder:** If low nibble > 9 OR AF = 1 → AL += 06h, AF = 1. If high nibble > 9 OR CF = 1 → AL += 60h, CF = 1. In our example both nibbles were ≤ 9, so DAA was a no-op.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: 8086 Bus Interface Unit (BIU) and Execution Unit (EU) — Data Flow for the Topic Instructions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BIU [BUS INTERFACE UNIT]
        nodeQueue[Instruction Queue 6 bytes]
        nodeSeg[Segment Registers CS DS ES SS]
        nodeIP[Instruction Pointer IP]
        nodeAddr[Address Adder 20 bit]
    end
    subgraph EU [EXECUTION UNIT]
        nodeALU[ALU 16 bit]
        nodeRegs[General Registers AX BX CX DX]
        nodePtr[Pointer Index SP BP SI DI]
        nodeFlags[FLAGS Register 16 bit]
    end
    subgraph EXT [EXTERNAL BUS]
        nodeMem[Memory 1 MB]
        nodeIO[I O Ports 64K]
    end
    nodeQueue --> nodeAddr
    nodeSeg --> nodeAddr
    nodeIP --> nodeAddr
    nodeAddr --> nodeMem
    nodeAddr --> nodeIO
    nodeALU <--> nodeRegs
    nodeALU <--> nodeFlags
    nodeALU <--> nodePtr
    nodeRegs <--> nodeMem
    nodePtr <--> nodeMem
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram captures the architecture inside which **all the Additional Data Transfer and 16-bit Arithmetic Instructions** operate. The EU performs arithmetic using `nodeALU`, while the BIU fetches data from `nodeMem` and `nodeIO` (critical for `IN`/`OUT` and string instructions).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: PUSH/POP Stack Frame Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStart([Initial State SP points to top of stack])
    nodePUSH[PUSH AX executed]
    nodeDec[SP decrements by 2]
    nodeStore[High byte of AX stored at SS SP+1]
    nodeStore2[Low byte of AX stored at SS SP]
    nodeMid([Stack now contains AX])
    nodePop[POP BX executed]
    nodeRead[Word read from SS SP]
    nodeLoad[BX gets the word]
    nodeInc[SP increments by 2]
    nodeEnd([Original State Restored])
    nodeStart --> nodePUSH --> nodeDec --> nodeStore --> nodeStore2 --> nodeMid
    nodeMid --> nodePop --> nodeRead --> nodeLoad --> nodeInc --> nodeEnd
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: MUL/IMUL Flag Behaviour

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OperandLoad: AX loaded DX cleared
    OperandLoad --> Compute: Multiply in hidden register
    Compute --> CheckResult: Result size compared with 16 bits
    CheckResult --> HiZero: DX equals 0
    CheckResult --> HiNonZero: DX not equal 0
    HiZero --> FlagsOK: CF 0 OF 0
    HiNonZero --> FlagsOverflow: CF 1 OF 1
    FlagsOK --> [*]
    FlagsOverflow --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: ADC-Based 32-bit Add Operation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as AX Register
    participant B as BX Register
    participant F as FLAGS Register
    participant M as Memory SUM
    A->>A: Load low word of NUM1
    B->>B: Load low word of NUM2
    A->>A: ADD AX BX
    A->>F: Carry flag set if overflow
    A->>M: Store low word result
    A->>A: Load high word of NUM1
    B->>B: Load high word of NUM2
    A->>F: Read carry flag value
    A->>A: ADC AX BX adds with carry
    A->>M: Store high word result
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Decision Tree: DAA Adjustment Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeStart([After ADD instruction completes])
    nodeLowCheck{AL lower nibble greater than 9 OR AF equals 1}
    nodeLowAdjust[Add 06h to AL set AF to 1]
    nodeHighCheck{AL upper nibble greater than 9 OR CF equals 1}
    nodeHighAdjust[Add 60h to AL set CF to 1]
    nodeDone([AL now contains valid BCD])
    nodeStart --> nodeLowCheck
    nodeLowCheck -- yes --> nodeLowAdjust --> nodeHighCheck
    nodeLowCheck -- no --> nodeHighCheck
    nodeHighCheck -- yes --> nodeHighAdjust --> nodeDone
    nodeHighCheck -- no --> nodeDone
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
> *Explain the function of the `XLAT` / `XLATB` instruction in the 8086 microprocessor with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `XLAT` (Translate) instruction is a special table-lookup byte transfer instruction. It replaces the contents of register `AL` with a byte from a lookup table pointed to by the sum of registers `BX` and `AL`.

$$\text{Operation:} \quad AL \leftarrow [BX + AL]$$

**Example use case:** Converting a hexadecimal digit (0–15 in `AL`) into its 7-segment display code. Suppose `BX = 2000h` and the lookup table starts at memory address `2000h` containing the codes for digits 0–9.

```
MOV  BX, 2000h      ; BX points to table base
MOV  AL, 05h        ; digit to convert is 5
XLAT                ; AL ← [2005h] → 7-seg code for 5
```

After execution, `AL` contains the 7-segment code fetched from address `BX + AL = 2005h`. It is a one-byte instruction (opcode `D7h`) with no operand. **Marks awarded: definition (1) + formula (1) + example (1).**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – December 2023, Model Paper]**
> *Differentiate between the `MUL` and `IMUL` instructions of the 8086 microprocessor. Show the flag behaviour when `AX = 8000h` and `BX = 0002h`.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | `MUL` | `IMUL` |
|---|---|---|
| Sign of operands | Unsigned only | Signed (two's complement) |
| Example: `AX=8000h, BX=0002h` | `MUL`: `8000h × 0002h = 1\,0000h` → `AX=0000h, DX=0001h`, `CF=OF=1` | `IMUL`: `–32768 × 2 = –65536` (cannot fit in 16 bits) → overflow indicated by `OF=CF=1` |

**Flag behaviour for `MUL BX`:**
- $DX\!:\!AX = AX \times BX = 8000_{16} \times 0002_{16} = 1\,0000_{16}$
- Result stored: $DX = 0001_{16}$, $AX = 0000_{16}$
- Since $DX \neq 0$, both `CF` and `OF` are set to **1**, indicating the result did not fit in 16 bits.
- `SF`, `ZF`, `AF`, `PF` are **undefined** after `MUL`.

**Marks awarded: difference table (2) + flag derivation (1).**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> ### **Question A (14 marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Section]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with neat diagrams the various string instructions of the 8086 microprocessor. Discuss the role of the `DF` flag, `CLD`, and `STD` instructions.

**(b) [7 marks)** Write an 8086 assembly language program to move a block of 10 words from one memory location to another using the `MOVSW` instruction with the `REP` prefix. Explain each instruction.

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Solution:**

**String Instructions in 8086:**
The 8086 microprocessor provides five basic string instructions, each having a byte (`B`) and word (`W`) variant:

1. `MOVS` / `MOVSB` / `MOVSW` — Move String
2. `LODS` / `LODSB` / `LODSW` — Load String
3. `STOS` / `STOSB` / `STOSW` — Store String
4. `CMPS` / `CMPSB` / `CMPSW` — Compare String
5. `SCAS` / `SCASB` / `SCASW` — Scan String

**Default operand addressing:**
- **Source operand** is pointed to by `DS:SI` (Data Segment + Source Index).
- **Destination operand** is pointed to by `ES:DI` (Extra Segment + Destination Index).

**Direction Flag (DF) and its control instructions:**
- `DF = 0` (set by `CLD`) → indices auto-**increment** after each operation.
- `DF = 1` (set by `STD`) → indices auto-**decrement** after each operation.

**Detailed operation of each string instruction:**

| Instruction | Operation | Index Adjustment |
|---|---|---|
| `MOVSB` | `[ES:DI] ← [DS:SI]` | If DF=0: SI+=1, DI+=1; if DF=1: SI–=1, DI–=1 |
| `MOVSW` | `[ES:DI] ← [DS:SI]` (16-bit) | If DF=0: SI+=2, DI+=2; if DF=1: SI–=2, DI–=2 |
| `LODSB` | `AL ← [DS:SI]` | Adjust SI by ±1 |
| `LODSW` | `AX ← [DS:SI]` (16-bit) | Adjust SI by ±2 |
| `STOSB` | `[ES:DI] ← AL` | Adjust DI by ±1 |
| `STOSW` | `[ES:DI] ← AX` (16-bit) | Adjust DI by ±2 |
| `CMPSB` | Sets flags as if `[DS:SI] – [ES:DI]` | Adjust SI and DI by ±1 |
| `CMPSW` | Sets flags as if word compare | Adjust SI and DI by ±2 |
| `SCASB` | Sets flags as if `AL – [ES:DI]` | Adjust DI by ±1 |
| `SCASW` | Sets flags as if `AX – [ES:DI]` | Adjust DI by ±2 |

**REP Prefix:** `REP MOVSW` repeats `MOVSW` while `CX ≠ 0`, decrementing `CX` each iteration. `REPE`/`REPNE` add the `ZF` condition.

**(7 marks split: introduction + table (3) + DF/CLD/STD explanation (2) + REP prefix (2))**

---

**(b) Solution — Assembly Program:**

```
        ; Program to move 10 words from SOURCE to DESTINATION
        ; Assume: SOURCE is at offset 1000h in DS
        ;         DESTINATION is at offset 2000h in ES

        CLD                  ; DF = 0, indices will auto-increment (1 mark)
        MOV  AX, 1000h       ; Load source segment into DS (1 mark)
        MOV  DS, AX
        MOV  AX, 2000h       ; Load destination segment into ES (1 mark)
        MOV  ES, AX
        MOV  SI, 1000h       ; SI points to source offset (1 mark)
        MOV  DI, 2000h       ; DI points to destination offset (1 mark)
        MOV  CX, 000Ah       ; CX = 10 (count of words to move) (1 mark)
        REP  MOVSW           ; Repeat MOVSW until CX = 0 (1 mark)
        HLT                  ; Halt the processor
```

**Explanation:**
- `CLD` ensures forward direction.
- `REP MOVSW` performs the following loop: copies word at `[DS:SI]` to `[ES:DI]`, then increments `SI` and `DI` by 2, and decrements `CX`. Continues until `CX = 0`.
- After execution, all 10 words (20 bytes total) are copied.

**Marks awarded: instruction-by-instruction explanation with each line carrying partial marks (typically 1 per instruction used correctly).**

---

> ### **Question B (14 marks)** — [KTU University Exam – December 2023, Module 2 Section]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the 8086 instructions used for 16-bit arithmetic operations: `ADD`, `ADC`, `SUB`, `SBB`, `CMP`. Show how `ADC` is used for multi-precision addition with a numerical example.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat example, explain the `DAA` and `DAS` instructions. Why are these instructions still relevant in automotive dashboard designs?

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Solution:**

| Instruction | Syntax | Operation | Flags Affected |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ADD` | `ADD dst, src` | $dst \leftarrow dst + src$ | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF |
| `ADC` | `ADC dst, src` | $dst \leftarrow dst + src + CF$ | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF |
| `SUB` | `SUB dst, src` | $dst \leftarrow dst - src$ | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF |
| `SBB` | `SBB dst, src` | $dst \leftarrow dst - src - CF$ | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF |
| `CMP` | `CMP dst, src` | Computes `dst - src`, discards result, sets flags only | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF |

**Multi-precision addition example using ADC:**

*Add two 32-bit numbers: `12345678h` and `11121314h`*

```
        MOV  AX, 5678h       ; AX = low word of first number
        MOV  BX, 1234h       ; BX = high word of first number
        MOV  CX, 1314h       ; CX = low word of second number
        MOV  DX, 1211h       ; DX = high word of second number

        ADD  AX, CX          ; AX = 5678h + 1314h = 698Ch, CF = 0
        ADC  BX, DX          ; BX = 1234h + 1211h + 0 = 2445h

        ; Final result: BX:AX = 2445h : 698Ch = 2445 698C16
        ; Verification: 1234 567816 + 1211 131416 = 2445 698C16 ✓
```

**Key principle:** The `ADD` instruction sets `CF` when the low-word addition overflows. `ADC` then propagates that `CF` into the high-word addition, producing a correct 32-bit result using a 16-bit ALU.

**(Marks: instruction table (3) + numerical example (4) = 7)**

---

**(b) Solution:**

**DAA (Decimal Adjust AL after Addition):**

Adjusts the result of an `ADD` operation on packed BCD values to produce a valid packed BCD result.

**Algorithm:**
1. If lower nibble of `AL > 9` OR `AF = 1`, then `AL ← AL + 6` and `AF ← 1`.
2. If upper nibble of `AL > 9` OR `CF = 1`, then `AL ← AL + 0x60` and `CF ← 1`.

**Example:** `AL = 39h (BCD 39), BL = 48h (BCD 48)`

```
ADD  AL, BL          ; AL = 39h + 48h = 81h
DAA                  ; AL = 81h + 60h = E1h (but wait — check lower nibble)
```

*Re-trace carefully:*
- After `ADD`: `AL = 81h = 1000\,0001_2`
- Lower nibble = `1 ≤ 9`, AF = 0 → no low adjustment.
- Upper nibble = `8 ≤ 9`, CF = 0 → no high adjustment.
- Final `AL = 81h` ✓ (correct BCD 81).

Now consider `AL = 59h + 48h`:
- After `ADD`: `AL = 59h + 48h = A1h`
- Lower nibble `1 ≤ 9` → no low adjust.
- Upper nibble `A > 9` → `AL = A1h + 60h = 01h`, `CF = 1`.
- Final `AL = 01h` with carry `1` → meaning the correct BCD result is `101` (i.e., `59 + 48 = 107`).

**DAS (Decimal Adjust AL after Subtraction):**

Similar algorithm but performs subtraction correction:
- If lower nibble of `AL > 9` OR `AF = 1`, then `AL ← AL - 6` and `AF ← 1`.
- If upper nibble of `AL > 9` OR `CF = 1`, then `AL ← AL - 0x60` and `CF ← 1`.

**Relevance in Automotive Dashboards:**

> [!NOTE]
> Even today, automotive instrument clusters display information like **odometer readings, trip meters, fuel consumption**, and **engine hour counters** in **packed BCD format** because BCD-to-7-segment conversion is hardware-trivial (one decoder chip per digit). Although modern microcontrollers use C-code libraries, the legacy instruction set knowledge (`DAA`/`DAS`) is essential for engineers maintaining older ECUs and for designing low-level bootloaders in automotive-grade firmware where BCD math is more efficient than binary-coded decimal arithmetic libraries.

**(Marks: DAA algorithm + example (3.5) + DAS algorithm + automotive relevance (3.5) = 7)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mention `CX` for `REP` prefix**: Always state that `CX` holds the iteration count and is decremented each loop. **(–1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Confusing `MOVSB` addressing**: Students often forget that source is always at `DS:SI` and destination at `ES:DI` — NOT both at `DS`. **(–1 mark)**
>
> 3. **`MUL` flag statement error**: Writing "`MUL` affects all flags." Wrong — only `CF` and `OF` are defined; others are *undefined*. **(–1 to 2 marks)**
>
> 4. **`CBW` vs `CWD` confusion**: `CBW` sign-extends `AL` to `AX`. `CWD` sign-extends `AX` to `DX:AX`. Many students swap them. **(–1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Missing the `CF` propagation in `ADC`/`SBB`**: Forgetting to write the explicit equation `dst ← dst + src + CF` (not just `dst + src`). **(–1 mark)**
>
> 6. **`AAA` vs `DAA` confusion**: `AAA` is for ASCII (unpacked BCD), `DAA` is for packed BCD. Exam questions often test this distinction. **(–1 mark)**
>
> 7. **`INC` does NOT affect CF**: Students write "`INC` sets the carry flag." This is FALSE — `INC` preserves `CF`. **(–1 mark)**
>
> 8. **Order of bytes in `PUSH`**: When writing the memory layout after `PUSH AX`, remember the 8086 is **little-endian** — low byte stored at lower address. Wrong byte ordering loses **(–1 mark)**.
>
> 9. **String index adjustment size**: After `MOVSW`, indices change by **2**, not by 1. **(–1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Bullets — Memorise Before Exam:**

- **Additional Data Transfer Instructions** = Everything beyond basic `MOV`: `LAHF`, `SAHF`, `PUSHF`, `POPF`, `PUSH`, `POP`, `XCHG`, `XLAT`, `IN`, `OUT`, `MOVS`, `LODS`, `STOS`, `CMPS`, `SCAS`.
- **`PUSH`** decrements `SP` by 2 **before** storing.
- **`POP`** reads from `[SS:SP]` **then** increments `SP` by 2.
- **8086 is little-endian**: low byte at lower address in memory and on stack.
- **`XLAT`** performs `AL ← [BX + AL]` — a one-byte instruction (`D7h`).
- **`IN`/`OUT`** with 8-bit immediate port address use only the lower 256 ports; for 64K ports you must use `DX`.
- **String instructions** always use `DS:SI` (source) and `ES:DI` (destination).
- **`CLD` → DF = 0** (forward), **`STD` → DF = 1** (backward).
- **`REP`** repeats while `CX ≠ 0`. **`REPE`** repeats while `CX ≠ 0` AND `ZF = 1`. **`REPNE`** repeats while `CX ≠ 0` AND `ZF = 0`.
- **`MUL` (unsigned)**: `AX ← AL × src8` or `DX:AX ← AX × src16`. Result stored in **double-width** destination.
- **`IMUL` (signed)**: Same as `MUL` but treats operands as **two's complement**.
- **`DIV` (unsigned)**: `AL ← quotient`, `AH ← remainder` (8-bit divisor) or `AX ← quotient`, `DX ← remainder` (16-bit divisor).
- **`ADC`** and **`SBB`** are essential for **multi-precision arithmetic**.
- **`INC` does NOT modify CF**; `DEC` does NOT modify CF. (All other arithmetic flags are updated.)
- **`CBW`** sign-extends `AL` → `AX`. **`CWD`** sign-extends `AX` → `DX:AX`.
- **`NEG`** computes two's complement: `NEG x = NOT x + 1`.
- **`DAA`** adjusts packed BCD after `ADD`. **`DAS`** adjusts packed BCD after `SUB`.
- **`AAA`** adjusts unpacked ASCII BCD after `ADD`. **`AAS`** adjusts after `SUB`.
- **`AAM`** corrects BCD result after `MUL`. **`AAD`** prepares BCD dividend before `DIV`.
- **`CMP`** sets flags **as if** it performed a `SUB`, but discards the result.
- **`LAHF`** loads AH with the low 8 bits of FLAGS (SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF).
- **`SAHF`** is the inverse — stores AH back into those flag bits.
- **After `MUL`/`IMUL`, only `CF` and `OF` are defined** — all other flags are undefined.
- **After `DIV`/`IDIV`, all flags are undefined.**
- **8086 ALU is 16 bits wide**, but addresses are 20 bits (achieved via segment:offset).
- **Stack segment must be 16-byte aligned** for efficient `PUSH`/`POP`.
- **Automotive connection**: All these instructions form the instruction-set foundation used in ECU firmware, OBD-II scanners, dashboard controllers, and sensor calibration routines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logic Operation: Rotate, Compare, Counter and Time Delays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Logic Operation: Rotate, Compare, Counter and Time Delivers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Logic Operations in the 8051 Microcontroller

In the context of the **8051 Microcontroller** (the most widely used MCU in automotive ECUs, body controllers, instrument clusters, and ABS modules), **Logic Operations** refer to a set of instructions that manipulate individual bits, rotate bit patterns through registers, compare the magnitude of two operands, build binary counters, and generate precise hardware time delays — all without requiring complex arithmetic.

The four pillar operations covered in this module are:

1. **Rotate Instructions** (RL, RLC, RR, RRC)
2. **Compare Instruction** (CMP)
3. **Counters** (Software-implemented binary up/down counters)
4. **Time Delays** (Programmed delay loops using timers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT746 Module 2):**
> *"Study of 8051 Instructions – Logic Operations: Rotate, Compare, Counter and Time Delays."*
> The emphasis is on instruction-level behaviour, flag-register modification, delay calculation derivation, and counter design — all of which are recurring 14-mark KTU questions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

| Concept | Engineering Meaning | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotate Instruction** | Cyclically shift bits left/right (with or without carry) | A rotating circular conveyor belt where the last item moves to the first position |
| **Compare (CMP)** | Subtracts A from operand, discards result, only updates flags | A weighing scale — only tells you which side is heavier, not the weight itself |
| **Counter** | A register that increments or decrements with each event | The odometer of a car — it ticks up every kilometre and triggers an event (service) at 10,000 km |
| **Time Delay** | A known, repeatable loop that consumes a fixed number of clock cycles | The 30-second microwave countdown — the clock ticks until zero |

## 1.3 Bit-Level Orientation — Where Flags Live

The **Program Status Word (PSW)** register in the 8051 holds the arithmetic/logic flag bits that logic operations depend on:

> [!NOTE]
> **PSW Register (Address D0H):**
> - **CY (PSW.7)** — Carry Flag — the key flag for all rotate operations.
> - **AC (PSW.6)** — Auxiliary Carry — used for BCD arithmetic.
> - **F0 (PSW.5)** — User-defined flag.
> - **RS1, RS0 (PSW.4, PSW.3)** — Register Bank Select.
> - **OV (PSW.2)** — Overflow Flag.
> - **P (PSW.0)** — Parity Flag (set if A has odd number of 1s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Of these flags, **CY** is the most critical for the operations in this module, because every **rotate** operation depends on it.

## 1.4 The General Need for These Instructions

In an automotive microcontroller (e.g., an 8081 derivative controlling a dashboard), logic operations are used for:

- **Serial communication shifting** (rotate instructions push bits into SBUF).
- **LED chasing displays** (rotation for scrolling light patterns).
- **Equality/inequality decisions** (CMP drives braking, airbag, fuel-cut decisions).
- **Event counting** (counting wheel pulses for ABS, RPM for tachometer).
- **Debouncing and timing** (time delays for button debouncing in steering controls).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rotate Instructions — The Four Variants

A **rotate** instruction cyclically shifts the bits of the accumulator. The 8051 provides **four** rotate instructions, all of which operate **only on the accumulator (A)**. After execution, only the **Carry (CY)** flag is affected (parity is also recomputed).

### 2.1.1 RL A — Rotate Accumulator Left (without carry)

The 8 bits of A shift left by one position. Bit 7 (MSB) wraps around to bit 0 (LSB).

$$
\text{New bit}_i = \text{Old bit}_{i-1}, \quad i = 7,6,\ldots,1
$$
$$
\text{New bit}_0 = \text{Old bit}_7
$$

**Why it matters:** Used for multiplying unsigned numbers by 2, and for building multi-byte shifts.

### 2.1.2 RLC A — Rotate Accumulator Left through Carry

A 9-bit rotation: the 9 bits are `[CY, A7, A6, A5, A4, A3, A2, A1, A0]`.

$$
\text{New CY} = \text{Old A}_7
$$
$$
\text{New A}_0 = \text{Old CY}
$$
$$
\text{New A}_i = \text{Old A}_{i-1}, \quad i = 7,6,\ldots,1
$$
