**Why it matters:** Used to test individual bits (rotate into CY, then JNC), and to build a 9-bit (or larger) shift register when CY holds an external bit.

### 2.1.3 RR A — Rotate Accumulator Right (without carry)

The 8 bits shift right by one position. Bit 0 (LSB) wraps to bit 7 (MSB).

$$
\text{New bit}_i = \text{Old bit}_{i+1}, \quad i = 6,5,\ldots,0
$$
$$
\text{New bit}_7 = \text{Old bit}_0
$$

### 2.1.4 RRC A — Rotate Accumulator Right through Carry

A 9-bit rotation to the right. CY becomes the new MSB, and the old LSB becomes the new CY.

$$
\text{New CY} = \text{Old A}_0
$$
$$
\text{New A}_7 = \text{Old CY}
$$
$$
\text{New A}_i = \text{Old A}_{i+1}, \quad i = 6,5,\ldots,0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "through carry" matters:** When rotating **without** carry, the operation is a closed 8-bit loop — carry is only a *passive observer* and gets overwritten by the bit that rotates out. When rotating **through** carry, the operation is a 9-bit loop — carry participates in the rotation, enabling **bit-serial** algorithms and CY testing.

## 2.2 The CMP (Compare) Instruction

The 8051 family compare instruction has the form:

```
CJNE <dest-byte>, <src-byte>, <rel-addr>
```

This is **Compare and Jump if Not Equal**. It performs an **internal subtraction** of *src-byte* from *dest-byte* — but the result is **discarded**. Only the **CY** flag is updated:

$$
\text{Temp} = \text{dest-byte} - \text{src-byte} \quad \text{(result discarded)}
$$
$$
\text{CY} = 1 \;\; \text{if} \;\; \text{dest-byte} < \text{src-byte} \quad \text{(borrow needed)}
$$
$$
\text{CY} = 0 \;\; \text{if} \;\; \text{dest-byte} \geq \text{src-byte}
$$
$$
\text{Z} = 1 \;\; \text{if} \;\; \text{dest-byte} = \text{src-byte}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall (Common Mistake):** The 8051 has **no standalone CMP instruction** like the 8086. KTU examiners frequently test whether students know the **CJNE** is the de-facto CMP. Marks are lost when students write "CMP A, #data" which is 8086 syntax, not 8051.

**Post-CJNE, a subsequent JC / JNC tells you the direction of inequality:**

| Condition | CY flag | Typical Jump |
|---|---|---|
| dest < src | CY = 1 | Use **JC** (jump if carry) |
| dest > src | CY = 0 | Use **JNC** (jump if no carry) |
| dest = src | (no jump — falls through) | — |

## 2.3 Software Counter Design

A **software counter** is built using a register (R0–R7, an internal RAM byte, or DPTR) as a counter variable and using **DJNZ** (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero) as the loop driver.

**Generic single-loop up-counter skeleton:**
```
MOV R0, #00H        ; counter = 0
AGAIN: INC R0         ; counter++
      CJNE R0, #64H, AGAIN  ; loop until R0 = 100
```

**Generic down-counter skeleton (most common for delay/count):**
```
MOV R2, #count
BACK: DJNZ R2, BACK   ; loops until R2 = 0, total count+1 iterations
```

> [!NOTE]
> **DJNZ does NOT affect the carry flag.** It only decrements and sets the internal zero flag (Z) used for the branch decision. This is critical when computing exact cycle counts.

## 2.4 Time Delay — The Fundamental Derivation

A **time delay subroutine** is a programmed loop that consumes a known number of **machine cycles**. The 8051 uses a **12-clock oscillator cycle** to produce **1 machine cycle**. Therefore:

$$
T_{\text{machine cycle}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}
$$

where **f_osc** is the quartz crystal oscillator frequency (commonly **11.0592 MHz** for serial communication, **12 MHz** for academic KTU problems).

### 2.4.1 The "Delay-Counting Equation"

For a single-loop delay using DJNZ:

$$
\text{Delay} = \big[\, T_{\text{loop body}} \times (\text{count}+1) + T_{\text{overhead}}\,\big]
$$

For a **nested two-loop delay** (the most common KTU problem):

$$
T_{\text{delay}} = T_{\text{outer setup}} + \sum_{i=1}^{N+1}\big[\,T_{\text{inner setup}} + T_{\text{inner body}}\times(M+1)\,\big]
$$

where:

- **N** = the value loaded into the outer counter register.
- **M** = the value loaded into the inner counter register.
- **T_loop body** = sum of cycle counts of all instructions *inside* the inner loop.
- **T_setup** = cycle counts of MOV, DJNZ etc. surrounding the loop.

### 2.4.2 The Universal Delay Table (Most Repeated KTU Problem)

The following delays assume **XTAL = 11.0592 MHz** → 1 machine cycle = **12/11.0592 MHz ≈ 1.085 μs**. For the standard KTU academic problem they usually round to **1 μs per cycle** assuming 12 MHz.

| Instruction | Bytes | Machine Cycles |
|---|---|---|
| MOV Rn, #data | 2 | 1 |
| DJNZ Rn, label | 2 | 2 |
| DJNZ direct, label | 3 | 2 |
| LCALL addr16 | 3 | 2 |
| RET | 1 | 2 |
| NOP | 1 | 1 |
| ACALL addr11 | 2 | 2 |
| DEC Rn | 1 | 1 |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Key Point | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Machine cycle period | $T_{\text{mc}} = \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$ | seconds |
| Single-loop delay | $T = [\text{T}_{\text{loop}} \times (n+1) + T_{\text{ovh}}]$ | seconds |
| Nested delay (two loops) | $T = [T_o + (N+1)(T_{is} + T_{ib}(M+1))]$ | seconds |
| Three-loop delay | $T = [T_o + (P+1)(T_{io} + (N+1)(T_{is} + T_{ib}(M+1)))]$ | seconds |
| CJNE = Compare | Sets CY if dest $<$ src | Z=1 if equal |
| DJNZ cycles | **2 cycles** per iteration | last iter = 1 cycle (fall-through) |
| Rotate via carry | New CY = bit shifted out | 9-bit rotation |
| Rotate w/o carry | 8-bit closed loop | CY = bit shifted out |
| Maximum single DJNZ count | **255** (one byte) | 0 means 256 iterations |
| Maximum nested count | **255 × 255 = 65,025** iterations | 2-byte |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The last iteration of DJNZ takes only **1 machine cycle** (because it falls through and doesn't jump), while all other iterations take **2 machine cycles**. Skipping this gives a 1-cycle error that examiners consistently mark down.

## 2.6 Real-World Automotive Utility

| Concept | Automotive Use Case |
|---|---|
| Rotate A (RLC) | Serial transmission of ECU diagnostic data over CAN's underlying UART |
| CJNE | Compare throttle position with target — drives fuel injection duration |
| DJNZ Counter | Count wheel speed pulses; trigger ABS actuation every N pulses |
| Time Delay | Debounce brake-pedal switch; blink turn indicator at 1 Hz |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Time Delay for a Nested Loop

**Problem (KTU standard):** Calculate the time delay generated by the following 8051 subroutine, assuming **XTAL = 11.0592 MHz**.

```assembly
DELAY: MOV R3, #200      ; outer counter (N = 200)
HERE:  MOV R2, #250      ; inner counter (M = 250)
       DJNZ R2, HERE     ; inner loop
       DJNZ R3, BACK     ; outer loop  (BACK label = HERE)
       RET
BACK = HERE
```

> [!NOTE]
> We must use the corrected label: the second DJNZ jumps back to `HERE`, not to a new label. Many textbooks write it as "DJNZ R3, HERE" with a label `AGAIN:`. Both forms are equivalent as long as the loop structure is correct.

### Step 1 — Compute the Machine Cycle Period

The 8051 uses 12 oscillator clocks per machine cycle. With XTAL = 11.0592 MHz:

$$
T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
T_{\text{mc}} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \;\mu\text{s}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify Cycle Counts of Each Instruction

From the KTU cycle table:

| Instruction | Machine Cycles |
|---|---|
| MOV R3, #200 | 1 |
| MOV R2, #250 | 1 |
| DJNZ R2, HERE | 2 (except last iteration = 1) |
| DJNZ R3, HERE | 2 (except last iteration = 1) |
| RET | 2 |

### Step 3 — Compute the Inner Loop Delay (One Iteration)

The inner loop body consists of only **DJNZ R2, HERE**. Each iteration takes **2 cycles**, except the last one (when R2 = 0) which takes **1 cycle** before falling through.

Total cycles for the inner loop (counting the last iteration separately):

$$
\text{Inner cycles} = 2 \times M + 1 \times 1 = 2M + 1
$$

Substituting M = 250:

$$
\text{Inner cycles} = 2(250) + 1 = 501 \text{ cycles per outer iteration}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "+1" is the final non-jumping iteration of DJNZ. If you write **2 × (M+1)** you will *over-count by 1 cycle per outer iteration*. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for this.

### Step 4 — Compute the Outer Loop Body

The outer loop body consists of:

1. `MOV R2, #250` → 1 cycle
2. The inner loop → **501 cycles** (computed above)
3. `DJNZ R3, HERE` → 2 cycles (except last outer iteration = 1 cycle)

For the non-final outer iterations:

$$
\text{Outer body (non-final)} = 1 + 501 + 2 = 504 \text{ cycles}
$$

For the **final** outer iteration (when R3 becomes 0 and falls through):

$$
\text{Outer body (final)} = 1 + 501 + 1 = 503 \text{ cycles}
$$

### Step 5 — Sum Over All Outer Iterations

Total cycles for outer loop:

$$
\text{Outer cycles} = 504 \times (N - 1) + 503 = 504 \times 199 + 503
$$

$$
= 100{,}296 + 503 = 100{,}799 \text{ cycles}
$$

### Step 6 — Add the Final MOV R3 and RET

$$
\text{Total cycles} = 1 \; (\text{MOV R3}) + 100{,}799 \; (\text{outer}) + 2 \; (\text{RET})
$$

$$
\text{Total cycles} = 100{,}802 \text{ cycles}
$$

### Step 7 — Convert Cycles to Time

$$
T_{\text{delay}} = 100{,}802 \times 1.085 \;\mu\text{s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{delay}} \approx 109{,}370 \;\mu\text{s} \approx 109.37 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU accepted approximation:** If the problem states **XTAL = 12 MHz**, then $T_{\text{mc}} = 1 \;\mu\text{s}$ and the answer simplifies to exactly **100,802 μs ≈ 100.8 ms**.

### 3.1.1 Closed-Form Delay Formula

From the derivation above, the **general closed-form** for a two-loop nested delay (where the outer loop contains the inner loop body and a final DJNZ) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{delay}} &= \Big[T_{\text{set}} + \big((N+1)\big)\big(T_{\text{iset}} + T_{\text{ibody}}(M+1) - 1\big) \\
&\quad + T_{\text{DJNZ outer}} \cdot N + 1\Big] \times T_{\text{mc}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the standard structure with $T_{\text{set}} = 1$ (one MOV R3) and $T_{\text{iset}} = 1$ (one MOV R2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{delay cycles}} &= 1 + (N+1)\big[1 + 2(M+1) - 1\big] + 2N + 1 \\
&= 1 + (N+1)(2M+2) + 2N + 1 \\
&= 1 + (N+1) \cdot 2(M+1) + 2N + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Plugging N = 200, M = 250:

$$
T = 1 + 201 \times 502 + 400 + 1 = 1 + 100{,}902 + 401 = 101{,}304
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The discrepancy between **100,802** (manual derivation) and **101,304** (closed form) arises because the closed form counts the *final* inner loop's last iteration as 2 cycles instead of 1. **Always re-derive on paper for a KTU answer** — don't blindly plug into formulas.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Bit Testing using RLC

**Problem:** Write a sequence of instructions to determine if bit 4 of the accumulator is 1, using the **RLC A** instruction.

### Solution Derivation

Initial accumulator: A = `x x x x b4 x x x x` (where `b4` is the bit of interest, x is "don't care").

$$
\text{After 1 RLC A:}\quad \text{CY} = b4, \quad \text{A} = \text{b4 x x x x x x x (rolled in)}
$$

Now we test CY:

```
HERE: RLC A        ; rotate A left through carry, CY ← A7 (originally b4)
      JNC HERE     ; if CY = 0, then bit was 0, branch
      ; CY = 1 means bit was 1, continue
```

**Trace:** After RLC, CY holds b4. JNC falls through if b4 = 0, executes if b4 = 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important:** RLC modifies the accumulator! If you must preserve A, first copy A to B (`MOV B, A`), perform the RLC test on B, then restore (`MOV A, B`). KTU examiners will deduct 2 marks if you destroy A without comment.

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Counter Design for Events

**Problem:** Design a counter that counts **100 external events** arriving on a port pin (P1.0), and sets P2.7 HIGH when the count reaches 100.

### Derivation

We treat the counter register (R7) as the event-counting variable. We poll P1.0 each iteration, and only increment when a transition (rising edge) is detected — implemented with a previous-state comparison.

```assembly
       MOV R7, #00H         ; counter = 0
       MOV R6, #00H         ; previous state = 0
POLL:  MOV A, P1            ; read port
       ANL A, #01H          ; isolate P1.0
       MOV R5, A            ; current state in R5
       XRL A, R6            ; check for change
       JZ NO_EDGE           ; no change → skip
       MOV A, R5            ; restore current state
       MOV R6, A            ; update previous state
       ; now check if it was a rising edge
       JNB ACC.0, POLL      ; if new state is 0, ignore
       INC R7               ; rising edge: count++
       CJNE R7, #100, POLL  ; loop until 100
       SETB P2.7            ; set output
       SJMP $               ; stop
NO_EDGE:
       SJMP POLL
```

**Cycle Analysis:**
- The inner loop is unbounded by event frequency — it spins until a rising edge occurs.
- For each event (rising edge), 1 increment of R7 occurs.
- When R7 = 100, the CJNE falls through and P2.7 is set.

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive context:** This is exactly the algorithm used by a **tachometer ECU** to count **flywheel teeth** for engine RPM computation. The 100-count threshold could be 100 flywheel teeth in one crankshaft revolution.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of All Logic Operations

| Instruction | Operates On | Affects Flags | Cycle Count | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RL A | A | CY, P | 1 | Unsigned multiply by 2 (in A) |
| RLC A | A + CY | CY, P | 1 | Bit testing, serial shift |
| RR A | A | CY, P | 1 | Unsigned divide by 2 |
| RRC A | A + CY | CY, P | 1 | Bit testing, serial shift |
| CJNE A, direct, rel | A vs direct | CY only | 2 | Compare with internal RAM |
| CJNE A, #imm, rel | A vs imm | CY only | 2 | Compare with immediate |
| CJNE Rn, #imm, rel | Rn vs imm | CY only | 2 | Compare register |
| CJNE @Ri, #imm, rel | @Ri vs imm | CY only | 2 | Compare RAM pointed |
| DJNZ Rn, rel | Rn | none | 2 | Counter / loop |
| DJNZ direct, rel | direct byte | none | 2 | Counter / loop |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rotate-Through-Carry Functional Flow (RLC A)

The diagram below shows the **9-bit rotation** of `RLC A` — i.e., the operation that takes the 9-bit entity `[CY, A]` and rotates it left by one position.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CY_OLD[Old CY] -->|new A bit 0| A0
    A7[Old A bit 7] -->|new CY| CY_NEW[New CY]
    A6[Old A bit 6] -->|new A bit 7| A7N[New A bit 7]
    A5[Old A bit 5] -->|new A bit 6| A6N[New A bit 6]
    A4[Old A bit 4] -->|new A bit 5| A5N[New A bit 5]
    A3[Old A bit 3] -->|new A bit 4| A4N[New A bit 4]
    A2[Old A bit 2] -->|new A bit 3| A3N[New A bit 3]
    A1[Old A bit 1] -->|new A bit 2| A2N[New A bit 2]
    A0[Old A bit 0] -->|new A bit 1| A1N[New A bit 1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** Each labelled box represents a *bit position* before and after the RLC. The arrows show the data flow during one RLC cycle. Note that **CY and bit A7 swap roles** — this is the defining behaviour of rotate-through-carry.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Compare (CJNE) Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Execute CJNE dest, src, rel] --> B[Compute temp = dest - src internally]
    B --> C{Is temp equal to zero?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Z = 1, CY = 0]
    C -->|No| E[Z = 0]
    E --> F{Is dest less than src? borrow occurred?}
    F -->|Yes| G[CY = 1]
    F -->|No| H[CY = 0]
    D --> I{Is rel jump target reached?}
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -->|Z=0| J[Jump to rel address]
    I -->|Z=1| K[Fall through, continue next instruction]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Time Delay Nested Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Subroutine Entry LCALL] --> M1[MOV R3, N outer counter]
    M1 --> M2[MOV R2, M inner counter]
    M2 --> D1{DJNZ R2 not zero}
    D1 -->|Yes 2 cycles| M2
    D1 -->|No 1 cycle fall through| D2{DJNZ R3 not zero}
    D2 -->|Yes 2 cycles| M2
    D2 -->|No 1 cycle fall through| R[RET 2 cycles]
    R --> X[Return to caller]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram reading guide:** The transition arrows labelled "2 cycles" are the jump paths; the "1 cycle fall through" arrows are the terminal iteration when the counter reaches zero. The total cycle count is the sum of all arrows traversed.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Software Counter State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init : MOV R7, #00H
    Init --> WaitEdge : Begin polling P1.0
    WaitEdge --> EdgeDetected : Rising edge on P1.0
    EdgeDetected --> Inc : INC R7
    Inc --> Compare : CJNE R7, #LIMIT, WaitEdge
    Compare --> WaitEdge : R7 not equal LIMIT
    Compare --> Trigger : R7 = LIMIT
    Trigger --> SetOutput : SETB P2.7
    SetOutput --> [*] : Halt execution
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive context:** This state machine is the *exact* algorithm used by an **RPM counter** in an instrument cluster. Each "rising edge" is a **flywheel tooth pulse**; the `LIMIT` is the number of teeth per crankshaft revolution (e.g., 60); the output drives the **tachometer needle**.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Bit Testing Using RLC A

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Load A with test value] --> A2[Save A to B register MOV B, A]
    A2 --> A3[Execute RLC A four times to move bit 4 into CY]
    A3 --> A4{Check CY flag}
    A4 -->|CY = 1| A5[Bit was 1, take action A]
    A4 -->|CY = 0| A6[Bit was 0, take action B]
    A5 --> A7[Restore original A MOV A, B]
    A6 --> A7
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "four times" in the diagram refers to rotating the test bit into the CY position. For testing bit 4, you rotate 4 times if you test MSB-first, or 5 times if you test LSB-first. **Always verify the count** by tracing the diagram before writing the code.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between the rotate instructions `RL A` and `RLC A` in the 8051. Mention the flag(s) affected.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `RL A` | `RLC A` |
|---|---|---|
| Bits rotated | 8 (A only) | 9 (A + CY) |
| Carry involvement | CY is **not** part of rotation; it only **receives** the bit shifted out from A7 | CY is **part of** the rotation; old CY becomes new A0 |
| Operation | 8-bit closed loop | 9-bit closed loop |
| Flag affected | CY, P | CY, P |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation tip:** A simple difference table is sufficient for 3 marks. Avoid lengthy prose — examiners want a **structured comparison**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**What is the purpose of the `CJNE` instruction in the 8051? How is it used to compare two operands?**

**Model Answer:**

`CJNE` stands for **Compare and Jump if Not Equal**. It performs an **internal subtraction** of the source operand from the destination operand, but **discards the result**. Only the **CY flag** is updated as follows:

- If `dest < src` → CY = 1
- If `dest ≥ src` → CY = 0
- If `dest = src` → no jump (Z condition prevents jump)

**Example:**
```assembly
MOV A, #45H
MOV R1, #50H
CJNE A, #45H, NEXT    ; A = 45H, imm = 45H, so equal — no jump
```
Here, since `dest = src`, no jump occurs and execution falls through.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3-mark tip:** Always mention **which flags** are affected (CY), and give a **one-line assembly example**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

#### [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the four rotate instructions of the 8051 microcontroller with suitable diagrams. State the flag(s) affected by each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the time delay generated by the following 8051 subroutine, assuming the crystal frequency is **11.0592 MHz**. Show all steps of the calculation clearly. **(7 marks)**

```assembly
DELAY: MOV R3, #200
HERE:  MOV R2, #250
       DJNZ R2, HERE
       DJNZ R3, BACK
       RET
BACK:  NOP
       RET
```

---

#### Solution to (a)

**The four rotate instructions** are listed below:

**1. RL A — Rotate Accumulator Left (without carry):**
8-bit rotation. Bit 7 wraps to bit 0. CY receives bit 7's old value.

```
       CY   A7 A6 A5 A4 A3 A2 A1 A0
     [ ... ]   <────────────────  (one position shift left, bit 7 wraps to bit 0)
```

**2. RLC A — Rotate Accumulator Left through Carry:**
9-bit rotation. The 9 bits `[CY, A7..A0]` rotate as a unit. Old A7 → new CY. Old CY → new A0.

```
[ CY   A7  A6  A5  A4  A3  A2  A1  A0 ]   ← rotate
[ A7  A6  A5  A4  A3  A2  A1  A0   CY ]  → result
```

**3. RR A — Rotate Accumulator Right (without carry):**
8-bit rotation. Bit 0 wraps to bit 7. CY receives bit 0's old value.

**4. RRC A — Rotate Accumulator Right through Carry:**
9-bit rotation. Old A0 → new CY. Old CY → new A7.

**Flag affected:** **CY** is the only flag affected (along with **P**, parity of A). OV, AC are unaffected.

---

#### Solution to (b)

**Step 1: Machine cycle period**
$$
T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592\,\text{MHz}} = 1.085 \;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2: Cycle counts**

| Instruction | Cycles |
|---|---|
| MOV R3, #200 | 1 |
| MOV R2, #250 | 1 |
| DJNZ R2, HERE | 2 (last iter = 1) |
| DJNZ R3, BACK | 2 (last iter = 1) |
| NOP | 1 |
| RET | 2 |

**Step 3: Inner loop (R2 = 250)**
$$
\text{Inner cycles} = 2 \times 250 + 1 = 501 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 4: Outer loop body (R3 = 200, non-final iteration)**
$$
1 \; (\text{MOV R2}) + 501 \; (\text{inner}) + 2 \; (\text{DJNZ R3}) = 504 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 5: Final outer iteration**
$$
1 + 501 + 1 = 503 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 6: Total outer cycles**
$$
504 \times 199 + 503 = 100{,}296 + 503 = 100{,}799 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 7: Add setup and epilogue**
$$
1 \; (\text{MOV R3}) + 100{,}799 \; (\text{outer}) + 1 \; (\text{NOP}) + 2 \; (\text{RET}) = 100{,}803 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 8: Convert to time**
$$
T_{\text{delay}} = 100{,}803 \times 1.085 \;\mu\text{s} \approx 109{,}371 \;\mu\text{s} \approx 109.37 \text{ ms}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

#### [KTU University Exam – June 2022]

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain how the `CJNE` instruction works in the 8051. How is it used to determine if a value in the accumulator is **greater than, less than, or equal to** a reference value? Give an example program. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **software counter** that counts events arriving on **P1.0**. When the count reaches **150**, set **P2.5 HIGH**. Provide the assembly code with comments. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a)

**CJNE — Compare and Jump if Not Equal:**

The instruction performs `dest - src` internally, discards the result, and updates the **CY** flag based on the comparison:

```
dest < src  → CY = 1
dest ≥ src  → CY = 0
dest = src  → no jump occurs
```

**Flowchart:**
```
         ┌─────────────────────┐
         │  Start CJNE         │
         └─────────┬───────────┘
                   ▼
         ┌─────────────────────┐
         │ Compute temp=dest-src│
         └─────────┬───────────┘
                   ▼
            ┌────────────┐
            │ temp == 0 ?│
            └─┬───────┬──┘
              │Yes    │No
              ▼       ▼
         (no jump)  ┌────────────┐
                    │dest < src ?│
                    └──┬──────┬──┘
                       │Yes  │No
                       ▼     ▼
                  CY=1   CY=0
                       │     │
                       ▼     ▼
                    (jump to rel)  (jump to rel)
```

**Program to compare A with reference value #50H:**
```assembly
       MOV A, P0         ; read value
       CJNE A, #50H, CHK ; compare
       SJMP EQUAL        ; A = 50H → equal branch
CHK:   JC LESS           ; if CY=1, A < 50H
       SJMP GREATER      ; else A > 50H
LESS:  ; ... actions for A < 50H
       SJMP DONE
GREATER: ; ... actions for A > 50H
EQUAL: ; ... actions for A = 50H
DONE:  SJMP DONE
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **JC after CJNE tests for "less than"** because CJNE sets CY=1 only when dest < src.

---

#### Solution to (b)

**Software Counter Program:**

```assembly
       MOV R7, #00H         ; counter = 0
       MOV R6, #00H         ; previous state of P1.0
POLL:  MOV A, P1            ; read port
       ANL A, #01H          ; isolate P1.0 (bit 0)
       MOV R5, A            ; current state
       XRL A, R6            ; check if state changed
       JZ NOCHANGE          ; if no change, keep polling
       MOV A, R5
       MOV R6, A            ; update previous state
       ANL A, #01H
       JNB ACC.0, POLL      ; if new state is LOW, ignore falling edge
       INC R7               ; rising edge → increment counter
       CJNE R7, #150, POLL  ; loop until count = 150
       SETB P2.5            ; trigger output
       HERE: SJMP HERE      ; halt
NOCHANGE:
       SJMP POLL
```

**Explanation:**
1. R7 is the **event counter**, initialised to 0.
2. R6 holds the **previous state** of P1.0 (initialised to 0).
3. The loop polls P1.0 continuously.
4. On a **rising edge** (LOW→HIGH transition), R7 increments.
5. When R7 = 150, the CJNE falls through and P2.5 is set HIGH.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cycle count:** The polling loop is event-driven, not time-driven, so it has no fixed cycle count — the time depends on the input frequency. This is exactly how **automotive wheel-speed sensors** are polled in real ECUs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the last DJNZ iteration takes 1 cycle, not 2.** This 1-cycle error propagates and causes significant time-delay calculation errors. Deducted: 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Writing `CMP` instead of `CJNE`.** The 8051 has no standalone CMP. Examiners mark this as a fundamental misconception. Deducted: 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing the rotate direction.** Students often mix up RL/RR or RLC/RRC. The 8051 uses **"rotate left" = toward MSB**. Deducted: 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply cycles by $T_{\text{mc}}$ at the end.** Students compute cycle counts correctly but skip the final time conversion. Deducted: 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Modifying the accumulator when only CY is needed.** In bit-testing problems, students forget to save A before doing RLC. Deducted: 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] The **8051 has only 4 rotate instructions**, all on the **accumulator**: RL A, RLC A, RR A, RRC A.
- [ ] RL and RR are **8-bit closed-loop** rotations; RLC and RRC are **9-bit rotations through CY**.
- [ ] After any rotate, **CY receives the bit that was rotated out**.
- [ ] The 8051 has **no standalone CMP** — use **CJNE** instead.
- [ ] CJNE performs `dest - src`, **discards the result**, and sets **CY** based on the comparison.
- [ ] After CJNE: **CY = 1** ↔ `dest < src`; use **JC** to branch to "less than" code.
- [ ] **DJNZ** is the building block of all software counters and time delays.
- [ ] DJNZ cycles: **2 cycles** for jumping iterations, **1 cycle** for the final fall-through.
- [ ] One **machine cycle** = **12 oscillator clocks**; $T_{\text{mc}} = 12 / f_{\text{osc}}$.
- [ ] For **12 MHz** crystal, $T_{\text{mc}} = 1 \;\mu\text{s}$. For **11.0592 MHz**, $T_{\text{mc}} \approx 1.085 \;\mu\text{s}$.
- [ ] **Maximum** single-loop count = **255**; nested two-loop maximum = **255 × 255 = 65,025** iterations.
- [ ] RLC and RRC are essential for **bit-serial I/O** and **bit testing**.
- [ ] Automotive uses: serial comms (rotate), braking threshold (compare), wheel pulse counting (DJNZ counter), debouncing switches (time delay).
- [ ] Always **save and restore the accumulator** when using RLC for bit testing.
- [ ] The **PSW** register holds the flags: **CY (PSW.7)**, **AC (PSW.6)**, **F0 (PSW.5)**, **RS1/RS0 (PSW.4/3)**, **OV (PSW.2)**, **P (PSW.0)**.
- [ ] **P (parity flag)** is set if the accumulator has an **odd** number of 1s (even parity convention).
- [ ] For long delays, prefer **hardware timers** (covered in a later module) over software delay loops.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 8085 Interrupts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** 8085 Interrupts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: 8085 Interrupts - Foundational Concepts

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

In the architecture of the **Intel 8085 microprocessor**, an **Interrupt** is defined as an external or internal asynchronous signal (or condition) that temporarily halts the main program execution, forcing the CPU to transfer control to a dedicated subroutine known as an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. Upon completion of the ISR, the processor resumes execution of the main program from the exact point where it was interrupted, using the saved return address pushed onto the **Stack Memory (RAM)**.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT746)** syllabus, the 8085 interrupt structure is classified along two fundamental dimensions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Axis 1: Source of Generation**
> - **Hardware Interrupts:** Triggered by external pins (TRAP, RST 7.5, RST 6.5, RST 5.5, INTR).
> - **Software Interrupts:** Triggered by the execution of dedicated `RST n` instructions embedded in the program (RST 0 through RST 7).
>
> **Classification Axis 2: Hardware Behavior**
> - **Vectored Interrupts:** The interrupting device provides the address of the ISR directly to the CPU (TRAP, RST 7.5, RST 6.5, RST 5.5, and RST 0–7 via INTR).
> - **Non-Vectored Interrupts:** The interrupting device does not supply an address; the CPU must poll to identify the source (INTR in its raw form).

The 8085 possesses **5 dedicated hardware interrupt pins** and supports **8 software restart (RST) instructions**, making a total of **13 interrupt sources**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Office Worker and the Telephone

Imagine a dedicated office worker (the **CPU**) deeply engrossed in writing a critical report (the **Main Program**). Suddenly, the telephone rings (an **Interrupt Request**). The worker has a choice based on a priority rulebook:

- If the call is from the **Managing Director (TRAP)** on a dedicated red line, the worker MUST stop immediately and attend. This call **cannot be ignored** (Non-Maskable).
- If the call is from a **Department Head (RST 7.5/6.5/5.5)**, the worker checks the office policy (the **Interrupt Mask Register**). If the policy says "do not disturb," the call is politely declined (Masked). If allowed, the worker pauses the report, notes the page number (saves the **Program Counter (PC)** to the stack), takes the call, resolves the issue, returns to the exact page, and continues writing.
- If the call is from an **unknown external party via the intercom (INTR)**, the worker must first look up *who* is calling by checking a register (polling), then decide.

The **Standard Metric** for measuring interrupt performance is the **Interrupt Latency**, defined as the time delay between the Interrupt Request (IRQ) assertion and the start of the first instruction of the ISR. For the 8085, the maximum latency is bounded by the longest instruction execution time (~**13.8 µs** at **2 MHz** clock).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Emphasis (2024 Scheme):** Students must explicitly distinguish between *maskable* and *non-maskable* interrupts, and remember that the **Interrupt Enable (INTE) flip-flop** is the master gate controlled by the `EI` and `DI` instructions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Architecture of the 8085 Interrupt System

The 8085 microprocessor integrates a sophisticated **Interrupt Control Logic** within its internal ALU/Control block. The hardware interrupt pins are summarized below:

### 2.1.1 Hardware Interrupt Pins

**1. TRAP (Pin 6, Non-Maskable, Highest Priority, Edge & Level Triggered, Vectored)**
- Highest priority interrupt. Cannot be masked by the `DI` instruction or by the SIM mask bits.
- Used for critical, catastrophic events (e.g., power failure, emergency stop in automotive ECUs).
- Vector Address: **0024H**.

**2. RST 7.5 (Pin 7, Maskable, Edge Triggered, Vectored)**
- Rising-edge triggered; the request is stored internally and remembered until serviced.
- Vector Address: **003CH**.

**3. RST 6.5 (Pin 8, Maskable, Level Triggered (High), Vectored)**
- Active HIGH level; request must remain HIGH until acknowledged.
- Vector Address: **0034H**.

**4. RST 5.5 (Pin 9, Maskable, Level Triggered (High), Vectored)**
- Active HIGH level; request must remain HIGH until acknowledged.
- Vector Address: **002CH**.

**5. INTR (Pin 10, Maskable, Level Triggered (High), Non-Vectored)**
- General-purpose interrupt. The external device must place a **RESTART instruction opcode** (RST 0–7) on the data bus using the `INTA` (Interrupt Acknowledge) signal from the CPU.
- This is the only hardware interrupt that does not have a fixed vector; the vector is provided by the peripheral (e.g., 8259 PIC, 8279, 8257).

### 2.1.2 Software Interrupts: RST 0 to RST 7

When the programmer inserts an instruction like `RST 5` in the code, the CPU treats it as a software interrupt:
- It saves the current PC onto the stack.
- It jumps to the fixed vector address corresponding to that RST number.

**Why does the vector address increment by 8?**
Each ISR is allocated an **8-byte block** of memory. A block of 8 bytes is sufficient for a short ISR (since the 8085 typically handles short subroutines); for longer ISRs, the 8-byte block contains a jump to the actual ISR location. The vector is calculated as:

$$\text{Vector Address} = 8 \times n \quad \text{(in Hexadecimal)}$$

where $n$ is the RST number (0 to 7).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **8085 Interrupt Vector Address Reference Table (High-Yield for KTU Exams)**

| Interrupt Source | Type | Trigger | Maskable? | Vector Address (Hex) | Decimal Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **TRAP** | Hardware | Edge + Level | No (NMI) | **0024H** | 36 |
| **RST 7.5** | Hardware | Rising Edge | Yes | **003CH** | 60 |
| **RST 6.5** | Hardware | Level High | Yes | **0034H** | 52 |
| **RST 5.5** | Hardware | Level High | Yes | **002CH** | 44 |
| **INTR** (via RST 0) | Hardware | Level High | Yes | **0000H** | 0 |
| **INTR** (via RST 1) | Hardware | Level High | Yes | **0008H** | 8 |
| **INTR** (via RST 2) | Hardware | Level High | Yes | **0010H** | 16 |
| **INTR** (via RST 3) | Hardware | Level High | Yes | **0018H** | 24 |
| **INTR** (via RST 4) | Hardware | Level High | Yes | **0020H** | 32 |
| **INTR** (via RST 5) | Hardware | Level High | Yes | **0028H** | 40 |
| **INTR** (via RST 6) | Hardware | Level High | Yes | **0030H** | 48 |
| **INTR** (via RST 7) | Hardware | Level High | Yes | **0038H** | 56 |
| **RST 0** (Software) | SW Instruction | — | Always | **0000H** | 0 |
| **RST 1** (Software) | SW Instruction | — | Always | **0008H** | 8 |
| **RST 2** (Software) | SW Instruction | — | Always | **0010H** | 16 |
| **RST 3** (Software) | SW Instruction | — | Always | **0018H** | 24 |
| **RST 4** (Software) | SW Instruction | — | Always | **0020H** | 32 |
| **RST 5** (Software) | SW Instruction | — | Always | **0028H** | 40 |
| **RST 6** (Software) | SW Instruction | — | Always | **0030H** | 48 |
| **RST 7** (Software) | SW Instruction | — | Always | **0038H** | 56 |

> [!NOTE]
> **RST 7.5 Memory Conflict:** Notice that the hardware RST 7.5 has vector **003CH**, but the software `RST 7` has vector **0038H**. These are **different** addresses. The hardware pins get a "shifted" vector (multiplied by 8, then +4 for the 7.5) to provide a unique mapping.

### 2.2.1 SIM Instruction (Set Interrupt Mask) - Accumulator Bit Map

The `SIM` instruction is a **one-byte instruction (opcode 30H)** that reads the Accumulator's bit pattern and uses it to configure the interrupt mask and serial output. The bit layout of the Accumulator before executing `SIM` is:

$$\text{Accumulator} = \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{D7} & \text{D6} & \text{D5} & \text{D4} & \text{D3} & \text{D2} & \text{D1} & \text{D0} \\ \hline \text{SOD} & \text{SDE} & \text{XXX} & \text{R7.5} & \text{MSE} & \text{M7.5} & \text{M6.5} & \text{M5.5} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

| Bit | Symbol | Full Name | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **D7** | SOD | Serial Output Data | Bit to be sent out on the SOD pin. |
| **D6** | SDE | Serial Data Enable | If 1, the SOD bit is allowed to be clocked out. If 0, SOD line stays high-impedance. |
| **D5** | XXX | Don't Care | Reserved; not used. |
| **D4** | R7.5 | Reset RST 7.5 | If 1, the pending RST 7.5 request latch is reset (cleared). |
| **D3** | MSE | Mask Set Enable | If 1, the masks in D2, D1, D0 are active. If 0, masks remain unchanged. |
| **D2** | M7.5 | Mask for RST 7.5 | 1 = Mask (disable) RST 7.5; 0 = Unmask (enable). |
| **D1** | M6.5 | Mask for RST 6.5 | 1 = Mask (disable) RST 6.5; 0 = Unmask (enable). |
| **D0** | M5.5 | Mask for RST 5.5 | 1 = Mask (disable) RST 5.5; 0 = Unmask (enable). |

### 2.2.2 RIM Instruction (Read Interrupt Mask) - Accumulator Result

The `RIM` instruction (opcode 20H) loads the Accumulator with the current status of the interrupt and serial subsystems:

$$\text{Accumulator after RIM} = \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{D7} & \text{D6} & \text{D5} & \text{D4} & \text{D3} & \text{D2} & \text{D1} & \text{D0} \\ \hline \text{SID} & \text{I7.5} & \text{I6.5} & \text{I5.5} & \text{IE} & \text{M7.5} & \text{M6.5} & \text{M5.5} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

| Bit | Symbol | Full Name | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **D7** | SID | Serial Input Data | Current logic level on the SID pin. |
| **D6** | I7.5 | Pending RST 7.5 | 1 = RST 7.5 request is pending. |
| **D5** | I6.5 | Pending RST 6.5 | 1 = RST 6.5 request is pending. |
| **D4** | I5.5 | Pending RST 5.5 | 1 = RST 5.5 request is pending. |
| **D3** | IE | Interrupt Enable | Status of the master INTE flip-flop (1 = enabled). |
| **D2** | M7.5 | Current Mask 7.5 | Current mask state for RST 7.5. |
| **D1** | M6.5 | Current Mask 6.5 | Current mask state for RST 6.5. |
| **D0** | M5.5 | Current Mask 5.5 | Current mask state for RST 5.5. |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **automotive electronic control units (ECUs)**, the 8085 (or its modern successors like 8086/ARM Cortex) uses interrupts extensively:
- **TRAP:** Connected to the airbag crash sensor for instantaneous deployment.
- **RST 7.5:** Engine RPM sensor (crankshaft position) - edge-triggered suits the periodic pulse nature.
- **RST 6.5:** Coolant temperature sensor alarm - level-triggered, stays high until acknowledged.
- **INTR:** Connected to peripherals via the 8259 PIC for multi-source management (CAN bus, ABS sensors, infotainment).
- **SIM/RIM:** Used to selectively disable interrupts during critical fuel injection timing calculations (masking) and to read sensor states via the serial input pin (SID).

## 2.4 Priority Resolution Logic

The 8085 resolves simultaneous interrupts using a fixed hardware priority encoder:

$$\text{Priority Order (High} \to \text{Low):} \quad \text{TRAP} \gt \text{RST 7.5} \gt \text{RST 6.5} \gt \text{RST 5.5} \gt \text{INTR}$$

If a higher-priority interrupt occurs while a lower-priority ISR is executing, the 8085 **nests** the interrupts (provided the master INTE flip-flop was re-enabled by `EI` inside the ISR and the higher-priority interrupt is not masked).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 2: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of the RST Vector Address Formula

The 8085 designers allocated 8 bytes of memory to each RST vector location. This provides 8 bytes (0000H–0007H, 0008H–000FH, etc.) for a short ISR or a jump instruction. The vector address for `RST n` (where $n \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$) is derived as follows:

$$\text{Vector Address} = n \times 8_{10}$$

where $n \times 8$ is expressed in hexadecimal.

### Worked Example 1: Calculate the vector address for `RST 4`

$$\text{Vector} = 4 \times 8_{10} = 32_{10}$$

Converting $32_{10}$ to hexadecimal:

$$\begin{aligned}
32 \div 16 &= 2 \text{ remainder } 0 \\
\text{Vector} &= \mathbf{20H}
\end{aligned}$$

### Worked Example 2: Calculate the vector address for `RST 7.5`

The hardware RST 7.5 has a vector of $7.5 \times 8 = 60_{10}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
60 \div 16 &= 3 \text{ remainder } 12 \\
\text{Vector} &= 3 \times 16 + 12 = \mathbf{3CH}
\end{aligned}$$

This is why RST 7.5 has vector 003CH. The fractional part (0.5) of the multiplier is precisely why the address is 4 bytes higher than RST 7 (which is at 0038H).

## 3.2 Analytical Example: Decoding a SIM Instruction Value

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
An 8085 program executes the following sequence to configure interrupts. The Accumulator is loaded with the value **1AH** before `SIM`. Decode this value and explain what happens to each interrupt. The current master interrupt enable (INTE) is OFF.

$$\text{Accumulator} = \text{1AH} = \text{0001 1010}_2$$

### Step 1: Convert 1AH to Binary
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{1AH} &= (1 \times 16^1) + (\text{A} \times 16^0) \\
&= 16 + 10 = 26_{10} \\
26_{10} &= 16 + 8 + 2 = 2^4 + 2^3 + 2^1 \\
&= \text{0001 1010}_2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Map each bit to its SIM function

$$\text{Bit Map: } \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{D7} & \text{D6} & \text{D5} & \text{D4} & \text{D3} & \text{D2} & \text{D1} & \text{D0} \\ \hline 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ \hline \text{SOD} & \text{SDE} & \text{XXX} & \text{R7.5} & \text{MSE} & \text{M7.5} & \text{M6.5} & \text{M5.5} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

### Step 3: Interpret each bit

- **SOD (D7) = 0:** Serial output data bit is 0 (only relevant if SDE=1).
- **SDE (D6) = 0:** Serial data output is **disabled**; SOD pin is not driven.
- **XXX (D5) = 0:** Don't care.
- **R7.5 (D4) = 1:** The pending RST 7.5 request latch is **RESET** (cleared, if it was pending).
- **MSE (D3) = 1:** The mask bits D2-D0 are **ACTIVE** (will be applied).
- **M7.5 (D2) = 0:** RST 7.5 is **UNMASKED** (enabled).
- **M6.5 (D1) = 1:** RST 6.5 is **MASKED** (disabled).
- **M5.5 (D0) = 0:** RST 5.5 is **UNMASKED** (enabled).

### Step 4: Final State
- **TRAP:** Still active (non-maskable; SIM has no effect on TRAP).
- **RST 7.5:** Enabled, and any pending request has been explicitly cleared.
- **RST 6.5:** Disabled.
- **RST 5.5:** Enabled.
- **INTR:** Disabled because the master INTE flip-flop is OFF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** The master INTE flip-flop is set ONLY by the `EI` instruction. Until `EI` is executed, **no maskable interrupt** (RST 7.5/6.5/5.5 or INTR) will be acknowledged, regardless of the individual mask bits.

## 3.3 Analytical Example: Interpreting a RIM Result

**Problem Statement:**
After a system reset and before the program executes `EI`, the `RIM` instruction is executed and the Accumulator reads **28H**. What is the status of the interrupt system?

$$\text{28H} = 40_{10} = \text{0010 1000}_2$$

$$\text{Bit Map: } \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{D7} & \text{D6} & \text{D5} & \text{D4} & \text{D3} & \text{D2} & \text{D1} & \text{D0} \\ \hline 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \hline \text{SID} & \text{I7.5} & \text{I6.5} & \text{I5.5} & \text{IE} & \text{M7.5} & \text{M6.5} & \text{M5.5} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

- **SID = 0:** Logic LOW on serial input pin.
- **I7.5 = 0:** No RST 7.5 pending.
- **I6.5 = 1:** RST 6.5 request is **PENDING**.
- **I5.5 = 0:** No RST 5.5 pending.
- **IE = 1:** Interrupt enable flip-flop is **ON**.
- **M7.5 = 0, M6.5 = 0, M5.5 = 0:** All RST 7.5/6.5/5.5 are **UNMASKED**.

### Conclusion
The CPU will **immediately service the RST 6.5 interrupt** at the end of the current instruction, since IE=1, M6.5=0, and I6.5=1. It will jump to vector address **0034H**.

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation: Interrupt Latency Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
An 8085 system runs at a clock frequency of **2 MHz**. The longest instruction in the main loop is `LXI H, FFFFH` (3 machine cycles = 13 T-states). Calculate the **maximum interrupt latency** for a level-triggered interrupt like RST 6.5.

### Step 1: Calculate the T-state duration
$$T = \frac{1}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} = 0.5 \, \mu s$$

### Step 2: Calculate the maximum instruction execution time
$$T_{max} = \text{T-states} \times T = 13 \times 0.5 \, \mu s = 6.5 \, \mu s$$

### Step 3: Add the interrupt response overhead
The 8085 requires 6 additional T-states after the current instruction completes to push PC and fetch the ISR:

$$T_{overhead} = 6 \times 0.5 \, \mu s = 3.0 \, \mu s$$

### Step 4: Calculate total maximum latency
$$T_{latency} = T_{max} + T_{overhead} = 6.5 + 3.0 = \mathbf{9.5 \, \mu s}$$

This is the worst-case delay between asserting RST 6.5 HIGH and the CPU fetching the first instruction of the ISR. For real-time automotive control, this metric is critical for sensor sampling accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the 8085 Interrupt Handling Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete decision-making process executed by the 8085 hardware whenever an interrupt is asserted.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Interrupt Signal Arrives at CPU Pin"]
    nodeB{"Is Signal = TRAP?"}
    nodeC["Acknowledge Immediately: NMI Path"]
    nodeD{"Master INTE Flip-Flop Set EI?"}
    nodeE["Ignore Request CPU Continues Main Program"]
    nodeF{"Is Individual Interrupt Masked SIM Bit?"}
    nodeG["Ignore Request CPU Continues Main Program"]
    nodeH["Complete Current Instruction"]
    nodeI["Push PC onto Stack Decrement SP Twice"]
    nodeJ["Generate INTA Signal Read Vector from Data Bus"]
    nodeK["Load PC with Vector Address"]
    nodeL["Execute ISR: Save Registers, Process Data"]
    nodeM["Restore Registers from Stack"]
    nodeN["Execute RET Instruction Pop PC from Stack"]
    nodeO["Resume Main Program Execution"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeC
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeD
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeE
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeF
    nodeF -- MASKED --> nodeG
    nodeF -- UNMASKED --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The decision diamonds (`{}`) represent the hardware logic gates inside the 8085's Interrupt Control Unit.
- Rectangular nodes represent state changes or memory operations.
- The flow for a **TRAP** (NMI) bypasses the masking checks entirely, confirming its non-maskable status.

## 4.2 Interrupt Priority Cascade Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX["Simultaneous Interrupt Requests on Multiple Pins"]
    nodeY["Priority Encoder inside 8085 Control Unit"]
    nodeZ{"Identify Highest Priority Request"}
    pA["TRAP: Vector 0024H Highest Priority Non Maskable"]
    pB["RST 7.5: Vector 003CH Rising Edge Latched"]
    pC["RST 6.5: Vector 0034H Level Triggered"]
    pD["RST 5.5: Vector 002CH Level Triggered"]
    pE["INTR: Vector Provided by Peripheral 0000H to 0038H"]
    pF["CPU Services the Selected Interrupt Only"]

    nodeX --> nodeY
    nodeY --> nodeZ
    nodeZ --> pA
    nodeZ --> pB
    nodeZ --> pC
    nodeZ --> pD
    nodeZ --> pE
    pA --> pF
    pB --> pF
    pC --> pF
    nodeD["RST 5.5 Vector 002CH Level Triggered"] --> pF
    pE --> pF
```

**Engineering Interpretation:**
When multiple pins are asserted simultaneously, the 8085's internal priority encoder forces the CPU to service only the highest-priority pending interrupt. Once that ISR begins, lower-priority interrupts are held pending until the current ISR completes (or, if nested interrupts are enabled via `EI` inside the ISR, a higher-priority interrupt can preempt a lower-priority one).

## 4.3 SIM Instruction Bit-Level Functional Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeAcc["Accumulator Contents 8 Bits"]
    nodeSOD["Bit D7 SOD Serial Out Data"]
    nodeSDE["Bit D6 SDE Serial Out Enable"]
    nodeDON["Bit D5 XXX Not Used"]
    nodeRST["Bit D4 R7.5 Reset Pending RST 7.5 Latch"]
    nodeMSE["Bit D3 MSE Mask Set Enable"]
    nodeM7["Bit D2 M7.5 Mask for RST 7.5"]
    nodeM6["Bit D1 M6.5 Mask for RST 6.5"]
    nodeM5["Bit D0 M5.5 Mask for RST 5.5"]
    nodeSIM["SIM Instruction Opcode 30H Activated"]
    nodeEff["Effective Configuration Applied to Interrupt Logic"]

    nodeAcc --> nodeSOD
    nodeAcc --> nodeSDE
    nodeAcc --> nodeDON
    nodeAcc --> nodeRST
    nodeAcc --> nodeMSE
    nodeAcc --> nodeM7
    nodeAcc --> nodeM6
    nodeAcc --> nodeM5
    nodeSOD --> nodeSIM
    nodeSDE --> nodeSIM
    nodeDON --> nodeSIM
    nodeRST --> nodeSIM
    nodeMSE --> nodeSIM
    nodeM7 --> nodeSIM
    nodeM6 --> nodeSIM
    nodeM5 --> nodeSIM
    nodeSIM --> nodeEff
```

**Interpretation:** The `SIM` instruction is essentially a parallel-to-control conversion: it takes the 8-bit accumulator and routes each bit to a specific hardware control line. The **MSE (D3)** acts as a "master switch" for the mask bits; if MSE=0, the mask bits are ignored and the previous mask configuration is preserved.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define an interrupt. List the hardware interrupts of the 8085 microprocessor and identify which one is non-maskable.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **interrupt** is an external or internal asynchronous signal that temporarily suspends the main program execution and forces the CPU to execute a dedicated subroutine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, after which normal program execution resumes from the point of interruption.

The **8085 has five hardware interrupts**: TRAP, RST 7.5, RST 6.5, RST 5.5, and INTR. Among these, **TRAP is the non-maskable interrupt (NMI)**. It cannot be disabled by the `DI` instruction or by the SIM mask bits and is used for critical events such as power failure or emergency stop conditions. **(1 Mark for definition, 1 Mark for listing, 1 Mark for identifying TRAP as NMI).**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is the function of the SIM and RIM instructions in the 8085? Mention the role of the MSE bit.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **SIM (Set Interrupt Mask):** This instruction is used to **mask or unmask** the RST 7.5, RST 6.5, and RST 5.5 interrupts and to reset the RST 7.5 pending latch. It also handles the **serial output data (SOD)** through the SID/SOD pins.
- **RIM (Read Interrupt Mask):** This instruction is used to **read the current status** of the interrupt masks, the pending interrupt requests, the master Interrupt Enable flip-flop, and the serial input data (SID).
- **MSE (Mask Set Enable) bit:** This is bit D3 of the Accumulator before executing SIM. If MSE = 1, the mask bits (D2, D1, D0) are applied to the interrupt hardware. If MSE = 0, the current mask configuration is left unchanged. **(1 Mark each for SIM, RIM, MSE function).**

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Master INTE:** Many students correctly mask individual interrupts via SIM but forget that the **master INTE flip-flop** (set by `EI`) is the ultimate gate. If INTE is OFF, NO maskable interrupt (including unmasked RST pins) is acknowledged. Marks are deducted for omitting `EI` in sequence diagrams.
> 2. **TRAP Misconception:** A common error is stating that TRAP can be masked by DI. **TRAP CANNOT BE MASKED BY DI** or SIM. It is a Non-Maskable Interrupt.
> 3. **Vector Address Confusion:** RST 7.5 has vector **003CH**, not 0038H. Software `RST 7` has 0038H. This distinction is a frequent 1-mark deduction point.
> 4. **RST 7.5 Latch:** Since RST 7.5 is edge-triggered, the request is latched. It must be explicitly cleared via SIM (with R7.5=1) or it will trigger repeatedly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**(a)** Explain the interrupt structure of the 8085 microprocessor with a neat diagram. Describe the vectored and non-vectored interrupts with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the SIM and RIM instructions of the 8085 with the accumulator bit format. Show the procedure to enable RST 6.5 and RST 5.5 while masking RST 7.5 using the SIM instruction. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) - 7 Marks:**

The 8085 microprocessor has a sophisticated interrupt handling mechanism comprising 5 hardware interrupt pins and 8 software interrupts (RST 0–7).

**Hardware Interrupts:**
- **TRAP (Pin 6):** Highest priority, non-maskable, edge and level triggered. Vector: **0024H**. Used for critical events like power failure.
- **RST 7.5 (Pin 7):** Maskable, rising edge triggered, vector **003CH**. Request is latched internally.
- **RST 6.5 (Pin 8):** Maskable, HIGH level triggered, vector **0034H**.
- **RST 5.5 (Pin 9):** Maskable, HIGH level triggered, vector **002CH**.
- **INTR (Pin 10):** Maskable, HIGH level triggered, **non-vectored**. The peripheral must place an RST instruction opcode on the data bus during `INTA`.

**Software Interrupts:** RST 0 through RST 7 (8 instructions), each jumping to vector $8 \times n$:

| RST n | Vector (Hex) |
| :--- | :--- |
| RST 0 | 0000H |
| RST 1 | 0008H |
| RST 2 | 0010H |
| RST 3 | 0018H |
| RST 4 | 0020H |
| RST 5 | 0028H |
| RST 6 | 0030H |
| RST 7 | 0038H |

**Vectored vs Non-Vectored:**
- **Vectored Interrupts:** The CPU automatically knows the ISR address because it is hardwired. Examples: TRAP, RST 7.5, RST 6.5, RST 5.5, and software RST 0–7.
- **Non-Vectored Interrupts:** The CPU does not know the ISR address. The external device (via `INTA`) supplies an 8-bit RST opcode on the data bus. The CPU multiplies this opcode by 8 to get the vector. Example: INTR. **(Full 7 marks: 3 for listing, 2 for vector table, 2 for vectored/non-vectored distinction).**

**Part (b) - 7 Marks:**

**SIM Instruction (Set Interrupt Mask):**
Opcode **30H**. Reads the Accumulator's bit pattern to configure the interrupt mask subsystem and serial output.

**Accumulator bit layout for SIM:**
$$\text{ACC} = \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{D7} & \text{D6} & \text{D5} & \text{D4} & \text{D3} & \text{D2} & \text{D1} & \text{D0} \\ \hline \text{SOD} & \text{SDE} & \text{XXX} & \text{R7.5} & \text{MSE} & \text{M7.5} & \text{M6.5} & \text{M5.5} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

**RIM Instruction (Read Interrupt Mask):**
Opcode **20H**. Loads the Accumulator with the current status of all interrupt sources, masks, the master INTE flip-flop, and the SID pin.

**Accumulator bit layout after RIM:**
$$\text{ACC} = \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{D7} & \text{D6} & \text{D5} & \text{D4} & \text{D3} & \text{D2} & \text{D1} & \text{D0} \\ \hline \text{SID} & \text{I7.5} & \text{I6.5} & \text{I5.5} & \text{IE} & \text{M7.5} & \text{M6.5} & \text{M5.5} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

**Procedure to Enable RST 6.5 & RST 5.5, Mask RST 7.5:**

**Step 1:** Determine the required bit values.
- D7 (SOD) = 0 (don't care)
- D6 (SDE) = 0 (serial disabled)
- D5 (XXX) = 0 (don't care)
- D4 (R7.5) = 0 (do not reset pending RST 7.5)
- D3 (MSE) = 1 (apply masks)
- D2 (M7.5) = 1 (MASK RST 7.5)
- D1 (M6.5) = 0 (UNMASK RST 6.5)
- D0 (M5.5) = 0 (UNMASK RST 5.5)

**Step 2:** Form the binary pattern.
$$\text{ACC} = \text{0000 0100}_2 = \text{04H}$$

**Step 3:** Write the assembly sequence.
```text
MVI A, 04H      ; Load 04H into accumulator
SIM             ; Execute Set Interrupt Mask instruction
EI              ; Enable master interrupt flip-flop
```
**(Full 7 marks: 2 for SIM format, 2 for RIM format, 3 for the worked example with calculation).**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**(a)** Compare the 8085 interrupts TRAP, RST 7.5, RST 6.5, RST 5.5, and INTR in terms of vector address, trigger type, maskability, and priority. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the interrupt handling process in the 8085 with a flowchart. Explain the role of the stack pointer during interrupt servicing. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) - 7 Marks:**

**Detailed Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | TRAP | RST 7.5 | RST 6.5 | RST 5.5 | INTR |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pin Number** | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 | 10 |
| **Vector Address** | 0024H | 003CH | 0034H | 002CH | Device-supplied (8 × n) |
| **Trigger Type** | Edge + Level (Rising edge & High) | Rising Edge | High Level | High Level | High Level |
| **Maskable?** | NO (NMI) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Priority** | 1 (Highest) | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 (Lowest) |
| **Vectored?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Edge/Level Latched?** | No (re-entrant guard) | YES (latched) | No | No | No |

**Key Distinctions:**
1. **TRAP** is the only non-maskable interrupt. It is recognized even if `DI` has been executed and SIM masks are set. It is used exclusively for catastrophic events.
2. **RST 7.5** is the only rising-edge-triggered hardware interrupt among the maskable group. Because the pulse might be short, the request is **internally latched** until serviced.
3. **RST 6.5 and RST 5.5** are level-triggered. The peripheral must hold the request line HIGH until the CPU acknowledges it, otherwise the request is lost.
4. **INTR** is the only non-vectored hardware interrupt. The CPU does not know the source; it must generate `INTA` and read the RST opcode (placed by the peripheral, e.g., 8259 PIC) on the data bus.
5. **Priority** is hardwired in descending order: TRAP > RST 7.5 > RST 6.5 > RST 5.5 > INTR. **(Full 7 marks: 5 for complete table, 2 for key distinctions).**

**Part (b) - 7 Marks:**

**Interrupt Handling Process:**

1. **Interrupt Request:** An external device asserts one of the 8085 interrupt pins (e.g., RST 6.5 goes HIGH).

2. **Mask Check:** If the interrupt is maskable, the CPU checks:
   - Is the master INTE flip-flop ON (set by `EI`)?
   - Is the individual interrupt unmasked (based on SIM)?

3. **Instruction Completion:** The CPU finishes the currently executing instruction completely. No mid-instruction interrupt is allowed.

4. **Stack Push of PC:** The CPU pushes the 16-bit Program Counter (return address) onto the stack. This requires:
   - Decrement SP by 1, write high byte of PC to stack.
   - Decrement SP by 1, write low byte of PC to stack.

5. **Vector Address Load:** The CPU loads the PC with the vector address of the interrupt (e.g., 0034H for RST 6.5). For INTR, the CPU first issues `INTA` to fetch the RST opcode.

6. **ISR Execution:** The CPU executes the Interrupt Service Routine. The ISR should begin with **PUSH** instructions (to save other registers like B, C, D, E, H, L, A, flags) and end with corresponding **POP** instructions followed by **RET**.

7. **Return from Interrupt:** The `RET` instruction pops the saved PC from the stack, returning control to the main program at the exact instruction following the interrupt point.

**Role of the Stack Pointer (SP):**
- The **Stack Pointer (SP) is a 16-bit register** that always points to the **top of the stack** in RAM.
- During interrupt servicing, the SP is **decremented twice** to push the 16-bit PC onto the stack memory.
- During the return (`RET`), the SP is **incremented twice** as the PC is popped back.
- The stack follows the **LIFO (Last In First Out)** principle, which is critical for **nested interrupts** — if a higher-priority interrupt occurs during an ISR, its return address is pushed on top, and `RET` will bring execution back to the lower-priority ISR first, which then returns to the main program.
- A **stack overflow** can occur if the ISR is deeply nested or uses excessive PUSH operations, potentially corrupting program data. Engineers must size the stack memory appropriately (typically 256 bytes for simple 8085 systems).

**Flowchart (as already shown in Section 4.1):** The CPU checks the TRAP pin first → checks INTE → checks individual mask → completes instruction → pushes PC → loads vector → executes ISR → pops PC via RET. **(Full 7 marks: 3 for process steps, 2 for SP role, 2 for flowchart explanation).**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **8085 Interrupts — Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exams**

- **Total Hardware Interrupts:** 5 (TRAP, RST 7.5, RST 6.5, RST 5.5, INTR).
- **Total Software Interrupts:** 8 (RST 0 to RST 7).
- **Vector Address Formula:** For software `RST n`, Vector = $n \times 8$ (in Hex). For hardware pins: TRAP=0024H, RST 7.5=003CH, RST 6.5=0034H, RST 5.5=002CH, INTR is device-supplied.
- **TRAP is Non-Maskable:** Cannot be disabled by `DI` or SIM. Highest priority.
- **Master Gate:** The INTE flip-flop is the master switch; set by `EI`, reset by `DI`. Without `EI`, no maskable interrupt is acknowledged.
- **RST 7.5 is Edge-Triggered and Latched:** Use SIM with R7.5=1 to clear the pending latch.
- **RST 6.5 and RST 5.5 are Level-Triggered:** Request must be held HIGH until acknowledged.
- **INTR is Non-Vectored:** CPU must use `INTA` to read the RST opcode from the data bus (placed by external hardware like 8259).
- **SIM Opcode:** 30H. RIM Opcode: 20H.
- **SIM Bit D3 (MSE):** Must be 1 for mask bits D2/D1/D0 to take effect.
- **RIM Bit D3 (IE):** Returns the current status of the master INTE flip-flop.
- **Priority Order (High to Low):** TRAP > RST 7.5 > RST 6.5 > RST 5.5 > INTR.
- **Stack Behavior:** PC is auto-pushed on interrupt and auto-popped on `RET`. ISRs should manually PUSH/POP other registers.
- **Automotive Application:** TRAP → Crash/airbag, RST 7.5 → Crank pulse, RST 6.5 → Temperature alarm, INTR → Peripheral hub via 8259 PIC.
- **Latency Bound:** Maximum = (Longest instruction T-states + 6 overhead T-states) × Clock period.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microcontrollers and Embedded Processors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Microcontrollers and Embedded Processors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Microcontroller?

A **Microcontroller (MCU)** is a single-chip, self-contained computer system that integrates a **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **Memory (ROM/RAM)**, **I/O Ports**, **Timers/Counters**, **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADC)**, and **Serial Communication Interfaces** onto a single Integrated Circuit (IC) die. It is purpose-built for real-time, dedicated control tasks rather than general-purpose computing.

In the automotive context, microcontrollers are the "electronic brains" embedded inside almost every subsystem — from the Engine Control Unit (ECU), Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), and Airbag Control Unit (ACU) to the Body Control Module (BCM) and Infotainment clusters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Exact):** A microcontroller is a compact, integrated circuit designed to govern a specific operation in an embedded system, combining a processor core, memory, and programmable I/O peripherals on a single chip.

## 1.2 What is an Embedded Processor?

An **Embedded Processor** is a microprocessor (or microcontroller) that forms the computational core of an **Embedded System** — a computing platform dedicated to a specific function within a larger mechanical or electrical system. Embedded processors are optimized for **determinism, low power, and real-time response** rather than raw computational throughput.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Garage"**
> - A **Microprocessor** is like a *raw engine* — powerful, but it needs tires, seats, steering, dashboard, and fuel tank to actually drive anywhere (it needs external RAM, ROM, I/O, ADC, etc. to function).
> - A **Microcontroller** is like a *fully built car* — the engine, dashboard, fuel system, and wheels are all integrated into one chassis, ready to drive straight out of the factory (everything is on a single chip).
> - An **Embedded Processor** is the *onboard computer inside that car* — it specifically controls fuel injection, anti-lock braking, climate control, and airbag deployment, all in real-time.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Clock Frequency Range:** **8 MHz – 300 MHz** (automotive MCUs typically run between 40 MHz and 200 MHz)
- **Operating Voltage (Automotive):** **5 V (TTL)** or **3.3 V (CMOS)**; CAN bus transceivers operate at **ISO 11898 standard**.
- **Bit-Widths Used in Auto:** **8-bit** (low-end BCM), **16-bit** (mid-range like MSP430), **32-bit** (high-end ARM Cortex-M/R)
- **Automotive Grade Standard:** **AEC-Q100** qualification for IC reliability.
- **Operating Temperature (Automotive Grade):** **−40 °C to +125 °C** (Grade 1)

## 1.5 Microprocessors vs. Microcontrollers

| Parameter | Microprocessor (MPU) | Microcontroller (MCU) |
|---|---|---|
| Integration | CPU only | CPU + Memory + I/O on one chip |
| Cost | Higher (external components needed) | Lower (fewer external parts) |
| Power | High | Low (suitable for battery ECU) |
| Application | PC, Servers | Automotive ECUs, IoT, Appliances |
| Example | Intel i7, AMD Ryzen | Freescale S12, Renesas RH850, STM32 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Foundation: Harvard vs. Von Neumann

The two foundational processor architectures are central to MCU design.

### A. Von Neumann Architecture
- A **single shared memory** holds both program instructions and data.
- Uses a **single bus** for instruction fetch and data access.
- **Bottleneck** — known as the *Von Neumann bottleneck* — occurs when CPU must wait for bus access.

### B. Harvard Architecture
- **Physically separate memories** for program code (ROM/Flash) and data (RAM).
- Uses **two independent buses** (instruction bus + data bus).
- Allows **simultaneous** instruction fetch and data access → faster throughput.
- Most modern automotive MCUs (ARM Cortex-M, Renesas RH850, Infineon AURIX) use a **Modified Harvard** variant for cache, while keeping separate code/data Flash banks.

## 2.2 Instruction Set Philosophies: CISC vs. RISC

### A. CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)
- Single instruction can perform **multiple low-level operations**.
- Example: The **Freescale HC11 / S12** family (legacy 8/16-bit automotive MCUs).
- Many addressing modes; variable-length instructions.

### B. RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)
- Small, highly-optimized instruction set; **fixed instruction length**; **load/store architecture**.
- Typically **single-cycle execution** via pipelining.
- Example: **ARM Cortex-M series** (Cortex-M0/M3/M4/M7), **RISC-V**, **PowerPC (used in older automotive MCUs)**.
- Dominates modern automotive ECUs due to deterministic timing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Most contemporary automotive microcontrollers follow a **RISC architecture with a Modified Harvard memory model**, exemplified by ARM Cortex-M cores used in NXP, Infineon, and STMicroelectronics automotive MCUs.

## 2.3 Core Subsystems of an Automotive MCU

The modern automotive MCU integrates the following functional blocks:

1. **CPU Core** — typically 32-bit ARM Cortex-R/M
2. **Program Memory** — Flash (typically **256 KB to 8 MB**)
3. **Data Memory** — SRAM (typically **8 KB to 256 KB**)
4. **I/O Ports** — General-purpose digital I/O
5. **Timers / PWM Modules** — for fuel injection timing, motor control
6. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** — reads sensor voltages
7. **Communication Interfaces:**
   - **CAN (Controller Area Network)** — ISO 11898, dominant in-vehicle network
   - **LIN (Local Interconnect Network)** — low-cost sub-bus
   - **FlexRay / Automotive Ethernet** — for high-bandwidth (ADAS, infotainment)
   - **SPI / I2C / UART** — for sensor/actuator peripherals
8. **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** — resets MCU if software hangs (critical for safety)
9. **ECC Memory** — Error-Correcting Code memory for ISO 26262 compliance
10. **Voltage Regulator & POR (Power-On Reset)**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine Control Unit (ECU):** Uses a 32-bit MCU (e.g., NXP S32K, Infineon AURIX TC3xx) running at **100–300 MHz** to compute fuel injection, ignition timing, and emissions in **real-time loops of 1–10 ms**.
- **ABS / ESC Modules:** Use a **dual-core lockstep MCU** for functional safety (ISO 26262 ASIL-D).
- **Body Control Modules (BCM):** Use low-cost **8/16-bit MCUs** (e.g., NXP S08) to control lights, wipers, and door locks.
- **Infotainment & ADAS:** Use powerful **embedded processors (e.g., NXP i.MX8, TI Jacinto)** running Linux/QNX.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Standard | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|
| ADC Resolution | $N = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | bits ($n$ = resolution bits) |
| ADC Quantization Step | $Q = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$ | Volts / step |
| Timer Frequency | $f_{T} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{Prescaler}$ | Hz |
| PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{TOTAL}} \times 100$ | % |
| CAN Bus Bit Rate | $BR = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{2 \cdot (BRP+1) \cdot (1 + T_{SEG1} + T_{SEG2})}$ | bits/sec |
| CPU Execution Time | $T_{exec} = \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{f_{CLK}}$ | seconds |
| MIPS Rating | $MIPS = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{10^6}$ | Millions of Instructions / sec |
| Power Dissipation (CMOS) | $P_d = C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | Watts |
| ISO 26262 ASIL Levels | A, B, C, D | Safety Integrity |
| AEC-Q100 Temp Range | $-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+125^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Grade 1 |
| Bit-weight conversion | $D = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^i$ | decimal value |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ADC Quantization Error Analysis

A 12-bit ADC (typical in automotive sensor interfaces) converts an analog sensor voltage to a digital value. Let's derive the quantization step and worst-case quantization error.

### Step 1: Define the Reference Voltage
A typical automotive 12-bit ADC has a reference voltage from a regulated supply:
$$V_{REF} = 5.0 \text{ V}$$

### Step 2: Define the ADC Resolution
For an $n$-bit ADC, the number of discrete output levels is:
$$L = 2^n$$

For $n = 12$:
$$L = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ levels}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Quantization Step (LSB Size)
The step size $Q$ is the smallest voltage change detectable:
$$Q = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$$

Substituting values:
$$Q = \dfrac{5.0}{4096}$$

Performing the division step-by-step:
$$Q = \dfrac{5.0}{4096} = 1.220703125 \text{ mV/LSB}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Maximum Quantization Error
The theoretical worst-case quantization error is half the LSB:
$$E_{max} = \pm \dfrac{Q}{2}$$

Substituting:
$$E_{max} = \pm \dfrac{1.2207 \text{ mV}}{2} = \pm 0.61035 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 5: Digital-to-Analog Conversion Formula
Given a digital output code $D$, the reconstructed analog voltage is:
$$V_{analog} = D \cdot Q = D \cdot \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$$

For example, if a throttle position sensor produces digital code $D = 2048$ (mid-scale):
$$V_{analog} = 2048 \times 1.2207 \text{ mV} = 2499.99 \text{ mV} \approx 2.5 \text{ V}$$

### Step 6: General Digital-to-Decimal Conversion
For an $n$-bit binary number with bits $b_{n-1} \ldots b_1 b_0$:
$$D = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b_i \cdot 2^i$$

Example — for the 8-bit byte $1011\,0100_2$:
$$D = (1 \cdot 2^7) + (0 \cdot 2^6) + (1 \cdot 2^5) + (1 \cdot 2^4) + (0 \cdot 2^3) + (1 \cdot 2^2) + (0 \cdot 2^1) + (0 \cdot 2^0)$$

Computing each term step-by-step:
$$D = 128 + 0 + 32 + 16 + 0 + 4 + 0 + 0 = 180_{10}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: PWM Duty Cycle for Fuel Injector

In a fuel injection system, the injector open-time is controlled by a PWM signal from the MCU. Suppose the MCU timer has a clock of $f_{CLK} = 16 \text{ MHz}$, and the timer period register is set to produce a PWM period of $T_{PWM} = 10 \text{ ms}$.

### Step 1: Compute the Total Count for One Period
$$N_{total} = f_{CLK} \cdot T_{PWM}$$

Substituting:
$$N_{total} = 16 \times 10^6 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-3} = 160{,}000 \text{ counts}$$

### Step 2: Compute Counts for a 25% Duty Cycle
$$N_{ON} = D \cdot N_{total}$$

For $D = 25\% = 0.25$:
$$N_{ON} = 0.25 \cdot 160{,}000 = 40{,}000 \text{ counts}$$

### Step 3: Injected Fuel (Inferred Real-World Utility)
The injector pulse width is the **injection duration** $t_i$, directly proportional to the fuel quantity $Q_f$:
$$Q_f = \dot{m}_{inj} \cdot t_i$$

where $\dot{m}_{inj}$ is the mass flow rate of the injector. This is the fundamental relation governing fuel metering in port-fuel injection and common-rail diesel systems.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Architectural Variants

| MCU Family | Architecture | Bit-Width | Automotive Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Freescale / NXP HC(S)08/S12 | Von Neumann / CISC | 8 / 16 | BCM, Lighting |
| NXP S32K1 | ARM Cortex-M4 (Modified Harvard / RISC) | 32 | Gateway, BCM |
| Infineon AURIX TC3xx | TriCore (Modified Harvard / RISC) | 32 | ECU, ADAS, ASIL-D |
| Renesas RH850 | Modified Harvard / RISC | 32 | Powertrain, Chassis |
| TI MSP430 | Von Neumann / RISC | 16 | Low-power sensor nodes |
| ARM Cortex-M7 | Modified Harvard / RISC | 32 | ADAS, motor control |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Internal Architecture of a Typical Automotive Microcontroller

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU[CPU Core - 32-bit ARM Cortex-M4]
    FLASH[Program Memory - Flash up to 2 MB]
    SRAM[Data Memory - SRAM up to 256 KB]
    EEP[EEPROM - 4 KB Calibration Data]
    GPIOA[GPIO Ports A B C D E]
    TIMER1[Timers - 8 / 16 / 32 bit]
    PWMA[PWM Modules - 6 Channels]
    ADC1[ADC - 12 bit 16 Channel]
    CANA[CAN 2.0B Controller]
    LINA[LIN 2.x Controller]
    SPII[SPI Interface]
    I2CI[I2C Interface]
    UARTA[UART]
    WDT1[Watchdog Timer - WDT]
    RST[Reset and POR Circuit]
    CLKGEN[Clock Generation Unit - PLL]

    CPU <--> FLASH
    CPU <--> SRAM
    CPU <--> EEP
    CPU <--> GPIOA
    CPU <--> TIMER1
    CPU <--> PWMA
    CPU <--> ADC1
    CPU <--> CANA
    CPU <--> LINA
    CPU <--> SPII
    CPU <--> I2CI
    CPU <--> UARTA
    CPU <--> WDT1
    CLKGEN --> CPU
    CLKGEN --> TIMER1
    CLKGEN --> CANA
    RST --> CPU
    RST --> WDT1
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram: Embedded Processor Operation in an Automotive ECU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS[Vehicle Sensors - TPS MAF Lambda O2 Crank] --> ADC[ADC Input Stage]
    ADC --> SIG[Signal Conditioning - Filter Clamp]
    SIG --> CPU[Embedded Processor - ARM Cortex M4]
    INP[Driver Inputs - Pedal Steering Switches] --> GPIO[GPIO / Interrupt Inputs]
    GPIO --> CPU
    CANIN[Other ECUs via CAN Bus] --> CANRX[CAN RX Buffer]
    CANRX --> CPU
    CPU --> ACT[Actuator Drivers - Injector Ignition Coil Motor Relay]
    CPU --> CANOUT[CAN TX - Broadcast Status]
    CPU --> WDT[Watchdog Refresh]
    WDT -.->|timeout| RESET[System Reset]
    CPU --> RTOS[RTOS Scheduler - OSEK / AUTOSAR]
    RTOS --> CPU
```

## 4.3 Diagram: Microprocessor vs. Microcontroller Functional Architecture

```mermaid

        MCUCPU --- INTCAN
    end
```
