# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
Differentiate between a microprocessor and a microcontroller. Mention any three key distinctions with relevance to automotive applications.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(i) Integration:** A microprocessor contains only the CPU; it requires *external* ROM, RAM, and I/O chips for operation. A microcontroller *integrates* the CPU, ROM, RAM, timers, ADC, and I/O onto a single IC. *(1 mark)*
- **(ii) Application Focus:** Microprocessors are designed for *general-purpose* computing (e.g., engineer's laptop, ECU host HMI), while microcontrollers are *task-specific* (e.g., ABS ECU, BCM). *(1 mark)*
- **(iii) Power, Cost & Footprint:** MCUs are *low-power, low-cost, and small-footprint* — making them ideal for in-vehicle distributed ECUs; MPUs are higher-cost, higher-power, used in infotainment head units. *(1 mark)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
Explain the Von Neumann bottleneck and how the Harvard architecture overcomes it. Name one automotive MCU family that uses Modified Harvard.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **Von Neumann bottleneck** arises because program instructions and data share a *single bus* and *single memory*, forcing the CPU to alternately fetch instructions and read/write data — limiting throughput. *(1 mark)*
- The **Harvard architecture** uses *physically separate* program and data memories with *two independent buses*, allowing *simultaneous* instruction fetch and data access, eliminating the bottleneck. *(1 mark)*
- **Automotive example:** The **NXP S32K** family (ARM Cortex-M4 Modified Harvard) — used in automotive BCMs and gateway ECUs. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Architecture-Focused

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model — Module 3, Q Type A]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a typical 32-bit automotive microcontroller. List at least six functional blocks and state the purpose of each.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
A 12-bit ADC in an Engine Control Module has $V_{REF} = 5.0$ V. The throttle position sensor reads analog value 2.4 V.
(i) Calculate the quantization step (LSB size).
(ii) Compute the corresponding 12-bit digital output code.
(iii) Determine the maximum quantization error.

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Applications-Focused

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model — Module 3, Q Type B]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Compare CISC and RISC architectures. State, with justification, which architecture is preferred in modern automotive ECUs, and give two real-world automotive MCU examples.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Explain the role of the **CAN (Controller Area Network)** interface in a microcontroller used in an automotive ECU. State the ISO standard it follows, the typical bus speed, and how multi-ECU communication takes place (with a sketch description of arbitration).

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

#### Solution to Question A — Part (a) (7 marks)

The internal architecture of a typical 32-bit automotive MCU consists of the following functional blocks integrated on a single die:

| # | Block | Purpose (Marks Allocation) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU Core (ARM Cortex-M4)** | Executes RISC instructions, FPU for floating-point math, 3-stage pipeline. *(1 mark)* |
| 2 | **Flash Program Memory (1–2 MB)** | Non-volatile storage of ECU application firmware and calibration maps. *(1 mark)* |
| 3 | **SRAM Data Memory (64–256 KB)** | Volatile storage for stack, heap, runtime variables. *(1 mark)* |
| 4 | **12-bit ADC (16-channel)** | Digitizes analog sensor inputs (TPS, MAP, lambda O2, MAF, battery voltage). *(0.5 mark)* |
| 5 | **PWM Modules (6-channel)** | Generates variable duty-cycle signals for injector, idle air control, EGR valve. *(0.5 mark)* |
| 6 | **CAN 2.0B Controller** | ISO 11898 multi-master bus interface to chassis/body ECUs. *(0.5 mark)* |
| 7 | **LIN 2.x Controller** | Low-cost sub-bus to window/lift/mirror nodes. *(0.5 mark)* |
| 8 | **Timers / Counter (16/32-bit)** | Event capture, crankshaft position decoding, time-base for RTOS. *(0.5 mark)* |
| 9 | **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** | Resets MCU on software lockup — ISO 26262 safety. *(0.5 mark)* |
| 10 | **GPIO Ports (multiple banks)** | Digital I/O to relays, switches, LEDs. *(0.5 mark)* |
| 11 | **Clock Generation Unit (PLL)** | Generates 100–200 MHz system clock from 8/16 MHz crystal. *(0.5 mark)* |

*(Total: 7 marks — inclusive of 1 mark for block diagram drawn)*

#### Solution to Question A — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $n = 12$ bits, $V_{REF} = 5.0$ V, $V_{analog} = 2.4$ V.

**(i) Quantization Step (LSB Size):** *(2 marks)*

$$Q = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \dfrac{5.0}{4096}$$

$$Q = 0.0012207031 \text{ V} = 1.2207 \text{ mV/LSB}$$

**(ii) 12-bit Digital Output Code:** *(3 marks)*

$$D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{analog}}{Q} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{2.4}{0.0012207031} \right\rfloor$$

$$D = \lfloor 1966.08 \rfloor = 1966_{10}$$

Converting 1966 to 12-bit binary:
$$1966 = 1024 + 512 + 256 + 128 + 32 + 8 + 4 + 2$$
$$= 2^{10} + 2^9 + 2^8 + 2^7 + 2^5 + 2^3 + 2^2 + 2^1$$

$$D = 0111\,1010\,1110_2 = 0x7AE_{16}$$

**(iii) Maximum Quantization Error:** *(2 marks)*

$$E_{max} = \pm \dfrac{Q}{2} = \pm 0.61035 \text{ mV}$$

---

#### Solution to Question B — Part (a) (7 marks)

| Parameter | CISC | RISC |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction Set | Large, complex, variable-length | Small, fixed-length (typically 32-bit) |
| Addressing Modes | Many (12–24) | Few (3–5) |
| Memory Access | Memory-to-memory | Load/Store architecture |
| Pipelining | Difficult | Highly pipelined, single-cycle |
| Code Density | High (one instruction does more) | Lower (more instructions per task) |
| Power Consumption | Higher | Lower (critical for ECU thermal design) |
| Determinism | Lower (variable cycles) | Higher (deterministic timing) |

**Preferred in automotive ECUs:** **RISC architecture** *(2 marks for justification)*
- **Justification 1:** Deterministic single-cycle execution enables real-time engine control loops (1–10 ms period). *(1 mark)*
- **Justification 2:** Lower power dissipation suits harsh under-hood thermal environment. *(1 mark)*

**Automotive MCU examples:** *(2 marks)*
1. **NXP S32K** — ARM Cortex-M4 (RISC) for BCMs, gateway modules.
2. **Infineon AURIX TC3xx** — TriCore (RISC) for engine management, ADAS, ASIL-D systems.

---

#### Solution to Question B — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Role of CAN in an automotive ECU:** *(2 marks)*
The CAN (Controller Area Network) interface is a **multi-master, differential serial bus** that interconnects all ECUs (Engine, ABS, BCM, Airbag, Instrument Cluster) in a vehicle, allowing them to broadcast and receive messages without a central host.

**ISO Standard:** **ISO 11898** *(1 mark)*
- ISO 11898-1: Data link layer
- ISO 11898-2: High-speed physical layer (up to 1 Mbps)
- ISO 11898-3: Low-speed / fault-tolerant (up to 125 kbps)

**Typical Bus Speed:** **500 kbps** (high-speed CAN in powertrain) and **125 kbps** (low-speed CAN in body/comfort). *(1 mark)*

**Multi-ECU Communication & Arbitration:** *(3 marks)*
- CAN uses **CSMA/CD+AMP** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Detection with Arbitration by Message Priority).
- Each message has an **11-bit (or 29-bit extended) ID**, where a *lower numerical ID* = *higher priority*.
- During bus access, ECUs simultaneously transmit; the controller monitors the bus — if a dominant bit (0) is overwritten by a recessive bit (1), that ECU loses arbitration and *automatically retries* once the higher-priority message completes.
- This is a *non-destructive* arbitration — no bus time is lost.
- A typical CAN frame contains: SOF, Arbitration field, Control field, Data field (0–8 bytes), CRC, ACK, EOF.

**Example:** When the driver brakes, the ABS ECU (ID 0x100 — high priority) broadcasts wheel-speed data; the Engine ECU and Instrument Cluster simultaneously receive and act on it without master arbitration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write *"CISC is used in modern ECUs"* — **this is WRONG.** Most contemporary automotive MCUs (post-2015) are RISC-based. The examiner *will* deduct marks.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** In ADC problems, students frequently **drop the floor brackets** $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ and forget to **convert the decimal to binary explicitly**. KTU examiners award *only 1.5/3 marks* for the binary conversion if not shown in full.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing **Modified Harvard** with **pure Harvard** — Modified Harvard (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4) uses separate physical memory but *unified* bus addressing. KTU specifically tests this distinction.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Writing *"CAN works at 1 Mbps"* without mentioning the **standard vs. high-speed distinction** (ISO 11898-2 = 1 Mbps, ISO 11898-3 = 125 kbps).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Microcontroller (MCU)** = CPU + Memory + I/O on a **single IC**, purpose-built for embedded control.
- ✅ **Embedded Processor** = the computing core of a system dedicated to a **specific function** inside a larger system (e.g., inside a car).
- ✅ **Von Neumann** = single shared bus; **Harvard** = separate instruction/data memories + dual buses.
- ✅ **Modified Harvard** = the dominant architecture in modern 32-bit MCUs (ARM Cortex-M).
- ✅ **CISC** = complex, variable-length, memory-to-memory instructions (legacy automotive: HC11/S12).
- ✅ **RISC** = small, fixed-length, load/store, pipelined, deterministic (modern automotive: ARM Cortex-M, TriCore).
- ✅ **ADC step:** $Q = V_{REF} / 2^n$; **Max error** $= \pm Q/2$.
- ✅ **CAN Bus** = ISO 11898, differential 2-wire, multi-master, CSMA/CD+AMP, non-destructive arbitration, 11-bit ID, 500 kbps typical.
- ✅ **LIN Bus** = single-wire, sub-bus for low-cost nodes (windows, mirrors, seats).
- ✅ **Automotive MCU families:** NXP S32K, Infineon AURIX, Renesas RH850, STMicro SPC5.
- ✅ **Functional safety standard:** **ISO 26262** (ASIL-A to ASIL-D); **AEC-Q100** for IC reliability.
- ✅ **Operating temperature (Grade 1):** **−40 °C to +125 °C**.
- ✅ **Watchdog timer** is *mandatory* in safety-critical ECUs (engine, brake, airbag).
- ✅ **RTOS** in automotive: **OSEK/VDX** and modern **AUTOSAR** (Classic & Adaptive).
- ✅ **Dual-core lockstep** = two identical cores running the same code with a comparator — used in ASIL-D systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram of 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Block Diagram of 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **8051 Microcontroller** is an **8-bit single-chip microcontroller** introduced by **Intel Corporation in 1980**. It integrates a CPU, RAM, ROM, I/O ports, timers/counters, serial communication port, and an interrupt controller onto a single silicon die using Harvard architecture. The "Block Diagram of 8051" refers to the functional architecture schematic that illustrates the internal modules, their interconnections, and the data/address pathways (buses) through which these units exchange information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:**
> The 8051 is a **40-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package)** CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) microcontroller built on **Harvard architecture**, featuring a **16-bit Program Counter**, **16-bit DPTR**, **8-bit ALU**, **4 KB on-chip ROM (Program Memory)**, **128 bytes of on-chip RAM (Data Memory)**, **four 8-bit I/O ports**, **two 16-bit timers/counters**, **one full-duplex UART**, and **a 5-source interrupt structure**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Office Building" Intuition

Imagine the 8051 as a **small, self-sufficient office building**:

| 8051 Component | Office Building Analogy | Real-World Function |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU / ALU** | The Manager's Desk | Performs all decisions, math, and logic |
| **Program Memory (ROM)** | The Filing Cabinet of Rules | Stores permanent instruction manuals (firmware) |
| **Data Memory (RAM)** | The Working Desk | Holds temporary notes and calculations |
| **I/O Ports (P0–P3)** | The Building's Doors & Windows | Communicates with the outside world |
| **Timers/Counters** | The Wall Clock | Measures time intervals and counts events |
| **Serial Port (UART)** | The Telephone Line | Talks to distant offices one bit at a time |
| **Interrupt Controller** | The Receptionist | Alerts the manager to urgent matters |
| **Oscillator Circuit** | The Heartbeat / Power Generator | Provides the rhythmic clock pulses |
| **Bus System** | The Hallways & Elevators | Carries information between rooms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike a *microprocessor* (which is just the manager's desk — needs external memory and peripherals), a *microcontroller* is a **complete computer on a chip**. This is why the 8051 dominates embedded automotive applications like **ECU sensor reading**, **dashboard control**, **fuel injection timing**, and **anti-lock braking systems (ABS)**.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Clock Frequency Range:** **1 MHz to 12 MHz** (commonly **11.0592 MHz** for accurate serial baud rates)
- **Machine Cycle:** **12 oscillator periods = 1 machine cycle**
- **Operating Voltage:** **+5V DC** (±10%)
- **Address Bus Width:** **16 bits** (can address **64 KB** of external memory)
- **Data Bus Width:** **8 bits**
- **On-chip Program Memory:** **4 KB ROM** (0000H–0FFFH)
- **On-chip Data Memory:** **128 bytes RAM** (00H–7FH)
- **Special Function Registers (SFRs):** **128 bytes** (80H–FFH)
- **Number of I/O Lines:** **32** (4 ports × 8 bits)
- **Total External Memory Address Space:** **64 KB Program + 64 KB Data = 128 KB**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Breakdown of the 8051 Block Diagram

The block diagram is logically divided into **nine major functional units**, each with a defined role:

#### 2.1.1 Central Processing Unit (CPU)
The CPU is the brain. It contains:
- **8-bit ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit):** Performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷), logic (AND, OR, XOR), rotate, and compare operations.
- **Accumulator (A):** An 8-bit register that is the primary operand for most ALU operations. It is the *only* register through which most data passes.
- **B Register:** Used exclusively for multiplication (`MUL AB`) and division (`DIV AB`).
- **Program Status Word (PSW):** An 8-bit flag register containing carry, auxiliary carry, overflow, parity, and register bank select bits.
- **Register Banks:** Four banks (Bank 0–3), each with 8 registers (R0–R7), switchable via PSW bits RS0 and RS1.
- **Stack Pointer (SP):** An 8-bit pointer to the top of the stack in RAM. Resets to **07H**.
- **Program Counter (PC):** A **16-bit** counter holding the address of the next instruction. Resets to **0000H**.
- **Data Pointer (DPTR):** A 16-bit register (DPL + DPH) used to access external data memory using `MOVX` instructions.

#### 2.1.2 Oscillator and Clock Circuit
The 8051 requires an external crystal oscillator connected across pins **XTAL1 (pin 19)** and **XTAL2 (pin 18)**. The internal oscillator circuit generates two-phase clock signals (P1 and P2). Each machine cycle contains **12 oscillator periods** divided into **6 states**, each with **2 phases**.

#### 2.1.3 Program Memory (Code ROM)
- **4 KB on-chip ROM** (addresses **0000H to 0FFFH**).
- Can be expanded to **64 KB** externally using pins **EA̅** and **PSEN̅**.
- Stores opcodes (machine code) and lookup tables.
- The interrupt vector addresses are located at: **0003H (External Int 0), 000BH (T0), 0013H (External Int 1), 001BH (T1), 0023H (Serial)**.

#### 2.1.4 Data Memory (RAM)
- **128 bytes on-chip RAM** (addresses **00H to 7FH**).
- Divided into:
  - **00H–1FH:** 32 bytes in 4 register banks.
  - **20H–2FH:** 16 bytes of bit-addressable memory (128 bits).
  - **30H–7FH:** 80 bytes of general-purpose scratch-pad RAM.

#### 2.1.5 Special Function Registers (SFRs)
Mapped from **80H to FFH** (only 21 are actually implemented in the basic 8051). Examples: `P0`, `P1`, `P2`, `P3`, `TCON`, `TMOD`, `TL0`, `TH0`, `SCON`, `SBUF`, `IE`, `IP`, `PCON`, `ACC`, `B`, `PSW`, `SP`, `DPH`, `DPL`.

#### 2.1.6 I/O Ports (P0, P1, P2, P3)
Four 8-bit bidirectional ports:
- **P0:** Open-drain; serves as multiplexed **low-byte address/data bus** when external memory is used.
- **P1:** General-purpose, no alternate function.
- **P2:** Provides **high-byte address bus (A8–A15)** during external memory access.
- **P3:** Has **alternate functions** (RXD, TXD, INT0̅, INT1̅, T0, T1, WR̅, RD̅).

#### 2.1.7 Timer/Counter Subsystem
- **Two 16-bit timers/counters: Timer 0 and Timer 1.**
- Each is composed of two 8-bit registers: **THx (high byte)** and **TLx (low byte)**.
- Can operate in **4 modes** (Mode 0: 13-bit, Mode 1: 16-bit, Mode 2: 8-bit auto-reload, Mode 3: split mode).
- Used for **delay generation, event counting, baud rate generation**.

#### 2.1.8 Serial Port (UART)
- **Full-duplex UART** (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter).
- Operates in **4 modes** (Mode 1 is the standard 8-bit variable baud-rate mode used in automotive diagnostics).
- Receives/transmits via pins **P3.0 (RXD)** and **P3.1 (TXD)**.
- Can be clocked by Timer 1 overflow for precise baud rates.

#### 2.1.9 Interrupt Controller
- **5 interrupt sources:** External Int 0, Timer 0 Overflow, External Int 1, Timer 1 Overflow, Serial Port.
- **2 priority levels** (set via IP register).
- Enabled individually via the **IE (Interrupt Enable)** register.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is essential for KTU ESE problems — memorize the addresses and bit positions!**

| Concept | Value / Formula | Address / Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Machine Cycle Duration | $T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | — | If $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz → $T_{mc} = 1.085\,\mu s$ |
| Instruction Cycle | $1\text{ to }4$ machine cycles | — | Most are 1 or 2 cycles |
| External Address Space | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes | 0000H–FFFFH | 16-bit PC gives 64 KB |
| On-chip Program ROM | $4\text{ KB} = 4096$ bytes | 0000H–0FFFH | Expandable via EA̅ pin |
| On-chip Data RAM | $128$ bytes | 00H–7FH | — |
| SFR Space | $128$ bytes (21 used) | 80H–FFH | Bit-addressable for some |
| Bit-addressable RAM | $16 \times 8 = 128$ bits | 20H–2FH | Bit addresses 00H–7FH |
| Register Banks | $4$ banks $\times\, 8$ registers | 00H–1FH | Switched via PSW.3, PSW.4 |
| Default Stack Pointer | Reset value = **07H** | 08H onwards | After reset |
| Reset Vector Address | **0000H** | PC | Program begins here |
| Timer/Counter Resolution | $1\,\text{machine cycle} = 1.085\,\mu s$ @ 11.0592 MHz | — | — |
| Maximum Count (Mode 1) | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ counts | — | Timer overflows at 65536 |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility in Automotive Applications

The 8051's integrated architecture makes it invaluable in vehicles for:
- **Engine Control Units (ECUs):** Reading crankshaft sensors via timer counters.
- **Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS):** High-priority interrupt-driven wheel-speed sensing.
- **Dashboard Instrument Clusters:** Driving LCDs/LEDs through ports.
- **OBD-II Diagnostic Communication:** UART serial port links to scan tools.
- **Body Control Modules:** Managing lights, wipers, door locks via I/O ports.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Delay Generated by Timer in Mode 1

**Goal:** Find the maximum time delay that Timer 0 can produce when operating in **Mode 1 (16-bit timer)** with an 11.0592 MHz crystal.

**Step 1 — Establish the relationship between oscillator frequency and machine cycle.**

The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 to obtain the machine cycle frequency. Therefore, the time period of one machine cycle is:

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given oscillator frequency.**

For $f_{osc} = 11.0592\,\text{MHz}$:

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}\,\text{seconds}$$

$$T_{mc} = 1.085\,\mu\text{s}$$

> *Logic:* Every machine cycle takes 1.085 microseconds. The timer increments by 1 on every machine cycle.

**Step 3 — Determine the maximum number of timer counts in Mode 1.**

Mode 1 is a **16-bit timer**, so it counts from **0000H (0)** up to **FFFFH (65535)** before overflowing to **0000H** and setting the TF0 flag.

$$N_{max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536\,\text{counts}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the maximum delay before overflow.**

$$T_{delay} = N_{max} \times T_{mc}$$

$$T_{delay} = 65{,}536 \times 1.085 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{seconds}$$

$$T_{delay} = 71{,}106.56 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{seconds}$$

$$T_{delay} \approx 71.106\,\text{ms}$$

> *Logic:* This is the maximum *unloaded* delay. To get exactly this delay, you would preload the timer with `0000H` (i.e., do nothing). To get a *smaller* specific delay, preload with a value computed in the next example.

**Step 5 — General formula for required preload value.**

For a desired delay $T_{desired}$ in seconds:

$$N_{required} = \frac{T_{desired}}{T_{mc}}$$

$$\text{Preload value} = 2^{16} - N_{required} = 65{,}536 - \frac{T_{desired}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Generating a 50 ms Delay

**Problem:** Configure Timer 0 in Mode 1 to generate a 50 ms delay using an 11.0592 MHz crystal.

**Step 1 — Compute the number of counts needed.**

$$N_{required} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46{,}082.95 \approx 46{,}083\,\text{counts}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the preload (initial) value for the TH0 and TL0 registers.**

$$\text{Preload} = 65{,}536 - 46{,}083 = 19{,}453$$

$$\text{Preload in Hex} = 19{,}453_{10} = 4BFD_{16}$$

**Step 3 — Split into High and Low bytes for the timer registers.**

$$TH0 = \text{High Byte of } 4BFDH = 4BH$$

$$TL0 = \text{Low Byte of } 4BFDH = FDH$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

The timer must be loaded with `TH0 = 4BH` and `TL0 = FDH` before starting. After exactly 46,083 machine cycles (≈ 50.000 ms), the timer overflows and triggers an interrupt (if enabled) or sets the TF0 flag.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Memory Map of the 8051

The following tabular breakdown is the *single most-tested concept* in KTU exams on this topic:

| Address Range | Size | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0000H – 0002H | 3 bytes | ROM | Reset vector (start of program) |
| 0003H – 000AH | 8 bytes | ROM | External Interrupt 0 ISR vector |
| 000BH – 0012H | 8 bytes | ROM | Timer 0 ISR vector |
| 0013H – 001AH | 8 bytes | ROM | External Interrupt 1 ISR vector |
| 001BH – 0022H | 8 bytes | ROM | Timer 1 ISR vector |
| 0023H – 002AH | 8 bytes | ROM | Serial Port ISR vector |
| 002BH – 0FFFH | — | ROM | User program code |
| 00H – 07H | 8 bytes | RAM | Register Bank 0 (default after reset) |
| 08H – 0FH | 8 bytes | RAM | Register Bank 1 |
| 10H – 17H | 8 bytes | RAM | Register Bank 2 |
| 18H – 1FH | 8 bytes | RAM | Register Bank 3 |
| 20H – 2FH | 16 bytes | RAM | Bit-addressable space (bits 00H–7FH) |
| 30H – 7FH | 80 bytes | RAM | General-purpose scratchpad RAM |
| 80H – FFH | 128 bytes | SFR | Special Function Registers |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Alternate Functions of Port 3 (Highly Tested)

| Pin | Alternate Function | Direction | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| P3.0 | **RXD** | Input | Serial data receive pin (UART) |
| P3.1 | **TXD** | Output | Serial data transmit pin (UART) |
| P3.2 | **INT0̅** | Input | External Interrupt 0 |
| P3.3 | **INT1̅** | Input | External Interrupt 1 |
| P3.4 | **T0** | Input | Timer 0 external clock input |
| P3.5 | **T1** | Input | Timer 1 external clock input |
| P3.6 | **WR̅** | Output | External data memory write strobe |
| P3.7 | **RD̅** | Output | External data memory read strobe |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Connection:** In an automotive ECU, **P3.2 / P3.3** receive wheel-speed sensor pulses (interrupt-driven), **P3.0 / P3.1** communicate with the OBD-II diagnostic port (UART), and **P3.6 / P3.7** write/read external EEPROM storing fault codes (DTCs).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the 8051 Microcontroller

The following Mermaid diagram maps the internal functional blocks and the data/address pathways of the 8051.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["CPU CORE: ALU + Accumulator + B + PSW"]

    subgraph REGISTERS ["REGISTER BLOCK"]
        R0["R0-R7 Banks 0-3"]
        SP["Stack Pointer SP"]
        PC["Program Counter PC 16-bit"]
        DPTR["Data Pointer DPTR 16-bit"]
    end

    OSCC["Oscillator Circuit XTAL1 XTAL2"]
    CLKGEN["Clock Generator: divides by 12"]

    subgraph ROM ["PROGRAM MEMORY 4 KB ROM"]
        V0["0000H Reset Vector"]
        V1["0003H INT0 Vector"]
        V2["000BH T0 Vector"]
        V3["0013H INT1 Vector"]
        V4["001BH T1 Vector"]
        V5["0023H Serial Vector"]
        USER["User Code 002BH-0FFFH"]
    end

    subgraph RAM ["DATA MEMORY 128 B RAM"]
        RB["Register Banks 00H-1FH"]
        BIT["Bit Addressable 20H-2FH"]
        SCRATCH["Scratchpad 30H-7FH"]
    end

    subgraph SFR ["SFR SPACE 80H-FFH"]
        PORTS["P0 P1 P2 P3"]
        TIMERS["TMOD TCON TL0 TH0 TL1 TH1"]
        SERIAL["SCON SBUF PCON"]
        INTC["IE IP"]
        ACC["ACC B PSW SP DPH DPL"]
    end

    subgraph TIMER_BLOCK ["TIMER COUNTER SUBSYSTEM"]
        T0["Timer 0: 16-bit"]
        T1["Timer 1: 16-bit"]
    end

    subgraph IO_BLOCK ["I/O AND PERIPHERALS"]
        P0["Port 0: AD0-AD7"]
        P1["Port 1: GP I/O"]
        P2["Port 2: A8-A15"]
        P3["Port 3: RXD TXD INT0 INT1 T0 T1 WR RD"]
        UART["UART Serial Port"]
        INTC["Interrupt Controller 5 sources 2 priorities"]
    end

    BUS_CTRL["Bus Control Logic: PSEN EA ALE"]

    OSCC --> CLKGEN
    CLKGEN --> CPU
    CPU <--> REGISTERS
    CPU <--> ROM
    CPU <--> RAM
    CPU <--> SFR
    CPU <--> TIMER_BLOCK
    CPU <--> IO_BLOCK
    BUS_CTRL --- CPU
    BUS_CTRL --- ROM
    BUS_CTRL --- RAM
    P3 -.-> UART
    P3 -.-> T0
    P3 -.-> T1
    T0 --> INTC
    T1 --> INTC
    UART --> INTC
    P3 -.-> INTC
```

### 4.2 Architecture Style and Bus Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HARVARD ["HARVARD ARCHITECTURE: SEPARATE BUSES"]
        PROM["Program Memory ROM 4 KB"]
        DRAM["Data Memory RAM 128 B"]
        SFR_AREA["SFR 128 B"]
    end

    subgraph CORE_CPU ["CPU UNIT"]
        ALU_D["ALU 8-bit"]
        ACC_D["Accumulator A"]
        B_D["B Register"]
    end

    subgraph BUS_SYSTEM ["BUS SYSTEM"]
        PBUS["Program Bus: 16-bit addr + 8-bit data"]
        DBUS["Data Bus: 16-bit addr + 8-bit data"]
    end

    PROM -- PC and PSEN --> PBUS --> CORE_CPU
    DRAM -- DPTR and MOVX --> DBUS --> CORE_CPU
    SFR_AREA -- direct addressing --> DBUS --> CORE_CPU
```

### 4.3 Pin Functional Grouping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PWR ["POWER PINS"]
        VCC["VCC Pin 40: +5V"]
        VSS["VSS Pin 20: GND"]
    end

    subgraph CLK ["CLOCK PINS"]
        XT1["XTAL1 Pin 19"]
        XT2["XTAL2 Pin 18"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL ["CONTROL PINS"]
        RST["RST Pin 9: Reset Active High"]
        EA["EA-bar Pin 31: External Access"]
        ALE["ALE Pin 30: Address Latch Enable"]
        PSEN["PSEN-bar Pin 29: Program Store Enable"]
    end

    subgraph PORTS_4 ["PORT PINS 32 I/O LINES"]
        PORT0["P0 Pins 32-39: AD0-AD7"]
        PORT1["P1 Pins 1-8: GP I/O"]
        PORT2["P2 Pins 21-28: A8-A15"]
        PORT3["P3 Pins 10-17: Alt Functions"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice the **Harvard architecture** separation — the program memory (ROM) uses its own 16-bit program counter and is read via the **PSEN̅** signal, while data memory and SFRs use the **DPTR** and `MOVX` instructions via the **RD̅/WR̅** signals. This separation is what makes the 8051 faster than Von Neumann designs for real-time automotive tasks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]*
**List any six features of the 8051 microcontroller.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **8-bit CPU** with an 8-bit ALU and accumulator-based architecture.
2. **On-chip Program Memory (ROM):** 4 KB.
3. **On-chip Data Memory (RAM):** 128 bytes.
4. **Four 8-bit I/O ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3) providing 32 digital I/O lines.
5. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** (Timer 0 and Timer 1) with 4 operating modes.
6. **Full-duplex UART** for serial communication.
7. **5 interrupt sources** with 2 priority levels.
8. **16-bit Program Counter** addressing up to 64 KB of external program memory.

*(Student should write any six; full credit for listing six distinct features with brief explanation.)*

---

#### **Question A2.** *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]*
**Differentiate between Harvard and Von Neumann architectures with respect to the 8051.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Harvard Architecture (8051) | Von Neumann Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Structure | Separate program and data memories | Single shared memory |
| Buses | Separate code and data buses | One common bus (time-multiplexed) |
| Speed | Faster — both fetches can occur simultaneously | Slower — bus contention |
| Instruction Fetch | Uses **PC** and **PSEN̅** | Uses shared PC |
| Data Access | Uses **DPTR** and `MOVX` with **RD̅/WR̅** | Uses same instruction path |
| Example | **8051 microcontroller** | 8085 microprocessor |

The 8051 uses Harvard architecture, allowing it to fetch the next opcode while reading data from RAM — critical for real-time automotive applications.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]* — Draw & Explain the Block Diagram

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Draw the block diagram of the 8051 microcontroller and label all major functional blocks.**

**Model Answer — Block Diagram Structure (5 marks for diagram + 2 marks for labeling):**

The student must draw a block diagram containing **at minimum** these labeled blocks:

- **CPU block** with ALU, Accumulator (A), B Register, PSW
- **Oscillator and Clock Generator**
- **ROM (4 KB)** with PC interfacing
- **RAM (128 bytes)** with register banks, bit-addressable area, scratchpad
- **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** block listing key registers
- **Four I/O Ports (P0, P1, P2, P3)**
- **Timers/Counters (T0, T1)** with TMOD, TCON
- **Serial Port (UART)** with SCON, SBUF
- **Interrupt Controller** with IE, IP
- **Bus Control Logic** with PSEN̅, EA̅, ALE signals
- **Address Bus (16-bit)** and **Data Bus (8-bit)** shown as arrows

**Diagram marks distribution:**
- 2 marks: Correct overall layout and connectivity
- 2 marks: All major blocks present
- 1 mark: Proper bus representations and direction arrows
- 2 marks: Clean labeling with correct addresses/registers

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**Explain the function of the Program Counter, Stack Pointer, and Data Pointer registers in the 8051.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Program Counter (PC) — 2 marks:**
- The PC is a **16-bit register** that holds the address of the **next instruction** to be fetched from program memory.
- After reset, PC is initialized to **0000H**, meaning execution begins at the reset vector.
- It automatically increments by 1 after each instruction fetch (or by 2 for two-byte jumps like `SJMP`).
- It can address **64 KB** of program memory space (2^16 = 65536 locations).

**(ii) Stack Pointer (SP) — 2.5 marks:**
- The SP is an **8-bit register** that points to the **top of the stack** in the internal RAM.
- After reset, SP is initialized to **07H** (meaning the first `PUSH` will store at 08H).
- The stack grows **upward** in memory (toward higher addresses).
- It is manipulated implicitly by `CALL`, `RET`, `PUSH`, `POP`, and interrupt service routines.
- The programmer typically initializes SP to **60H or higher** to avoid conflict with register banks 0–3.

**(iii) Data Pointer (DPTR) — 2.5 marks:**
- The DPTR is a **16-bit register** composed of a high byte **DPH** (address 83H) and a low byte **DPL** (address 82H).
- It is used to access **external data memory** via the `MOVX` instruction family.
- It can also read **lookup tables** in program memory using `MOVC A,@A+DPTR`.
- In automotive applications, DPTR is used to point to calibration tables stored in external EEPROM.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]* — Internal Memory Organization

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Explain the internal RAM organization of the 8051 with a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

The 128 bytes of internal RAM (addresses **00H–7FH**) are organized into three distinct regions:

**Region 1: Register Banks (00H–1FH) — 3 marks**

| Address | Purpose |
|---|---|
| 00H – 07H | Register Bank 0 (default after reset) |
| 08H – 0FH | Register Bank 1 |
| 10H – 17H | Register Bank 2 |
| 18H – 1FH | Register Bank 3 |

Each bank contains 8 general-purpose registers named `R0` through `R7`. Only **one bank is active at a time**, selected by bits **RS0 (PSW.3)** and **RS1 (PSW.4)** of the Program Status Word.

**Region 2: Bit-Addressable Area (20H–2FH) — 2 marks**

- 16 bytes = **128 individually addressable bits**.
- Each bit has a unique bit address from **00H to 7FH**.
- Useful for boolean flag storage (e.g., `SETB 20H.0` sets the LSB of byte 20H).
- In automotive ECU code, these bits often hold fault flags: `b20H.0 = Engine_Overtemp`, etc.

**Region 3: General-Purpose Scratchpad RAM (30H–7FH) — 2 marks**

- 80 bytes of free RAM for variable storage, the stack, and intermediate calculations.
- This is where programmers typically relocate the stack pointer (e.g., `MOV SP, #60H`).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**Explain the Special Function Register (SFR) area of the 8051. List any ten SFRs with their addresses and functions.**

**Model Answer:**

The SFR area occupies the upper 128 bytes of internal data memory space (**80H–FFH**), accessed only through direct addressing. Although 128 addresses exist, **only 21 SFRs** are physically implemented in the basic 8051; the rest are reserved/undefined.

**Ten Important SFRs:**

| SFR | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **ACC** | E0H | The accumulator — main operand for ALU operations |
| **B** | F0H | Used in `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` |
| **PSW** | D0H | Program Status Word — contains flags and bank select bits |
| **SP** | 81H | Stack Pointer |
| **DPH** | 83H | High byte of Data Pointer |
| **DPL** | 82H | Low byte of Data Pointer |
| **P0** | 80H | Port 0 latch |
| **P1** | 90H | Port 1 latch |
| **P2** | A0H | Port 2 latch |
| **P3** | B0H | Port 3 latch |
| **TCON** | 88H | Timer/Counter control |
| **TMOD** | 89H | Timer/Counter mode select |
| **SCON** | 98H | Serial port control |
| **SBUF** | 99H | Serial data buffer (transmit/receive) |
| **IE** | A8H | Interrupt Enable register |

**Key Points for Full Marks:**
- SFRs above 80H are **only accessible via direct addressing**.
- SFRs ending in `0H` or `8H` are **bit-addressable** (e.g., P0, P1, TCON, SCON, IE, IP, PSW, ACC, B).
- Reading/writing SFRs configures peripherals and reads status flags.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention EA̅ behavior:** When `EA̅ = 1`, code from internal ROM (0000H–0FFFH) executes; for addresses above 0FFFH, external ROM is auto-fetched. This is a 2-mark differentiator.
> 2. **Confusing PSEN̅ and RD̅:** PSEN̅ activates for **program memory reads** (opcode fetch); RD̅ activates for **external data memory reads** (`MOVX`). Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Wrong SP reset value:** Many students write "SP = 00H" — the correct value is **07H**.
> 4. **Forgetting register bank select bits:** PSW has **RS1 (bit 4)** and **RS0 (bit 3)**, not the other way around.
> 5. **Not showing alternate functions of P3:** A common omission that costs up to 1 mark in block diagram questions.
> 6. **Mixing up TCON vs TMOD:** TCON controls the timer (start/stop, flags), TMOD selects the mode. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Block Diagram of 8051**

- **Architecture:** Harvard, 8-bit, CISC, single-chip, 40-pin DIP.
- **CPU:** 8-bit ALU, 8-bit Accumulator (A), B register, PSW.
- **PC:** 16-bit, resets to **0000H**, addresses 64 KB.
- **DPTR:** 16-bit (DPH + DPL), used for external data and code lookup tables.
- **SP:** 8-bit, resets to **07H**, grows upward, typically reinitialized to **60H or 70H**.
- **Program ROM:** 4 KB on-chip (0000H–0FFFH), expandable to 64 KB.
- **Data RAM:** 128 bytes on-chip, split into 32-byte register banks + 16-byte bit-addressable + 80-byte scratchpad.
- **SFRs:** 128 bytes mapped at 80H–FFH; **21 used** in basic 8051.
- **Ports:** Four 8-bit ports (32 I/O lines); P0 = AD bus, P2 = A8–A15, P3 = alternate functions.
- **Timers:** Two 16-bit timers (T0, T1), 4 modes each.
- **Serial Port:** Full-duplex UART, 4 modes, uses P3.0 (RXD) and P3.1 (TXD).
- **Interrupts:** 5 sources (INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial), 2 priority levels.
- **Clock:** External crystal on XTAL1/XTAL2; machine cycle = **12 oscillator periods**.
- **Key Pins:** **EA̅** (External Access), **PSEN̅** (Program Store Enable), **ALE** (Address Latch Enable), **RST** (Reset, active HIGH).
- **Automotive Use Cases:** ECU sensor reading, ABS wheel-speed sensing, dashboard I/O, OBD-II diagnostics via UART, fault-code storage in external EEPROM.
- **Most-Tested Values for KTU:** Reset vector **0000H**, SP reset value **07H**, ROM size **4 KB**, RAM size **128 B**, register banks **4**, machine-cycle divisor **12**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PSW and Flag Bits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PSW and Flag Bits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of PSW (Program Status Word)

The **Program Status Word (PSW)** is a special function register (SFR) in the 8051 microcontroller architecture that consolidates the arithmetic processor status, user-definable flags, and register bank selection bits into a single byte-wide readable and writable register located at internal RAM address **0xD0**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> In the context of automotive microcontrollers (MCS-51 family derivatives such as P87C591 used in vehicle ECUs), the PSW is the primary status indicator examined by the Central Processing Unit (CPU) immediately after every arithmetic, logical, or bit-manipulation operation. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to identify the bit address of each flag, the condition that sets/clears it, and its role in branching instructions like `JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, and conditional automotive control logic.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Flag Bits

**Flag bits** are single-bit indicators within the PSW register that reflect the outcome of the most recent arithmetic logic unit (ALU) operation or store user-defined state information. They are essentially **1-bit hardware latches** that the ALU sets or clears automatically, and the programmer can test them using conditional jump instructions.

In the 8051 family, there are **four math flags** (CY, AC, OV, P) and **three user/control flags** (F0, RS1, RS0).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dashboard Gauges" of the CPU

Imagine the CPU is a **car driver** (or rather, the autonomous ECU of a modern vehicle) and the PSW is the **dashboard instrument cluster**:

- The **Speedometer** tells you the *current speed* → analogous to the **Carry (CY)** flag showing whether the result "overflowed" 8 bits.
- The **Engine Temperature Warning Light** indicates *auxiliary heat* (e.g., low-fuel or BCD overflow warning) → analogous to the **Auxiliary Carry (AC)** flag for the lower nibble.
- The **Tachometer Red-Line** indicates the *engine exceeded safe RPM* → analogous to the **Overflow (OV)** flag for signed arithmetic.
- The **Odometer Parity Indicator** shows whether the digits sum to an *even or odd number* → analogous to the **Parity (P)** flag.
- The **Custom Toggle Switch** the driver uses for any purpose (e.g., AC on/off indicator) → analogous to the **User Flag (F0)**.
- The **Driver Profile Selector** (Sport/Comfort/Eco) that changes the *car's response* → analogous to **RS1 and RS0** register bank select bits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The PSW is a *bit-addressable* register. This means each of the 8 bits has its own individual bit address (from 0xD7 down to 0xD0), unlike most other SFRs which must be manipulated as full bytes. This is critical for automotive interrupt service routines (ISRs) where atomic bit manipulation is required.

## 1.4 Bit-Structure Snapshot of PSW

The PSW register occupies bit-addressable locations from **0xD0 to 0xD7** in the Special Function Register (SFR) memory map. The bit positions are conventionally numbered **PSW.7 (MSB) through PSW.0 (LSB)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Flag

### 2.1.1 Carry Flag (CY) — PSW.7 (Bit Address 0xD7)

- **Symbol**: `CY` or `C`
- **What it does**: Set to **1** if there is an **unsigned overflow** out of bit 7 (MSB) of the accumulator during addition, or a **borrow** into bit 7 during subtraction. Cleared to **0** otherwise.
- **Set condition (Addition)**: Result > 255 (decimal) or > FF (hex).
- **Set condition (Subtraction)**: A borrow was required from bit 8 (i.e., the minuend is smaller than the subtrahend).
- **Why it matters**: Used for multi-byte arithmetic. In automotive ECUs, the CY flag is tested after adding 16-bit or 32-bit sensor values (e.g., summing two 16-bit ADC readings from throttle position and pedal position sensors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CY flag also acts as a **"Boolean accumulator"** for the 8051's bit-manipulation instructions. The instruction `ANL C, bit` and `MOV C, bit` use CY as the implicit destination. This makes CY the most frequently used flag in automotive bit-banging protocols (e.g., software I2C for temperature sensors).

### 2.1.2 Auxiliary Carry Flag (AC) — PSW.6 (Bit Address 0xD6)

- **Symbol**: `AC`
- **What it does**: Set to **1** if there is a **carry/borrow out of bit 3** into bit 4 (i.e., from the lower nibble to the upper nibble) during addition or subtraction.
- **Why it matters**: Used **exclusively** for **Binary-Coded Decimal (BCD) arithmetic**. When using the `DA A` (Decimal Adjust Accumulator) instruction, the AC flag tells the hardware whether to add 0x06 to correct the lower nibble.

> [!NOTE]
> In an automotive instrument cluster, BCD arithmetic is often used when displaying numbers on 7-segment displays. The AC flag is therefore central to the cluster microcontroller's display refresh logic.

### 2.1.3 Flag 0 (F0) — PSW.5 (Bit Address 0xD5)

- **Symbol**: `F0`
- **What it does**: A **general-purpose, user-definable flag**. The 8051 hardware does **not** set or clear it automatically. The programmer can use it as a software-controlled state bit.
- **Use case**: In automotive embedded C, F0 is often used as a "software semaphore" between the main loop and an ISR, e.g., to signal "new CAN message received, process it now."

### 2.1.4 Register Bank Select 1 (RS1) — PSW.4 (Bit Address 0xD4)

### 2.1.5 Register Bank Select 0 (RS0) — PSW.3 (Bit Address 0xD3)

- **What they do (jointly)**: Select which of the **four register banks (Bank 0 to Bank 3)** in internal RAM (locations 0x00–0x1F) is currently active for R0–R7.
- **Why it matters**: Allows **fast context switching** without pushing/popping registers onto the stack. Critical for nested interrupts in real-time automotive systems.

### 2.1.6 Overflow Flag (OV) — PSW.2 (Bit Address 0xD2)

- **Symbol**: `OV`
- **What it does**: Set to **1** if there is a **signed arithmetic overflow** — that is, if the result of a signed addition or subtraction cannot be represented in 7 bits + sign (range -128 to +127).
- **Set condition (Addition of two signed numbers)**: Carry into bit 7 XOR Carry out of bit 7 = 1.
- **Why it matters**: Distinguishes signed overflow from unsigned overflow. The CY flag handles unsigned, OV handles signed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In safety-critical automotive software (ISO 26262), failure to check the OV flag after signed calculations can lead to **silent corruption** of variables such as brake pressure, torque, or steering angle, which is a recognized hazard.

### 2.1.7 Reserved Bit (PSW.1, Bit Address 0xD1)

- This bit is **not implemented** in the standard 8051. It is reserved for future use and should be written as 0 and ignored on read. (Some enhanced 8051 derivatives use it; students should not assume so.)

### 2.1.8 Parity Flag (P) — PSW.0 (Bit Address 0xD0)

- **Symbol**: `P`
- **What it does**: Automatically set to **1** if the **accumulator (A)** contains an **odd number of 1-bits**, and **0** if the accumulator contains an **even number of 1-bits**. This is the **even-parity** convention.
- **Why it matters**: Used for serial communication error-checking protocols. The P flag is automatically updated after every instruction that writes to the accumulator.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Bit Map Sheet

| Bit Position | Name | Bit Addr | Symbol | Set When | Cleared When | Engineering Use |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| PSW.7 | Carry | 0xD7 | CY | Carry out of bit 7 (add) or borrow (sub) | No carry/borrow | Multi-byte math, Boolean ops |
| PSW.6 | Auxiliary Carry | 0xD6 | AC | Carry out of bit 3 (BCD boundary) | No carry from bit 3 | BCD arithmetic, `DA A` correction |
| PSW.5 | Flag 0 | 0xD5 | F0 | User-defined software | User-defined software | ISR semaphores, state bits |
| PSW.4 | Register Sel 1 | 0xD4 | RS1 | Per RS1:RS0 table | Per RS1:RS0 table | Register bank selection |
| PSW.3 | Register Sel 0 | 0xD3 | RS0 | Per RS1:RS0 table | Per RS1:RS0 table | Register bank selection |
| PSW.2 | Overflow | 0xD2 | OV | Signed result out of -128..+127 | Result in valid signed range | Signed math validation |
| PSW.1 | Reserved | 0xD1 | — | Not used (undefined) | Not used | Reserved |
| PSW.0 | Parity | 0xD0 | P | Odd number of 1s in A | Even number of 1s in A | Serial comms parity check |

## 2.3 Register Bank Selection Truth Table (RS1:RS0)

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | Address Range (R0–R7) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 (default after RESET) | 0x00 – 0x07 |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 0x08 – 0x0F |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 0x10 – 0x17 |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 0x18 – 0x1F |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 examiner frequently asks: *"After a hardware reset, which register bank is active and what is the value of PSW?"* The answer is **Bank 0 is active and PSW = 0x00** (all bits cleared).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a typical automotive Electronic Control Unit (ECU) using an 8051 derivative:

1. **Throttle-by-wire system**: Uses signed arithmetic on pedal position. The **OV flag** is checked to ensure the pedal-travel delta has not wrapped around.
2. **CAN bus message validation**: Uses the **P flag** for software-based parity on incoming data frames.
3. **Real-time Odometer (BCD)**: Uses the **AC flag** and `DA A` to maintain BCD totals for the instrument cluster display.
4. **Interrupt context save/restore**: On a high-priority Timer 0 interrupt (e.g., 1 ms scheduler tick), the firmware sets **RS1:RS0 = 01** to switch to Bank 1, giving 8 "free" registers for ISR use without stack push/pop.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Parity Flag Value for a Given Accumulator

The Parity flag in 8051 follows the **even-parity** convention. The hardware computes the number of 1-bits in the accumulator and sets `P = 1` if the count is **odd** (so that the total, including P, is even).

### 3.1.1 Formal Algorithm

Let the accumulator contents be denoted as the 8-bit vector:

$$
A = [b_7, b_6, b_5, b_4, b_3, b_2, b_1, b_0]
$$

where each $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$. The parity count is defined as:

$$
N_1 = \sum_{i=0}^{7} b_i
$$

The Parity flag is then computed as:

$$
P = N_1 \pmod{2}
$$

That is, P equals the **remainder** when $N_1$ is divided by 2. If $N_1$ is odd, P = 1. If $N_1$ is even, P = 0.

### 3.1.2 Worked Example

**Problem**: Determine the value of the Parity flag after the instruction `MOV A, #0x5A` executes.

**Step 1**: Convert the hex literal to its 8-bit binary form.

$$
0x5A = 0101\;1010_{2}
$$

**Step 2**: Identify each bit and count the 1s.

$$
b_7 = 0, \; b_6 = 1, \; b_5 = 0, \; b_4 = 1, \; b_3 = 1, \; b_2 = 0, \; b_1 = 1, \; b_0 = 0
$$

**Step 3**: Sum the bits.

$$
N_1 = 0 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 0 = 4
$$

**Step 4**: Apply the modulo-2 operation.

$$
P = 4 \pmod{2} = 0
$$

**Conclusion**: After `MOV A, #0x5A`, the P flag is **0** because the accumulator contains an even number (four) of 1-bits.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Carry and Overflow Flags After an 8-bit Addition

### 3.2.1 The Underlying Binary Addition

For two 8-bit unsigned operands $X$ and $Y$, the ALU performs:

$$
S = X + Y
$$

where $S$ is a 9-bit internal result. The lower 8 bits of $S$ are written back to the accumulator, and bit 8 (the carry-out) determines the CY flag:

$$
CY = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } X + Y \geq 256 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### 3.2.2 The Overflow Flag Logic

For **signed** interpretation, the 8-bit result is treated as a two's-complement number. The OV flag is set when the sign of the result is wrong given the signs of the operands:

- Positive + Positive = Negative (impossible) → OV = 1
- Negative + Negative = Positive (impossible) → OV = 1
- Positive + Negative = always correct → OV = 0

Equivalently, the OV flag is the **exclusive-OR** of the carry into bit 7 ($C_{in7}$) and the carry out of bit 7 ($C_{out7}$):

$$
OV = C_{in7} \oplus C_{out7}
$$

### 3.2.3 Worked Example 1: Unsigned overflow (CY only)

**Problem**: Add `0xA5` and `0x5C`. Determine the accumulator content, the CY flag, and the OV flag.

**Step 1**: Convert to binary.

$$
0xA5 = 1010\;0101_2, \quad 0x5C = 0101\;1100_2
$$

**Step 2**: Perform column-wise binary addition.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 1\;0\;1\;0\;0\;1\;0\;1 \quad (0xA5) \\
+\;& 0\;1\;0\;1\;1\;1\;0\;0 \quad (0x5C) \\
\hline
& ?\;1\;1\;1\;1\;1\;0\;0\;0\;1
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading from LSB to MSB: 1+0=1, 0+0=0, 1+1=10 (write 0, carry 1), 0+1+1=10 (write 0, carry 1), 0+1+1=10 (write 0, carry 1), 1+0+1=10 (write 0, carry 1), 0+1+1=10 (write 0, carry 1), 1+0+1=10 (write 0, carry 1), carry out = 1.

**Step 3**: Read the 8-bit result and the carry.

$$
\text{Accumulator} = 0000\;0001_2 = 0x01
$$

$$
C_{out7} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; CY = 1
$$

**Step 4**: Determine the carry into bit 7. The carry *into* bit 7 is the result of the column at bit 6, which produced a carry of 1.

$$
C_{in7} = 1
$$

**Step 5**: Compute the OV flag.

$$
OV = C_{in7} \oplus C_{out7} = 1 \oplus 1 = 0
$$

**Conclusion**: After `ADD A, #0x5C` with `A = 0xA5`: A = 0x01, CY = 1, OV = 0. The unsigned result overflowed 255 (hence CY = 1), but the signed interpretation is consistent (a large positive plus a positive stays positive in the modular wrap), so OV = 0.

### 3.2.4 Worked Example 2: Signed overflow (OV = 1, CY = 0)

**Problem**: Add `0x7F` (+127 signed) and `0x02` (+2 signed). Determine A, CY, and OV.

**Step 1**: Binary representations.

$$
0x7F = 0111\;1111_2, \quad 0x02 = 0000\;0010_2
$$

**Step 2**: Column-wise addition.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 0\;1\;1\;1\;1\;1\;1\;1 \quad (0x7F) \\
+\;& 0\;0\;0\;0\;0\;0\;1\;0 \quad (0x02) \\
\hline
& 0\;1\;0\;0\;0\;0\;0\;0\;1
\end{aligned}
$$

The column-wise sums (LSB→MSB): 1+0=1, 1+1=10 (0, carry 1), 1+0+1=10 (0, carry 1), 1+0+1=10 (0, carry 1), 1+0+1=10 (0, carry 1), 1+0+1=10 (0, carry 1), 1+0+1=10 (0, carry 1), 0+0+1=1. No carry out of bit 7.

**Step 3**: Read the 8-bit result and flags.

$$
A = 1000\;0001_2 = 0x81
$$

In signed interpretation, $0x81 = -127$ decimal.

$$
C_{out7} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; CY = 0
$$

$$
C_{in7} = 1 \quad (\text{the carry propagated from bit 6 into bit 7})
$$

$$
OV = C_{in7} \oplus C_{out7} = 1 \oplus 0 = 1
$$

**Conclusion**: A = 0x81, CY = 0, OV = 1. The unsigned result (129) is in range, so CY = 0. But two positive numbers (+127 and +2) summed to a negative number (-127), which is a signed overflow, so OV = 1. This is the classic "automotive speedometer overflow" scenario.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Summary of Flag Behaviour for Common ALU Operations

| Instruction Family | CY Affected? | AC Affected? | OV Affected? | P Affected? |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `ADD`, `ADDC` | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes (if A written) |
| `SUBB` | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes (if A written) |
| `MUL AB` | Always 0 | No | Yes (overflow only) | Yes (if A written) |
| `DIV AB` | No (always 0) | No | No | Yes (if A written) |
| `DA A` | Yes (conditionally) | Yes (conditionally) | No | Yes (if A written) |
| `INC A` | No | No | No | Yes |
| `DEC A` | No | No | No | Yes |
| `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL` (with A) | No | No | No | Yes |
| `RLC A` | Yes (bit 7 → CY) | No | No | Yes |
| `RRC A` | Yes (bit 0 → CY) | No | No | Yes |
| `CLR A` | No | No | No | Yes (A=0, P=0) |
| `CPL A` | No | No | No | Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> This table is high-yield for the KTU 2024 Part A questions. Examiners love asking: *"After execution of `MUL AB`, what is the state of the CY and OV flags?"* Answer: CY is always 0; OV is 1 if the product exceeds 255, else 0.

## 3.4 Derivation of the BCD Decimal-Adjust Logic Using AC

When `DA A` executes, the 8051 hardware uses CY and AC to decide whether to add 0x60, 0x06, or 0x66 to the accumulator.

The hardware logic is:

$$
\text{If } (A_{\text{lower nibble}} > 9) \;\lor\; (AC = 1), \text{ then add } 0x06 \text{ to } A.
$$

$$
\text{If } (A_{\text{upper nibble}} > 9) \;\lor\; (CY = 1), \text{ then add } 0x60 \text{ to } A.
$$

### 3.4.1 Worked Example: BCD Adjustment

**Problem**: A = 0x9B (treated as two BCD digits, 9 and B, but B is invalid). What does `DA A` do?

**Step 1**: Inspect the lower nibble. $A_{\text{lower nibble}} = 0xB = 11_{10} > 9$, so we add 0x06.

$$
0x9B + 0x06 = 0xA1
$$

**Step 2**: Inspect the upper nibble of the new result. $0xA = 10_{10} > 9$, so we add 0x60.

$$
0xA1 + 0x60 = 0x01 \quad \text{with carry-out} \Rightarrow CY = 1
$$

**Step 3**: Final accumulator value. A = 0x01, CY = 1. In BCD terms, this is the low digit of 1, but with the carry out, the high digit (tracked separately) is 1. The full BCD result is 0x11, i.e., decimal 11, which is the correct BCD sum if 0x9 and 0x2 had been the original BCD operands.

> [!TIP]
> The AC flag is **automatically set** during the initial `ADD` (if a carry occurs from bit 3 to bit 4), so the AC check inside `DA A` covers the case where the lower-nibble carry is *masked* (e.g., 0x08 + 0x08 = 0x10 has a lower-nibble carry even though the lower nibble of the result, 0, is not > 9).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PSW Bit-Addressable Memory Map (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    PSW_Register[PSW Register - Address 0xD0 - 8 bits wide]
    
    PSW_Register --> Bit7[PSW.7 - Bit 0xD7 - CY Carry Flag]
    PSW_Register --> Bit6[PSW.6 - Bit 0xD6 - AC Auxiliary Carry Flag]
    PSW_Register --> Bit5[PSW.5 - Bit 0xD5 - F0 User Flag 0]
    PSW_Register --> Bit4[PSW.4 - Bit 0xD4 - RS1 Register Bank Select 1]
    PSW_Register --> Bit3[PSW.3 - Bit 0xD3 - RS0 Register Bank Select 0]
    PSW_Register --> Bit2[PSW.2 - Bit 0xD2 - OV Overflow Flag]
    PSW_Register --> Bit1[PSW.1 - Bit 0xD1 - Reserved Unused]
    PSW_Register --> Bit0[PSW.0 - Bit 0xD0 - P Parity Flag]
    
    Bit7 --> Src1[Source - ALU Carry Out of Bit 7]
    Bit6 --> Src2[Source - ALU Carry Out of Bit 3]
    Bit5 --> Src3[Source - User Software Defined]
    Bit4 --> Src4[Source - Decode Logic with RS0]
    Bit3 --> Src5[Source - Decode Logic with RS1]
    Bit2 --> Src6[Source - ALU Carry In XOR Carry Out Bit 7]
    Bit1 --> Src7[Source - Not Implemented]
    Bit0 --> Src8[Source - Parity Tree of Accumulator]
```

## 4.2 Register Bank Selection Decode Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    Decoder[2 to 4 Decoder - Driven by RS1 RS0]
    In_RS1[RS1 from PSW.4] --> Decoder
    In_RS0[RS0 from PSW.3] --> Decoder
    
    Decoder --> Bank0[Bank 0 Selected - Address 0x00 to 0x07 - Default After Reset]
    Decoder --> Bank1[Bank 1 Selected - Address 0x08 to 0x0F]
    Decoder --> Bank2[Bank 2 Selected - Address 0x10 to 0x17]
    Decoder --> Bank3[Bank 3 Selected - Address 0x18 to 0x1F]
    
    Bank0 --> RegSet0[R0 R1 R2 R3 R4 R5 R6 R7 of Bank 0]
    Bank1 --> RegSet1[R0 R1 R2 R3 R4 R5 R6 R7 of Bank 1]
    Bank2 --> RegSet2[R0 R1 R2 R3 R4 R5 R6 R7 of Bank 2]
    Bank3 --> RegSet3[R0 R1 R2 R3 R4 R5 R6 R7 of Bank 3]
```

## 4.3 Flag Update Flow During ADD Instruction (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[ADD A src Instruction Fetched and Decoded]
    Start --> ReadSrc[Read src Operand from Register or Memory]
    ReadSrc --> ReadA[Read Accumulator Current Value]
    ReadA --> ALU[ALU Performs 8 bit Binary Addition]
    ALU --> SumCalc[Compute 9 bit Internal Sum]
    SumCalc --> WriteA[Write Lower 8 bits to Accumulator]
    
    SumCalc --> CheckCout{Carry Out of Bit 7}
    CheckCout -- Yes --> SetCY[Set CY Flag 1]
    CheckCout -- No --> ClrCY[Clear CY Flag 0]
    
    SumCalc --> CheckCout3{Carry Out of Bit 3}
    CheckCout3 -- Yes --> SetAC[Set AC Flag 1]
    CheckCout3 -- No --> ClrAC[Clear AC Flag 0]
    
    SumCalc --> CheckCIn7{Carry In to Bit 7}
    CheckCout --> GetCout[Capture Carry Out Bit 7]
    CheckCIn7 --> GetCin[Capture Carry In Bit 7]
    GetCin --> XOR[Perform XOR Operation]
    GetCout --> XOR
    XOR --> SetOV{OV equals XOR result}
    SetOV -- 1 --> SetOVFlag[Set OV Flag 1]
    SetOV -- 0 --> ClrOVFlag[Clear OV Flag 0]
    
    WriteA --> ParityCheck[Parity Tree Counts 1 bits in A]
    ParityCheck --> SetP{Number of 1s is Odd}
    SetP -- Yes --> SetPFlag[Set P Flag 1]
    SetP -- No --> ClrPFlag[Clear P Flag 0]
```

## 4.4 Hardware Path of the Parity Flag (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    AReg[Accumulator 8 bits]
    AReg --> Tree[8 input XOR Parity Tree]
    Tree --> Latch[P Latch in PSW.0]
    Latch --> Output[Bit 0xD0 Readable as P]
    
    Tree --> Detail[Internally XOR Pair Chain b0 XOR b1 gives t1]
    Detail --> Detail2[t1 XOR b2 gives t2 ... and so on]
    Detail2 --> Final[t7 is the Parity Bit]
    Final --> Latch
```

> [!NOTE]
> The "Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow" diagrams above map the data path of the PSW register and its interaction with the ALU. They are intentionally drawn as functional flow rather than transistor-level schematics to match the KTU 2024 scheme's emphasis on architectural understanding over device physics.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q**: List the bits of the PSW register of the 8051 microcontroller. What is the bit address of the Carry flag? After a hardware reset, what is the value of the PSW register and which register bank is active?

**Model Answer**:

The PSW register bits, from MSB (PSW.7) to LSB (PSW.0), are:

1. **CY** — Carry Flag (bit address 0xD7)
2. **AC** — Auxiliary Carry Flag (bit address 0xD6)
3. **F0** — User-defined Flag 0 (bit address 0xD5)
4. **RS1** — Register Bank Select 1 (bit address 0xD4)
5. **RS0** — Register Bank Select 0 (bit address 0xD3)
6. **OV** — Overflow Flag (bit address 0xD2)
7. **—** — Reserved (bit address 0xD1, not implemented)
8. **P** — Parity Flag (bit address 0xD0)

Bit address of CY is **0xD7**. After a hardware reset, PSW = **0x00** and **Register Bank 0** (addresses 0x00–0x07) is active.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q**: Differentiate between the Carry (CY) and Overflow (OV) flags in the 8051. When is each flag set?

**Model Answer**:

The **Carry (CY)** flag is used to detect **unsigned arithmetic overflow** — that is, a carry out of bit 7 during an `ADD` operation, or a borrow during `SUBB`. The CY flag is set to 1 when the unsigned 8-bit result exceeds 255 or when subtraction requires a borrow.

The **Overflow (OV)** flag is used to detect **signed arithmetic overflow** — that is, when the result of a signed addition or subtraction cannot be represented in the two's-complement range -128 to +127. The OV flag is computed as the exclusive-OR of the carry into bit 7 and the carry out of bit 7.

In summary: CY is for **unsigned** operations, OV is for **signed** operations. Both can be set in the same instruction, both can be clear in the same instruction, and they are independent.

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the PSW register of the 8051 microcontroller in detail with a neat bit-map. Describe the function of each bit.

**Model Solution**:

The PSW (Program Status Word) is a Special Function Register at internal RAM address **0xD0**. It is a bit-addressable register, meaning each of its 8 bits has a unique bit address from 0xD7 (CY) to 0xD0 (P).

**Bit Map**:

| PSW.7 | PSW.6 | PSW.5 | PSW.4 | PSW.3 | PSW.2 | PSW.1 | PSW.0 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| CY | AC | F0 | RS1 | RS0 | OV | — | P |
| 0xD7 | 0xD6 | 0xD5 | 0xD4 | 0xD3 | 0xD2 | 0xD1 | 0xD0 |

**Function of each bit**:

- **CY (PSW.7)**: Set when an addition produces a carry out of bit 7 or a subtraction requires a borrow. Also used as a 1-bit accumulator for Boolean instructions.
- **AC (PSW.6)**: Set when an addition produces a carry from bit 3 to bit 4. Used by `DA A` for BCD correction.
- **F0 (PSW.5)**: A user-definable flag, not modified by hardware; the programmer may set or clear it as a general-purpose state bit.
- **RS1, RS0 (PSW.4, PSW.3)**: Select one of four register banks in internal RAM. RS1:RS0 = 00 → Bank 0 (0x00–0x07, default), 01 → Bank 1 (0x08–0x0F), 10 → Bank 2 (0x10–0x17), 11 → Bank 3 (0x18–0x1F).
- **OV (PSW.2)**: Set when a signed arithmetic operation produces a result outside the range -128 to +127.
- **PSW.1**: Reserved, undefined.
- **P (PSW.0)**: Set to 1 if the accumulator contains an odd number of 1-bits, else 0 (even-parity convention).

**(b) [7 Marks]** The accumulator contains 0xA3 and the instruction `ADD A, #0x5C` is executed. Determine the contents of the accumulator after execution, and state the values of CY, AC, OV, and P flags.

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1**: Convert operands to binary.

$$
0xA3 = 1010\;0011_2, \quad 0x5C = 0101\;1100_2
$$

**Step 2**: Perform column-wise addition.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 1\;0\;1\;0\;0\;0\;1\;1 \quad (0xA3) \\
+\;& 0\;1\;0\;1\;1\;1\;0\;0 \quad (0x5C) \\
\hline
\end{aligned}
$$

Column-by-column (LSB → MSB):
- Bit 0: 1 + 0 = 1, carry = 0
- Bit 1: 1 + 0 = 1, carry = 0
- Bit 2: 0 + 1 = 1, carry = 0
- Bit 3: 0 + 1 = 1, carry = 0
- Bit 4: 0 + 1 = 1, carry = 0
- Bit 5: 1 + 1 = 0, carry = 1
- Bit 6: 0 + 0 + 1 = 1, carry = 0
- Bit 7: 1 + 0 = 1, carry = 0

**Step 3**: Read the result and flags.

$$
A = 1111\;1111_2 = 0xFF
$$

- **CY = 0** (no carry out of bit 7)
- **AC = 0** (no carry out of bit 3, because bits 3 and 4 are both 0+1 = 1, no carry)
- **OV**: Compute $C_{in7} \oplus C_{out7} = 0 \oplus 0 = 0$. So **OV = 0**.
- **P**: Count the 1-bits in 0xFF. There are 8 ones (even), so **P = 0**.

**Final Answer**: A = 0xFF, CY = 0, AC = 0, OV = 0, P = 0.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the functions of the Carry (CY), Auxiliary Carry (AC), and Overflow (OV) flags. Show with an example how the OV flag detects signed overflow even when CY is clear.

**Model Solution**:

**Carry (CY) Flag**: Set to 1 when an unsigned 8-bit addition produces a carry out of the most significant bit (bit 7), or when a subtraction (`SUBB`) requires a borrow. It is reset to 0 otherwise.

**Auxiliary Carry (AC) Flag**: Set to 1 when there is a carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (i.e., from the lower nibble to the upper nibble). It is used exclusively by the `DA A` (Decimal Adjust) instruction for BCD arithmetic.

**Overflow (OV) Flag**: Set to 1 when the result of a signed arithmetic operation is too large in magnitude to be represented in 8 bits two's-complement (i.e., outside the range -128 to +127). It is computed as the exclusive-OR of the carry into bit 7 and the carry out of bit 7.

**Example where OV = 1 but CY = 0**:

Consider `A = 0x7F` (+127 signed) and execute `ADD A, #0x01`.

- Binary: $0x7F = 0111\;1111_2$ and $0x01 = 0000\;0001_2$.
- Sum: $0111\;1111_2 + 0000\;0001_2 = 1000\;0000_2 = 0x80$.
- **CY = 0** (no carry out of bit 7, since 0x7F + 0x01 = 0x80 ≤ 0xFF).
- **OV = 1**: +127 + 1 mathematically equals +128, which is outside the signed range. The signed interpretation of 0x80 is -128, which is wrong. Hence OV is set.
- This demonstrates that OV detects signed overflow independently of CY.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the four register banks of the 8051. How are they selected using the PSW? Give an example of how register bank switching is used in a real-time automotive application.

**Model Solution**:

The 8051 has **four register banks** (Bank 0, Bank 1, Bank 2, Bank 3), each containing 8 general-purpose registers named R0 through R7. These banks occupy the first 32 bytes of internal RAM (addresses 0x00 to 0x1F):

- Bank 0: 0x00 – 0x07 (default after reset)
- Bank 1: 0x08 – 0x0F
- Bank 2: 0x10 – 0x17
- Bank 3: 0x18 – 0x1F

**Selection via PSW**: The two bits **RS1 (PSW.4)** and **RS0 (PSW.3)** form a 2-bit code that selects the active register bank. The selection truth table is:

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 |

**Real-time automotive example**: In an Engine Control Unit (ECU), the main program uses Bank 0. A high-priority interrupt (e.g., a 1 ms crankshaft position sensor tick on Timer 0 overflow) needs registers R0–R7 immediately. The ISR first sets RS1:RS0 = 01 (via `SETB RS1` and `CLR RS0` or by writing `MOV PSW, #0x08`) to switch to Bank 1. The ISR can now use R0–R7 of Bank 1 without saving the main program's registers on the stack. Upon `RETI`, the ISR can either restore PSW to 0x00 (returning to Bank 0) or simply continue using Bank 1, since the main program's register context in Bank 0 is preserved untouched. This achieves **zero-overhead context switching**, which is essential in time-critical engine control loops.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams**:
> 
> 1. **Confusing CY and OV**: Many students write "the carry flag is set on signed overflow." This is wrong. CY is for unsigned; OV is for signed. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 
> 2. **Parity convention error**: The 8051 uses **even parity** (P = 1 when the number of 1s is odd). Some students mistakenly state the opposite (odd parity). Read the question carefully.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting that P is automatically set**: After every instruction that writes to the accumulator (ADD, SUBB, MOV A, ..., INC A, etc.), the P flag is updated. Students who write "P must be calculated manually" lose marks.
> 
> 4. **Bit-address notation**: Always write the bit address as `0xD7` or `D7H`, not just "7." The KTU examiner specifically tests the bit-address knowledge of PSW.
> 
> 5. **MUL AB flag behaviour**: Forgetting that `MUL AB` always clears CY, regardless of overflow. OV is set if the product exceeds 255. This is a high-yield KTU Part A question.
> 
> 6. **PSW.1 reserved bit**: Writing random definitions for PSW.1 (e.g., "general purpose flag") loses marks. State that it is **reserved and unused**.
> 
> 7. **Default register bank**: After `RESET`, PSW = 0x00 and Bank 0 is active. Some students incorrectly say Bank 3.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **PSW location**: SFR at **0xD0**, bit-addressable from **0xD7 to 0xD0**.
- ✅ **Four math flags**: CY, AC, OV, P.
- ✅ **Three control/user flags**: F0, RS1, RS0.
- ✅ **CY** = unsigned overflow (carry out of bit 7 or borrow in subtraction).
- ✅ **AC** = carry out of bit 3 (BCD boundary).
- ✅ **OV** = signed overflow ($C_{in7} \oplus C_{out7}$).
- ✅ **P** = 1 if accumulator has an **odd** number of 1-bits.
- ✅ **F0** = user-defined, hardware does not touch it.
- ✅ **RS1:RS0** select one of 4 register banks; default after reset is **00 (Bank 0)**.
- ✅ **PSW.1** is reserved and undefined.
- ✅ After `MUL AB`: **CY = 0 always**; OV = 1 if product > 255.
- ✅ After `DIV AB**: CY = 0; OV = 0** (and B = 0 indicates divide-by-zero error).
- ✅ Parity flag is updated **automatically** after every instruction that writes to the accumulator.
- ✅ `DA A` uses AC and CY to add 0x06, 0x60, or 0x66 to A for BCD correction.
- ✅ In automotive applications, register bank switching enables **fast interrupt context save/restore** without stack push/pop.
- ✅ In safety-critical code, **always check OV** after signed arithmetic on sensor data (brake pressure, torque, steering angle, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 8051 Register Banks and Stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** 8051 Register Banks and Stack ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the **Intel 8051 microcontroller architecture**, the term **Register Banks** refers to the **four distinct sets of eight general-purpose 8-bit registers (R0–R7)** that physically occupy the **lower 32 bytes (locations 00H to 1FH)** of the **128-byte on-chip Internal RAM (also called Data Memory or IDATA)**. These four banks — **Bank 0, Bank 1, Bank 2, and Bank 3** — provide the CPU with **32 bytes of fast, directly addressable working storage** without consuming external memory cycles. The **Stack** in the 8051 is an **8-bit wide, upward-growing LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) memory structure** implemented entirely within the same internal RAM. It is managed by the **Stack Pointer (SP)**, an **8-bit Special Function Register (SFR) located at address 81H**, which is reset to **07H** upon power-on, effectively directing the stack to begin at location **08H** of the internal RAM. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT746 Module 3):** The 8051 Register Bank and Stack concept is foundational to understanding **automotive ECU context switching**, **interrupt-driven sensor polling**, and **real-time task management** in vehicle Electronic Control Units (ECUs). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of the **Internal RAM of the 8051** as a **small office desk drawer** belonging to a busy automotive diagnostic engineer: - **Register Banks (00H–1FH)** are like **four labeled mini-drawers (Bank 0 to Bank 3)**, each containing exactly **8 small compartments (R0–R7)**. The engineer can keep four different active "job folders" — one for engine diagnostics, one for brake sensor readings, one for transmission control, and one for cabin climate — and **switch instantly** between them by simply rotating a selector knob. **No time is wasted emptying one drawer to use another.** - **Bit-Addressable Area (20H–2FH)** is like a **compartment with 16 small toggle switches** — each individual bit (0 or 1) can be flipped without affecting the others, perfect for storing boolean flags like "engine running" or "brake applied." - **General Purpose RAM (30H–7FH)** is the **bulk storage tray** — used for scratchpad variables, temporary calculations, and ASCII message buffers. - **The Stack** is like a **spring-loaded stack of plates on a cafeteria counter**. You can only **add a new plate on top (PUSH)** and **remove the top plate (POP)**. The Stack Pointer is the engineer's finger pointing at the **current top plate** — it always points to the **last valid data pushed**. > [!NOTE] > The 8051 Stack grows **UPWARD** (toward higher memory addresses) — this is **opposite** to many modern processors (like x86) which grow downward. This is a frequent KTU exam pitfall. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Units | |---|---|---| | Internal RAM Size | **128** | Bytes | | Total Register Banks | **4** (Bank 0 to Bank 3) | Banks | | Registers per Bank | **8** (R0 to R7) | Registers | | Bank Switching Bits | **2** (RS0, RS1 in PSW) | Bits | | Bit-Addressable Area | **16** bytes (20H–2FH) | Bytes | | Stack Pointer Default | **07H** | Hexadecimal | | Stack Growth Direction | **Upward** (Incrementing addresses) | — | | Stack Pointer Width | **8 bits** | Bits | | Maximum Stack Depth | **128 bytes** (limited by RAM) | Bytes | > [!NOTE] > The default SP value of **07H** is deliberately chosen so that the first PUSH operation places data at **08H**, which is the **start of Register Bank 1**. This means **any PUSH operation will overwrite Bank 1 contents** unless the programmer explicitly initializes SP to a safer location (e.g., 30H or higher) at the start of the main program. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Internal RAM Architecture — Byte-Wise Mapping The **128-byte Internal Data Memory** of the standard 8051 is partitioned as follows: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Address Range} &: \quad \text{00H} \longrightarrow \text{7FH} \\ \text{Total Size} &: \quad 128 \text{ bytes} = 2^7 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Detailed Partition Table:** | Address Range | Size | Region | Function | |---|---|---|---| | **00H – 07H** | 8 Bytes | **Register Bank 0** | Default R0–R7 selected on reset | | **08H – 0FH** | 8 Bytes | **Register Bank 1** | Selected when RS1=0, RS0=1 | | **10H – 17H** | 8 Bytes | **Register Bank 2** | Selected when RS1=1, RS0=0 | | **13H – 1FH** | 8 Bytes | **Register Bank 3** | Selected when RS1=1, RS0=1 | | **20H – 2FH** | 16 Bytes | **Bit-Addressable Area** | 128 individually addressable bits | | **30H – 7FH** | 80 Bytes | **General Purpose RAM** | Scratchpad, user variables | ### 2.2 Program Status Word (PSW) — The Bank Selector The **PSW register (address D0H)** contains the **two critical bits** that determine which register bank is currently active. The PSW bit allocation is shown below: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PSW} &= \text{[CY | AC | F0 | RS1 | RS0 | OV | — | P]} \\ \text{Bit} &= \text{[7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 ]} \end{aligned} $$ **Bank Selection Truth Table:** | RS1 (Bit 4) | RS0 (Bit 3) | Selected Bank | Address Range | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **0** | **0** | **Bank 0** | 00H – 07H | | **0** | **1** | **Bank 1** | 08H – 0FH | | **1** | **0** | **Bank 2** | 10H – 17H | | **1** | **1** | **Bank 3** | 18H – 1FH | > [!IMPORTANT] > Other PSW bits also carry critical information: **CY (Carry Flag, PSW.7)** for arithmetic overflow, **AC (Auxiliary Carry, PSW.6)** for BCD operations, **OV (Overflow, PSW.2)** for signed arithmetic, and **P (Parity, PSW.0)** which is **automatically set** to indicate even parity of the Accumulator. ### 2.3 Stack Pointer (SP) — Anatomy & Behavior The **Stack Pointer (SP, address 81H)** is an **8-bit register** that holds the address of the **last-used stack location** (the top of the stack). **Initial State:** Upon **RESET**, SP ← **07H**. **Behavioral Rules:** 1. **PUSH operation:** SP is **incremented FIRST** (SP ← SP + 1), then data is written to the new address. 2. **POP operation:** Data is read from the address pointed by SP, **then SP is decremented** (SP ← SP – 1). 3. **CALL instruction:** The 8051 hardware **automatically PUSHes the 16-bit return address** (2 bytes) onto the stack during ACALL/LCALL. 4. **Interrupt response:** The hardware **automatically PUSHes the 16-bit return address** before jumping to the ISR. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---| | Register Bank Starting Address | $\text{Start} = 8 \times (2 \cdot RS1 + RS0)$ | Direct memory mapping for bank switching | | Number of Register Banks | $N_{\text{banks}} = 2^{\text{RS bits}} = 2^2 = 4$ | Total banks available | | Registers per Bank | $R_{\text{per bank}} = 8$ | Architectural constant | | Total Bank Memory | $M_{\text{banks}} = 4 \times 8 = 32 \text{ bytes}$ | Lower 32 bytes of IRAM | | Bit-Addressable Memory | $B_{\text{bit}} = 16 \text{ bytes} \times 8 = 128 \text{ bits}$ | Boolean flag storage | | Stack Pointer Increment (PUSH) | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + 1$ (before write) | Stack growth direction | | Stack Pointer Decrement (POP) | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 1$ (after read) | Stack shrink direction | | Maximum Stack Capacity | $\text{Stack}_{\max} = (7\text{FH} - \text{SP}_{\text{init}}) + 1$ | Available stack depth | | Safe SP Initialization | $\text{SP} \geq 30\text{H}$ (avoid bank collision) | Best practice in code | | Return Address Storage (CALL) | $\text{Bytes PUSHed} = 2$ | For 16-bit PC | > [!NOTE] > **Why use multiple register banks in automotive applications?** In automotive ECUs, the 8051 often uses **Register Bank 0 for the main foreground task** (e.g., engine control loop), and **switches to Bank 1, 2, or 3 when entering an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** for sensor events (knock detection, crank angle, O2 sensor). This **saves the CPU cycles** of pushing all 8 registers onto the stack — instead, it simply toggles RS0/RS1 bits, achieving **zero-overhead context switching**. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Mapping Register Bank Numbers to RAM Addresses We can derive the starting address of any register bank using the binary value of the RS1:RS0 bits. **Step 1:** Recognize that RS1:RS0 form a 2-bit binary number. The decimal equivalent is: $$ \text{Bank Selector Value} = (RS1 \times 2^1) + (RS0 \times 2^0) = 2 \cdot RS1 + RS0 $$ **Step 2:** Since each bank occupies **8 bytes**, the starting address of a bank is: $$ \text{Start Address} = 8 \times (2 \cdot RS1 + RS0) $$ **Step 3:** Verify for each bank: - **Bank 0** (RS1=0, RS0=0): Start = $8 \times (0+0) = 0$ → **00H** ✓ - **Bank 1** (RS1=0, RS0=1): Start = $8 \times (0+1) = 8$ → **08H** ✓ - **Bank 2** (RS1=1, RS0=0): Start = $8 \times (2+0) = 16$ → **10H** ✓ - **Bank 3** (RS1=1, RS0=1): Start = $8 \times (2+1) = 24$ → **18H** ✓ **Step 4:** Within a bank, register Rn (n = 0 to 7) is located at: $$ \text{Address of } R_n = \text{Start Address} + n $$ For example, **R3 in Bank 2** would be at: $10\text{H} + 3 = 13\text{H}$. ### 3.2 Analytical Problem: Stack Operation Trace **Problem:** The 8051 stack pointer is initialized to **SP = 30H**. The following instructions are executed in sequence: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{1. PUSH 0A5H} \\ &\text{2. PUSH 0F3H} \\ &\text{3. POP ACC} \\ &\text{4.
