POP 0BH (Internal RAM address)} \end{aligned} $$ Trace the state of SP and the affected RAM locations after each instruction. **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Initial State:** SP = **30H** --- **Instruction 1: PUSH 0A5H** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Action 1: Increment SP} \quad \text{SP} = 30\text{H} + 1 = 31\text{H} \\ &\text{Action 2: Write 0A5H to address 31H} \\ &\text{RAM[31H]} \leftarrow \text{0A5H} \\ &\text{SP final} = \text{31H} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Instruction 2: PUSH 0F3H** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Action 1: Increment SP} \quad \text{SP} = 31\text{H} + 1 = 32\text{H} \\ &\text{Action 2: Write 0F3H to address 32H} \\ &\text{RAM[32H]} \leftarrow \text{0F3H} \\ &\text{SP final} = \text{32H} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Instruction 3: POP ACC** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Action 1: Read from address 32H into ACC} \quad \text{ACC} \leftarrow \text{0F3H} \\ &\text{Action 2: Decrement SP} \quad \text{SP} = 32\text{H} - 1 = 31\text{H} \\ &\text{Note: RAM[32H] still contains 0F3H (data is not erased, only SP moves)} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Instruction 4: POP 0BH (Internal RAM address)** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Action 1: Read from address 31H} \quad \text{RAM[0BH]} \leftarrow \text{0A5H} \\ &\text{Action 2: Decrement SP} \quad \text{SP} = 31\text{H} - 1 = 30\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Final Summary Table:** | Step | Instruction | SP (Before) | SP (After) | RAM[31H] | RAM[32H] | ACC | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Start | — | 30H | 30H | xx | xx | xx | | 1 | PUSH 0A5H | 30H | **31H** | **0A5H** | xx | xx | | 2 | PUSH 0F3H | 31H | **32H** | 0A5H | **0F3H** | xx | | 3 | POP ACC | 32H | **31H** | 0A5H | 0F3H | **0F3H** | | 4 | POP 0BH | 31H | **30H** | 0A5H | 0F3H | 0F3H | ### 3.3 Analytical Problem: Interrupt Context Saving via Register Banks **Problem:** In an automotive ECU, the main loop uses **Bank 0** (RS1=0, RS0=0). A brake-pressure interrupt arrives. The ISR uses **Bank 1**. Demonstrate how the 8051 switches context. **Step-by-Step Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Step 1: Main code runs with Bank 0 active.} \\ &\quad \text{R0} = \text{sensor data of throttle position} \\ &\quad \text{R7} = \text{counter for ignition timing} \\ & \\ &\text{Step 2: Interrupt hardware automatically completes:} \\ &\quad \text{(a) Current PC (return address) is PUSHed onto stack} \\ &\quad \text{(b) Execution jumps to ISR vector address} \\ & \\ &\text{Step 3: First instruction of ISR sets register bank:} \\ &\quad \text{SETB RS1} \quad \text{(RS1 ← 1)} \\ &\quad \text{CLR RS0} \quad \text{(RS0 ← 0)} \\ &\quad \Rightarrow \text{Now Bank 1 (addresses 08H–0FH) is active} \\ & \\ &\text{Step 4: ISR uses R0–R7 of Bank 1 freely.} \\ &\quad \text{No PUSH/POP of R0–R7 needed!} \\ &\quad \text{CPU cycles saved} = 8 \text{ PUSHes} + 8 \text{ POPs} = 16 \text{ cycles} \\ & \\ &\text{Step 5: At RETI, hardware pops PC and resumes Bank 0 execution.} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **single most important automotive application** of register banks — it enables **deterministic, low-latency interrupt response** critical for safety systems like ABS, airbag deployment, and electronic stability control. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Reset vs. Bank Selection | Operation | RS1 | RS0 | Active Bank | Stack Pointer | |---|---|---|---|---| | After Power-On RESET | 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** | **07H** | | After `SETB RS1` | 1 | 0 | **Bank 2** | Unchanged | | After `SETB RS0` (with RS1=0) | 0 | 1 | **Bank 1** | Unchanged | | After `MOV PSW, #18H` | 1 | 1 | **Bank 3** | Unchanged | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Stack Pointer is NOT affected by changing the register bank**. They are **independent mechanisms**. A common KTU exam trick is to ask whether changing the bank also changes SP — it does **NOT**. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 8051 Internal RAM Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["8051 Internal Data Memory
128 Bytes (00H to 7FH)"] B["Register Banks
Lower 32 Bytes (00H to 1FH)"] C["Bit-Addressable Area
16 Bytes (20H to 2FH)"] D["General Purpose RAM
80 Bytes (30H to 7FH)"] E["Bank 0 (Default)
00H to 07H
R0 R1 R2 R3 R4 R5 R6 R7"] F["Bank 1
08H to 0FH
R0 R1 R2 R3 R4 R5 R6 R7"] G["Bank 2
10H to 17H
R0 R1 R2 R3 R4 R5 R6 R7"] H["Bank 3
18H to 1FH
R0 R1 R2 R3 R4 R5 R6 R7"] I["128 Bit-Addressable Bits
(00H to 7FH of bit address space)"] J["Scratchpad Variables
Buffers and Temporary Data"] A --> B A --> C A --> D B --> E B --> F B --> G B --> H C --> I D --> J ``` ### 4.2 Register Bank Selection Logic via PSW Bits ```mermaid flowchart LR P["PSW Register (D0H)"] R1["RS1 (Bit 4)"] R0["RS0 (Bit 3)"] DEC["2-to-4 Decoder Logic"] B0["Bank 0
00H to 07H"] B1["Bank 1
08H to 0FH"] B2["Bank 2
10H to 17H"] B3["Bank 3
18H to 1FH"] P --> R1 P --> R0 R1 --> DEC R0 --> DEC DEC -->|00| B0 DEC -->|01| B1 DEC -->|10| B2 DEC -->|11| B3 ``` ### 4.3 Stack Operation Flow (PUSH and POP) ```mermaid flowchart TB StartPush["Execute PUSH instruction"] IncSP1["Step A: SP is incremented
SP = SP + 1"] WriteData["Step B: Source data is written
to new SP address"] EndPush["Stack now has one more element"] StartPop["Execute POP instruction"] ReadData["Step A: Data is read from
current SP address"] DecSP["Step B: SP is decremented
SP = SP - 1"] EndPop["Stack now has one less element"] StartPush --> IncSP1 IncSP1 --> WriteData WriteData --> EndPush StartPop --> ReadData ReadData --> DecSP DecSP --> EndPop ``` ### 4.4 Automotive ECU Context Switching Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB Main["Main Loop - Engine Control
Using Register Bank 0
RS1=0, RS0=0"] BrakeInt["Brake Pressure Sensor Interrupt Triggered"] HardSave["Hardware Auto-Pushes
16-bit PC onto Stack"] SwitchBank["ISR Code: SETB RS1
Now Bank 1 is active"] ISRRunning["ISR Executes using R0-R7 of Bank 1
No need to push registers"] RETI["RETI instruction"] HardPop["Hardware Auto-Pops PC
Returns to Main Loop"] Resume["Main Loop resumes with Bank 0
RS1=0, RS0=0 intact"] Main --> BrakeInt BrakeInt --> HardSave HardSave --> SwitchBank SwitchBank --> ISRRunning ISRRunning --> RETI RETI --> HardPop HardPop --> Resume Resume --> Main ``` ### 4.5 Comparative Schematic: Bank 0 vs Bank 1 Memory Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Bank0["Bank 0 (00H to 07H) - Used by Main"] A0["R0 at 00H"] A1["R1 at 01H"] A2["R2 at 02H"] A3["R3 at 03H"] A4["R4 at 04H"] A5["R5 at 05H"] A6["R6 at 06H"] A7["R7 at 07H"] end subgraph Bank1["Bank 1 (08H to 0FH) - Used by ISR"] B0["R0 at 08H"] B1["R1 at 09H"] B2["R2 at 0AH"] B3["R3 at 0BH"] B4["R4 at 0CH"] B5["R5 at 0DH"] B6["R6 at 0EH"] B7["R7 at 0FH"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > The 8051 allows **only ONE register bank to be active at a time**. The assembly instructions `MOV A, R0` always refer to the currently selected bank, never all four banks simultaneously. This is a key exam concept. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Explain the four register banks of the 8051 microcontroller. How is the active bank selected?** **Model Answer:** The 8051 has **four register banks (Bank 0, Bank 1, Bank 2, Bank 3)**, each containing **8 general-purpose 8-bit registers (R0–R7)**. They occupy the **lower 32 bytes (00H–1FH)** of internal RAM. - **Bank 0:** 00H – 07H - **Bank 1:** 08H – 0FH - **Bank 2:** 10H – 17H - **Bank 3:** 18H – 1FH The active register bank is selected by the **RS1 (PSW.4) and RS0 (PSW.3)** bits of the **Program Status Word (PSW)**, which function as a 2-bit binary selector. Only one bank is accessible at a time through instructions like `MOV A, Rn`. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **What is the default value of the 8051 Stack Pointer after reset? Why is it considered unsafe?** **Model Answer:** The Stack Pointer (SP, SFR address 81H) is initialized to **07H** after power-on reset. This is considered **unsafe** because the very first `PUSH` operation will: 1. Increment SP from 07H to 08H. 2. Write data to address **08H**, which is the **start of Register Bank 1**. Thus, any stack activity immediately **overwrites Bank 1 registers** unless the programmer manually initializes SP to a safer location (typically **30H or 60H**) at the start of the program using `MOV SP, #30H`. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the internal RAM structure of the 8051 microcontroller. List the four register banks and their address ranges. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the PUSH and POP operations of the 8051 stack in detail with an example. What happens to SP in each case? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Internal RAM Structure and Register Banks (7 Marks):** The 8051 has **128 bytes of internal RAM (00H–7FH)** divided into three regions: **1. Register Banks (00H–1FH):** 32 bytes split into 4 banks of 8 registers each. | Bank | Address Range | Registers | |---|---|---| | Bank 0 | 00H – 07H | R0 – R7 | | Bank 1 | 08H – 0FH | R0 – R7 | | Bank 2 | 10H – 17H | R0 – R7 | | Bank 3 | 18H – 1FH | R0 – R7 | **2. Bit-Addressable Area (20H–2FH):** 16 bytes containing **128 individually addressable bits** (bit addresses 00H–7FH). Used for boolean flags. **3. General Purpose RAM (30H–7FH):** 80 bytes for variables, buffers, and the stack. Selection of active bank is done via **RS0 (PSW.3) and RS1 (PSW.4)** bits. Bank 0 is selected after reset. **(b) PUSH and POP Operations (7 Marks):** The 8051 stack is an **8-bit upward-growing LIFO** structure in internal RAM, managed by **SP (81H)**. **PUSH operation (Two Steps):** 1. **SP ← SP + 1** (Increment first) 2. **RAM[SP] ← Source Data** (Write to new SP address) **Example:** `PUSH 0A5H` when SP = 30H: - Step 1: SP becomes 31H - Step 2: RAM[31H] = 0A5H **POP operation (Two Steps):** 1. **Destination ← RAM[SP]** (Read current data) 2. **SP ← SP – 1** (Decrement after read) **Example:** `POP ACC` when SP = 32H: - Step 1: ACC ← RAM[32H] - Step 2: SP becomes 31H > **Important Note:** The POP operation does **NOT erase** data from RAM; it only moves the SP pointer downward. The data persists until overwritten. **Worked Example Trace (SP initially = 30H):** | Instruction | SP After | RAM Affected | |---|---|---| | PUSH 55H | 31H | RAM[31H] = 55H | | PUSH 77H | 32H | RAM[32H] = 77H | | POP ACC | 31H | ACC = 77H | | POP 10H | 30H | RAM[10H] = 55H | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the role of the PSW register in register bank selection. Show how the bank switching is performed with an assembly example. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss how the 8051 register bank feature is exploited in automotive interrupt handling. Why is this superior to a PUSH/POP-based approach? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) PSW Register and Bank Selection (7 Marks):** The **Program Status Word (PSW, address D0H)** is an 8-bit SFR whose **bits 4 and 3 (RS1 and RS0)** form a 2-bit binary code to select one of four register banks. **PSW Bit Layout:** | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | CY | AC | F0 | **RS1** | **RS0** | OV | — | P | **Bank Selection Table:** | RS1 | RS0 | Bank | Address | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | 0 | 00H–07H | | 0 | 1 | 1 | 08H–0FH | | 1 | 0 | 2 | 10H–17H | | 1 | 1 | 3 | 18H–1FH | **Assembly Example:** ```assembly MOV PSW, #00H ; Select Bank 0 MOV R0, #25H ; R0 in Bank 0 = 25H (at 00H) SETB RS0 ; Select Bank 1 (RS1=0, RS0=1) MOV R0, #50H ; R0 in Bank 1 = 50H (at 08H) MOV PSW, #18H ; Select Bank 3 directly MOV R7, #0FFH ; R7 in Bank 3 = FFH (at 1FH) ``` Note that `SETB RS0` toggles **only PSW.3**, leaving other PSW bits unaffected. This is the recommended method for bank switching during ISRs. **(b) Register Banks in Automotive Interrupt Handling (7 Marks):** In automotive ECUs, multiple sensor interrupts (crankshaft position, knock sensor, lambda sensor, wheel speed for ABS) must be serviced with **deterministic latency**. The 8051 register bank feature provides **zero-overhead context switching**: **Procedure:** 1. **Main task** runs using **Bank 0** (default). 2. On interrupt, the **8051 hardware automatically pushes the 16-bit return address (PC)** onto the stack. 3. The **ISR begins with `SETB RS1`** to switch to **Bank 1**. 4. The ISR uses **R0–R7 of Bank 1** for its local variables. 5. The main task's R0–R7 (in Bank 0) remain **untouched and preserved** automatically. 6. On `RETI`, hardware pops PC and the main task resumes **without restoring any registers** — they were never overwritten. **Superiority over PUSH/POP:** | Aspect | Register Bank Method | PUSH/POP Method | |---|---|---| | CPU Cycles | **0 cycles** for register saving | 16 cycles (8 PUSH + 8 POP) per switch | | Latency | **Deterministic** (fixed) | Variable based on number of registers | | Code Size | 2 instructions (SETB bits) | 16 instructions | | Stack Usage | None | Consumes 8 stack bytes | | Real-time Suitability | **Excellent** for hard real-time | Risky for safety-critical loops | This is why **automotive-grade 8051 derivatives** (e.g., Siemens SAB-C167, Infineon C500) retain and enhance this register bank concept for **powertrain and chassis control** modules. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes on this topic:** > > 1. **Stack growth direction error (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often write that the 8051 stack grows **downward**. It grows **UPWARD** (toward higher addresses). PUSH increments SP first; POP decrements SP last. > 2. **Confusing PUSH order with POP order (–1 mark):** In PUSH, the increment is **before** the write. In POP, the decrement is **after** the read. Reversing these will lose marks. > 3. **Forgetting that changing banks does NOT change SP (–1 mark):** Register bank selection (RS0/RS1) and Stack Pointer are **independent**. Switching banks does not move SP. > 4. **Believing default SP = 00H (–1 mark):** Default SP after reset is **07H**, not 00H. The very first PUSH will write to address **08H** (Bank 1's R0). > 5. **Wrong PSW bit positions (–1 mark):** RS1 is **PSW.4** and RS0 is **PSW.3**. Students sometimes swap these. > 6. **Stating all 128 RAM bytes are bit-addressable (–2 marks):** Only **20H–2FH** (16 bytes = 128 bits) is bit-addressable. The rest is byte-addressable. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] The 8051 internal RAM has **128 bytes** split into **4 register banks (32 bytes) + bit-addressable area (16 bytes) + general RAM (80 bytes)**. - [x] **Four register banks** (Bank 0–3), each with **R0–R7**, are selected via **RS1 (PSW.4) and RS0 (PSW.3)**. - [x] **Default bank after reset = Bank 0** (00H–07H). **Default SP = 07H**. - [x] The **stack grows UPWARD** (toward higher addresses). - [x] **PUSH** sequence: `SP++` THEN `write to RAM[SP]`. - [x] **POP** sequence: `read from RAM[SP]` THEN `SP--`. - [x] POP does **not erase** data — it only moves the SP pointer. - [x] The **Stack Pointer and Register Bank selector are independent** — changing banks does not affect SP. - [x] **Bit-addressable area is 20H–2FH** (128 bits: 00H–7FH in bit-address space). - [x] Register banks enable **zero-overhead context switching** — critical for real-time automotive interrupts (ABS, ECU, airbag controllers). - [x] Always **initialize SP to 30H or higher** at program start to avoid accidental Bank 1 corruption. - [x] `LCALL`/`ACALL` automatically push **2 bytes** (16-bit PC) onto the stack. - [x] The hardware automatically uses the stack for **return addresses** during subroutine calls and interrupts. - [x] In automotive design, **Bank 0 = main task, Bank 1–3 = ISRs** is the standard convention. - [x] Maximum stack depth from SP=30H: 80 bytes (30H to 7FH).

topics.title: Internal Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Internal Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Internal Memory** in the context of microprocessors and automotive microcontrollers refers to the *on-chip* semiconductor memory units that are physically integrated within the same silicon die as the Central Processing Unit (CPU) or Microcontroller Unit (MCU). It encompasses the entire hierarchy of storage elements that the processor can access directly without external bus cycles — namely **Registers**, **Cache Memory (L1/L2)**, and **Main On-chip RAM/ROM**.

In automotive applications, internal memory takes on mission-critical significance because it must reliably store:
- **Real-time sensor data** (engine RPM, throttle position, oxygen sensor values)
- **Calibration maps** (fuel injection lookup tables, ignition timing curves)
- **Diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs)** for OBD-II compliance
- **Bootloaders and firmware** for ECU operation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Internal Memory = Registers + Cache (if present) + On-chip ROM (Program Memory) + On-chip RAM (Data Memory) that reside within the same IC package as the processor core. It is directly addressable via the internal data/address bus.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Automotive Workshop Desk" Model

Imagine an automotive ECU as a **mechanic's workbench** inside a busy workshop:

| Workbench Element | Computer Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanics' **tool belts** (always worn) | **CPU Registers** | Fastest, smallest, holding the *current* job |
| **Top drawer of the desk** (recently used parts) | **Cache Memory (L1/L2)** | Quick retrieval, very limited capacity |
| **Lower shelf of the desk** (organized bins) | **On-chip RAM** | Working area for current job's components |
| **Wall-mounted file cabinet** (instruction manuals) | **On-chip ROM/Flash** | Permanent instructions — never leave the workshop |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Tip:** Just as a mechanic wouldn't walk across the workshop every time they needed a screwdriver (that would be like fetching data from *external* memory), the ECU keeps frequently used data *inside* the chip itself. This is what we call **Internal Memory** — it eliminates the slow "walk to the warehouse" (external memory access) and drastically cuts latency, which is critical when an airbag ECU must deploy in under **30 milliseconds**.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Internal Memory

```
Internal Memory
├── Volatile (Loses data when power OFF)
│   ├── Static RAM (SRAM)  → uses 6 transistors per bit
│   └── Dynamic RAM (DRAM) → uses 1 transistor + 1 capacitor per bit
└── Non-Volatile (Retains data without power)
    ├── Mask ROM (MROM)        → programmed at fabrication
    ├── Programmable ROM (PROM) → one-time programmable by user
    ├── Erasable PROM (EPROM)   → UV-erased, electrically programmed
    ├── Electrically EPROM (EEPROM) → byte-level electrical erase
    └── Flash Memory            → block-level electrical erase
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Reality Check:** Modern automotive MCUs (e.g., Infineon Aurix TC27x, NXP S32K1xx, Renesas RH850) predominantly use **embedded Flash** for program storage and **SRAM** for data — DRAM is *rarely* used internally because it requires constant refresh cycles that consume power and complicate functional-safety (ISO 26262) compliance.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Logic High Voltage (V_IH)** | **≥ 2.0 V** (TTL) / **≥ 0.7 × V_DD** (CMOS) | Minimum voltage recognized as '1' |
| **Logic Low Voltage (V_IL)** | **≤ 0.8 V** (TTL) / **≤ 0.3 × V_DD** (CMOS) | Maximum voltage recognized as '0' |
| **Automotive Temperature Grade** | **−40 °C to +125 °C** (AEC-Q100 Grade 1) | Internal memory must operate across this range |
| **Retention Time (Flash)** | **≥ 10 years** at 85 °C | Non-volatile data persistence |
| **Endurance (Flash cycles)** | **≥ 100,000 erase/write cycles** | Critical for ECU firmware updates |
| **Access Time (on-chip SRAM)** | **1–10 ns** | Typical for 100–300 MHz MCUs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Memory Organization & Hierarchical Operation

The internal memory operates in a strict **hierarchical pyramid**, where each level trades capacity for speed:

```
                ┌─────────────┐
                │  REGISTERS  │  ← 1–4 bytes, < 1 ns access
                ├─────────────┤
                │  L1 CACHE   │  ← 16–64 KB, 1–3 cycles
                ├─────────────┤
                │  L2 CACHE   │  ← 128 KB – 2 MB, 5–15 cycles
                ├─────────────┤
                │  ON-CHIP    │
                │  SRAM (RAM) │  ← 4 KB – 1 MB, 10–30 ns
                ├─────────────┤
                │  ON-CHIP    │
                │  FLASH/ROM  │  ← 256 KB – 16 MB, 30–100 ns
                └─────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Hierarchy?** A perfectly designed single-level memory would be the **fastest** AND **largest** AND **cheapest** — but physics forbids this. Faster memory uses more transistors per bit (SRAM = 6T), consumes more silicon area and power, and generates more heat. The hierarchy balances these trade-offs.

---

### 2.2 SRAM (Static Random Access Memory) — Cell Architecture

Each SRAM cell uses **6 CMOS transistors** (6T cell) arranged as two cross-coupled inverters forming a **bi-stable latch**:

```
      V_DD                       V_DD
       │                          │
      [T1]                       [T3]
       │                          │
   Bit─┤├─────┬───────┬─────┤├─Bit̄ (complement)
       │      │       │     │
      [T2]   [T4]   [T5]  [T6]
       │      │       │     │
      GND    WL      WL    GND
              (Word Line) (access transistors)
```

**Operating Principle:**
1. **Hold state:** Cross-coupled inverters (T1-T2 and T3-T4) maintain the stored bit as long as V_DD is present.
2. **Read:** Word Line (WL) goes HIGH → T5, T6 turn ON → Bit and Bit̄ lines connect to the cell, sense amplifiers read differential voltage.
3. **Write:** Word Line HIGH → drive Bit/Bit̄ lines with new data → overpowers the latch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Justification:** SRAM is the **dominant on-chip data memory** in automotive MCUs because it requires *no refresh cycles*, has deterministic access time (crucial for **real-time ECU loops** running at fixed 1–10 ms intervals), and survives automotive temperature ranges without complex timing calibration.

---

### 2.3 DRAM (Dynamic Random Access Memory) — Cell Architecture

Each DRAM cell uses **1 access transistor + 1 storage capacitor (1T1C)**:

```
    Bit Line ──┤├─────┬───── Capacitor ── GND
               T       │
                       │
                      WL (Word Line)
```

**Operating Principle:**
1. **Write:** WL HIGH → T conducts → charge flows onto capacitor.
2. **Read:** WL HIGH → charge shares with Bit Line → sense amplifier detects small voltage delta (≈ 30–50 mV).
3. **Refresh:** Capacitor leaks charge (typical retention ≈ 64 ms) → must be re-read & rewritten every refresh cycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse the refresh interval with access time. Refresh is a **background, periodic**, full-array operation — *not* triggered per access. In automotive ECUs, DRAM's mandatory refresh disrupts deterministic timing, making it *unsuitable* for safety-critical real-time loops (e.g., engine management, brake-by-wire).

---

### 2.4 Non-Volatile Memory (NVM) Technologies

| Type | Program Mechanism | Erase Mechanism | Granularity | Automotive Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mask ROM** | At wafer fabrication (photolithography mask) | Not erasable | Whole chip | High-volume fixed calibration (legacy ECUs) |
| **PROM** | Fuse/anti-fuse blown by current pulse | Not erasable | Per bit (one-time) | Rare — prototype validation |
| **EPROM** | Hot-electron injection (HEI) at high V_pp | **UV light** through quartz window (~20 min) | Whole chip | Obsolete — replaced by Flash |
| **EEPROM** | Fowler-Nordheim tunneling | Electrical, byte-by-byte | Byte | Storing OBD-II DTCs, immobilizer codes, VIN |
| **Flash (NOR)** | Channel hot-electron injection | Fowler-Nordheim tunneling, **block**-level | Sector/Block | Bootloader, application firmware |
| **Flash (NAND)** | FN tunneling | FN tunneling | Page/Block | External data storage (eMMC in infotainment) |

> [!NOTE]
> **NOR vs NAND Flash — Critical for Automotives:** NOR Flash allows **random byte access** and **execute-in-place (XIP)** — the CPU can fetch instructions directly from Flash without copying to RAM first. This is why automotive MCUs use NOR Flash for program memory. NAND Flash is *sequential* — used only for mass data storage (navigation maps, telematics logs).

---

### 2.5 Memory-Mapped I/O vs Isolated I/O

In automotive MCUs, internal peripherals (ADC, PWM, CAN, Timers) are accessed via two schemes:

| Aspect | Memory-Mapped I/O | Isolated (Port-Mapped) I/O |
|---|---|---|
| **Address Space** | I/O shares memory address space | Separate I/O address space |
| **Instructions** | Any memory instruction (MOV, ADD, etc.) | Special IN/OUT instructions (x86) |
| **Used by** | ARM Cortex-M, PowerPC, Aurix | Legacy 8085, 8086 |
| **Automotive MCU Example** | Infineon Aurix TC27x | None (modern RISC MCUs abandoned this) |

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = 2^n$ | Number of addressable memory locations ($N$) from $n$ address lines | locations (unitless) |
| 2 | $M_{total} = N \times W$ | Total memory capacity = locations × word width | bits / bytes |
| 3 | $T_{access} = t_{AA} + t_{setup}$ | Memory access time = address-to-output delay + setup time | seconds (ns) |
| 4 | $T_{cycle} = T_{access} + T_{recovery}$ | Memory cycle time = access + recovery for next op | seconds (ns) |
| 5 | $\text{Power} = C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | Dynamic power of CMOS memory cell | Watts |
| 6 | $\text{Density}_{SRAM} = \frac{\text{Area}_{6T}}{F^2}$ | SRAM cell density in terms of feature size $F$ | $\lambda^2$ or $F^2$ |
| 7 | $\text{Density}_{DRAM} = \frac{\text{Area}_{1T1C}}{F^2}$ | DRAM cell density in terms of feature size $F$ | $\lambda^2$ or $F^2$ |
| 8 | $\text{Refresh Rate} = \frac{1}{T_{retention}}$ | DRAM refresh frequency (typically 64 ms retention → 15.6 µs/row) | Hz |
| 9 | $\text{Hit Ratio} = \frac{\text{Cache Hits}}{\text{Total Accesses}}$ | Cache effectiveness (0 to 1) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $T_{avg} = H \cdot T_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot T_{main}$ | Average access time with cache hit ratio $H$ | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - $n$ address lines ⇒ exactly $2^n$ unique locations (no exceptions in KTU problems).
> - If a memory chip is $2^n \times m$, it requires $n$ address lines and $m$ data lines.
> - For byte-addressable systems, $2^n$ bytes; for word-addressable, $2^n$ words.
> - SRAM cell area ≈ $6F^2$ (where $F$ = minimum feature size).
> - DRAM cell area ≈ $1F^2$ → **~6× denser** than SRAM.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **Bosch Motronic ECU** for a gasoline engine:
- The **infineon Aurix TC27x** has 2 MB internal Flash, 192 KB SRAM.
- During cranking, the **firmware in Flash** executes via **XIP** — no RAM copy needed.
- The **calibration maps** (16×16 VE tables, spark advance tables) are stored in Flash and *copied to SRAM* on boot for fast lookup.
- The **OBD-II DTCs** are written to a dedicated **EEPROM emulation region** within Flash (modern MCUs emulate EEPROM in Flash due to higher endurance management via wear-leveling algorithms).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Address Lines from Memory Size

**Problem Setup:** A microcontroller has **32 KB** of internal SRAM. The memory is **byte-addressable**. Determine the number of address lines.

**Given:**
$$M = 32 \text{ KB} = 32 \times 1024 \text{ bytes} = 32768 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 1 — Express memory size as a power of 2:**

$$\begin{aligned}
32768 &= 2^{15} \\
\text{(verification: } 2^{10} &= 1024, \ 2^{15} = 32768 \text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the fundamental address equation $N = 2^n$:**

Since the memory has $N = 32768 = 2^{15}$ unique locations, and the number of address lines $n$ must satisfy $N = 2^n$:

$$2^n = 2^{15} \implies n = 15$$

**Step 3 — State the result clearly:**

> The microcontroller requires **15 address lines** ($A_0$ through $A_{14}$) to address the 32 KB SRAM. The address range is **0x0000 to 0x7FFF** in hexadecimal.

**Verification using hex:**
- Lower bound: $0x0000 = 0$
- Upper bound: $0x7FFF = 7 \times 16^3 + 15 \times 16^2 + 15 \times 16 + 15 = 32768 - 1 = 32767$
- Total locations: $32767 - 0 + 1 = 32768 = 32 \text{ KB}$ ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Address Decoding for Multiple Memory Chips

**Problem Setup:** A 16-bit automotive MCU must interface **two** 8 KB SRAM chips and **one** 16 KB Flash chip. Compute the address line splitting, chip select logic, and memory map.

**Given:**
- Chip 1 (SRAM_A): 8 KB
- Chip 2 (SRAM_B): 8 KB
- Chip 3 (Flash): 16 KB
- Total memory: $8 + 8 + 16 = 32 \text{ KB}$

**Step 1 — Total address lines for 32 KB:**

$$N_{total} = 32 \text{ KB} = 2^{15} \implies n_{total} = 15 \text{ address lines}$$

**Step 2 — Compute address lines per chip:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{8 KB SRAM} &= 2^{13} \implies 13 \text{ address lines each} \\
\text{16 KB Flash} &= 2^{14} \implies 14 \text{ address lines}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Build the address bus structure:**

```
Bits:    [A14] [A13] [A12] [A11] [A10] ... [A1] [A0]
         ├── Chip Select Decoding ──┤├── Internal to chip ──┤
```

For each chip, the **lower address lines** go to the chip; the **upper lines** form the chip-select logic.

**Step 4 — Derive Chip Select (CS) equations:**

| Chip | Capacity | Internal Lines (A0..A_n) | CS Lines | CS Active When | CS Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SRAM_A | 8 KB ($2^{13}$) | A0–A12 | A13, A14 | A14=0, A13=0 | $\overline{CS_A} = \overline{\overline{A14} \cdot \overline{A13}}$ |
| SRAM_B | 8 KB ($2^{13}$) | A0–A12 | A13, A14 | A14=0, A13=1 | $\overline{CS_B} = \overline{\overline{A14} \cdot A13}$ |
| Flash  | 16 KB ($2^{14}$) | A0–A13 | A14 | A14=1 | $\overline{CS_F} = \overline{A14}$ |

**Step 5 — Build the memory map:**

| Address Range (Hex) | Size | Active Chip |
|---|---|---|
| 0x0000 – 0x1FFF | 8 KB | SRAM_A |
| 0x2000 – 0x3FFF | 8 KB | SRAM_B |
| 0x4000 – 0x7FFF | 16 KB | Flash |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in Automotives:** The Flash is mapped to *high* addresses so that the reset vector (which an ARM Cortex-M3 fetches from address **0x0000 0000** on reset) comes from Flash — allowing **XIP (Execute-In-Place)** boot. SRAM is mapped *below* it as scratchpad RAM.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Memory Access Time Calculation

**Problem Setup:** A microcontroller operates at **100 MHz** ($T_{cycle} = 10 \text{ ns}$). The internal SRAM has an access time of $t_{AA} = 8 \text{ ns}$. Determine whether wait states are needed.

**Step 1 — State the timing relationship:**

For a memory access without wait states:
$$T_{cycle} \geq T_{access}$$
Otherwise, wait states must be inserted.

**Step 2 — Compare:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= 10 \text{ ns} \\
T_{access} &= t_{AA} + t_{setup} = 8 \text{ ns} + 1 \text{ ns} = 9 \text{ ns} \\
\text{Since } 10 \text{ ns} &> 9 \text{ ns} \implies \text{No wait state needed}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the number of wait states:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Wait States (WS)} &= \lceil \frac{T_{access}}{T_{cycle}} \rceil - 1 \\
&= \lceil \frac{9}{10} \rceil - 1 = 1 - 1 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive ECU Context:** Real automotive MCUs (e.g., Renesas RH850) often run the Flash at a **lower clock divider** than the CPU. The CPU might run at 200 MHz, but Flash access uses a 100 MHz divider, giving 2 CPU clock cycles per Flash access — equivalent to 0 wait states from the CPU's perspective.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cache Average Access Time

**Problem Setup:** An automotive MCU has an L1 cache with the following characteristics:
- Cache hit access time: $T_{cache} = 2 \text{ ns}$
- Main memory (Flash) access time: $T_{main} = 50 \text{ ns}$
- Cache hit ratio: $H = 0.92$

Compute the **average memory access time** $T_{avg}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the average access time formula:**

$$T_{avg} = H \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{main}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= (0.92)(2 \text{ ns}) + (1 - 0.92)(50 \text{ ns}) \\
&= (0.92)(2) + (0.08)(50) \text{ ns} \\
&= 1.84 + 4.0 \text{ ns} \\
&= 5.84 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compare against no-cache baseline:**

Without cache: $T_{avg} = T_{main} = 50 \text{ ns}$
With cache: $T_{avg} = 5.84 \text{ ns}$
Speedup factor: $\frac{50}{5.84} \approx 8.56 \times$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 92% hit rate *seems* high, but in a real-time ECU loop, even a **10% miss rate** causes 1 in 10 cycles to stall for 50 ns — this **jitter** can violate deterministic timing budgets. Automotive ASIL-D systems require **lockstep cores** and **deterministic memory access** — high-performance caches are often *avoided* or **disabled** in safety-critical paths.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Memory Capacity from Multiplexed Address Bus

**Problem Setup:** An automotive MCU uses a **multiplexed address/data bus** (like the 8051 external bus interface). The address bus is split into:
- **High-order address byte** latched externally using **ALE** (Address Latch Enable)
- **Low-order address byte** multiplexed with data

If the address lines $A_0 \dots A_{15}$ are used (16-bit address), find the memory capacity.

**Step 1 — Count address lines:**

$$n = 16 \text{ address lines}$$

**Step 2 — Apply $N = 2^n$:**

$$N = 2^{16} = 65536 \text{ locations} = 64 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 3 — Account for byte vs word organization:**

For byte-addressable memory: Total capacity = 64 KB
For 16-bit word-addressable memory: Total capacity = 64K × 2 bytes = 128 KB

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive MCU Example:** The **Infineon XC167CI** (used in many Bosch ECUs) uses a multiplexed bus with 24-bit external address (16 MB) and 16-bit data, allowing large external Flash for bootloader and calibration storage.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Memory Technology Selection for Automotive Subsystems

| Automotive Subsystem | Required Memory | Selected Technology | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engine ECU Firmware | Non-volatile, fast random read | **NOR Flash** | XIP support, deterministic read |
| Sensor Data Buffer | Volatile, fast write, small | **SRAM** | No refresh, deterministic timing |
| OBD-II DTC Storage | Non-volatile, byte-erasable | **EEPROM** (or Flash emulation) | Byte-level write, high retention |
| Immobilizer Secret Key | Non-volatile, write-once-read-many | **OTP ROM / Secure Flash** | Tamper-resistant, secure storage |
| Dashboard Display Buffer | Volatile, large, sequential | **DRAM (external)** or **SRAM** | High bandwidth, refresh accepted (display refresh anyway) |
| Telematics / Infotainment Storage | Non-volatile, large, sequential | **NAND Flash / eMMC** | High density, low cost per bit |
| ADAS Sensor Fusion Memory | Volatile, very fast, large | **LPDDR4 / SRAM** | Multi-GB/s throughput for camera/LiDAR |

---

### 3.7 Exhaustive Numerical Example — Automotive ECU Memory Map

**Problem:** An automotive MCU has the following internal memory resources:
- 1 MB Flash (for program code + bootloader)
- 128 KB SRAM (for data + stack + heap)
- 4 KB EEPROM (for OBD-II DTCs + VIN + immobilizer key)
- 32 KB Cache (L1, unified instruction/data)

Assuming **byte-addressable** memory with a 32-bit address bus ($2^{32} = 4 \text{ GB}$), construct the memory map.

**Step 1 — Convert each block to bytes:**
- Flash: $1 \text{ MB} = 2^{20} \text{ bytes}$
- SRAM: $128 \text{ KB} = 2^{17} \text{ bytes}$
- EEPROM: $4 \text{ KB} = 2^{12} \text{ bytes}$
- Cache: $32 \text{ KB} = 2^{15} \text{ bytes}$

**Step 2 — Compute address ranges (chosen by MCU vendor):**

| Region | Start Address | End Address | Size | Lines Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flash** | `0x0000 0000` | `0x000F FFFF` | 1 MB | A0–A19 (20 lines) |
| **SRAM** | `0x2000 0000` | `0x2001 FFFF` | 128 KB | A0–A16 (17 lines) |
| **EEPROM** | `0x4000 0000` | `0x4000 0FFF` | 4 KB | A0–A11 (12 lines) |
| **Cache (memory-mapped control)** | `0xE000 0000` | `0xE000 7FFF` | 32 KB | A0–A14 (15 lines) |

**Step 3 — Verify address line decoding:**

- Flash range $\rightarrow$ binary of $0x000F FFFF$ ends at bit 19, so A19=0 selects Flash (lower 1 MB region).
- SRAM range $\rightarrow$ upper unique bit is A16, so MCU decodes `A28..A17 = 0x2000_0` to assert SRAM CS.
- (Full 32-bit decode is done via combinational logic comparing upper address bits.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** When asked to "design a memory map," always:
> 1. Convert each block to bytes (powers of 2).
> 2. State the **total** address bus width.
> 3. Assign non-overlapping address ranges.
> 4. Identify which address bits form the **chip select** (decoded) vs. which go **directly to the chip** (offset within chip).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Internal Memory Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["CPU Core\nExecution Unit + ALU"]
    REG["Register File\n8 / 16 / 32 x 32-bit\nAccess: < 1 cycle"]
    L1["L1 Cache\nSRAM, 16-64 KB\n2-3 cycle latency"]
    L2["L2 Cache (if present)\n128 KB - 2 MB\n5-15 cycle latency"]
    SRAM["On-chip SRAM\n4 KB - 1 MB\nVolatile Data Memory"]
    FLASH["On-chip NOR Flash\n256 KB - 16 MB\nProgram Memory XIP"]
    EEPROM["On-chip EEPROM\nor EEPROM Emulation\nin Flash 2 KB - 64 KB"]
    DTC["OBD-II DTCs\nVIN, Immobilizer Keys"]

    CPU ==> REG
    CPU ==> L1
    L1 -.->|miss| L2
    L2 -.->|miss| SRAM
    L2 -.->|miss| FLASH
    CPU <--> SRAM
    CPU <--> FLASH
    SRAM <-->|read/write| DTC
    DTC <--> EEPROM
    FLASH -.->|emulates| EEPROM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows `==>` denote *direct, fast* access paths. Dashed arrows `-.->` denote *fallback* paths (cache miss → main memory). The SRAM↔EEPROM link shows runtime data movement for diagnostic logging.

---

### 4.2 SRAM Cell — 6T Structure with Read/Write Paths

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VDD1["V_DD"]
    VDD2["V_DD"]
    GND1["GND"]
    GND2["GND"]
    WL["Word Line (WL)"]
    BL["Bit Line (BL)"]
    BLB["Bit Line Bar (BL̄)"]

    P1["PMOS T1"]
    P2["PMOS T3"]
    N1["NMOS T2"]
    N2["NMOS T4"]
    N3["NMOS T5 (Access)"]
    N4["NMOS T6 (Access)"]

    VDD1 --- P1
    P1 --- N1
    N1 --- GND1

    VDD2 --- P2
    P2 --- N2
    N2 --- GND2

    N1 ---|"Stored node Q"| N3
    N2 ---|"Stored node Q̄"| N4

    WL --- N3
    WL --- N4

    BL --- N3
    BLB --- N4

    P1 ---|"cross-couple"| N2
    P2 ---|"cross-couple"| N1
```

---

### 4.3 Memory Interfacing Block Diagram (Automotive MCU)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MCU["Automotive MCU\n(e.g., Aurix TC27x)"]

    subgraph INT_MEM ["Internal Memory Block"]
        FLS["Flash Controller\n+ 2 MB Flash"]
        RAM["SRAM Controller\n+ 192 KB SRAM"]
        EE["EEPROM Emulation\nin Flash"]
    end

    subgraph PERIPH ["On-chip Peripherals"]
        ADC["ADC\n(12-bit, 1 MSPS)"]
        PWM["PWM\n(Engine Timing)"]
        CAN["CAN FD\n(Automotive Bus)"]
        TIM["Timer/Capture\n(Crankshaft Sense)"]
    end

    EXT["External Bus\nfor off-chip memory"]

    MCU -->|"XIP Fetch"| FLS
    MCU -->|"Data R/W"| RAM
    MCU -->|"Store DTCs"| EE
    MCU -->|"Sensor Read"| ADC
    MCU -->|"Drive Actuator"| PWM
    MCU -->|"Vehicle Network"| CAN
    MCU -->|"Engine Position"| TIM
    FLS --> EXT
    RAM --> EXT
```

---

### 4.4 Memory Map Visualization (Automotive MCU Address Space)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAP ["32-bit Address Space (4 GB)"]
        direction TB
        R0["0x0000 0000 - 0x000F FFFF\nFlash (1 MB)\n--- BOOT REGION ---"]
        R1["0x0001 0000 - 0x1FFF FFFF\nReserved"]
        R2["0x2000 0000 - 0x2001 FFFF\nSRAM (128 KB)\n--- DATA REGION ---"]
        R3["0x2002 0000 - 0x3FFF FFFF\nReserved"]
        R4["0x4000 0000 - 0x4000 0FFF\nEEPROM (4 KB)\n--- DTC REGION ---"]
        R5["0x4000 1000 - 0x5FFF FFFF\nPeripherals"]
        R6["0x6000 0000 - 0x7FFF FFFF\nExternal Memory"]
        R7["0x8000 0000 - 0xDFFF FFFF\nReserved"]
        R8["0xE000 0000 - 0xE00F FFFF\nSystem Control / Cache"]
        R9["0xE010 0000 - 0xFFFF FFFF\nVendor Specific"]
    end

    R0 --> R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6 --> R7 --> R8 --> R9
```

---

### 4.5 Flash Programming & EEPROM Emulation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START([ECU Boot / Power ON])
    INIT["Initialize Flash Controller\n& EEPROM Emulation Driver"]
    COPY["Copy Calibration Maps\nfrom Flash to SRAM"]
    RUN([Run Engine Control Loop])

    DTC_OCCUR{DTC Occurred?}
    WRITE_EE["Write DTC to\nEEPROM Emulation Sector\n(via Flash write)"]
    WEAR["Wear-Leveling Algorithm\n(distribute writes across sectors)"]
    CONFIRM["Confirm Write\n(verify & commit)"]

    NEXT([Continue Loop])

    START --> INIT --> COPY --> RUN
    RUN --> DTC_OCCUR
    DTC_OCCUR -->|Yes| WRITE_EE --> WEAR --> CONFIRM --> NEXT
    DTC_OCCUR -->|No| NEXT
    NEXT --> RUN
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Wear-Leveling?** A single Flash sector has ~100K erase cycles. If the same DTC sector were erased every time, it would fail in ~3 years (100K writes ÷ ~100 writes/day = 1000 days). Wear-leveling distributes writes across 10 sectors → extends life to ~30 years, satisfying the **15-year automotive service life** requirement.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Differentiate between SRAM and DRAM. State one reason why SRAM is preferred over DRAM for internal data memory in automotive microcontrollers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | SRAM | DRAM |
|---|---|---|
| Cell Structure | 6 transistors (6T) per bit | 1 transistor + 1 capacitor (1T1C) per bit |
| Density | Low (~6× larger area per bit) | High (~6× denser) |
| Refresh | **Not required** | **Required** every ~64 ms |
| Access Time | Fast (1–10 ns) | Slower (50–100 ns) |
| Power | Higher static leakage | Lower per-bit, but refresh overhead |
| Cost per bit | Higher | Lower |

**Why SRAM is preferred in automotive MCUs (1 mark):**
SRAM does **not require periodic refresh cycles**, providing **deterministic access time**. This is critical for real-time engine control loops where any jitter (e.g., a stalled refresh cycle) could cause incorrect fuel injection timing. ISO 26262 ASIL-rated automotive MCUs (e.g., Aurix TC27x, RH850) exclusively use SRAM for on-chip data memory.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**

**What is meant by Execute-In-Place (XIP)? Why is NOR Flash used as the program memory in automotive ECUs instead of NAND Flash?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**XIP Definition (1.5 marks):**
Execute-In-Place is the ability of a processor to **fetch and execute instructions directly from non-volatile memory** (NOR Flash) without first copying them into RAM. The CPU issues a normal instruction fetch on the address bus, and the Flash responds with instruction opcodes in real time.

**Why NOR Flash (not NAND) (1.5 marks):**
- NOR Flash supports **random byte-level address access** with short access times (~30–100 ns), enabling XIP.
- NAND Flash supports only **sequential page/block access** (~25 µs latency for first byte) — the CPU cannot fetch instructions directly.
- Automotive ECUs must boot and run immediately from Flash (no RAM copy allowed during safety-critical startup, e.g., airbag deployment). NOR Flash satisfies this; NAND does not.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Module 3 Question]**

**(a)** Explain the organization of a **6T SRAM cell** with a neat diagram. Describe the read and write operations. Why is SRAM used as cache memory in processors? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automotive microcontroller has **64 KB of internal Flash** and **8 KB of internal SRAM**, both byte-addressable. Design the complete memory map showing the address range, number of address lines, and chip-select logic for each memory block. Assume the remaining address space is unused. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A (a):**

**6T SRAM Cell — Construction (3 marks):**

The 6T SRAM cell consists of:
- **Two cross-coupled CMOS inverters** (T1-PMOS + T2-NMOS) and (T3-PMOS + T4-NMOS), forming a bi-stable latch.
- **Two NMOS access transistors** (T5 and T6), controlled by the Word Line (WL).
- Two storage nodes **Q** and **Q̄** (complementary).
- Two bit lines **BL** and **BL̄**.

```
      V_DD              V_DD
       │                 │
      [T1]              [T3]
       │                 │
   Q ──┤├──┬──────┬────┤├── Q̄
       │   │      │     │
      [T2][T5]  [T6]  [T4]
       │   │      │     │
      GND WL     WL    GND
           │      │
          BL     BL̄
```

**Read Operation (2 marks):**
1. Pre-charge both BL and BL̄ to V_DD/2.
2. Activate WL (HIGH) → T5 and T6 turn ON.
3. The stored value at Q (say '0') pulls BL to GND via T2-T5.
4. The stored value at Q̄ (say '1') keeps BL̄ near V_DD.
5. The **sense amplifier** detects the differential voltage (ΔV ≈ V_DD/2) and amplifies it to full logic levels.

**Write Operation (1.5 marks):**
1. Activate WL (HIGH).
2. Drive BL with new data value (V_DD for '1' or GND for '0').
3. Drive BL̄ with the *complement*.
4. The bit-line drivers **overpower** the cross-coupled latch, flipping the stored state.
5. Deactivate WL → latch holds new state indefinitely (as long as V_DD is present).

**Why SRAM for Cache (0.5 mark):**
SRAM's **no-refresh**, **symmetric read/write**, and **nanosecond access time** make it ideal for cache — where speed and deterministic behavior are paramount, and capacity (and cost per bit) are secondary.

---

#### **Solution to Question A (b):**

**Memory Map Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert capacities to powers of 2:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Flash} &= 64 \text{ KB} = 2^{16} \text{ bytes} \\
\text{SRAM} &= 8 \text{ KB} = 2^{13} \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Total} &= 72 \text{ KB} \approx 2^{17} = 128 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Address bus width:**
For 128 KB total, we need **17 address lines** ($A_0$ to $A_{16}$).

**Step 3 — Internal address lines per chip:**
- Flash (64 KB): $A_0$ to $A_{15}$ (16 lines)
- SRAM (8 KB): $A_0$ to $A_{12}$ (13 lines)
- Decoded lines (chip select): $A_{13}, A_{14}, A_{15}, A_{16}$ (4 lines)

**Step 4 — Choose address ranges:**

Assign Flash to **lower** addresses (for boot vector) and SRAM to **upper**:

| Memory | Start (Hex) | End (Hex) | Size | CS Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flash | 0x0000 | 0xFFFF | 64 KB | $A_{16} = 0$ |
| SRAM | 0x10000 | 0x11FFF | 8 KB | $A_{16}=1, A_{15}=0, A_{14}=0, A_{13}=0$ |
| Unused | 0x12000 | 0x1FFFF | — | — |

**Step 5 — Chip-Select Logic Equations:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{CS_{Flash}} &= \overline{\overline{A_{16}}} = A_{16} \\
\overline{CS_{SRAM}} &= \overline{A_{16} \cdot \overline{A_{15}} \cdot \overline{A_{14}} \cdot \overline{A_{13}}}
\end{aligned}$$

(Implemented using AND-OR-INVERT gates or a 3-to-8 decoder with $A_{16}, A_{15}, A_{14}, A_{13}$ as inputs.)

**Step 6 — Verification:**
- Flash addresses: $0x0000$ to $0xFFFF$ → $65536$ bytes = **64 KB** ✓
- SRAM addresses: $0x10000$ to $0x11FFF$ → $8192$ bytes = **8 KB** ✓

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Module 3 Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **internal memory hierarchy** of a microprocessor. Discuss how **cache memory** improves the average memory access time, with a numerical example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **EEPROM** and **Flash memory** in detail. Explain how modern automotive MCUs use **EEPROM emulation in Flash** for storing OBD-II diagnostic trouble codes (DTCs). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a):**

**Memory Hierarchy Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
┌─────────────────────────┐
│   CPU REGISTERS         │  ← 1-4 bytes, < 1 ns
├─────────────────────────┤
│   L1 CACHE (SRAM)       │  ← 16-64 KB, 1-3 ns
├─────────────────────────┤
│   L2 CACHE (SRAM)       │  ← 128 KB-2 MB, 5-15 ns
├─────────────────────────┤
│   MAIN MEMORY (SRAM)    │  ← 4 KB-1 MB, 10-30 ns
├─────────────────────────┤
│   SECONDARY STORAGE     │  ← Flash/ROM, 30-100 ns
│   (NOR Flash)           │
└─────────────────────────┘
```

(Each level is larger and slower than the one above.)

**Cache Memory Concept (2 marks):**

Cache memory is a small, fast SRAM placed between the CPU and the slower main memory. It exploits the **principle of locality**:
- **Temporal Locality:** If a memory location is accessed, it is likely to be accessed again soon.
- **Spatial Locality:** If a location is accessed, nearby locations are likely to be accessed next.

The cache stores recently used data and, on access, first checks if the requested address is present (**hit**). If yes → return data in ~2 ns. If no (**miss**) → fetch from main memory (slower) and store in cache.

**Numerical Example (2 marks):**

Given:
- $T_{cache} = 5 \text{ ns}$
- $T_{main} = 100 \text{ ns}$
- Hit ratio $H = 0.90$

**Without cache:**
$$T_{avg, \text{no cache}} = T_{main} = 100 \text{ ns}$$

**With cache:**
$$T_{avg, \text{cache}} = H \cdot T_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot T_{main}$$
$$T_{avg, \text{cache}} = (0.90)(5) + (0.10)(100) = 4.5 + 10 = 14.5 \text{ ns}$$

**Speedup:**
$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{100}{14.5} \approx 6.9 \times$$

> The cache provides a **6.9× speedup** in average access time, despite the slow main memory.

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b):**

**EEPROM vs Flash Memory Comparison (3 marks):**

| Feature | EEPROM | Flash (NOR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Erase Granularity** | **Byte-level** (single byte) | **Block-level** (sector, 64 KB–256 KB) |
| **Write Granularity** | Byte-level | Page or word-level |
| **Erase Mechanism** | Fowler-Nordheim tunneling | Fowler-Nordheim tunneling |
| **Write Mechanism** | Tunneling (slow, ~5 ms/byte) | Hot-electron injection (faster) |
| **Cell Size** | Larger (~2 transistors/bit) | Smaller (~1 transistor/bit) |
| **Endurance** | ~1,000,000 cycles/byte | ~100,000 cycles/block |
| **Density** | Lower | Higher |
| **Use Case** | Small data storage (DTCs, settings) | Large firmware storage |

**EEPROM Emulation in Flash for OBD-II DTC Storage (4 marks):**

Modern automotive MCUs (e.g., Infineon Aurix, NXP S32K) often **lack dedicated EEPROM hardware** to reduce silicon cost. Instead, they **emulate EEPROM within a reserved region of Flash memory** using software algorithms:

**Mechanism:**

1. **Sector Reservation:** A portion of Flash (e.g., 32 KB) is reserved for EEPROM emulation, divided into multiple **equal-sized sectors** (e.g., 4 sectors of 8 KB each).

2. **Wear-Leveling Algorithm:** Since each Flash sector can only be erased ~100,000 times, the algorithm **distributes writes across sectors** to extend total endurance:
   - Sector 1 → writes 0 to ~25,000 DTCs.
   - Sector 2 → writes next 25,000.
   - Sector 3 → writes next 25,000.
   - Sector 4 → writes next 25,000.
   - Total effective endurance: **~100,000 × 4 = 400,000 DTCs** — adequate for the vehicle's 15-year service life.

3. **Write Operation:** A new DTC is appended to the **active sector** as a small record (DTC code + status byte + timestamp). Old sectors become read-only.

4. **Garbage Collection:** When the active sector fills up, the algorithm:
   - Copies all *valid* records to a fresh sector.
   - Erases the old sector.
   - Designates the fresh sector as the new active.

5. **Read Operation:** A linear or indexed scan over all sectors reconstructs the full DTC list for OBD-II scan tools (e.g., reading via CAN/UDS protocol `Service 0x19 — ReadDTCInformation`).

**Advantages of EEPROM Emulation:**
- Saves silicon cost (no separate EEPROM IP block).
- Higher density (Flash cells are smaller than EEPROM cells).
- Easier fabrication process integration.

**Disadvantages:**
- More complex software (wear-leveling, garbage collection).
- Slower write latency (page-program + sector-erase).
- Higher RAM footprint for sector buffers.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Internal Memory Questions:**
>
> 1. **Address Line Calculation:** Students often forget to convert KB to bytes using $\times 1024$ (power of 2), or they confuse KB with KiB. Always state $N = 2^n$ explicitly and solve for $n$.
>
> 2. **SRAM vs DRAM Confusion:** Many students write "DRAM is faster than SRAM" — this is **wrong**. SRAM is faster (no refresh overhead). DRAM is *denser* and *cheaper per bit*, not faster.
>
> 3. **NOR vs NAND Flash:** Confusing these leads to losing 2–3 marks in 14-mark questions. Remember: **NOR = random access, XIP-capable, for program memory**; **NAND = sequential access, for mass storage**.
>
> 4. **EEPROM vs Flash Erase Granularity:** The single most common error is writing "Flash can erase a single byte like EEPROM." Flash requires **block/sector erase** — always state this.
>
> 5. **Cache Hit Ratio Units:** When asked for "improvement," always quote the **speedup factor** (a ratio) — never state the absolute time saved without context.
>
> 6. **Memory Map Design:** A common mistake is *overlapping* address ranges (e.g., assigning Flash to 0x0000–0xFFFF AND SRAM to 0x0000–0x1FFF). Always verify ranges are **mutually exclusive** and cover the requested capacities exactly.
>
> 7. **Forgetting Automotive Context:** For a course titled "Microprocessors and Controllers **in Automotives**," always reference the *automotive relevance* (OBD-II, ECU, ISO 26262, real-time constraints) — examiners allocate up to **2 bonus marks** for contextual relevance.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Internal Memory**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Internal memory = Registers + Cache + On-chip RAM + On-chip ROM/Flash, all within the MCU die.
>
> ✅ **Volatile vs Non-Volatile:** Volatile loses data on power-off (SRAM, DRAM); Non-volatile retains (ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash).
>
> ✅ **SRAM Cell:** 6T (six transistors) per bit — bi-stable latch — no refresh required — fast access (~1–10 ns).
>
> ✅ **DRAM Cell:** 1T1C (one transistor + one capacitor) per bit — requires periodic refresh (~64 ms) — higher density.
>
> ✅ **Automotive Preference:** **SRAM for data, NOR Flash for program, EEPROM (or emulated) for small non-volatile data.**
>
> ✅ **ROM Hierarchy:** Mask ROM → PROM → EPROM → EEPROM → Flash (increasing flexibility, decreasing cost per bit).
>
> ✅ **Flash Types:** **NOR** = random byte access, XIP-capable, used for program memory. **NAND** = sequential block access, used for mass storage.
>
> ✅ **Key Formula:** $N = 2^n$ (locations from address lines) and $M = N \times W$ (total memory in bits/bytes).
>
> ✅ **Cache Average Access:** $T_{avg} = H \cdot T_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot T_{main}$.
>
> ✅ **Wait States:** $WS = \lceil T_{access}/T_{cycle} \rceil - 1$. Zero wait states when CPU clock ≤ memory access time.
>
> ✅ **Automotive Memory Requirements:** AEC-Q100 Grade 1 (−40 °C to +125 °C), ≥10-year retention, ≥100K endurance cycles, deterministic access time, ISO 26262 functional safety compliance.
>
> ✅ **EEPROM Emulation:** Software algorithm over Flash with wear-leveling + garbage collection to extend endurance and meet 15-year vehicle service life.
>
> ✅ **Address Decoding:** Higher address bits → chip select logic; lower bits → chip-internal addressing.
>
> ✅ **Execute-In-Place (XIP):** CPU fetches and runs instructions directly from NOR Flash without copying to RAM — enables fast deterministic boot in safety-critical systems.
>
> ✅ **Pitfall to Avoid:** Never confuse "faster" (SRAM) with "denser" (DRAM); never state that Flash erases byte-by-byte like EEPROM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I/O Port Usage in 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** I/O Port Usage in 8051 ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **I/O Ports in 8051 — Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)** > The 8051 Microcontroller provides **four bidirectional 8-bit Input/Output (I/O) ports**, designated as **P0, P1, P2, and P3**. Each port occupies a dedicated Special Function Register (SFR) address in the internal RAM memory map and may be addressed as a complete byte or as individual bit-addressable locations. The ports serve as the physical gateway through which the CPU interfaces with external automotive peripherals such as sensors (MAP, MAF, O2), actuators (fuel injectors, relays, solenoids), and communication buses (CAN, LIN). **Standard SFR Addresses (KTU High-Yield):** | Port | SFR Address | Bit Addressable | Alternate Functions | |------|-------------|-----------------|---------------------| | **P0** | 80H | Yes (80H–87H) | AD0–AD7 (Multiplexed Address/Data bus) | | **P1** | 90H | Yes (90H–97H) | None (General Purpose I/O) | | **P2** | A0H | Yes (A0H–A7H) | A8–A15 (High Address Bus) | | **P3** | B0H | Yes (B0H–B7H) | RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Vehicle's Dashboard Switch Panel** > Think of the four 8051 ports as the **switch and indicator panel inside an automobile dashboard**. > - Each port is a **bank of 8 switches** (8 bits = 8 switches). > - The driver (the CPU) presses a switch (writes a '1' or '0') to turn ON the headlamp (actuator), and reads a switch position to know if the **door is open** (sensor input). > - **Port 0** is special — it is the *bare* contact with no pull-up resistor (like a switch with no spring), so in automotive ECU designs we must add an external pull-up resistor to make it work reliably. > - **Port 3** is the *multi-function* stalk on the steering column — the same switch can be a turn signal, wiper control, or high-beam, depending on whether we use it in normal or alternate-function mode. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > **Key 8051 Port Specifications:** > - **Number of Ports:** **4** (P0, P1, P2, P3) — 32 I/O lines total > - **Port Width:** **8 bits** each > - **Current Drive Capability (per pin):** **~10 mA sink/source** (Standard 8051) — used in KTU standard answer sheets > - **Pull-up Resistor (P1, P2, P3):** Internal ≈ **~30 kΩ to +5 V** (weak internal pull-ups) > - **Port 0 Output Drive:** **Open-Drain** — requires external **~10 kΩ pull-up resistors** to Vcc > - **Reset State of All Ports:** **FFH (All 1s)** > - **Operating Voltage:** **+5 V DC ± 10%** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Internal Structure of an 8051 Port Pin Every I/O port pin in the 8051 contains the following internal sub-blocks: 1. **D-Latch (SFR bit):** Stores the value written by the CPU. 2. **Output Driver (FET pair):** Push-pull (P1, P2, P3) or open-drain (P0). 3. **Internal Pull-up Resistor:** Present in P1, P2, P3 (≈ 30 kΩ); **absent in P0**. 4. **Input Buffer (Read Pin):** A tri-state buffer used to read the **physical voltage** on the pin. 5. **Read Latch (Read SFR):** Used during **Read-Modify-Write** instructions (e.g., `ANL P1, A`). ### 2.2 Why 'Read Pin' vs 'Read Latch' Matters (The "Read-Modify-Write Trap") > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Classic KTU Pitfall — Read-Modify-Write Instructions** > Instructions like `INC P1`, `ANL P1, A`, `ORL P1, A`, `CPL P1.x`, `SETB P1.x`, `CLR P1.x` read the **LATCH**, not the physical pin. This protects the port from corruption when an output pin is being driven by an external device (e.g., a switch in an automotive sensor cluster). > However, plain `MOV A, P1` reads the **physical pin voltage**, which is essential for genuine input operations. ### 2.3 Port 3 Alternate Functions (High-Yield KTU Topic) > [!NOTE] > When a bit of P3 is set to '**1**' in the latch, the corresponding alternate function becomes active. The alternate function automatically overrides the normal I/O. | P3 Bit | Alternate Function | Automotive Use Case | |--------|-------------------|---------------------| | **P3.0** | **RxD** (Serial Input) | OBD-II diagnostic data receive | | **P3.1** | **TxD** (Serial Output) | OBD-II diagnostic data transmit | | **P3.2** | **INT0̄** (External Interrupt 0) | Crank position sensor interrupt | | **P3.3** | **INT1̄** (External Interrupt 1) | Wheel speed sensor (ABS) interrupt | | **P3.4** | **T0** (Timer 0 Input) | Engine RPM measurement | | **P3.5** | **T1** (Timer 1 Input) | Fuel pulse timing | | **P3.6** | **WR̄** (External Write) | External memory write strobe | | **P3.7** | **RD̄** (External Read) | External memory read strobe | ### 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes | |---------|----------------|---------------| | Total I/O Lines | $N_{IO} = 4 \times 8 = 32$ | bits | | Port Output Voltage (High) | $V_{OH} \approx V_{CC} - 0.45 \text{ V}$ | **~4.5 V** typical | | Port Output Voltage (Low) | $V_{OL} \approx 0.45 \text{ V}$ | when sinking 1.6 mA | | Input High Voltage Threshold | $V_{IH(min)} = 2.0 \text{ V}$ | CMOS logic level | | Input Low Voltage Threshold | $V_{IL(max)} = 0.8 \text{ V}$ | CMOS logic level | | Port 0 External Pull-up | $R_{PU} \approx 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ | to $V_{CC}$ | | Time to Toggle a Bit | $T_{toggle} = 12 \times T_{osc} = 1 \text{ µs}$ | for 12 MHz crystal | | Address/Data De-multiplexing | $\text{ALE} \rightarrow \text{Latch (74HC573)}$ | for Port 0 expansion | | Read-Modify-Write Instruction | Reads **Latch** | Avoids pin-read corruption | | Pure MOV from Port | Reads **Physical Pin** | Used for input reading | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Why Port 0 Needs an External Pull-up Resistor Consider the electrical model of a Port 0 output transistor. The pull-up path in P0 is a **pass transistor (FET)** that is **active ONLY when the latch contains a '1'**. However, when used as a general I/O (not in external memory mode), the upper transistor is **disabled**. **Step 1: Latch holds '1' (Logic High output)** - The internal FET Q1 (upper) is **OFF** in non-address mode. - Output is therefore **floating** — no current path to Vcc. - Voltage at pin = **indeterminate** unless external pull-up is added. **Step 2: Latch holds '0' (Logic Low output)** - The lower FET Q2 is **ON**, pulling the pin to **GND (0 V)**. - This path is solid — no external resistor needed for LOW output. **Conclusion (KTU Mark-Worthy Statement):** > When Port 0 is used as a general-purpose output, an **external pull-up resistor (typically $R_{PU} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$)** to $V_{CC}$ is mandatory. Without it, the pin cannot drive a HIGH level reliably — a common interview/KTU question on 8051. ### 3.2 Derivation: Bit Manipulation for Automotive LED Control **Problem (Typical KTU Part A):** Write an 8051 assembly program to blink **P1.5** (an automotive dashboard LED indicator) with a delay of ~50 ms using 12 MHz crystal. **Step 1: Set P1.5 as output** We must write '1' to P1.5 initially so the internal pull-up activates and the pin can be driven LOW by the LED's anode through the microcontroller. $$P1.5 = 0 \quad \text{(LED ON — pin LOW, current sinks through MCU)}$$ **Step 2: Toggle P1.5 using CPL instruction** The `CPL` instruction complements the bit — but remember it reads the **latch**, so it is safe here. **Step 3: Generate 50 ms delay using a 12 MHz crystal** - Machine cycle = $12 \times T_{osc} = 12 \times \frac{1}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \text{ µs}$ - For a single register loop of 255 iterations: $255 \times 1 \text{ µs} = 255 \text{ µs}$ - For 50 ms: $N = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ µs}}{255 \text{ µs}} \approx 196$ inner loop counts **Complete Program:** ```assembly ORG 0000H MOV P1, #0FFH ; Initialize P1 as input-safe (all HIGH) LOOP: CPL P1.5 ; Toggle LED (Read-Modify-Write on LATCH) ACALL DELAY ; Call 50 ms delay SJMP LOOP ; Repeat forever DELAY: MOV R0, #196 ; Outer loop counter NEXT: MOV R1, #255 ; Inner loop counter DLY1: DJNZ R1, DLY1 ; 255 × 1 µs = 255 µs DJNZ R0, NEXT ; × 196 = ~50 ms RET END ``` **Analytical Matrix — Bit Operations Summary:** | Instruction | Operation | Reads | Bit-Addressable? | |-------------|-----------|-------|-----------------| | `MOV P1, #55H` | Byte write to port | — | No (byte only) | | `SETB P1.5` | Set bit to 1 | Latch | Yes | | `CLR P1.5` | Clear bit to 0 | Latch | Yes | | `CPL P1.5` | Complement bit | Latch | Yes | | `JB P1.5, LABEL` | Jump if bit = 1 | Pin | Yes | | `MOV C, P1.5` | Move bit to Carry | Pin | Yes | | `MOV A, P1` | Read entire port | **Pin** | No | | `ANL P1, A` | AND with accumulator | **Latch** | No | ### 3.3 Derivation: Reading a Switch with Debouncing (Automotive Door Sensor) **Problem:** Read a switch connected to P3.2 (which is also INT0̄) and toggle P1.0 on each press. **Step 1: Configure P3.2 as input** $$P3.2 = 1 \quad \text{(Write '1' to enable internal pull-up)}$$ **Step 2: Configure P1.0 as output** $$P1.0 = 0 \quad \text{(Initial state)}$$ **Step 3: Wait for switch press (active LOW)** The switch when pressed connects P3.2 to ground → pin reads '0'. **Step 4: Software Debounce Delay** A typical mechanical switch bounces for **~10–20 ms**. We must wait this long to avoid false triggers. **Program:** ```assembly MOV P3, #0FFH ; P3 as input MOV P1, #00H ; P1 as output SETB P1.0 ; Initial LED ON WAIT_HI:JB P3.2, WAIT_HI ; Wait for switch press (LOW) ACALL DEBOUNCE ; ~20 ms debounce JB P3.2, WAIT_HI ; Verify still pressed CPL P1.0 ; Toggle LED WAIT_LO:JNB P3.2, WAIT_LO ; Wait for release ACALL DEBOUNCE ; Debounce release SJMP WAIT_HI ; Repeat DEBOUNCE:MOV R0, #80 D1: MOV R1, #255 D2: DJNZ R1, D2 DJNZ R0, D1 RET ``` --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 8051 Port Architecture — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU[CPU Bus
Address/Data/Control] --> SFR[Special Function Register
SFR Latch Bit] SFR --> Q1[Q1 Upper FET
Pull-up Transistor] SFR --> Q2[Q2 Lower FET
Pull-down Transistor] Q1 --> PIN[PORT PIN] Q2 --> PIN PIN --> READBUF[Input Read Buffer] READBUF --> CPU PIN --> EXTPIN[External Pin Px.y] CPU -.Read Latch Path.-> SFR CPU -.Read Pin Path.-> READBUF EXTPIN -.External Pull-up 10k.-> VCC[Vcc +5V] EXTPIN -.Switch to GND.-> GND[Ground 0V] ``` ### 4.2 Port 0, P1, P2, P3 — Internal Configuration Comparison ```mermaid graph LR subgraph P0[Port 0 - Open Drain] P0LATCH[Latch 80H] --> P0Q1[Lower FET Only] P0Q1 --> P0PIN[Pin] P0EXT[External 10k Pull-up] --> P0PIN end subgraph P1[Port 1 - Push Pull] P1LATCH[Latch 90H] --> P1Q1[Upper FET ~30k] P1LATCH --> P1Q2[Lower FET] P1Q1 --> P1PIN[Pin] P1Q2 --> P1PIN end subgraph P2[Port 2 - Push Pull + A8-A15] P2LATCH[Latch A0H] --> P2Q1[Upper FET] P2LATCH --> P2Q2[Lower FET] P2Q1 --> P2PIN[Pin] P2Q2 --> P2PIN end subgraph P3[Port 3 - Push Pull + Alt Functions] P3LATCH[Latch B0H] --> P3Q1[Upper FET] P3LATCH --> P3Q2[Lower FET] P3Q1 --> P3PIN[Pin] P3Q2 --> P3PIN P3AF[Alt Function Mux
RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1
T0, T1, WR, RD] --> P3PIN end ``` ### 4.3 I/O Port Programming Flow in an Automotive ECU ```mermaid flowchart TD START([ECU Power-On Reset]) --> INIT[Initialize All Ports
MOV P0, FFH
MOV P1, 00H
MOV P2, FFH
MOV P3, FFH] INIT --> SENSORS[Read Sensor Inputs
P1 - Throttle Position
P3.2 - Crank Sensor
P3.3 - Wheel Speed] SENSORS --> PROCESS[Process in CPU
Compute Injection Timing
Compute Ignition Advance] PROCESS --> ACTUATE[Drive Actuator Outputs
P0 - LCD Display via Mux
P1.0 - Fuel Pump Relay
P1.5 - Warning LED
P2 - External Memory Bus] ACTUATE --> COMMS[Communication
P3.0 RxD - CAN Rx
P3.1 TxD - CAN Tx] COMMS --> DELAY[Wait 1ms Loop Time] DELAY --> SENSORS ``` ### 4.4 Read-Modify-Write Mechanism ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CPU as CPU participant LATCH as SFR Latch participant PIN as Physical Pin participant EXT as External Device Note over CPU,EXT: Read-Modify-Write Instruction (e.g., ANL P1, A) CPU->>LATCH: READ LATCH contents LATCH-->>CPU: Return latch value (NOT pin) CPU->>CPU: Perform logical operation CPU->>LATCH: WRITE result back to latch Note over CPU,EXT: Pure Read Instruction (e.g., MOV A, P1) EXT->>PIN: Drive pin voltage CPU->>PIN: READ physical pin voltage PIN-->>CPU: Return actual external level ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between the read-pin and read-latch operations of an 8051 I/O port. Why is this distinction important during Read-Modify-Write instructions?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Read-Pin | Read-Latch | |--------|----------|------------| | **What is read?** | Physical voltage on the pin | Value stored in the SFR latch | | **Used by** | `MOV A, Px` (input reading) | `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CPL`, `INC`, `SETB`, `CLR` on Px | | **Purpose** | Genuine input from external device | Avoid corruption during bit manipulation | **Importance (1 Mark):** When an output pin is being externally forced (e.g., by an automotive relay), reading the *pin* would mistakenly overwrite other bits during a bit instruction. Reading the *latch* ensures only the intended bit is modified — this is the KTU-mandated explanation. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Why does Port 0 require external pull-up resistors when used as a general-purpose I/O port? State the typical resistance value.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. Port 0 has an **open-drain output** structure. (1 Mark) 2. The internal pull-up transistor is **active only during external memory access** (to act as the AD0–AD7 bus). In normal I/O mode, it is **disabled**, leaving the pin floating when outputting a logic '1'. (1 Mark) 3. Therefore, an **external pull-up resistor** (typically **$R_{PU} \approx 10 \text{ k}\Omega$** to Vcc) is required to pull the line HIGH reliably. (1 Mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer ANY ONE full question from each pair. Both questions carry equal marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3] **(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of an 8051 I/O port pin**. Highlight the role of the SFR latch, output driver, and the input read buffer. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the **alternate functions of Port 3** of the 8051. Explain how the alternate function is activated and give **two automotive application examples** for these pins. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):** **Introduction (1 Mark):** The 8051 has 4 I/O ports (P0, P1, P2, P3), each of 8 bits, with each bit having a similar internal structure. **Internal Sub-blocks (4 Marks):** 1. **SFR Latch (D-type flip-flop):** Stores the data written by the CPU via instructions like `MOV P1, A`. Provides one bit of memory per port pin. 2. **Output Driver Stage:** Consists of two complementary FETs (Q1 upper, Q2 lower): - In **P1, P2, P3**: A push-pull configuration with an internal weak pull-up (~30 kΩ). - In **P0**: Only the lower FET exists (open-drain) — upper FET used only for external memory address bus. 3. **Input Read Buffer:** A tri-state buffer that connects the **physical pin voltage** to the internal data bus when a `MOV A, Px` instruction is executed. 4. **Read-Latch Path:** A separate path that reads the **SFR latch content** for Read-Modify-Write instructions. **Working (2 Marks):** - **Output Mode:** CPU writes to latch → output driver sources/sinks current at the pin. - **Input Mode:** Latch must first be written with '1' to disable the lower FET; the pin is then read via the read buffer. - **Bi-directional:** Achieved dynamically by writing '1' to the latch and then reading the pin. **Conclusion:** This dual-path design is the key feature that makes 8051 ports truly bidirectional and safe for automotive sensor interfacing. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):** **Alternate Functions of Port 3 (4 Marks):** | P3 Bit | Function | Description | |--------|----------|-------------| | P3.0 | RxD | Serial data input (UART) | | P3.1 | TxD | Serial data output (UART) | | P3.2 | INT0̄ | External interrupt 0 | | P3.3 | INT1̄ | External interrupt 1 | | P3.4 | T0 | Timer 0 external input | | P3.5 | T1 | Timer 1 external input | | P3.6 | WR̄ | External RAM write strobe | | P3.7 | RD̄ | External RAM read strobe | **Activation Logic (2 Marks):** The alternate function is activated when the corresponding P3 **latch bit is set to '1'**. The internal hardware automatically routes the peripheral signal to the pin. The user can still read the physical pin via the input buffer. **Automotive Applications (1 Mark each):** 1. **P3.2 (INT0̄):** Connected to the **crankshaft position sensor** in an EFI system to trigger fuel injection timing on every tooth of the reluctor wheel. 2. **P3.0/P3.1 (RxD/TxD):** Used for **OBD-II diagnostics** communication between the ECU and the diagnostic scan tool, or interfaced to a CAN/LIN transceiver for in-vehicle networking. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3] **(a)** Compare the **structures of Port 0 and Port 1** of the 8051. Why is Port 0 used as the multiplexed address/data bus in 8051 systems with external memory? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write an 8051 assembly language program to **read a switch connected to P3.2** and **toggle an LED on P1.0** on every valid press. Include **software debouncing of 20 ms**. Assume a 12 MHz crystal. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):** **Comparison Table (4 Marks):** | Feature | Port 0 | Port 1 | |---------|--------|--------| | Output Type | Open-Drain | Push-Pull | | Internal Pull-up | **None** (in I/O mode) | Yes (~30 kΩ) | | External Pull-up Required | Yes (~10 kΩ) | No | | Alternate Function | AD0–AD7 (Address/Data) | None | | Sink/Source Current | Higher (8 mA sink) | ~10 mA standard | | Reset Value | FFH | FFH | **Why Port 0 is Multiplexed Address/Data Bus (3 Marks):** 1. The 8051 has a **16-bit external address bus** (A0–A15) and an **8-bit data bus** (D0–D7) — but to **save package pins** (DIP-40 has only 40 pins), the designers multiplexed the lower address and data onto P0. 2. **Port 0 alone** serves as the 8-bit data bus AND the lower 8 bits of the address bus **time-multiplexed**. 3. **Port 2** provides the **upper 8 address bits (A8–A15)**. 4. An external **74HC573 latch** (or 74LS373) captures the lower address using the **ALE** strobe signal, then P0 carries the data — this is called **address/data de-multiplexing**. 5. The **open-drain structure** allows Port 0 to be tri-stated easily when external memory is not being accessed, preventing bus contention. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):** **Program (5 Marks):** ```assembly ORG 0000H MOV P3, #0FFH ; P3 as input (enable internal pull-ups) MOV P1, #00H ; P1 as output SETB P1.0 ; Initial LED ON MAIN: JB P3.2, MAIN ; Wait until switch pressed (LOW) ACALL DEBOUNCE ; 20 ms debounce JB P3.2, MAIN ; Verify still pressed (debounce) WAIT_REL:JNB P3.2, WAIT_REL ; Wait for switch release ACALL DEBOUNCE ; Debounce release CPL P1.0 ; Toggle LED SJMP MAIN ; Repeat DEBOUNCE:MOV R0, #40 LOOP1: MOV R1, #250 LOOP2: DJNZ R1, LOOP2 ; 250 × 1 µs = 250 µs DJNZ R0, LOOP1 ; × 40 = 10 ms × 2 ≈ 20 ms RET END ``` **Debounce Calculation (2 Marks):** - Machine cycle = $12 / 12 \text{ MHz} = 1 \text{ µs}$ - Inner loop: $250 \times 1 \text{ µs} = 250 \text{ µs}$ - Outer loop: $40 \times 250 \text{ µs} = 10 \text{ ms}$ per call - Two calls (press + release) give ~20 ms total debounce ✓ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting to write '1' to the latch before reading a port** → The output FET stays ON and fights the external signal. **(−1 to −2 marks)** > 2. **Confusing Read-Pin and Read-Latch** during Read-Modify-Write instructions — leads to wrong explanations for Part A. **(−2 marks)** > 3. **Not mentioning external pull-up for Port 0** — this is a favorite KTU question; missing the resistance value loses 1 mark. > 4. **Wrong delay calculation** — always state $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{osc} = 1 \text{ µs}$ for 12 MHz crystal explicitly. > 5. **Forgetting to set P3 latch bit to '1'** when using P3 alternate functions — alternate function will NOT activate. > 6. **Using `MOV A, P1` to read latch** — examiners specifically test the *Read-Modify-Write* concept; do not confuse. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — I/O Port Usage in 8051:** - **4 Ports:** P0 (80H), P1 (90H), P2 (A0H), P3 (B0H); all **bit-addressable**. - **Total I/O lines:** **32 bits** (4 × 8). - **Reset value:** All ports = **FFH**. - **Port 0** = **Open-Drain** → needs **external 10 kΩ pull-up** to Vcc for I/O mode. - **Port 1, 2, 3** = **Push-Pull** with **internal ~30 kΩ pull-ups**. - **Port 0 + Port 2** = External memory address/data bus (with ALE + 74HC573 latch). - **Port 3** = Alternate functions: **RxD, TxD, INT0̄, INT1̄, T0, T1, WR̄, RD̄**. - **To make a pin input:** Write **'1'** to its latch bit (disables lower FET). - **Read-Modify-Write instructions** (e.g., `ANL`, `CPL`, `SETB`, `CLR`, `INC`) → read the **LATCH**. - **Pure `MOV A, Px`** → reads the **physical pin** (true input). - **Port drive current:** **~10 mA per pin** typical. - **Voltage levels:** $V_{OH} \approx 4.5 \text{ V}$, $V_{OL} \approx 0.45 \text{ V}$, $V_{IH} \geq 2.0 \text{ V}$, $V_{IL} \leq 0.8 \text{ V}$. - **Automotive use cases:** Crankshaft sensor → INT0̄ (P3.2), OBD-II → RxD/TxD (P3.0/P3.1), Engine RPM → T0 (P3.4), Dashboard LEDs → P1.x, LCD via P0 multiplexing. - **Debounce time for mechanical switches in ECU:** Typically **10–20 ms** using software delay loops. - **One machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods** → for 12 MHz crystal = **1 µs** per machine cycle.

topics.title: Types of Special Function Registers and their uses in 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Types of Special Function Registers and their uses in 8051 # 📘 MODULE 3 — Special Function Registers (SFRs) of the 8051 Microcontroller ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** are dedicated on-chip memory-mapped registers inside the **8051 microcontroller** that occupy the upper **128 bytes** of the **internal RAM** space, i.e., the address range **0x80 to 0xFF**. Each SFR is **bit-addressable** (where applicable) and is used to **control peripherals, configure I/O ports, manage timers/counters, handle serial communication, and service interrupts** — they form the *control plane* between the CPU and the embedded hardware.
