In the 8051 architecture, the internal data memory (256 bytes) is divided into two parts: | Address Range | Region | Purpose | |---------------|--------|---------| | **0x00 – 0x1F** | Register Banks (R0–R7 × 4) | General-purpose working registers | | **0x20 – 0x2F** | Bit-addressable RAM | 16 bytes = 128 individually addressable bits | | **0x30 – 0x7F** | General-purpose RAM | Scratch pad for user data | | **0x80 – 0xFF** | **SFR Area** | Special Function Registers (control & status) | Only **21 of the 128 possible SFR addresses** are physically implemented in the standard 8051; the rest are "reserved" and must not be accessed by user programs. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Cockpit Dashboard" of the 8051 > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine the 8051 microcontroller as a small car engine. The **CPU** is the driver, the **ALU** is the engine block, and the **SFRs are the dashboard instruments and switches** — the speedometer (timer), the fuel gauge (port pins), the ignition key (PCON), the gear shifter (PSW), and the indicator lights (interrupt flags). The driver cannot "see" the engine's internal state directly; he can only interact with the engine *through* these dashboard instruments. Similarly, the programmer cannot directly command the 8051 hardware — he must write into and read from the appropriate SFRs to make things happen. ### 1.3 Why SFRs Matter in Automotive Applications The 8051 (and its derivatives like the **MCS-51 family**, used in ECUs, body controllers, instrument clusters, and dashboard electronics in older vehicles) relies entirely on SFRs to: - Read **sensor values** (via ports P1, P3). - Generate **PWM signals** for motor control (using Timer SFRs). - Communicate with other ECUs over **CAN/LIN buses** (using SBUF, SCON). - Handle **interrupts** from ABS sensors, airbags, or crank-shaft pickups (using IE, IP, TCON). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standard Metric:** The SFR area is exactly **128 bytes (0x80–0xFF)**. In the standard 8051, addresses **0x80–0xFF** are mapped to SFRs, while in the **8052** the upper 128 bytes are *also* accessible as extra data RAM via indirect addressing. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of SFRs The 21 implemented SFRs of the standard 8051 are logically classified into **five functional groups**: | Group | SFRs | Primary Function | |-------|------|------------------| | **1. CPU Core Registers** | **A (ACC), B, PSW, SP, DPTR (DPH + DPL)** | Arithmetic, addressing, status | | **2. I/O Port Latches** | **P0, P1, P2, P3** | Latch data for the four 8-bit ports | | **3. Timer/Counter Registers** | **TMOD, TCON, TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1** | Two 16-bit timers/counters | | **4. Serial Port Registers** | **SCON, SBUF, PCON** | UART communication + power modes | | **5. Interrupt Control Registers** | **IE, IP** | Enable & prioritize the 5 interrupt sources | > [!NOTE] > **Bit-addressable SFRs** (those whose individual bits can be set/cleared using instructions like `SETB bit` and `CLR bit`) are: **A, B, PSW, P0, P1, P2, P3, IE, IP, SCON, TCON** — their addresses end in `0x0` or `0x8` (i.e., multiples of 8). ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | SFR | Address | Bit-Addr? | Reset Value | Primary Use | |-----|---------|-----------|-------------|-------------| | **A (ACC)** | 0xE0 | ✅ Yes | 0x00 | Accumulator for all ALU ops | | **B** | 0xF0 | ✅ Yes | 0x00 | Used in MUL & DIV | | **PSW** | 0xD0 | ✅ Yes | 0x00 | Flags: CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, P | | **SP** | 0x81 | ❌ No | 0x07 | Points to top of stack | | **DPH / DPL** | 0x83 / 0x82 | ❌ No | 0x00 | 16-bit data pointer (DPTR) | | **P0** | 0x80 | ✅ Yes | 0xFF | Port 0 latch | | **P1** | 0x90 | ✅ Yes | 0xFF | Port 1 latch | | **P2** | 0xA0 | ✅ Yes | 0xFF | Port 2 latch | | **P3** | 0xB0 | ✅ Yes | 0xFF | Port 3 latch (also alt. functions) | | **TMOD** | 0x89 | ❌ No | 0x00 | Timer mode select | | **TCON** | 0x88 | ✅ Yes | 0x00 | Timer control & flags | | **TH0, TL0** | 0x8C, 0x8A | ❌ No | 0x00 | Timer 0 high/low byte | | **TH1, TL1** | 0x8D, 0x8B | ❌ No | 0x00 | Timer 1 high/low byte | | **SCON** | 0x98 | ✅ Yes | 0x00 | Serial control | | **SBUF** | 0x99 | ❌ No | 0x00 | Serial data buffer (TX/RX) | | **PCON** | 0x87 | ❌ No | 0x00 | Power control (SMOD bit) | | **IE** | 0xA8 | ✅ Yes | 0x00 | Interrupt enable | | **IP** | 0xB8 | ✅ Yes | 0x00 | Interrupt priority | ### 2.3 The PSW (Program Status Word) — Bit-by-Bit Deep Dive > [!IMPORTANT] > **The PSW is the most exam-relevant SFR.** It is the *flag register* and *register-bank selector*. KTU regularly asks its bit layout. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PSW} &= \underbrace{CY}_{PSW.7}\;\underbrace{AC}_{PSW.6}\;\underbrace{F0}_{PSW.5}\;\underbrace{RS1}_{PSW.4}\;\underbrace{RS0}_{PSW.3}\;\underbrace{OV}_{PSW.2}\;\underbrace{--}_{PSW.1}\;\underbrace{P}_{PSW.0} \end{aligned} $$ | Bit | Symbol | Function | |-----|--------|----------| | **PSW.7** | **CY** | **Carry Flag** — set if an arithmetic op produces a carry/borrow out of bit 7 | | **PSW.6** | **AC** | **Auxiliary Carry** — carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (used in BCD arithmetic) | | **PSW.5** | **F0** | **User-defined Flag 0** — general purpose | | **PSW.4–3** | **RS1, RS0** | **Register Bank Select** (00=Bank 0, 01=Bank 1, 10=Bank 2, 11=Bank 3) | | **PSW.2** | **OV** | **Overflow Flag** — set on signed arithmetic overflow | | **PSW.0** | **P** | **Parity Flag** — automatically set to 1 if the ACC has an odd number of 1's | ### 2.4 Timer SFR Architecture The 8051 has **two 16-bit timers** (Timer 0 and Timer 1). Each timer is split into a **high byte (THx)** and a **low byte (TLx)** register. > [!NOTE] > **TMOD** is *not* bit-addressable. It is configured as one byte by writing the mode into the upper or lower nibble. > - Upper nibble (T1): **GATE, C/T̄, M1, M0** > - Lower nibble (T0): **GATE, C/T̄, M1, M0** The four timer **modes** are governed by M1/M0: | M1 | M0 | Mode | Description | |----|----|------|-------------| | 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 13-bit timer (8048 compatibility) | | 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 16-bit timer (most common) | | 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 8-bit auto-reload | | 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | Split timer (Timer 0 only) | The **TCON** register has the **TFx** (timer overflow flag) and **TRx** (timer run control) bits, plus the **edge flags** for external interrupts (IE0, IT0, IE1, IT1). # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Register Bank Selection — Worked Mechanism When the 8051 resets, **RS1 = RS0 = 0**, so the active register bank is **Bank 0** (R0–R7 at 0x00–0x07). To switch to **Bank 2**, the programmer must write to PSW bits 4 and 3. **Step-by-step derivation of the bank-switch instruction:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Desired bank} &= \text{Bank 2} \quad (\text{R0–R7 at } 0\text{x10–0x17}) \\ \text{Encoding} &\Rightarrow \text{RS1} = 1,\ \text{RS0} = 0 \\ \text{Instruction sequence} &\Rightarrow \text{SETB PSW.4} \quad ;\ \text{set RS1} \\ &\quad \text{CLR PSW.3} \quad ;\ \text{clear RS0} \\ \text{Verification} &\Rightarrow \text{Now } R0 \text{ is at address } 0\text{x10} \text{ instead of } 0\text{x00} \end{aligned} $$ **Intuition:** Think of the register bank selector as a *floor selector* in an elevator. There are four floors (banks) of 8 offices (R0–R7) each. The combination of RS1 and RS0 picks the floor. ## 3.2 Derivation of Timer Delay (Mode 1) — 16-bit Timer This is the **most frequently asked derivation in KTU university papers**. Given a crystal frequency, derive the timer reload value for a specific delay. **Given:** - Crystal frequency $f_{osc} = 12\,\text{MHz}$ - Machine cycle period $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ - Required delay $= 50\,\text{ms}$ **Step 1 — Compute the machine cycle frequency:** $$ \begin{aligned} f_{MC} &= \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{12 \times 10^{6}}{12} = 1 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz} = 1\,\text{MHz} \\ T_{MC} &= \frac{1}{f_{MC}} = 1\,\mu\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard 8051 rule:** 1 machine cycle = **12 oscillator periods**. KTU examiners *love* this constant. **Step 2 — Compute the number of timer counts required:** $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \frac{\text{Desired Delay}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}}{1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}} \\ &= 50{,}000 \text{ counts} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Determine the 16-bit reload value:** In Mode 1, the timer counts UP from the reload value to **65536 (0x10000)**, then overflows. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Timer Value} &= 2^{16} - N = 65536 - 50000 = 15536 \\ \text{Hex form} &\Rightarrow 15536_{10} = 0\text{x3CB0} \\ \text{Therefore}\quad TH0 &= 0\text{x3C},\quad TL0 = 0\text{xB0} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Program snippet (Assembly):** ```assembly MOV TMOD, #01H ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit) MOV TH0, #3CH ; Load high byte MOV TL0, #0B0H ; Load low byte SETB TR0 ; Start Timer 0 HERE: JNB TF0, HERE ; Wait until overflow flag is set CLR TR0 ; Stop timer CLR TF0 ; Clear overflow flag ``` ## 3.3 Serial Port Baud Rate Derivation For **Mode 1** UART (8-bit UART, variable baud rate using Timer 1 overflow), the baud rate equation is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Baud Rate} &= \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})} \end{aligned} $$ where **SMOD** is bit 7 of PCON (doubler). Solving for **TH1** when SMOD = 0 and we want 9600 baud with an 11.0592 MHz crystal: $$ \begin{aligned} 9600 &= \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})} \\ 256 - \text{TH1} &= \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 12 \times 9600} = 3 \\ \text{TH1} &= 253 = 0\text{xFD} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **The 11.0592 MHz crystal is the standard automotive-grade crystal for 8051 systems** because it is an exact multiple of standard baud rates (9600, 19200, 57600), giving 0% error. ## 3.4 Interrupt Enable (IE) Register Bit Layout $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IE} &= EA\;\bar{}\;-\;\text{ET2}\;\text{ES}\;\text{ET1}\;\text{EX1}\;\text{ET0}\;\text{EX0} \\ \text{Address} &= 0\text{xA8} \end{aligned} $$ | Bit | Symbol | Enables | |-----|--------|---------| | **IE.7** | **EA** | Global enable (must be 1 to allow any interrupt) | | IE.6 | – | Reserved | | IE.5 | **ET2** | Timer 2 overflow (8052 only) | | IE.4 | **ES** | Serial port interrupt | | IE.3 | **ET1** | Timer 1 overflow | | IE.2 | **EX1** | External interrupt 1 (P3.3) | | IE.1 | **ET0** | Timer 0 overflow | | IE.0 | **EX0** | External interrupt 0 (P3.2) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 8051 Internal Data Memory Map ```mermaid flowchart TB A["8051 Internal Data Memory
256 Bytes (0x00 to 0xFF)"] A --> B["Lower 128 Bytes
(0x00 to 0x7F)"] A --> C["Upper 128 Bytes
(0x80 to 0xFF)"] B --> B1["Register Banks
0x00-0x1F
(Bank 0-3, R0-R7)"] B --> B2["Bit-Addressable RAM
0x20-0x2F
(128 bits)"] B --> B3["General Purpose RAM

(No physical register)"] C1 --> S1["CPU Core

TMOD, TCON, TH0-1, TL0-1"] C1 --> S4["Serial SFRs
SCON, SBUF, PCON"] C1 --> S5["Interrupt SFRs
IE, IP"] style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff style C fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff style C1 fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff style C2 fill:#616161,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 SFR Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR CPU["8051 CPU Core

f_osc = 12 MHz"] DIV12["Divide by 12
f_MC = 1 MHz"] PRES["Prescaler and Mode Logic
(TMOD bits)"] TL0["TL0
