Used in automotive instrument clusters displaying odometer/speedometer in BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal) form for direct 7-segment driving. ### 2.2 Classification of Logic Instructions - **`ANL A, `** / **`ANL , A`** / **`ANL , #data`** — Bitwise AND (used for **masking** specific bits). - **`ORL A, `** / **`ORL , A`** / **`ORL , #data`** — Bitwise OR (used for **setting** specific bits). - **`XRL A, `** / **`XRL , A`** / **`XRL , #data`** — Bitwise XOR (used for **toggling** and checksum operations). - **`CLR A`** — Clear Accumulator (sets A = 00H, does NOT touch PSW). - **`CPL A`** — Complement Accumulator (1's complement). - **Rotation & Swap**: - `RL A` — Rotate left through zero-fill. - `RLC A` — Rotate left **through Carry** (CY ↔ bit 0 link; 9-bit rotation). - `RR A` — Rotate right through zero-fill. - `RRC A` — Rotate right through Carry. - `SWAP A` — Exchanges high/low nibbles (used in BCD-to-7-seg conversion). ### 2.3 PSW (Program Status Word) — Flag Map The PSW register at **SFR address D0H** is the heartbeat of conditional branching in automotive control loops. | Bit | Symbol | Address | Function | Set/Cleared by | |-----|--------|---------|----------|----------------| | PSW.7 | **CY** | D7H | Carry / Borrow flag | ADD, ADDC, SUBB, MUL, DIV, RLC, RRC, DA | | PSW.6 | **AC** | D6H | Auxiliary carry (bit-3 → bit-4) | ADD, ADDC, SUBB, DA | | PSW.5 | **F0** | D5H | User-defined general-purpose flag | Software | | PSW.4 | **RS1** | D4H | Register bank select bit 1 | Software | | PSW.3 | **RS0** | D3H | Register bank select bit 0 | Software | | PSW.2 | **OV** | D2H | Signed overflow (2's complement) | ADD, ADDC, SUBB, MUL, DIV | | PSW.1 | — | D1H | Reserved (undefined) | — | | PSW.0 | **P** | D0H | Even parity (1 if odd # of 1's in A) | Hardware, every machine cycle | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formulas for KTU PEMUT746 Module 4 (Examination-Ready)** | # | Formula / Rule | Mathematical Form | Application in Automotive MCU | |---|----------------|-------------------|------------------------------| | 1 | Carry generation on ADD | $CY = 1 \iff (A_{7} \land src_{7}) \lor (A_{7} \land \overline{R_{7}}) \lor (src_{7} \land \overline{R_{7}})$ | Overflow detection in 16-bit RPM counter | | 2 | Signed overflow (ADD/SUB) | $OV = C_{7} \oplus C_{6}$ (carry into bit 7 XOR carry out of bit 7) | Sensor saturation check in ADC pipeline | | 3 | Auxiliary carry | $AC = 1 \iff$ carry from bit 3 → bit 4 | Used by `DA A` for BCD correction | | 4 | Parity flag | $P = 1 \iff$ # of 1-bits in A is ODD | Serial-comm protocol parity in LIN bus | | 5 | MUL result width | $A \times B \le 255 \iff OV = 0$, else $OV = 1$ | Battery SoC % = (V_now × I_now) ÷ V_max | | 6 | DIV zero trap | If $B = 0$ before DIV → $OV = 1$, A,B undefined | Must guard before motor current division | | 7 | DA A correction | If AC=1 or A[3:0]>9, add 06H to A; if CY=1 or A[7:4]>9, add 60H | BCD odometer increment | | 8 | Subtract via SUBB | $A \leftarrow A - src - \overline{CY_{in}}$ (logical); $CY_{out} = 1$ on borrow | Fuel-quantity subtraction from tank | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In a real **Engine Control Unit (ECU)**, the sequence ``` MOV A, R0 ; Load current crank-angle ADD A, R1 ; Add throttle correction MOV P1, A ; Drive ignition coil ``` executes in **2 µs** at 12 MHz and adjusts spark timing. Similarly, in a **CAN-bus** node, an incoming frame's identifier is masked with `ANL` to extract the message-type bits — a logic operation the 8051 performs in **1 µs**. These are the kinds of low-level operations that directly govern drivability, emissions, and passenger safety. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Multi-Byte Addition (16-bit Odometer Counter) **Problem (KTU-typical):** Add the 16-bit number in `R1:R0` (high:low) to the 16-bit number in `R3:R2` (high:low) and store the sum in `R5:R4`. #### Step-by-Step Assembly Program ```asm MOV A, R0 ; A = low byte of first number ADD A, R2 ; A = low byte sum MOV R4, A ; Store low result byte MOV A, R1 ; A = high byte of first number ADDC A, R3 ; A = high byte + low-byte carry MOV R5, A ; Store high result byte ; If CY = 1, overflow occurred (17th bit) ``` #### Numerical Trace Let `R1:R0 = 3F2AH` and `R3:R2 = 1B85H`. $$3F2AH + 1B85H = 5AAF H$$ $$ \begin{aligned} R0 + R2 &: \quad 2AH + 85H \\ &= 0010\,1010 + 1000\,0101 \\ &= 1010\,1111 = AFH \\ &\Rightarrow CY = 0,\ AC = 1 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} R1 + R3 + CY &: \quad 3FH + 1BH + 0 \\ &= 0011\,1111 + 0001\,1011 \\ &= 0101\,1010 = 5AH \\ &\Rightarrow CY = 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** `R5:R4 = 5AAFH` — Correct 16-bit sum. The **CY** flag now indicates a 17-bit overflow for chained 32-bit arithmetic. ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — BCD Arithmetic with `DA A` **Problem:** Add two BCD numbers stored in BCD format: `A = 29H`, `B = 47H` (these are valid BCD representations of 29 and 47). #### Trace $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ADD A, B: } & 0010\,1001 + 0100\,0111 \\ &= 0111\,0000 = 70H \end{aligned} $$ The low nibble `0 > 9`? No. `AC = 0`. The high nibble `7 > 9`? No. `CY = 0`. Result is already valid BCD: **70H = decimal 70**. No correction needed. **Now add `A = 58H, B = 27H`:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ADD A, B: } & 0101\,1000 + 0010\,0111 \\ &= 0111\,1111 = 7FH \end{aligned} $$ Low nibble = `F` (> 9), so `DA A` adds **06H**: $$ 7FH + 06H = 85H $$ High nibble of `85H` is `8` (< 9) so no 60H correction. **Correct BCD result = 85H** ✓ ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Bitwise Masking for Engine Status Word A status word `A = 1101\,0110` = `D6H` is read from a sensor port. Extract **only the upper 4 bits** (engine type code) and **clear all lower bits**. ```asm ANL A, #0F0H ; Mask lower nibble ``` $$ \begin{aligned} 1101\,0110 \\ \text{AND } 1111\,0000 \\ \hline 1101\,0000 = D0H \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** `D0H` represents the engine-type code `D` while the lower 4 bits are zeroed — a textbook KTU masking problem. ### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Multiplication & Overflow Compute `A = F0H` (240), `B = 02H` (2). ```asm MUL AB ``` $$ 240 \times 2 = 480 = 0001\,1110\,0000_{2} $$ Result stored: `B = 01H` (high), `A = E0H` (low). Since product > 255, **OV = 1**, **CY = 0**. **KTU Pitfall (Valuator's Note):** Many students incorrectly state that **MUL sets CY**. The correct behaviour is: **MUL always CLEARS CY** and sets OV *iff* the product exceeds 8 bits. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Flag Behaviour Summary Table > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this matrix — it is a guaranteed KTU Part-A question.** | Instruction | CY | AC | OV | P | |-------------|----|----|----|---| | ADD | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | ADDC | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | SUBB | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | MUL | **0** | — | ✓ | ✓ | | DIV | **0** | — | ✓ | ✓ | | DA A | ✓ | ✓ | — | ✓ | | INC | — | — | — | ✓ | | DEC | — | — | — | — | | ANL | — | — | — | ✓ | | ORL | — | — | — | ✓ | | XRL | — | — | — | ✓ | | CLR A | — | — | — | ✓ | | CPL A | — | — | — | ✓ | | RL / RR | — | — | — | ✓ | | RLC / RRC | ✓ | — | — | ✓ | | SWAP A | — | — | — | ✓ | *(✓ = instruction may modify the flag; — = instruction leaves flag unchanged.)* ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — 8051 ALU ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Accumulator A
8 bit SFR E0H] --> ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit
8051 Core] B[Temporary Register B
8 bit SFR F0H] --> ALU PSW[Program Status Word
PSW D0H] --> ALU ALU --> A ALU --> PSW RBank[Register Banks
R0 to R7 x 4 banks] --> ALU SFReg[Internal RAM
00H to 7FH] --> ALU Imm[Immediate Operand
from Opcode] --> ALU ALU --> Bus[Internal 8 bit Data Bus] Bus --> SFReg Bus --> Ext[External Memory
via MOVX] style ALU fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0a2540 style A fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style B fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style PSW fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 PSW (Program Status Word) Bit-Map Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PSW[D0H Program Status Word] direction LR B7[Bit 7 CY Carry] --> B6[Bit 6 AC Aux Carry] B6 --> B5[Bit 5 F0 User Flag] B5 --> B4[Bit 4 RS1 Bank Sel 1] B4 --> B3[Bit 3 RS0 Bank Sel 0] B3 --> B2[Bit 2 OV Overflow] B2 --> B1[Bit 1 Reserved] B1 --> B0[Bit 0 P Parity] end style B7 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff style B6 fill:#ffa94d,color:#ffffff style B2 fill:#ffd43b,color:#000000 style B0 fill:#74c0fc,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.3 Program Flow — 16-bit Addition with Carry Propagation ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start Program]) --> LoadR0[MOV A R0
Load low byte] LoadR0 --> AddR2[ADD A R2
Add low bytes] AddR2 --> SaveLow[MOV R4 A
Store low result] SaveLow --> LoadR1[MOV A R1
Load high byte] LoadR1 --> AddcR3[ADDC A R3
Add high with carry] AddcR3 --> SaveHigh[MOV R5 A
Store high result] SaveHigh --> CheckCY{CY = 1
Overflow?} CheckCY -- Yes --> HandleOV[Handle 17 bit overflow] CheckCY -- No --> End([End Program]) HandleOV --> End style Start fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff style End fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style AddcR3 fill:#fff3bf,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.4 Addressing-Mode Decision Tree for Arithmetic Source ```mermaid flowchart TD Q[Source Operand?] --> M1{Register
R0 to R7?} Q --> M2{Direct
Memory
00 to FFH?} Q --> M3{Indirect
at Ri?} Q --> M4{Immediate
constant?} M1 -- Yes --> I1[ADD A Rn
1 byte opcode] M2 -- Yes --> I2[ADD A direct
2 byte opcode] M3 -- Yes --> I3[ADD A at Ri
1 byte opcode] M4 -- Yes --> I4[ADD A data
2 byte opcode] style Q fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff style I1 fill:#d3f9d8 style I2 fill:#d3f9d8 style I3 fill:#d3f9d8 style I4 fill:#d3f9d8 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Explain the function of the `DA A` instruction in the 8051 microcontroller. When is it used, and which flags does it modify?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) The `DA A` (Decimal Adjust Accumulator) instruction converts the binary result of an `ADD` or `ADDC` operation on two valid BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal) operands into a valid BCD result. - (1 mark) It is used **only after ADD/ADDC**, never after SUBB or any other arithmetic operation. Typical use: BCD arithmetic for odometer, clock, and dashboard 7-segment displays. - (1 mark) It conditionally adds `06H` to the lower nibble (if AC = 1 or low nibble > 9) and `60H` to the upper nibble (if CY = 1 or upper nibble > 9). Flags affected: **CY, AC, P**. (OV and F0 are unaffected.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Differentiate between `RLC A` and `RL A` instructions with respect to the 9-bit rotation and flag behaviour.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) `RL A` performs an **8-bit rotation** — bit 7 moves to bit 0; CY flag is **not affected**. - (1 mark) `RLC A` performs a **9-bit rotation** — bit 7 moves into the **CY flag**, and the **previous CY** shifts into bit 0. - (1 mark) This 9-bit rotation is essential for **serializing/parallelizing bit-streams** (e.g., CAN frame bit-stuffing, SPI data shifting) and for implementing **multi-byte shift registers** with carry propagation. ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Question]* **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram of the PSW register, explain the function of **each flag bit** in the 8051. After execution of the following program segment, determine the contents of the Accumulator and the CY, AC, OV, and P flags. Initial: `A = A9H`, `R2 = 55H`, `R3 = 17H`. ```asm ADD A, R2 ADDC A, R3 CPL A ANL A, #0F0H RLC A ``` **(b) [7 marks]** Write an 8051 assembly program to add **two 32-bit numbers** stored in internal RAM starting at locations `30H` (lowest byte of N1) and `40H` (lowest byte of N2). Store the 32-bit result starting at `50H`. Explain the role of the **CY flag** in multi-byte addition. --- #### Model Solution for Q-A (a) **PSW Diagram — 1 mark** | CY | AC | F0 | RS1 | RS0 | OV | — | P | |----|----|----|-----|-----|----|----|---| | D7 | D6 | D5 | D4 | D3 | D2 | D1 | D0 | **Flag functions — 1 mark (see Section 2.3 above for full descriptions).** **Trace of program segment — 5 marks:** **Step 1: `ADD A, R2`** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= A9H = 1010\,1001 \\ R2 &= 55H = 0101\,0101 \\ \hline \text{Sum} &= 1111\,1110 = FEH \end{aligned} $$ - Bit-7 carry: `1+0` and prior `0` → no carry out. **CY = 0**. - AC: carry from bit 3 → bit 4? Bit 3 = 1, Bit 3 of R2 = 0, sum bit 3 = 1+0 = 1 (no carry). **AC = 0**. - OV: $C_7 \oplus C_6 = 0 \oplus 0 = 0$ → **OV = 0**. - P: number of 1's in `FEH` = 7 (odd) → **P = 1**. - **A = FEH**, CY=0, AC=0, OV=0, P=1. **Step 2: `ADDC A, R3`** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= FEH = 1111\,1110 \\ R3 &= 17H = 0001\,0111 \\ CY_{in} &= 0 \\ \hline \text{Sum} &= 1\,0001\,0101 = 115H \end{aligned} $$ Result truncated to 8 bits: **A = 15H**, **CY = 1** (carry out from bit 7). - AC: bit 3 carry. $1+1+0=0$ carry 1, then $1+1+1 = 1$ carry 1. Carry out from bit 3 = 1 → **AC = 1**. - OV: $C_7 \oplus C_6 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$ → **OV = 0**. - P: 1's in `15H` = 3 (odd) → **P = 1**. - **A = 15H**, CY=1, AC=1, OV=0, P=1. **Step 3: `CPL A`** `A = 15H = 0001\,0101` → complemented → `1110\,1010` = **EAH**. Flags **unchanged**: CY=1, AC=1, OV=0, P=1. **Step 4: `ANL A, #0F0H`** `EAH AND F0H` = `1110\,1010 AND 1111\,0000` = `1110\,0000` = **E0H**. - P: 1's in `E0H` = 3 (odd) → **P = 1**. - **A = E0H**, CY=1, AC=1, OV=0, P=1. **Step 5: `RLC A`** - 9-bit rotation: bit 7 = 1 goes to CY; old CY=1 goes to bit 0. - Old A = `1110\,0000`; after RLC → `1100\,0001` = **C1H**. - **CY = 1** (old bit 7 of A). - P: 1's in `C1H` = 3 (odd) → **P = 1**. **Final Result: A = C1H, CY = 1, AC = 1, OV = 0, P = 1.** --- #### Model Solution for Q-A (b) ```asm ; 32-bit addition: N1 at 30H-33H, N2 at 40H-43H ; Result at 50H-53H (with overflow at 54H if any) MOV R0, #30H ; pointer to N1 MOV R1, #40H ; pointer to N2 MOV R2, #50H ; pointer to result MOV R3, #04H ; loop counter = 4 bytes CLR C ; clear carry before 1st add LOOP: MOV A, @R0 ADDC A, @R1 MOV @R2, A INC R0 INC R1 INC R2 DJNZ R3, LOOP ; loop 4 times JNC DONE MOV 54H, #01H ; store 17th overflow byte DONE: SJMP DONE ``` **Role of CY flag (3 marks):** - The Carry flag acts as the **"17th bit"** of the arithmetic unit, propagating the overflow from each lower-byte addition to the next higher byte. - Without `CY` chaining, two 8-bit ADDs cannot produce a correct 16-bit result; with `ADDC`, multi-byte sums of arbitrary length are constructed byte-by-byte — identical to how humans add column-by-column. - The final CY state after the last byte indicates whether the result overflowed the 32-bit field — crucial in automotive odometers that must roll over without error. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Q-A (b):** > Many students forget to `CLR C` before the first `ADDC`. The 8051 has no plain `ADD` for the chain — it is `ADDC` throughout, so the **initial carry MUST be cleared** explicitly, or the lowest byte will be off by 1. Deduct 2 marks for this oversight. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **(a) [7 marks]** Write an 8051 ALP to compute the **sum of the first N natural numbers** stored in internal RAM location `30H`. Store the 8-bit result in `31H` and a possible carry (sum > 255) in `32H`. **(b) [7 marks]** Explain the following instructions with one example of each in an automotive context: (i) `XRL direct, A` (ii) `SWAP A` (iii) `MUL AB` (iv) `DIV AB` --- #### Model Solution for Q-B (a) ```asm MOV R0, 30H ; R0 = N (loop bound) MOV R1, #00H ; sum low byte MOV R2, #00H ; carry/overflow byte MOV A, #00H MOV R3, A ; i = 0 LOOP: MOV A, R3 INC A ; A = i+1 MOV R3, A ADD A, R1 ; A = sum + i MOV R1, A ; update low byte JNC SKIP ; if no carry, skip INC R2 ; else propagate carry SKIP: DJNZ R0, LOOP MOV 31H, R1 MOV 32H, R2 SJMP $ ``` **Trace** (for N=10, expected sum = 55): - Iteration 1: A=1, R1=0, sum=1, CY=0, R2=0. - Iteration 2: A=2, sum=3, CY=0, R2=0. - … - Iteration 10: A=10, sum=55, CY=0, R2=0. - **Result:** `31H = 37H` (55 decimal), `32H = 00H`. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Q-B (a):** A common error is using `INC` on a register to also flag-check. `INC R1` does NOT update CY. The proper approach is to add to A and check **CY after ADD**, not to increment R1. Deduct 2 marks for using INC instead of ADD for chaining. --- #### Model Solution for Q-B (b) **(i) `XRL direct, A`** — Bitwise XOR of a memory location with the Accumulator. Result stored back in memory. Used in automotive **checksum verification** of CAN frames: the receiver XORs incoming bytes cumulatively; a final `XRL` against the expected checksum confirms data integrity. **(ii) `SWAP A`** — Exchanges the high and low 4-bit nibbles of the Accumulator. Used in **BCD-to-7-segment conversion** of dashboard displays, where the high nibble (tens digit) and low nibble (units digit) must be processed separately for multiplexing. **(iii) `MUL AB`** — Multiplies unsigned A × B; high byte in B, low byte in A. Used in computing **instantaneous electrical power** $P = V \times I$ for Battery Management System (BMS) state-of-charge calculations, where the result may exceed 255 (e.g., 12V × 30A = 360), necessitating the OV-flag check. **(iv) `DIV AB`** — Divides A by B; quotient in A, remainder in B. Used in **fuel-injection pulse-width calculation** where the injector open time (in µs) must be split into an integer and a fractional part for PWM generation. Must guard against `B = 0` to avoid the OV=1 trap. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **🔥 KTU Module-4 Rapid-Fire Revision — 30-Second Checklist** - ☐ **Accumulator (A, SFR E0H) is the universal operand** for every arithmetic/logic op. - ☐ There is **NO plain SUB** — use `CLR C` followed by `SUBB`. - ☐ `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` are the only **4-cycle** arithmetic instructions; both **clear CY** but may set OV. - ☐ `INC` and `DEC` do **NOT** affect the Carry flag (loop counter pitfall). - ☐ `SUBB` sets CY = 1 on **unsigned borrow**. - ☐ `DA A` works **only after ADD/ADDC**; never after SUBB. - ☐ `ANL` = mask / clear bits; `ORL` = set bits; `XRL` = toggle bits. - ☐ `RLC A` is a **9-bit rotation** (CY ↔ bit 0 link); `RL A` is pure 8-bit. - ☐ `SWAP A` exchanges the two nibbles of the Accumulator. - ☐ `CLR A` only clears A — does **NOT** affect any PSW flag. - ☐ The **Parity (P) flag** reflects odd parity of A — updated by nearly every instruction that writes to A. - ☐ **OV = $C_7 \oplus C_6$** — the carry into bit 7 XOR carry out of bit 7. - ☐ For **multi-byte arithmetic**, always `CLR C` before the first `ADDC`; chain with `ADDC` (not `ADD`) for the rest. - ☐ In **automotive BCD instrument clusters**, use `ADD A, #01H` followed by `DA A` to step the odometer. - ☐ `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` use the **B register** as the second operand; B is also used for `MOV B, ...` and `CJNE` — do not overwrite accidentally.

topics.title: Jump, Loop and Call Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Jump, Loop and Call Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **8086 microprocessor** architecture and its derivatives (relevant to automotive Electronic Control Units or ECUs), **control transfer instructions** are a class of assembly language instructions that alter the normal sequential flow of program execution by modifying the **Instruction Pointer (IP)** register, and in the case of inter-segment transfers, the **Code Segment (CS)** register as well.

These instructions are classified into three major categories per the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Jump Instructions (Unconditional & Conditional)**: Transfer execution to a target memory address either based on a fixed operand (unconditional) or based on the state of the **Flag Register** (conditional).
2. **Loop Instructions**: A specialized form of conditional jump that automatically decrements **CX** and branches based on its zero/non-zero status.
3. **Call & Return Instructions**: Transfer control to a subroutine (procedure) and subsequently return control back to the instruction following the CALL, using the **Stack** as a temporary memory buffer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The 8086 microprocessor serves as the foundational teaching architecture for this module because real-world automotive ECUs (e.g., Bosch, Continental) historically evolved from 8-bit (MCS-51) and 16-bit (80186/80196) Intel cores before transitioning to 32-bit PowerPC and ARM Cortex-R architectures. Mastering these control transfer instructions is **mandatory** for understanding ECU firmware boot-loaders, interrupt service routines, and sensor polling routines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Navigation Model

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. The sequential execution of the 8086 is like driving on a single-lane road where you must pass every milestone (instruction) one by one:

- **Jump Instruction → A Highway Exit Sign**: When you reach a particular milestone (instruction), the jump simply reroutes your car to another city (memory address) immediately, without any "return ticket". You never come back to the original road.
- **Loop Instruction → A Roundabout (Traffic Circle)**: A loop is like a roundabout where you keep circling until a condition is met. For example, "make 4 round trips to drop 4 passengers" — this is exactly how the `LOOP` instruction works: it decrements `CX` and continues until `CX = 0`.
- **CALL Instruction → A Detour to a Service Center**: When your car has a problem, you take a detour to a service center (subroutine). The service center fixes the issue and hands you back a **token** with the exact highway exit number where you deviated. After the service, you use the token to return to the main highway. The "token" is the **return address pushed onto the stack**, and the "return trip" is the **RET instruction**.

> [!NOTE]
> In an **Engine Control Unit (ECU)**, the ignition control subroutine is a perfect real-world example of a CALL instruction. The main fuel-injection loop CALLs the ignition-timing subroutine every 60° of crankshaft rotation. After computing the spark advance, the subroutine RETurns to the main loop exactly where it left off.

## 1.3 The Three Registers Involved

Every control transfer instruction in the 8086 manipulates one or more of the following registers:

| Register | Full Name | Role in Control Transfer |
|---|---|---|
| **IP** | Instruction Pointer | Holds the **offset** of the next instruction to execute |
| **CS** | Code Segment | Holds the **base address** of the code segment |
| **CX** | Counter Register | Used implicitly by the `LOOP` family of instructions |

The physical address of the next instruction is always calculated as:

$$
\text{Physical Address} = (\text{CS} \times 16) + \text{IP}
$$

The multiplier of **16** (i.e., shifting CS left by 4 bits) is fundamental — this is because the 8086 uses a **20-bit address bus** to access **1 MB** of memory, while its segment registers are only 16 bits wide.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Unconditional Jump Instruction (`JMP`)

The `JMP` instruction transfers control to the target address unconditionally. It comes in **three addressing modes**:

### 2.1.1 Near Jump (Intrasegment)
- **Syntax**: `JMP label`
- **Operand size**: 16-bit offset only
- **Effect**: `IP ← IP + 16-bit displacement`
- **Range**: **−32,768 to +32,767 bytes** from the current IP
- **No segment change**: CS remains unchanged.

### 2.1.2 Short Jump (Near Jump Variant)
- **Syntax**: `JMP SHORT label`
- **Operand size**: 8-bit signed displacement
- **Range**: **−128 to +127 bytes** from the current IP
- **More compact**: Only **2 bytes** of machine code (opcode + 8-bit offset).

### 2.1.3 Far Jump (Intersegment)
- **Syntax**: `JMP FAR PTR label`
- **Operand size**: 32-bit (16-bit segment + 16-bit offset)
- **Effect**: `IP ← new offset; CS ← new segment`
- **Range**: Anywhere within the **1 MB** address space.

## 2.2 Conditional Jump Instructions

Conditional jumps test the state of specific **flags** in the **Flag Register (FR)**. The 8086 supports **18 conditional jumps**, categorized as follows:

### 2.2.1 Conditional Jumps Based on Individual Flags

| Mnemonic | Condition Tested | Flag Inspected |
|---|---|---|
| `JZ` / `JE` | Jump if Zero / Equal | ZF = 1 |
| `JNZ` / `JNE` | Jump if Not Zero / Not Equal | ZF = 0 |
| `JC` | Jump if Carry | CF = 1 |
| `JNC` | Jump if No Carry | CF = 0 |
| `JS` | Jump if Sign (negative) | SF = 1 |
| `JNS` | Jump if No Sign (positive) | SF = 0 |
| `JO` | Jump if Overflow | OF = 1 |
| `JNO` | Jump if No Overflow | OF = 0 |
| `JP` / `JPE` | Jump if Parity Even | PF = 1 |
| `JNP` / `JPO` | Jump if Parity Odd | PF = 0 |

### 2.2.2 Conditional Jumps for Signed Comparisons

| Mnemonic | Condition | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `JE` / `JNE` | Equal / Not Equal | ZF = 1 / ZF = 0 |
| `JG` / `JNLE` | Greater / Not Less or Equal | (SF⊕OF)=0 AND ZF=0 |
| `JGE` / `JNL` | Greater or Equal / Not Less | (SF⊕OF)=0 |
| `JL` / `JNGE` | Less / Not Greater or Equal | (SF⊕OF)=1 |
| `JLE` / `JNG` | Less or Equal / Not Greater | (SF⊕OF)=1 OR ZF=1 |

### 2.2.3 Conditional Jumps for Unsigned Comparisons

| Mnemonic | Condition | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `JE` / `JNE` | Equal / Not Equal | ZF = 1 / ZF = 0 |
| `JA` / `JNBE` | Above / Not Below or Equal | CF=0 AND ZF=0 |
| `JAE` / `JNB` | Above or Equal / Not Below | CF = 0 |
| `JB` / `JNAE` | Below / Not Above or Equal | CF = 1 |
| `JBE` / `JNA` | Below or Equal / Not Above | CF=1 OR ZF=1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the distinction between signed and unsigned?**
> Consider comparing `0xFFFF` with `0x0001`. Interpreted as **signed**, `0xFFFF` is **−1**, which is less than `+1` → `JL` works. Interpreted as **unsigned**, `0xFFFF` is **65535**, which is greater than `1` → `JA` works. The **same bit pattern** triggers different jumps. In an automotive ECU, sensor readings like coolant temperature are **unsigned** (0–255°C mapped to 0x00–0xFF), while signed arithmetic is used for error offsets and PID controller outputs.

## 2.3 Loop Instructions

The 8086 provides a **dedicated instruction** for implementing loops, which implicitly uses the **CX register** as the counter:

| Mnemonic | Full Name | Condition to Continue Loop |
|---|---|---|
| `LOOP` | Loop while CX ≠ 0 | Decrement CX; if CX ≠ 0, jump |
| `LOOPE` / `LOOPZ` | Loop while CX ≠ 0 AND ZF = 1 | Both conditions must hold |
| `LOOPNE` / `LOOPNZ` | Loop while CX ≠ 0 AND ZF = 0 | Both conditions must hold |

**Operational sequence** of `LOOP label`:
1. `CX ← CX − 1`
2. If `CX ≠ 0`, then `IP ← IP + 8-bit displacement`
3. Else, fall through to the next sequential instruction

> [!NOTE]
> Loop instructions use an **8-bit signed displacement**, limiting them to a range of **−128 to +127 bytes** from the instruction following the LOOP.

## 2.4 Call and Return Instructions

The `CALL` instruction invokes a subroutine, and `RET` returns control to the calling program.

### 2.4.1 Near CALL (Intrasegment)
- **Syntax**: `CALL NEAR PTR label`
- **Effect**:
  1. `SP ← SP − 2`
  2. `Memory[SS:SP] ← IP` (return address pushed onto stack)
  3. `IP ← IP + 16-bit displacement`

### 2.4.2 Far CALL (Intersegment)
- **Syntax**: `CALL FAR PTR label`
- **Effect**:
  1. `SP ← SP − 2`
  2. `Memory[SS:SP] ← CS`
  3. `SP ← SP − 2`
  4. `Memory[SS:SP] ← IP`
  5. `CS ← new segment; IP ← new offset`

### 2.4.3 Near RET
- **Effect**:
  1. `IP ← Memory[SS:SP]`
  2. `SP ← SP + 2`

### 2.4.4 Far RET
- **Effect**:
  1. `IP ← Memory[SS:SP]`; `SP ← SP + 2`
  2. `CS ← Memory[SS:SP]`; `SP ← SP + 2`

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Physical Address | $(\text{CS} \times 16) + \text{IP}$ | Bytes |
| Memory Capacity (8086) | $2^{20}$ | Bytes (1 MB) |
| Short Jump Range | $-128$ to $+127$ | Bytes (8-bit signed) |
| Near Jump Range | $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$ | Bytes (16-bit signed) |
| Far Jump Range | $0$ to $1{,}048{,}575$ | Bytes (20-bit unsigned) |
| Loop Displacement Range | $-128$ to $+127$ | Bytes |
| Stack Growth per Near CALL | $2$ | Bytes |
| Stack Growth per Far CALL | $4$ | Bytes |
| LOOP Counter | $1$ to $65{,}536$ | Iterations (max) |
| Signed Comparison Jump | $(SF \oplus OF)$ | Logical |
| Unsigned Above Jump | $CF = 0 \land ZF = 0$ | Logical |
| Unsigned Below Jump | $CF = 1$ | Logical |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TYPOGRAPHY NOTE**: Throughout these notes, vertical bars for absolute value or cardinality are written as `\vert` in the table column headers to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. In free-flowing math, they are written as $\vert x \vert$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Physical Address Calculation

**Problem**: Given `CS = 4000H` and `IP = 1234H`, calculate the physical address of the next instruction.

**Step 1**: Recall that the 8086 forms a 20-bit physical address by shifting the segment register left by 4 bits (multiplying by 16) and adding the offset.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Address} &= (\text{CS} \times 16) + \text{IP} \\
&= (0 \times 4000 \ll 4) + 0 \times 1234 \\
&= 0 \times 40000 + 0 \times 1234 \\
&= 0 \times 41234
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Convert hexadecimal to decimal for verification.

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 \times 41234 &= (4 \times 16^4) + (1 \times 16^3) + (2 \times 16^2) + (3 \times 16^1) + (4 \times 16^0) \\
&= (4 \times 65536) + (1 \times 4096) + (2 \times 256) + (3 \times 16) + (4 \times 1) \\
&= 262144 + 4096 + 512 + 48 + 4 \\
&= 266804 \text{ decimal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: The next instruction will be fetched from physical address **0x41234**, which is **266,804** in decimal. This is well within the 1 MB (1,048,576 bytes) address space.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Short Jump Displacement Calculation

**Problem**: A short jump instruction is located at physical address `0x10200`. The target label is at physical address `0x10250`. Calculate the 8-bit displacement value stored in the machine code.

**Step 1**: Determine the byte length of the short jump machine instruction.
The 8086 short jump occupies **2 bytes**: 1 byte opcode (EBH) + 1 byte signed displacement.

**Step 2**: Calculate the IP value AFTER the fetch of the jump instruction.

$$
\text{IP}_{\text{after fetch}} = 0 \times 10200 + 2 = 0 \times 10202
$$

**Step 3**: Calculate the displacement.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Displacement} &= \text{Target Address} - \text{IP}_{\text{after fetch}} \\
&= 0 \times 10250 - 0 \times 10202 \\
&= 0 \times 004E \\
&= 78 \text{ decimal}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $78$ is positive and $\vert 78 \vert \le 127$, this fits within the 8-bit signed range. The assembler encodes this as the byte `0x4E`.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Range Boundaries of Short Jump

**Step 1**: The displacement is a signed 8-bit value, so its minimum and maximum values are:

$$
\text{Displacement}_{\min} = -2^{7} = -128
$$

$$
\text{Displacement}_{\max} = 2^{7} - 1 = +127
$$

**Step 2**: The IP after fetching the 2-byte short jump is `IP_current + 2`. Therefore, the reachable addresses are:

$$
\text{Target}_{\min} = \text{IP}_{\text{current}} + 2 + (-128) = \text{IP}_{\text{current}} - 126
$$

$$
\text{Target}_{\max} = \text{IP}_{\text{current}} + 2 + 127 = \text{IP}_{\text{current}} + 129
$$

**Step 3**: The reachable range is therefore **126 bytes backward** to **129 bytes forward** from the start of the short jump instruction.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Number of Loop Iterations

**Step 1**: The LOOP instruction uses the CX register, which is 16 bits wide.

$$
\text{CX}_{\max} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65535
$$

**Step 2**: However, if the programmer initializes `CX = 0`, the LOOP instruction first decrements to `0xFFFF` (65,535), so the loop executes **65,536 times**.

$$
\text{Iterations}_{\max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536
$$

**Step 3**: In an automotive application such as **ADC sampling**, if you need exactly **65,536** samples per cycle, you would write:

```assembly
MOV CX, 0000H      ; CX = 0 (which becomes 65536 after decrement)
NEXT_SAMPLE:
   ; Read ADC value from port 0x100
   IN AL, 100H
   ; Store AL in memory buffer
   MOV [BX], AL
   INC BX
   LOOP NEXT_SAMPLE  ; Decrements CX, jumps if CX ≠ 0
```

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Near CALL Stack Operation

**Problem**: Just before a `CALL NEAR PTR SUB1` instruction executes, `SP = 0x2000` and `IP = 0x1450`. Trace the stack pointer and the stack contents.

**Step 1**: Before the CALL, the stack pointer is at `0x2000` (top of stack). The return address to be saved is the **IP of the NEXT instruction**, which is `IP + 3` because the near CALL is 3 bytes long (opcode + 2-byte displacement).

$$
\text{Return Address} = 0 \times 1450 + 3 = 0 \times 1453
$$

**Step 2**: The stack pointer is decremented by 2 (because near call pushes one 16-bit value).

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{after decrement}} = 0 \times 2000 - 2 = 0 \times 1FFE
$$

**Step 3**: The return address is written to the new top of the stack.

$$
\text{Memory}[0 \times 1FFE] = 0 \times 1453
$$

**Step 4**: The IP is loaded with the target address.

$$
\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = 0 \times 1450 + \text{Displacement}_{\text{SUB1}}
$$

**Step 5**: When the subroutine finishes and `RET` executes, the value at `0x1FFE` is popped back into IP, and SP becomes `0x2000` again, seamlessly resuming the main program.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control Flow Diagram — Classification of 8086 Control Transfer Instructions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["8086 Control Transfer Instructions"] --> B["Jump Instructions"]
    A --> C["Loop Instructions"]
    A --> D["Call and Return Instructions"]

    B --> B1["Unconditional Jump JMP"]
    B --> B2["Conditional Jump"]
    B1 --> B1A["Short Jump 8-bit displacement"]
    B1 --> B1B["Near Jump 16-bit displacement"]
    B1 --> B1C["Far Jump 32-bit segment and offset"]
    B2 --> B2A["Single Flag Tests JZ JC JS JO JP"]
    B2 --> B2B["Unsigned Comparison JA JAE JB JBE"]
    B2 --> B2C["Signed Comparison JG JGE JL JLE"]

    C --> C1["LOOP CX not equal to 0"]
    C --> C2["LOOPE CX not equal to 0 and ZF equals 1"]
    C --> C3["LOOPNE CX not equal to 0 and ZF equals 0"]

    D --> D1["CALL Near Intrasegment"]
    D --> D2["CALL Far Intersegment"]
    D --> D3["RET Near"]
    D --> D4["RET Far"]
```

## 4.2 Stack Operation Sequence During a Far CALL and Far RET

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as Main Program
    participant SP as Stack Pointer SP
    participant Stack as Memory Stack
    participant Sub as Subroutine

    Note over Main,SP: State before CALL: IP equals 3000H, CS equals 2000H, SP equals 1000H

    Main->>SP: SP decreases by 2 to 0FFE H
    Main->>Stack: Push CS equals 2000H at SS colon SP
    Main->>SP: SP decreases by 2 to 0FFC H
    Main->>Stack: Push IP equals 3003H at SS colon SP
    Main->>Sub: Load new CS and new IP from instruction operand

    Note over Sub: Subroutine executes here

    Sub->>Stack: Pop IP equals 3003H from SS colon SP
    Sub->>SP: SP increases by 2 to 0FFE H
    Sub->>Stack: Pop CS equals 2000H from SS colon SP
    Sub->>SP: SP increases by 2 to 1000 H
    Sub->>Main: Execution resumes at next sequential instruction
```

## 4.3 LOOP Instruction Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Encounter LOOP label"] --> Dec["Step 1: CX decreases by 1"]
    Dec --> Test{"Step 2: Is CX not equal to 0?"}
    Test -- "Yes CX not 0" --> Jump["Step 3: IP equals IP plus 8-bit displacement"]
    Jump --> Body["Execute loop body again"]
    Body --> Start
    Test -- "No CX equals 0" --> Exit["Step 4: Fall through to next sequential instruction"]
    Exit --> End["Loop complete"]
```

## 4.4 ECU Firmware Block Diagram — Application in Automotives

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAIN["Main ECU Loop at 0x0000"]
        M1["Initialize Peripherals"]
        M2["Read Crank Position Sensor"]
        M3{"RPM exceeds Threshold?"}
        M4["Idle Fuel Strategy"]
        M5["CALL Subroutine Calculate Ignition Timing"]
    end

    subgraph SUB["Subroutine at 0x4000"]
        S1["Save Registers on Stack PUSH"]
        S2["Read MAP and MAF Sensors"]
        S3["Compute Spark Advance"]
        S4["Write to Ignition Coil Driver"]
        S5["RET Restore Registers and Return"]
    end

    M5 -- "Push IP onto Stack" --> S1
    S5 -- "Pop IP from Stack" --> M1

    M3 -- "No Low RPM" --> M4
    M3 -- "Yes High RPM" --> M5
    M4 --> M1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in real-world automotive design**: The CALL/RET mechanism allows engineers to write **modular, reusable firmware**. For example, the ignition-timing subroutine can be called from multiple places (cold-start routine, cruise control, traction control) without duplicating code. This is the **8086 foundation** upon which modern AUTOSAR-compliant ECU architectures are built.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between a near jump and a far jump in the 8086 microprocessor. Mention the range of each.**

**Model Answer**:

A near jump (`JMP NEAR PTR label`) modifies only the **Instruction Pointer (IP)** while leaving the **Code Segment (CS)** register unchanged. It uses a **16-bit signed displacement**, providing a range of **−32,768 bytes to +32,767 bytes** from the current IP value. The machine code occupies **3 bytes**.

A far jump (`JMP FAR PTR label`) modifies **both IP and CS**, allowing transfer to any location within the **1 MB** address space. It uses a **32-bit operand** (16-bit segment + 16-bit offset) and occupies **5 bytes** of machine code.

| Parameter | Near Jump | Far Jump |
|---|---|---|
| Registers Modified | IP only | IP and CS |
| Operand Size | 16-bit displacement | 32-bit (segment:offset) |
| Machine Code Size | 3 bytes | 5 bytes |
| Range | $\pm 32$ KB | $1$ MB |
| Typical Use | Local branches within a segment | Transfers across modules |

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Explain the operation of the `LOOP` instruction. What happens if CX is initially loaded with 0000H?**

**Model Answer**:

The `LOOP label` instruction performs two operations:
1. **CX ← CX − 1**
2. If `CX ≠ 0`, then `IP ← IP + 8-bit signed displacement`.

The 8-bit displacement restricts the loop target to **−128 to +127 bytes** from the instruction following the LOOP.

If CX is initially loaded with `0000H`, then after the first decrement, CX becomes `FFFFH` (which is 65,535 in decimal). Since `CX ≠ 0`, the loop continues. This effectively creates a **65,536 iteration loop**, which is the maximum number of iterations possible.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> - **Forgetting to decrement CX before the test**: Students often write "loop while CX > 0" implying the test happens BEFORE decrement. The 8086 decrements FIRST.
> - **Mixing signed and unsigned jumps**: Using `JA` (unsigned above) when the data is signed gives wrong results in sensor calibration code.
> - **Ignoring stack segment overflow**: Repeated CALL without matching RET corrupts the stack pointer and causes ECU firmware to crash (in real-world: triggers watchdog reset).
> - **Forgetting the CALL instruction size**: When calculating the return address, students must remember NEAR CALL = 3 bytes and FAR CALL = 5 bytes.
> - **Assuming LOOP works on registers other than CX**: LOOP implicitly uses CX only.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the operation of the CALL and RET instructions in 8086. Differentiate between near and far CALL. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

The `CALL` instruction transfers control to a subroutine, while `RET` returns control to the instruction immediately following the CALL. The **Stack** (pointed to by the **SS:SP** pair) is used to store the return address.

#### Near CALL Sequence (3-byte instruction):

1. `SP ← SP − 2` (stack pointer decremented)
2. `Memory[SS:SP] ← IP` (current IP pushed as return address)
3. `IP ← IP + 16-bit displacement`

#### Near RET Sequence:

1. `IP ← Memory[SS:SP]` (return address popped)
2. `SP ← SP + 2`

#### Far CALL Sequence (5-byte instruction):

1. `SP ← SP − 2`
2. `Memory[SS:SP] ← CS`
3. `SP ← SP − 2`
4. `Memory[SS:SP] ← IP`
5. `CS ← new segment`, `IP ← new offset`

#### Far RET Sequence:

1. `IP ← Memory[SS:SP]`; `SP ← SP + 2`
2. `CS ← Memory[SS:SP]`; `SP ← SP + 2`

**Diagram (Near CALL/RET)**:

```
       Main Program                         Stack Memory
       -------------                        -------------
IP→    CALL SUB1                  SP→      | (empty)   |   0x2000
       NEXT_INSTRUCTION   ←--RET--         | 0x1453    |   0x1FFE  ← return address pushed
       ...                                -------------
                                           
       After CALL, IP loads SUB1's address
       After RET, IP pops 0x1453 and resumes at NEXT_INSTRUCTION
```

| Feature | Near CALL | Far CALL |
|---|---|---|
| Bytes Pushed to Stack | 2 (IP only) | 4 (IP and CS) |
| CS Modified? | No | Yes |
| Bytes in Machine Code | 3 | 5 |
| Use Case | Subroutine within same segment | Subroutine in different segment |

**(b) Write an 8086 assembly language program to find the sum of the first N natural numbers, where N is a single-digit number stored at memory location 2000H. Store the result at 2001H. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

```assembly
        MOV CL, [2000H]      ; Load N into CL
        MOV CH, 00H          ; Clear CH to make CX a proper counter
        MOV BL, 00H          ; BL will accumulate the sum, start at 0
        MOV DL, 01H          ; DL is the current natural number, start at 1

NEXT:   ADD BL, DL           ; Sum = Sum + current number
        INC DL               ; Move to next natural number
        DEC CL               ; Decrement counter
        JNZ NEXT             ; If CL ≠ 0, repeat the loop

        MOV [2001H], BL      ; Store final sum in memory
        HLT                  ; Stop execution
```

**Step-by-step trace** (for N = 5):

| Iteration | DL (current) | BL (sum) | CL (counter) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial | 1 | 0 | 5 |
| 1 | 2 | 1 | 4 |
| 2 | 3 | 3 | 3 |
| 3 | 4 | 6 | 2 |
| 4 | 5 | 10 | 1 |
| 5 | 6 | 15 | 0 → EXIT |

**Final Result**: `Memory[2001H] = 0x0F` (which is 15 decimal).

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Solution using LOOP instruction**: Students may replace `DEC CL / JNZ NEXT` with a single `LOOP NEXT` instruction by loading CX with N at the start. However, this requires that the loop body is within **−128 to +127 bytes** of the LOOP instruction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) Explain the various conditional jump instructions of 8086. Give an example program segment to find the largest of two unsigned numbers. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

The 8086 provides **18 conditional jump instructions**, all of which test the **Flag Register**. The displacement is always **8-bit signed** (−128 to +127 bytes).

**Classification**:

1. **Flag-based jumps (10)**:
   - `JZ`/`JNZ`: Zero flag tests
   - `JC`/`JNC`: Carry flag tests
   - `JS`/`JNS`: Sign flag tests
   - `JO`/`JNO`: Overflow flag tests
   - `JP`/`JNP`: Parity flag tests

2. **Unsigned comparison jumps (6)**:
   - `JA` (Above: CF=0 AND ZF=0)
   - `JAE` (Above or Equal: CF=0)
   - `JB` (Below: CF=1)
   - `JBE` (Below or Equal: CF=1 OR ZF=1)
   - `JE` (Equal: ZF=1)
   - `JNE` (Not Equal: ZF=0)

3. **Signed comparison jumps (4)**:
   - `JG` (Greater: ZF=0 AND SF=OF)
   - `JGE` (Greater or Equal: SF=OF)
   - `JL` (Less: SF≠OF)
   - `JLE` (Less or Equal: ZF=1 OR SF≠OF)

**Program to find the largest of two unsigned numbers**:

Assume two unsigned numbers are stored at memory locations `3000H` and `3001H`. Store the larger one at `3002H`.

```assembly
        MOV AL, [3000H]      ; Load first number into AL
        MOV BL, [3001H]      ; Load second number into BL
        CMP AL, BL           ; Compare AL with BL by performing AL - BL
        JAE FIRST_IS_BIG     ; If AL ≥ BL (unsigned), jump to FIRST_IS_BIG
        ; Else, BL is bigger
        MOV [3002H], BL      ; Store BL (the larger) at 3002H
        JMP DONE             ; Skip the other path

FIRST_IS_BIG:
        MOV [3002H], AL      ; Store AL (the larger) at 3002H

DONE:   HLT                  ; End of program
```

**Explanation**:
- `CMP AL, BL` performs `AL − BL` internally and sets the flags but does NOT store the result.
- `JAE FIRST_IS_BIG` jumps if `CF = 0` (no borrow occurred during subtraction, i.e., AL ≥ BL in unsigned terms).
- The unconditional `JMP DONE` prevents the program from incorrectly overwriting the result.

**(b) Explain the LOOP, LOOPE, and LOOPNE instructions in detail. What is the maximum number of times a LOOP can iterate? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

#### 1. LOOP label

This is the standard loop instruction. It performs:
1. `CX ← CX − 1`
2. If `CX ≠ 0`, then `IP ← IP + 8-bit displacement`.

**Example**:
```assembly
        MOV CX, 0005H        ; Initialize counter to 5
NEXT:   ; Some instruction body
        LOOP NEXT            ; Decrements CX; jumps if CX ≠ 0
```

This loop executes the body exactly **5 times**.

#### 2. LOOPE / LOOPZ label

"Loop While Equal" — performs:
1. `CX ← CX − 1`
2. If `CX ≠ 0` **AND** `ZF = 1`, then jump.

This is useful for **searching operations** where you want to continue scanning until a mismatch or end of array:

```assembly
        MOV CX, 0010H        ; Scan up to 16 bytes
        MOV SI, 2000H        ; Source pointer
        MOV AL, 'A'          ; Search for character 'A'
NEXT:   CMP [SI], AL         ; Compare memory with search key
        INC SI               ; Advance pointer
        LOOPE NEXT           ; Continue if equal (ZF=1) and CX≠0
        ; Fall through: either mismatch or count exhausted
```

#### 3. LOOPNE / LOOPNZ label

"Loop While Not Equal" — performs:
1. `CX ← CX − 1`
2. If `CX ≠ 0` **AND** `ZF = 0`, then jump.

This continues as long as the compared values are **NOT equal**.

#### Maximum Iterations:

Since CX is a 16-bit register:

$$
\text{Iterations}_{\max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536
$$

This maximum is reached when CX is **initialized to 0000H**. After the first decrement, CX becomes `FFFFH` (65,535), and the loop continues until CX reaches 0 again — a total of **65,536 iterations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Tip**: In automotive firmware, LOOP instructions are often avoided in time-critical interrupt service routines (ISRs) because they use **8-bit displacement**, which limits flexibility. Instead, **conditional jumps with explicit `DEC CX`** are preferred for predictable timing analysis.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Control Transfer = IP Modification**: Any instruction that alters the Instruction Pointer changes the program flow.
- **Physical Address**: $\text{PA} = (\text{CS} \times 16) + \text{IP}$. Always remember the segment is shifted LEFT by 4 bits (multiplied by 16).
- **JMP (Unconditional)**: 3 types — Short (8-bit), Near (16-bit), Far (32-bit). Ranges are $\pm 127$, $\pm 32$K, and $1$ MB respectively.
- **Conditional Jumps**: Always use **8-bit signed displacement**, range $\pm 127$ bytes. There are **18** conditional jumps, all based on the Flag Register.
- **Signed vs Unsigned**: Use `JG/JL` for signed and `JA/JB` for unsigned. They use the SAME flag bits but interpret them differently.
- **LOOP**: Auto-decrements **CX** (not any other register) and tests for zero. Range $\pm 127$ bytes. Max iterations = **65,536**.
- **LOOPE / LOOPZ**: Loops only if **CX ≠ 0 AND ZF = 1**. Used for scanning.
- **LOOPNE / LOOPNZ**: Loops only if **CX ≠ 0 AND ZF = 0**. Used for finding mismatches.
- **CALL vs JMP**: CALL pushes a return address onto the stack; JMP does not. **Always pair CALL with RET**.
- **Near CALL**: Pushes **2 bytes (IP only)** onto the stack. Far CALL pushes **4 bytes (IP and CS)**.
- **Stack Discipline**: `CALL` decrements SP; `RET` increments SP. A mismatched CALL/RET causes stack corruption — a common cause of automotive ECU crashes.
- **Real-World Automotive Use**:
  - `CALL` → Reusable subroutines (e.g., sensor calibration, fault detection).
  - `LOOP` → ADC sampling, timeout counters, debounce delays.
  - `JNZ`/`JZ` → Decision-making based on sensor thresholds (e.g., over-temperature cutoff).
  - `JC` → Critical error detection (e.g., sensor short-circuit protection).

### Frequently Asked Short Questions in KTU Exams
1. Differentiate between JMP and CALL.
2. What is the role of CX in the LOOP instruction?
3. State the maximum range of a short jump and a near jump.
4. Explain the difference between LOOP, LOOPE, and LOOPNE.
5. What happens when LOOP is executed with CX = 0000H?

### Memory Aids for the Flag-Based Jumps
- **"A Above, B Below"** → Unsigned comparisons.
- **"G Greater, L Less"** → Signed comparisons.
- **"E Equal, N Not"** → Common suffix for both.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I/O Port Programming
topics.content:
# I/O Port Programming in Automotive Microcontrollers > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** I/O Port Programming --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **I/O Port Programming** is the process of configuring, controlling, and manipulating the bidirectional digital Input/Output pins of a microcontroller using dedicated Special Function Registers (SFRs) to interface with external automotive hardware such as sensors, actuators, switches, relays, and communication lines. In the context of the **PIC16F877A** (widely used in KTU syllabus and automotive Body Control Modules — BCMs), every I/O port is governed by **three core registers**: | Register | Full Form | Function | |----------|-----------|----------| | **TRISx** | Tri-State Register | Direction control (1 = Input, 0 = Output) | | **PORTx** | Port Register | Reads logic level on the physical pin / latches output data | | **LATx** | Latch Register | Writes output data without RMW (Read-Modify-Write) hazards | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The distinction between **PORT** and **LAT** is a high-yield question. *PORT reads the pin voltage*, whereas *LAT reads the internal output latch*. This becomes critical during Read-Modify-Write operations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Post Office" Model Imagine a microcontroller as a **Post Office** with multiple service windows (ports). Each window has: - A **counter clerk (TRIS register)** who decides whether the window is for **accepting letters (Input)** or **dispatching parcels (Output)**. - A **mailbox (PORT register)** where incoming letters are dropped. - A **dispatch tray (LAT register)** where outgoing parcels are placed for the courier. When a customer (the CPU) wants to: - **Receive** a letter → sets TRIS=1, then reads PORT. - **Send** a parcel → sets TRIS=0, then writes to LAT. If the clerk mistakenly sets TRIS=0 when expecting input (or vice versa), the postal system malfunctions — exactly how an incorrectly configured I/O pin causes hardware damage or signal errors in an ECU. ### 1.3 I/O Pin Structure in CMOS Logic Each microcontroller I/O pin in automotive ECUs is essentially a **CMOS totem-pole** driver with a **TTL-Schmitt trigger input**, surrounded by **ESD protection diodes** and an optional **weak pull-up resistor**. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants Used in I/O Design:** > - **V_IL (max)** = **0.15 × V_DD** = 0.75 V (logic LOW threshold for 5V V_DD) > - **V_IH (min)** = **0.25 × V_DD + 0.8** = 2.05 V (logic HIGH threshold for 5V V_DD) > - **V_OH (min)** = **4.4 V** (driving capability) > - **I_OH / I_OL** = **±20 mA** (per pin source/sink current, PIC16F877A) > - **ESD protection** = up to **±2000 V** (Human Body Model) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Tri-Directional I/O Architecture A single I/O pin in the PIC microcontroller can be modeled as a controlled bidirectional gate: 1. **CMOS Output Driver (Push-Pull):** A P-channel MOSFET on top sourcing current, and an N-channel MOSFET on the bottom sinking current. 2. **Schmitt Trigger Input Buffer:** Provides **hysteresis** to reject noise — crucial in the noisy automotive environment (ignition coils, solenoids, EMI from motors). 3. **TRIS Latch:** Controls whether the output driver is **active** (TRIS=0) or in **high-impedance Hi-Z** state (TRIS=1). 4. **Weak Pull-up Transistor:** Optional internal resistor (~10–50 kΩ) enabled via the **RBPU** bit (OPTION_REG register) or WPUx bits (in newer PICs). 5. **Analog Multiplexer:** Routes analog signals to the ADC instead of the digital input buffer when the pin is configured as analog (e.g., AN0–AN13 on PIC16F877A). ### 2.2 Bit Manipulation Instructions (KTU-Favorite) Microchip's PIC instruction set provides atomic (single-cycle) bit-level instructions that do **not** invoke the problematic Read-Modify-Write path: | Mnemonic | Operation | Example | |----------|-----------|---------| | **BSF** *f, b* | Bit Set f at position b | `BSF PORTB, 3` → RB3 = 1 | | **BCF** *f, b* | Bit Clear f at position b | `BCF PORTB, 5` → RB5 = 0 | | **BTFSS** *f, b* | Bit Test, Skip if Set | Conditional branch | | **BTFSC** *f, b* | Bit Test, Skip if Clear | Conditional branch | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why BSF/BCF are safer than `PORTB |= 0x08;`?** > C-style `|=` compiles to **read-modify-write**, which can corrupt adjacent bits during high-speed switching (a known issue called **RMW hazard**). BSF/BCF operate directly on the LAT register's internal bus, bypassing the pin read. ### 2.3 Read-Modify-Write (RMW) Hazard — A Real Automotive Problem Suppose `PORTB = 0b00001111` (low nibble HIGH) and you write `PORTB = PORTB | 0x40;` to set RB6. If at that exact moment an external circuit is driving RB0 LOW (high-current sink), the **read-back** will see RB0=0, and after OR-ing and writing back, RB0 gets reset to 0 (unintended). This is the **RMW hazard**. **Solution:** Use the **LAT register** for outputs and **BSF LATB, 6** instead. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Remarks | |---------|-----------------|-----------------| | Logic HIGH Threshold | $V_{IH} \geq 0.25 \cdot V_{DD} + 0.8$ | Volts (TTL Schmitt) | | Logic LOW Threshold | $V_{IL} \leq 0.15 \cdot V_{DD}$ | Volts | | Output Source Current | $I_{OH} \leq 20$ | mA per pin | | Output Sink Current | $I_{OL} \leq 20$ | mA per pin | | Port Sink Total | $\sum I_{OL} \leq 200$ | mA per port | | LED Series Resistor | $R = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_F}{I_F}$ | Ohms | | Pull-up Resistor (external) | $R_{PU} = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{IH}}{I_{LEAK,max}}$ | Typically 4.7 kΩ–10 kΩ | | Debounce Time (SW) | $t_d \approx 10$ | ms (mechanical switch) | | Schmitt Hysteresis | $V_H = V_{IH} - V_{IL}$ | Typically **0.6 V** at 5V V_DD | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility:** In automotive BCMs (Body Control Modules), I/O ports drive turn signals, brake lights, headlights (via MOSFETs), read door switches, brake-pedal sensors, and ignition switches. Each is governed by these thresholds and current limits. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: LED Current-Limiting Resistor Calculation **Problem:** Design a circuit to drive an automotive dashboard indicator LED using RB0 of PIC16F877A. Given: - $V_{DD}$ = **5.0 V** - LED forward voltage $V_F$ = **2.1 V** (red LED) - Desired LED current $I_F$ = **15 mA** - Microcontroller $I_{OL,max}$ = **25 mA** (safe limit) **Step 1:** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the loop: $$\begin{aligned} V_{DD} &= V_F + V_R + V_{OL} \\ \end{aligned}$$ where $V_R$ is the voltage across the series resistor, and $V_{OL}$ is the LOW-level output voltage of the pin (~0.6 V). **Step 2:** Solve for $V_R$: $$\begin{aligned} V_R &= V_{DD} - V_F - V_{OL} \\ V_R &= 5.0 - 2.1 - 0.6 \\ V_R &= 2.3 \text{ V} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Apply Ohm's Law: $$\begin{aligned} R &= \dfrac{V_R}{I_F} \\ R &= \dfrac{2.3}{0.015} \\ R &= 153.33 \text{ }\Omega \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Choose the next-highest **E12 standard value**: **$R = 150\ \Omega$**. **Step 5:** Recompute actual current: $$\begin{aligned} I_{F,actual} &= \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_F - V_{OL}}{R} \\ I_{F,actual} &= \dfrac{5.0 - 2.1 - 0.6}{150} \\ I_{F,actual} &= 15.33 \text{ mA} \quad (\text{within } I_{OL,max}) \end{aligned}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Switch Debounce Time Window A mechanical automotive switch (e.g., door switch, brake pedal) exhibits **contact bounce** for typically **5–20 ms**. The CPU must ignore intermediate transitions. **Step 1:** Define the Nyquist-like sampling bound: $$\begin{aligned} t_{sample} &\geq 2 \cdot t_{bounce,max} \\ t_{sample} &\geq 2 \cdot 10 \text{ ms} \\ t_{sample} &\geq 20 \text{ ms} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the polling delay (using Timer0, prescaler 1:256, F_osc = 20 MHz): $$\begin{aligned} T_{instr} &= \dfrac{4}{F_{osc}} = \dfrac{4}{20 \times 10^6} = 200 \text{ ns} \\ T_{tick} &= T_{instr} \times 256 = 200 \text{ ns} \times 256 = 51.2 \text{ }\mu s \\ N_{ticks} &= \dfrac{20 \text{ ms}}{51.2 \text{ }\mu s} \approx 390 \text{ ticks} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Load Timer0 preload: $$\begin{aligned} TMR0_{preload} &= 256 - 390 \pmod{256} \\ TMR0_{preload} &= 256 - 134 \\ TMR0_{preload} &= 122 \end{aligned}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: I/O Configuration Decision Tree | Application | TRIS Setting | LAT Initial | Pull-up | Notes | |-------------|--------------|-------------|---------|-------| | LED indicator (active-LOW) | 0 (Output) | 1 (OFF) | Disabled | Sink current when LAT=0 | | Relay coil driver (via MOSFET) | 0 (Output) | 0 (OFF) | Disabled | Use flyback diode | | Push-button (active-LOW) | 1 (Input) | — | **Enabled** | External 10 kΩ also acceptable | | Hall sensor (push-pull) | 1 (Input) | — | **Disabled** | Sensor drives both rails | | Open-drain sensor (e.g., LM35) | 1 (Input) | — | **Enabled** | Pull-up mandatory | | Open-drain bus (e.g., 1-Wire) | 1 (Input) | 0 (active pull) | **Enabled** | Bidirectional via TRIS toggle | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 I/O Pin Internal Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR cpuBus["CPU Internal Data Bus"] trisLatch["TRIS Latch"] dataLatch["Data LAT Latch"] pmos["P-MOS Driver"] nmos["N-MOS Driver"] esdUp["ESD Diode to V_DD"] esdDn["ESD Diode to V_SS"] schmitt["Schmitt Trigger Buffer"] pullup["Weak Pull-up Transistor"] pin["Physical Pin RBx"] analogMux["Analog Mux to ADC"] vdd["V_DD Supply"] vss["V_SS Ground"] cpuBus --> trisLatch cpuBus --> dataLatch trisLatch --> pmos trisLatch --> nmos dataLatch --> pmos dataLatch --> nmos pmos --> pin nmos --> pin pullup --> pin pin --> esdUp pin --> esdDn esdUp --> vdd esdDn --> vss pin --> schmitt schmitt --> cpuBus pin --> analogMux ``` ### 4.2 Read/Write Operation Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD start(["Application Code Executes"]) step1["Step 1: Configure TRIS Register"] step2{"Direction?"} outPath["Step 2a: TRISx = 0 (Output Mode)"] inPath["Step 2b: TRISx = 1 (Input Mode)"] step3a["Step 3a: Write to LATx Register"] step3b["Step 3b: Enable Pull-up (RBPU bit)"] step4a["Step 4a: Output Drivers Drive Pin"] step4b["Step 4b: Schmitt Trigger Latches Input"] step5a["Step 5a: External Load Activated (LED / Relay)"] step5b["Step 5b: Read PORTx Register"] decision{"Valid Logic Level?"} actA["Actuator / Light operates"] actB["Decision based on input"] retry["Re-sample after debounce delay"] endA(["End"]) endB(["End"]) start --> step1 --> step2 step2 -->|Output| outPath --> step3a --> step4a --> step5a --> endA step2 -->|Input| inPath --> step3b --> step4b --> step5b --> decision decision -->|Yes| actB --> endB decision -->|Noisy / Bouncing| retry --> step4b ``` ### 4.3 Typical Automotive LED Indicator Circuit ```mermaid flowchart LR mcuPin["PIC16F877A RB0 Pin"] res["Series Resistor R = 150 ohm"] led["Indicator LED V_F = 2.1 V"] gnd["Ground V_SS"] mcuPin --> res --> led --> gnd note1["Sink current when LATB0 = 0
Driving 15 mA"] mcuPin -.- note1 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Explain the function of the TRIS register in a PIC microcontroller. How does setting TRISB = 0xF0 configure Port B?* **Model Answer:** The **TRIS (Tri-State) register** controls the direction of each I/O pin. Writing **'1'** to a TRIS bit configures the corresponding pin as an **Input** (high-impedance), while writing **'0'** configures it as an **Output** (active driver enabled). For **TRISB = 0xF0** (binary `1111 0000`): - **RB7, RB6, RB5, RB4** → set to **1** → configured as **Inputs** - **RB3, RB2, RB1, RB0** → set to **0** → configured as **Outputs** Therefore, the upper nibble of Port B acts as an input port (e.g., for reading switches), and the lower nibble acts as an output port (e.g., for driving LEDs). *(3 marks: 1 for TRIS definition, 1 for binary conversion, 1 for input/output identification)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Differentiate between PORT and LAT registers in PIC microcontrollers. When is LAT preferred?* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | PORT Register | LAT Register | |--------|---------------|--------------| | **Read** | Returns **actual voltage on physical pin** | Returns **last value written to output latch** | | **Write** | Reads pin, modifies, writes back (RMW) | Writes directly to latch, bypasses pin read | | **Use case** | Reading digital inputs | Driving digital outputs reliably | The **LAT register is preferred** when performing **bit manipulation** on output pins (e.g., `BSF LATB, 3`) to avoid **Read-Modify-Write hazards**, where intermediate noise or load transients on adjacent pins can corrupt the operation. *(3 marks: 1.5 for difference, 1.5 for RMW explanation)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of an I/O pin** in a PIC16F877A microcontroller. List all the functional blocks. **(7 marks)** *(b)* Write an embedded C program for PIC16F877A (using XC8 compiler) to **read four push-button switches connected to RB0–RB3** and display their status on **four LEDs connected to RD0–RD3**. Explain the debounce technique used. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Internal I/O Pin Architecture — 7 Marks** The internal structure of a single PIC16F877A I/O pin contains the following functional blocks: 1. **CMOS Output Driver:** A complementary pair of P-channel and N-channel MOSFETs forming a **push-pull totem-pole** output. Active only when TRIS bit = 0. 2. **Data Latch (LAT):** Stores the logic level to be driven on the pin when configured as output. 3. **TRIS Latch:** Controls the direction — '1' = Input (Hi-Z), '0' = Output (driver enabled). 4. **Schmitt Trigger Input Buffer:** Converts analog pin voltage into clean digital logic with **hysteresis** (typically 0.6 V at 5V V_DD), providing noise immunity — essential for the EMI-rich automotive environment. 5. **Weak Pull-up Transistor (~10–50 kΩ):** Optional internal pull-up enabled via the **RBPU** bit in the OPTION_REG (clearing RBPU enables pull-ups globally on PORTB). 6. **ESD Protection Diodes:** Two clamp diodes (one to V_DD, one to V_SS) protect against electrostatic discharge (rated **±2000 V HBM**). 7. **Analog Multiplexer:** Routes the pin to the **10-bit ADC** module when the corresponding bit in the **ANSEL/ADCON1** register is configured for analog. ``` [Functional Block Diagram — see SECTION 4.1] ``` *(Marks: 1 for diagram, 6 for block descriptions — 1 mark each)* **(b) Embedded C Program — 7 Marks** ```c #include // Configuration bits (for 20 MHz crystal) #pragma config FOSC = HS, WDTE = OFF, PWRTE = ON, BOREN = ON #define _XTAL_FREQ 20000000 // Debounce delay function (~20 ms) void debounce_delay(void) { for (volatile unsigned int i = 0; i < 3000; i++); } void main(void) { // Configure PORTB as input (TRISB = 0x0F for RB0-RB3 input) TRISB = 0x0F; // RB0-RB3 inputs, RB4-RB7 unused (set as input) // Enable weak pull-ups on PORTB OPTION_REGbits.nRBPU = 0; // Clear RBPU to ENABLE pull-ups // Configure PORTD as output (TRISD = 0x00) TRISD = 0x00; PORTD = 0x00; // All LEDs OFF initially while (1) { unsigned char switchStatus = PORTB & 0x0F; // Read lower nibble // Apply software debounce debounce_delay(); if ((PORTB & 0x0F) == switchStatus) { PORTD = switchStatus; // Mirror switches to LEDs } } } ``` **Debounce Technique Explanation (1 mark):** The program reads the switch status, waits for **20 ms**, then **re-reads**. Only if both readings match, the value is considered valid. This filters out mechanical contact bounce (typically 5–15 ms) without requiring external RC filters. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *(a)* Explain the **Read-Modify-Write (RMW) problem** in PIC microcontroller I/O programming. How does the LAT register solve it? Illustrate with an example. **(7 marks)** *(b)* Design an **automotive turn-indicator system** using PIC16F877A. The system must blink two LEDs (Left = RB0, Right = RB1) at **1 Hz with 50% duty cycle** when corresponding push-buttons are pressed. Write the C program and explain the hardware interface. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) RMW Problem — 7 Marks** The **Read-Modify-Write hazard** occurs when a single-bit operation on a port register inadvertently alters neighboring bits due to physical pin behavior. **Mechanism:** Consider `PORTB = 0b00001111;` (low nibble = HIGH). C code `PORTBbits.RB4 = 1;` compiles internally to: 1. **READ** the entire PORTB register → reads current pin voltages. 2. **MODIFY** bit 4 in the read value (set it to 1). 3. **WRITE** the modified value back. If an external load is **briefly driving RB0 LOW** (e.g., during inductive load switching — common in automotive relay coils), the read-back sees `0b00001110`. After OR-ing, the write restores `0b00011110` — **RB0 is unintentionally cleared**. **How LAT Solves This (4 marks):** Writing to `LATB` performs the bit set/clear **directly on the internal data latch** without sampling the physical pin. Therefore: ```c LATBbits.LATB4 = 1; // Atomic — no RMW hazard ``` Microchip's enhanced mid-range PICs also provide dedicated **BSF LATx, b** and **BCF LATx, b** instructions that operate on a single bit without affecting other bits. **Example — Turn-Signal Application:** In automotive BCMs, multiple I/O lines switch simultaneously (e.g., brake light + turn signal + reverse light). Using `PORTB` for bit manipulation can cause flickering; `LATB` is the recommended safe practice. --- **(b) Turn-Indicator System Design — 7 Marks** **Hardware Interface:** | Component | Pin | Configuration | |-----------|-----|---------------| | Left Push-button (SW_L) | RB4 | Input, internal pull-up | | Right Push-button (SW_R) | RB5 | Input, internal pull-up | | Left LED (via 150 Ω resistor) | RB0 | Output, sink current | | Right LED (via 150 Ω resistor) | RB1 | Output, sink current | ```c #include #define _XTAL_FREQ 20000000 void main(void) { TRISB = 0xF0; // RB4-RB7 inputs, RB0-RB3 outputs OPTION_REGbits.nRBPU = 0; // Enable pull-ups on PORTB LATB = 0x00; // All LEDs OFF while (1) { // Left indicator if (!(PORTB & 0x10)) { // SW_L (RB4) pressed LATBbits.LATB0 = 1; // LED ON __delay_ms(500); LATBbits.LATB0 = 0; // LED OFF __delay_ms(500); } // Right indicator else if (!(PORTB & 0x20)) { // SW_R (RB5) pressed LATBbits.LATB1 = 1; __delay_ms(500); LATBbits.LATB1 = 0; __delay_ms(500); } else { LATB &= 0xF0; // Both LEDs OFF } } } ``` **Hardware Explanation:** - **Switches** are wired between the PIC pin and **GND** (active-LOW configuration). The internal pull-up keeps the pin HIGH when the button is open; pressing the button pulls it LOW. - **LEDs** are connected with **cathode to the PIC pin** and **anode to V_DD via 150 Ω resistor**. The PIC **sinks current** when the output is LOW — this is the safer configuration since N-channel drivers are typically stronger than P-channel drivers in CMOS. - **1 Hz, 50% duty cycle** = 500 ms ON + 500 ms OFF (computed using a 20 MHz crystal, allowing accurate `__delay_ms`). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing TRIS logic:** Writing "TRIS=1 means output" — this is the **#1 most common mistake**. Always remember: **TRIS=1 → Input (Hi-Z)**; **TRIS=0 → Output (driven)**. > 2. **Forgetting to set ADC pins as digital:** When using PORTA as digital I/O on PIC16F877A, students forget to configure **ADCON1 = 0x06** to disable analog inputs. This causes the pins to read as random values. > 3. **Missing pull-up configuration:** Forgetting `OPTION_REGbits.nRBPU = 0;` when reading switches — pin "floats" and reads random values. > 4. **Using `PORT` instead of `LAT` for outputs:** Loses 1 mark on the RMW question — examiners specifically test this distinction. > 5. **No debounce in switch-reading code:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if software debounce or hardware RC filter is missing. > 6. **Wrong LED current direction:** Driving LEDs without calculating series resistor = exceeds $I_{OL,max}$ → pin damage → examiner flags as design error. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **TRISx** = Direction control (**1 = Input, 0 = Output**) — **Mnemonic:** "**T**ris = **T**hink of Input as **1**" or remember "TRIS=1, pin is free (input)". - **PORTx** = Read pin voltage (for inputs) / Write pin (has RMW hazard for outputs). - **LATx** = Atomic write to output latch (preferred for outputs). - **BSF / BCF** are atomic bit instructions — use them with **LAT** for safety. - **Pull-ups:** Enable via `OPTION_REGbits.nRBPU = 0` (global) on PORTB; required for active-LOW switches. - **ADC conflict:** Configure **ADCON1 = 0x06** to make PORTA digital. - **Current limit:** **$I_{OL} \leq 20$ mA per pin**, $\sum I_{OL} \leq 200$ mA per port. - **LED resistor formula:** $R = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_F - V_{OL}}{I_F}$ - **Debounce time:** Use **$t_d \geq 20$ ms** for mechanical switches (automotive-grade components). - **Schmitt Trigger Hysteresis:** ~**0.6 V** at 5V V_DD — provides EMI immunity critical in automotive environments. - **Automotive use cases:** Turn signals, brake lights, door switches, ignition key, relay coils, sensor reads (via ADC), CAN bus transceivers (via dedicated pins). - **Open-drain buses** (1-Wire, LIN) require pull-ups even on bidirectional PIC pins. - **ESD rating:** ±2000 V HBM — still use TVS diodes for external automotive connectors.

topics.title: Programming 8051 Timers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Programming 8051 Timers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition The **8051 Timer/Counter** is a dedicated hardware peripheral module inside the Intel MCS-51 microcontroller family that performs two fundamental functions: (a) **Timing** — measuring precise internal time intervals by counting machine cycles derived from the oscillator, and (b) **Counting** — tallying external events (negative-edge pulses) on dedicated input pins. The original 8051 contains **two 16-bit Timer/Counters** designated **Timer 0** and **Timer 1**, which are programmed through a set of Special Function Registers (SFRs): **TMOD (0x89)**, **TCON (0x88)**, **TH0/TL0**, and **TH1/TL1**. In automotive embedded systems (ECU body controllers, instrument clusters, airbag timers), the 8051 timers are used to generate **PWM signals for motor speed control**, debounce switches, schedule fuel injection events, and flash dashboard indicators at precise intervals. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Programming 8051 timers requires mastery of the TMOD and TCON register bit-fields, the four operating modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3), timer value calculation, and assembly/C-language initialization sequences. ### Conceptual Analogy — The Stopwatch & Counter Stamp Imagine a digital stopwatch (the **Timer**) that increments itself every time the CPU's internal heartbeat (oscillator) pulses — you set a starting number, and it counts upward. When it overflows past its maximum (e.g., 65535 + 1), it "rings a bell" (sets the **TFx overflow flag**), exactly like a microwave beeping when countdown ends. Now imagine instead a factory worker at a conveyor belt (the **Counter**) pressing a tally counter every time a box passes a sensor — here, the increment is **external**, not internal. The 8051 Timer/Counter does both jobs with one hardware block — controlled by a single switch called **C/T̅** in the TMOD register. - **Internal Clock Source (Timer):** Machine cycle = **12 / FOSC**. With a **11.0592 MHz crystal**, one tick occurs every **1.085 µs**. - **External Event Source (Counter):** Negative-edge transition on pin **T0 (P3.4)** or **T1 (P3.5)**. - **Standard Crystal for KTU Labs:** **FOSC = 11.0592 MHz** (chosen because it generates exact baud-rate divisors for serial communication). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The TMOD Register (Address 0x89, NOT bit-addressable) ``` Bit: 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 0 +--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+ TMOD = | GATE1 | C/T1 | T1M1 | T1M0 | GATE0 | C/T0 | T0M1 | T0M0 | +--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+ \____________Timer 1_________/ \____________Timer 0_________/ ``` | Bit | Name | Function | |:---:|:----:|:---------| | **GATE** | Gate Control | When **GATE = 1**, timer runs only while `INTx` pin is HIGH **AND** `TRx = 1`. Used for pulse-width measurement. | | **C/T̅** | Counter/Timer Select | **C/T̅ = 0 → Timer mode** (internal clock). **C/T̅ = 1 → Counter mode** (external pin T0/T1). | | **M1, M0** | Mode Bits | Select operating mode (see table below). | ### 2.2 The TCON Register (Address 0x88, bit-addressable) | Bit | Name | Address | Function | |:---:|:----:|:-------:|:---------| | 7 | **TF1** | 0x8F | Timer 1 Overflow Flag — set by hardware, cleared by software. | | 6 | **TR1** | 0x8E | Timer 1 Run Control Bit — **TR1 = 1 starts** the timer. | | 5 | **TF0** | 0x8D | Timer 0 Overflow Flag. | | 4 | **TR0** | 0x8C | Timer 0 Run Control Bit. | | 3 | IE1 | 0x8B | External Interrupt 1 edge flag. | | 2 | IT1 | 0x8A | External Interrupt 1 type (edge/level). | | 1 | IE0 | 0x89 | External Interrupt 0 edge flag. | | 0 | IT0 | 0x88 | External Interrupt 0 type. | ### 2.3 The Four Operating Modes | M1 | M0 | Mode | Width | Description | |:--:|:--:|:----:|:-----:|:------------| | 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 13-bit | Legacy 8048 compatibility. Uses 8 bits of THx + 5 bits of TLx. Rarely used today. | | 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 16-bit | Most common. Full 16-bit range: **0 → 65535**. Must reload manually. | | 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 8-bit auto-reload | TLx counts 0→255; on overflow it auto-reloads from THx. Ideal for **fixed baud-rate generators** and precise repetitive interrupts. | | 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | Split (Timer 0 only) | TL0 acts as 8-bit timer controlled by TR0; TH0 acts as 8-bit timer controlled by TR1. Timer 1 effectively stops in this mode. | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Timer Math > [!NOTE] > **Assume FOSC = 11.0592 MHz unless the question states otherwise.** All KTU problems use this value. $$ \text{Machine Cycle Period } T_{MC} = \frac{12}{F_{OSC}} $$ $$ T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085\,\mu s $$ $$ \text{Delay} = (2^n - \text{Timer Value}) \times T_{MC} $$ where **n** is the number of bits in the mode (13, 16, or 8). > **Critical Note:** When using the LaTeX table, NEVER use vertical pipes for absolute value. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, e.g., `$\vert x \vert$`, to avoid breaking the markdown parser. | Mode | n | Range of Timer Value | Max Delay (with 11.0592 MHz) | |:----:|:-:|:--------------------:|:----------------------------:| | 0 | 13 | 0 to 8191 | $8192 \times 1.085\,\mu s \approx 8.89\,ms$ | | 1 | 16 | 0 to 65535 | $65536 \times 1.085\,\mu s \approx 71.11\,ms$ | | 2 | 8 | 0 to 255 | $256 \times 1.085\,\mu s \approx 277.8\,\mu s$ | ### 2.5 Real-World Automotive Utility - **Dashboard indicator flash**: Mode 1 with interrupt, generating a precise 1 Hz blink. - **Wiper interval timer**: Mode 2 with auto-reload — never drifts. - **Knock-sensor event counter**: Counter mode counts pulses from a piezoelectric knock sensor. - **PWM for motor control**: Timer interrupt toggles a pin at controlled duty cycles for DC motor speed. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems ### 3.1 Derivation — Generating an Arbitrary Delay **Goal:** Find the value to load into THx:TLx in **Mode 1 (16-bit)** for a desired delay **D** at oscillator **FOSC**. The timer increments once every machine cycle. Starting from the loaded value **V**, the timer overflows after **(2¹⁶ − V)** increments. Each increment takes **TMC = 12 / FOSC** seconds. Therefore: $$ D = (2^{16} - V) \times \frac{12}{F_{OSC}} $$ Solving for **V**: $$ V = 2^{16} - \frac{D \times F_{OSC}}{12} $$ For **FOSC = 11.0592 MHz** and converting **D** to microseconds: $$ V = 65536 - \frac{D\,[\mu s]}{1.085} $$ The High and Low bytes are split as: $$ THx = \left\lfloor \frac{V}{256} \right\rfloor \qquad TLx = V \bmod 256 $$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — KTU Classic Problem > **Problem:** Using **Timer 0 in Mode 1**, generate a delay of **50 ms**. Assume FOSC = **11.0592 MHz**. Write the value to load into TH0 and TL0. **Step 1 — Calculate the Timer Value V:** $$ V = 65536 - \frac{D \times F_{OSC}}{12} $$ $$ V = 65536 - \frac{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 11.0592 \times 10^6}{12} $$ $$ V = 65536 - \frac{50 \times 11.0592}{12} $$ $$ V = 65536 - \frac{552.96}{12} = 65536 - 46.08 \times 10^{?} $$ Let us redo the arithmetic cleanly: $$ V = 65536 - \left(\frac{50 \times 11.0592}{12}\right) = 65536 - 46.08 \times 10^{?} $$ Correctly: $$ V = 65536 - \left(\frac{50000\,\mu s}{1.085\,\mu s}\right) $$ $$ V = 65536 - 46082.95 \approx 65536 - 46083 = 19453 $$ **Step 2 — Convert 19453 to Hexadecimal:** $$ 19453_{10} = 4BF5_{16} $$ **Step 3 — Split into High and Low bytes:** $$ TH0 = 4B_{16} = 0x4B $$ $$ TL0 = F5_{16} = 0xF5 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification:** Loaded value = 0x4BF5 = 19453. Overflows after $(65536 - 19453) = 46083$ ticks. $46083 \times 1.085\,\mu s \approx 50.0\,ms$. ✓ --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Mode 2 Auto-Reload > **Problem:** Configure **Timer 1 in Mode 2** to generate a continuous **200 µs** tick (used to flash an LED in an automotive dashboard). FOSC = **11.0592 MHz**. **Step 1 — Compute timer value for 200 µs:** $$ V = 256 - \frac{200}{1.085} = 256 - 184.33 \approx 72 $$ **Step 2 — Convert 72 to hex:** $$ 72_{10} = 48_{16} $$ **Step 3 — Load both TH1 and TL1 with 0x48:** $$ TH1 = 0x48 \quad (\text{reload value}) $$ $$ TL1 = 0x48 \quad (\text{initial count}) $$ On overflow, TL1 automatically reloads from TH1 — no software reload needed, giving **jitter-free precision**. --- ### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Counter Mode Application > **Problem:** An automotive crankshaft sensor delivers **1000 pulses per second** on pin T1 (P3.5).
