Configure **Timer 1 as Counter** to count these pulses and raise an overflow flag after **1000 counts**. FOSC = 11.0592 MHz. **Step 1 — Choose Mode 1 (16-bit counter).** Initial value required: $$ V = 65536 - 1000 = 64536 $$ **Step 2 — Convert to hex:** $$ 64536_{10} = FC18_{16} $$ **Step 3 — Configure TMOD for Counter mode:** Set **C/T̅1 = 1** in TMOD. TMOD value (Timer 1 = counter, Mode 1; Timer 0 not used): $$ TMOD = 0x50 = 0101\,0000_2 $$ $$ TH1 = 0xFC \quad TL1 = 0x18 $$ **Step 4 — Start the counter:** Set **TR1 = 1** by executing `SETB TR1`. After exactly 1000 crankshaft pulses, **TF1 = 1**. > [!NOTE] > **Counter mode ignores FOSC** — the formula uses pulse-count instead of microseconds. Mode choice and bit manipulation are what matter. --- ### 3.5 Standard 8051 Assembly Code Skeleton (for reference) ``` MOV TMOD, #01H ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit), Timer mode AGAIN: MOV TH0, #4BH ; Load high byte for 50 ms MOV TL0, #0F5H ; Load low byte SETB TR0 ; Start Timer 0 HERE: JNB TF0, HERE ; Wait for overflow flag CLR TR0 ; Stop Timer 0 CLR TF0 ; Clear overflow flag SJMP AGAIN ; Repeat for continuous timing ``` ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Timer 0 Initialization Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart([Begin Program]) --> nodeA[Step 1: Configure TMOD register
Select Mode and Timer or Counter] nodeA --> nodeB[Step 2: Load TH0 with high byte of initial value] nodeB --> nodeC[Step 3: Load TL0 with low byte of initial value] nodeC --> nodeD[Step 4: Set TR0 to 1 using SETB TR0] nodeD --> nodeE[Step 5: Timer starts counting machine cycles internally] nodeE --> nodeF{Step 6: Has TF0 become 1?} nodeF -- No --> nodeE nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[Step 7: Hardware sets TF0 flag automatically] nodeG --> nodeH[Step 8: Software clears TR0 and TF0] nodeH --> nodeI{Step 9: Repeat or stop?} nodeI -- Repeat --> nodeB nodeI -- Stop --> nodeEnd([End Timer Operation]) ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal 8051 Timer Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph oscBlock[Crystal Oscillator Block] osc["FOSC 11.0592 MHz Crystal"] end osc --> divide["Divide by 12
Machine Cycle Generator"] divide --> mux{TMOD C/T-bar bit selection} mux -- C/T-bar equals 0 --> timerLogic[Internal Timer Logic] mux -- C/T-bar equals 1 --> externalPin["External Pin T0 P3.4 or T1 P3.5"] externalPin --> counterLogic[External Counter Logic] timerLogic --> registers[(THx and TLx Register Pair)] counterLogic --> registers registers --> overflowCheck{Count exceeds maximum?} overflowCheck -- Yes --> flagSet[Set TFx Flag in TCON] overflowCheck -- No --> registers flagSet --> interruptRequest[Optional Interrupt to CPU] flagSet --> userCode[CPU Polls TFx in software] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Mode Comparison Summary ```mermaid flowchart TD modeSelect{M1 M0 bit selection in TMOD} modeSelect -- 0 0 --> m0[Mode 0: 13-bit timer
Legacy 8048 compatibility
Max delay about 8.89 ms] modeSelect -- 0 1 --> m1[Mode 1: 16-bit timer
Most flexible
Max delay about 71.11 ms] modeSelect -- 1 0 --> m2[Mode 2: 8-bit auto reload
THx reloads TLx on overflow
Best for baud rate generation] modeSelect -- 1 1 --> m3[Mode 3: Split timer Timer 0 only
TL0 8-bit plus TH0 8-bit
Timer 1 becomes inactive] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Explain the function of the C/T̅ bit in the TMOD register of the 8051 microcontroller.* **Model Answer:** The **C/T̅** bit (Counter/Timer select) in the TMOD register selects the clock source for the corresponding timer. When **C/T̅ = 0**, the timer takes its clock from the **internal oscillator** (after division by 12) — this is **Timer mode**. When **C/T̅ = 1**, the timer counts **external negative-edge pulses** arriving on pin **T0 (P3.4)** for Timer 0 or **T1 (P3.5)** for Timer 1 — this is **Counter mode**. *(3 marks: 1 for register name, 1 for timer mode meaning, 1 for counter mode meaning.)* **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Differentiate between Mode 1 and Mode 2 of the 8051 timer.* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Mode 1 | Mode 2 | |:--------|:-------|:-------| | Width | **16-bit** (THx + TLx both 8-bit) | **8-bit** (TLx only; THx holds reload value) | | Range | 0 to 65535 | 0 to 255 | | Reload | **Manual** by software after each overflow | **Automatic** — THx reloads TLx on overflow | | Use case | Long, one-shot delays | Continuous repetitive interrupts, baud-rate generation | --- ### Part B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks Each) #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the TMOD register format of the 8051 microcontroller. List all four operating modes. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A 8051 system uses an 11.0592 MHz crystal. Write an 8051 assembly program to generate a **delay of 30 ms** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1**. Show the value loaded into TH0 and TL0 with full calculation. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution to Q.A(a):** The TMOD register (address 0x89) is a non-bit-addressable SFR that configures the operating mode of Timer 0 and Timer 1. ``` TMOD: GATE1 | C/T1 | T1M1 | T1M0 | GATE0 | C/T0 | T0M1 | T0M0 (Timer 1) (Timer 0) ``` - **GATE**: When GATE = 1, timer is enabled only if `INTx` pin is HIGH **and** `TRx = 1`. Used for pulse-width measurement. - **C/T̅**: Selects internal timer (0) or external counter (1). - **M1, M0**: Mode selection bits. | M1 | M0 | Mode | Width | Function | |:--:|:--:|:----:|:-----:|:---------| | 0 | 0 | Mode 0 | 13-bit | 8048 compatibility | | 0 | 1 | Mode 1 | 16-bit | Most common, fully software-controlled | | 1 | 0 | Mode 2 | 8-bit auto-reload | THx reloads TLx automatically | | 1 | 1 | Mode 3 | Split | Timer 0 only — TL0 + TH0 act as two 8-bit timers | *(7 marks: 2 for TMOD diagram, 2 for bit descriptions, 3 for the mode table.)* --- **Model Solution to Q.A(b):** **Step 1 — Timer value formula:** $$ V = 65536 - \frac{D\,[\mu s] \times F_{OSC}}{12} = 65536 - \frac{30\,000}{1.085} $$ **Step 2 — Evaluate:** $$ V = 65536 - 27649.77 \approx 65536 - 27650 = 37886 $$ **Step 3 — Convert to hexadecimal:** $$ 37886_{10} = 93FE_{16} $$ **Step 4 — Split into TH0 and TL0:** $$ TH0 = 0x93 \qquad TL0 = 0xFE $$ **Step 5 — 8051 Assembly Program:** ```assembly ORG 0000H MOV TMOD, #01H ; Timer 0, Mode 1, software start, timer mode LOOP: MOV TH0, #93H ; Load high byte for 30 ms delay MOV TL0, #0FEH ; Load low byte SETB TR0 ; Start Timer 0 WAIT: JNB TF0, WAIT ; Loop until overflow flag is set CLR TR0 ; Stop Timer 0 CLR TF0 ; Clear overflow flag for next iteration SJMP LOOP ; Repeat continuously END ``` *(7 marks: 3 for calculation, 2 for hex split, 2 for assembly code.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]** **(a)** Explain the TCON register. What is the role of the TRx and TFx bits? *(7 marks)* **(b)** Configure **Timer 1 in Mode 2** to generate a continuous **500 µs interrupt tick** using an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Show calculation and write the initialization code. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution to Q.B(a):** The **TCON register (0x88)** is bit-addressable and controls the run/flag bits of both timers as well as external interrupt configuration. | Bit | Symbol | Address | Function | |:---:|:------:|:-------:|:---------| | 7 | TF1 | 8FH | Timer 1 Overflow — set by hardware on overflow | | 6 | TR1 | 8EH | Timer 1 Run — TR1 = 1 starts the timer | | 5 | TF0 | 8DH | Timer 0 Overflow | | 4 | TR0 | 8CH | Timer 0 Run | | 3–0 | IE1, IT1, IE0, IT0 | — | External interrupt bits | **Role of TRx (Timer Run):** TRx is a software-controlled bit. Writing `1` to TRx **starts** the corresponding timer (provided GATE = 0 or GATE = 1 with `INTx` HIGH). Writing `0` halts it. **Role of TFx (Timer Flag):** TFx is **automatically set by hardware** when the timer/counter overflows from its maximum count (FFFFH in Mode 1, FFH in Mode 2). It must be **cleared by software** before the next overflow check. This flag can be polled in code (`JNB TFx, target`) or used to trigger an interrupt (if enabled in IE register). *(7 marks: 3 for table, 2 for TRx, 2 for TFx.)* --- **Model Solution to Q.B(b):** **Step 1 — Compute timer value for 500 µs in Mode 2 (8-bit):** $$ V = 256 - \frac{500}{1.085} = 256 - 460.83 $$ Since 500 µs exceeds the maximum Mode 2 delay of 277.8 µs (see KTU Formula Sheet), **Mode 2 alone is insufficient**. We must either (i) cascade two Mode 2 timers, or (ii) use Mode 1 instead. Following the question text strictly, we proceed assuming the student should reduce the requirement. **Alternatively, use 250 µs** (within Mode 2 limit): $$ V = 256 - \frac{250}{1.085} = 256 - 230.41 \approx 26 = 0x1A $$ **Step 2 — Initialize both TH1 (reload) and TL1 (count) to 0x1A.** **Step 3 — Assembly Code:** ```assembly MOV TMOD, #20H ; Timer 1, Mode 2, software start, timer mode MOV TH1, #1AH ; Reload value for 250 µs tick MOV TL1, #1AH ; Initial TL1 = 0x1A SETB TR1 ; Start Timer 1 LOOP: JNB TF1, LOOP ; Wait for overflow CLR TF1 ; Clear flag for next tick CPL P1.0 ; Toggle pin (e.g., LED or buzzer) SJMP LOOP ; Auto-reload occurs automatically END ``` *(7 marks: 2 for calculation, 2 for register setup, 3 for code.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Wrong crystal assumption.** Always assume **FOSC = 11.0592 MHz** unless stated. Using 12 MHz gives wrong answers. > 2. **Confusing C/T̅.** This is **active-LOW** internally but the bit value is **1 = Counter, 0 = Timer**. Many students invert this. > 3. **Forgetting to clear TFx.** Hardware sets it; software must clear it — otherwise the timer appears to "never finish". > 4. **Mixing up THx/TLx split.** The split is `TH = upper byte`, `TL = lower byte`. Use `TH = V / 256`, `TL = V mod 256` (or `V AND 0xFF`). > 5. **Mode 2 overflow.** Students forget that Mode 2 max delay is only ~278 µs at 11.0592 MHz — anything longer requires Mode 1 or cascading. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **8051 has two 16-bit timers**: Timer 0 and Timer 1, controlled by TMOD and TCON. - **TMOD** (0x89) selects **mode**, **C/T̅**, and **GATE** for each timer; it is **NOT bit-addressable** — load as a full byte. - **TCON** (0x88) holds the run bits (**TR0/TR1**) and overflow flags (**TF0/TF1**); it **IS bit-addressable**. - **Four Modes**: Mode 0 (13-bit), Mode 1 (16-bit — most common), Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload), Mode 3 (split, Timer 0 only). - **Standard Crystal**: **FOSC = 11.0592 MHz** → **TMC = 1.085 µs**. - **Key Formula**: $V = 2^n - \dfrac{D \times F_{OSC}}{12}$ where *n* = mode width. - **Split bytes**: `THx = V >> 8`, `TLx = V & 0xFF`. - **Counter mode** ignores oscillator — only external pulses on **P3.4 (T0)** or **P3.5 (T1)** count. - **GATE = 1** is for hardware-controlled timing (e.g., measuring pulse width on `INTx`). - **Mode 2 is preferred** for serial-port baud-rate generation (e.g., 9600 baud) because auto-reload eliminates jitter. - **Always clear TFx in software** after detecting overflow; otherwise subsequent polls misbehave. - **Automotive uses**: Wiper interval timing, dashboard LED blink, fuel injection scheduling, motor PWM via interrupt.

topics.title: Serial Port Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Serial Port Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Serial Port Programming** in the context of 8051/automotive microcontrollers refers to the structured development of firmware that configures and manipulates the on-chip **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)** hardware block to perform **asynchronous, full-duplex, frame-oriented data exchange** between the microcontroller and external peripherals (such as ECUs, OBD-II diagnostic tools, GPS modules, Bluetooth transceivers, and infotainment systems in modern vehicles).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Serial Port Programming is the technique of initializing the microcontroller's serial communication registers, configuring the baud-rate generator, transmitting/receiving data bytes serially, and managing the associated interrupt service routines in accordance with the asynchronous serial communication protocol.*

The serial port in 8051-family microcontrollers is built around three primary hardware elements:
1. **SBUF (Serial Buffer Register)** — a dual-register physically (one for transmit, one for receive) sharing the same address.
2. **SCON (Serial Control Register)** — mode selection, flags, and 9th-bit storage.
3. **PCON (Power Control Register)** — contains the **SMOD** bit that doubles the baud rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Communication Modes (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Mode 0:** 8-bit shift register, fixed baud rate = F_OSC / 12 (synchronous).
> - **Mode 1:** 8-bit UART, variable baud rate (Timer 1 overflow), **most commonly used**.
> - **Mode 2:** 9-bit UART, fixed baud rate = F_OSC / 32 or F_OSC / 64.
> - **Mode 3:** 9-bit UART, variable baud rate (Timer 1 overflow).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two people standing far apart trying to talk using a **single two-way walkie-talkie**:
- The walkie-talkie is the **UART** hardware.
- The button you press to speak is the **SBUF write** (transmit).
- The speaker through which you listen is the **SBUF read** (receive).
- The agreed-upon **speed of talking** (e.g., one word per second) is the **baud rate**.
- The specific **structure of your message** ("start-voice-data-stop") is the **frame format**.

If both people do not agree on the *speed* (baud rate) and *structure* (frame), the communication is garbled — just like a serial link. **Serial Port Programming** is the act of configuring the "walkie-talkie" (microcontroller) so that both ends understand each other perfectly.

> [!TIP]
> **Why serial in automotives?** Wiring a 16-bit or 32-bit parallel bus across a vehicle is impractical. A single wire carrying bits one after another (serial) reduces harness weight, cost, and complexity — critical for CAN, LIN, and K-Line automotive networks which are *built upon* UART-style framing.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Standard baud rates used in vehicles:** **9600 bps** (most common for OBD-II), **19200 bps**, **115200 bps**.
- **Standard crystal frequencies:** **11.0592 MHz** (chosen because 11.0592 MHz / 12 = 921.6 kHz, which divides cleanly to give exact 9600 baud). Also **12 MHz** and **16 MHz**.
- **Frame bits in Mode 1:** **1 start bit + 8 data bits + 1 stop bit = 10 bits per frame**.
- **Industry reference standards:** **EIA-232 (RS-232)**, **EIA-422**, **EIA-485** (for K-Line/LIN physical layers).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 8051 Serial Port — Operational Architecture

The 8051 contains a **single on-chip UART** that can operate in four modes selected via the **SM0** and **SM1** bits of the **SCON** register. Communication is full-duplex, meaning transmission and reception can occur simultaneously because there are two physically separate 8-bit registers behind the address `SBUF` (one write-only, one read-only).

#### 2.1.1 SCON Register (Serial Control — Bit Addressable, Address = 98H)

| Bit | Symbol | Reset | Function |
|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----|
| 7 | **SM0** | 0 | Serial Mode bit 0 |
| 6 | **SM1** | 0 | Serial Mode bit 1 |
| 5 | **SM2** | 0 | Multiprocessor communication enable |
| 4 | **REN** | 0 | Receiver Enable (must be 1 to receive) |
| 3 | **TB8** | 0 | 9th transmitted bit (Modes 2, 3) |
| 2 | **RB8** | 0 | 9th received bit (Modes 2, 3) |
| 1 | **TI** | 0 | Transmit Interrupt flag (set by HW at end of byte) |
| 0 | **RI** | 0 | Receive Interrupt flag (set by HW at end of byte) |

#### 2.1.2 PCON Register (Power Control — Not Bit Addressable, Address = 87H)

Only the MSB is relevant for serial communication:

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|:----:|:----:|:----|
| 7 | **SMOD** | Baud-rate doubler. If 1, baud rate in Modes 1, 2, 3 is doubled. |
| 6–0 | — | Power-saving bits (not used for serial). |

### 2.2 The Three Initialization Steps (KTU Exam Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> **A 8051 serial port MUST be initialized in this exact sequence** — examiners will deduct marks for missing any step.

**Step 1 — Set the Baud Rate (Program Timer 1).**
Choose Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)**. Load `TH1` with the reload value calculated from the desired baud rate.

**Step 2 — Configure SCON.**
Select the mode (SM0, SM1), enable receiver (REN = 1), clear TI/RI flags.

**Step 3 — Start Timer 1.**
Set `TR1 = 1` via the `TCON` register to begin baud-rate generation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:----:|:----|:----|:----|:----|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Period | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{F_{OSC}}$ | seconds | 8051 uses 12 clocks per MC |
| 2 | Timer 1 Overflow Rate (Mode 2) | $f_{overflow} = \dfrac{F_{OSC}}{12 \times (256 - X)}$ | Hz | $X$ is the TH1 reload value |
| 3 | Mode 1, 3 Baud Rate (SMOD=0) | $Baud = \dfrac{F_{OSC}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - X)} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \cdot F_{OSC}}{384 \cdot (256 - X)}$ | bps | Variable baud rate |
| 4 | Mode 1 TH1 Reload Value | $X = 256 - \dfrac{F_{OSC}}{384 \cdot Baud}$ | decimal | Solve for TH1 |
| 5 | Mode 2 Baud Rate (SMOD=0) | $Baud = \dfrac{F_{OSC}}{64}$ | bps | Fixed |
| 6 | Mode 2 Baud Rate (SMOD=1) | $Baud = \dfrac{F_{OSC}}{32}$ | bps | Doubled |
| 7 | Mode 0 Baud Rate | $Baud = \dfrac{F_{OSC}}{12}$ | bps | Fixed (synchronous) |
| 8 | Frame Time (Mode 1) | $T_{frame} = \dfrac{10}{Baud}$ | seconds | 1 start + 8 data + 1 stop |
| 9 | Characters per Second | $CPS = \dfrac{Baud}{10}$ | chars/s | At 9600 baud → 960 cps |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The 11.0592 MHz crystal is the *de facto* automotive/embedded standard because it yields a clean integer TH1 = **0xFD = 253** for 9600 baud (SMOD=0), avoiding the 0.16% error that a 12 MHz crystal would produce (which corrupts UART frames at higher speeds).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OBD-II Diagnostics (ISO 9141 / ISO 14230 K-Line):** Uses UART at 10.4 kbps on a single wire — the very same Mode 1 programming concepts apply.
- **In-Vehicle Infotainment (IVI):** GPS modules stream NMEA sentences at 9600 baud via UART.
- **Bluetooth/Wi-Fi Modules (HC-05, ESP8266):** Configured and commanded through AT commands over UART.
- **ECU-to-ECU Communication:** Legacy buses and bootloader firmware updates rely on UART bootloaders.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Calculating TH1 Reload Value for 9600 Baud

**Given:** $F_{OSC} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$, Desired $Baud = 9600$, SMOD = 0, Timer 1 in Mode 2.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation:**

$$X = 256 - \frac{F_{OSC}}{384 \cdot Baud}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$X = 256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times 9600}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$384 \times 9600 = 3{,}686{,}400$$

**Step 4 — Perform the division:**

$$\frac{11{,}059{,}200}{3{,}686{,}400} = 3$$

**Step 5 — Subtract from 256:**

$$X = 256 - 3 = 253$$

**Step 6 — Convert to hexadecimal (KTU standard):**

$$253_{10} = FD_{H}$$

**Step 7 — Verify the resulting baud rate:**

$$Baud_{actual} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - 253)} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 12 \times 3} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{1152} = 9600 \text{ bps} \checkmark$$

**Result:** **TH1 = 0xFD = 253 decimal.**

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Finding the Required Crystal for an Integer TH1

**Goal:** Find $F_{OSC}$ such that 4800 baud yields an integer TH1 with SMOD = 0.

**Step 1 — Start from the baud-rate equation:**

$$Baud = \frac{F_{OSC}}{384 \cdot (256 - X)}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $F_{OSC}$:**

$$F_{OSC} = 384 \cdot Baud \cdot (256 - X)$$

**Step 3 — Pick the smallest possible $(256 - X)$ for a 12 MHz class system.** Try $(256 - X) = 3$:

$$F_{OSC} = 384 \times 4800 \times 3 = 5.5296 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 4 — Try $(256 - X) = 6$ for a 12 MHz class system:**

$$F_{OSC} = 384 \times 4800 \times 6 = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The 11.0592 MHz crystal again emerges, demonstrating why it is universally used in UART-based automotive ECUs.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Error Analysis for Non-Standard Crystals

**Given:** $F_{OSC} = 12 \text{ MHz}$, Target $Baud = 9600$, SMOD = 0.

**Step 1 — Compute the theoretical TH1 value:**

$$X = 256 - \frac{12 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times 9600} = 256 - 3.2552 = 252.7448$$

**Step 2 — Round to nearest integer: $X = 253$.**

**Step 3 — Compute the actual baud rate achieved:**

$$Baud_{actual} = \frac{12 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - 253)} = \frac{12 \times 10^{6}}{1152} = 10416.67 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the percentage error:**

$$\% \text{Error} = \left( \frac{10416.67 - 9600}{9600} \right) \times 100\% = 8.51\%$$

**Interpretation:** An 8.51% error exceeds the UART receiver's tolerable ±2.2% tolerance, leading to **framing errors**. This is why **12 MHz crystals are avoided** in serial-heavy automotive designs.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common TH1 Values for F_OSC = 11.0592 MHz

| Target Baud | SMOD | (256 - X) | TH1 (Hex) | TH1 (Dec) | Error |
|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:|
| 1200 | 0 | 24 | F8 | 248 | 0.00% |
| 2400 | 0 | 12 | F4 | 244 | 0.00% |
| 4800 | 0 | 6 | FA | 250 | 0.00% |
| 9600 | 0 | 3 | FD | 253 | 0.00% |
| 19200 | 0 | 1.5 | — | — | not integer → use SMOD=1 |
| 19200 | 1 | 3 | FD | 253 | 0.00% |
| 28800 | 1 | 2 | FE | 254 | 0.00% |
| 57600 | 1 | 1 | FF | 255 | 0.00% |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 UART Frame Format (Mode 1) — Standard Automotive OBD-II Frame

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Idle as Idle Line (HIGH)
    participant Start as Start Bit (LOW)
    participant D0 as D0 (LSB)
    participant D1 as D1
    participant D2 as D2
    participant D3 as D3
    participant D4 as D4
    participant D5 as D5
    participant D6 as D6
    participant D7 as D7 (MSB)
    participant Stop as Stop Bit (HIGH)
    Idle->>Start: Line pulled LOW to signal start
    Start->>D0: First bit transmitted
    D0->>D1: Next bit
    D1->>D2: Next bit
    D2->>D3: Next bit
    D3->>D4: Next bit
    D4->>D5: Next bit
    D5->>D6: Next bit
    D6->>D7: Final data bit
    D7->>Stop: Line returns HIGH for stop bit
    Stop->>Idle: Ready for next byte
```

> [!NOTE]
> The line **rests HIGH (logic 1)** when idle — this is the key reason UART is robust. If the line is broken or disconnected, the receiver sees a continuous "idle" and can detect the fault.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of 8051 Serial Port

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CPU Core] --> B[SBUF Write Buffer]
    A --> C[SBUF Read Buffer]
    B --> D[Transmit Shift Register]
    D --> E[TXD Pin P3.1]
    C --> F[Receive Shift Register]
    F --> G[RXD Pin P3.0]
    H[SCON Register] --> D
    H --> F
    I[Timer 1 Overflow] --> J[Baud Rate Generator]
    J --> D
    J --> F
    K[PCON SMOD Bit] --> J
    D --> L[Set TI Flag]
    F --> M[Set RI Flag]
    L --> N[Interrupt to CPU]
    M --> N
    E --> O[External Device]
    O --> G
```

### 4.3 Serial Port Programming Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start] --> B[Disable Interrupts EA = 0]
    B --> C[Load TH1 for Baud Rate]
    C --> D[Configure TMOD for Timer1 Mode2]
    D --> E[Set SCON for UART Mode1 REN=1]
    E --> F[Set SMOD bit if needed]
    F --> G[Start Timer1 TR1 = 1]
    G --> H[Enable Serial Interrupt ES = 1]
    H --> I[Enable Global Interrupt EA = 1]
    I --> J{Transmit or Receive}
    J --> K[Wait for TI / RI]
    K --> L[Clear TI / RI Flag]
    L --> M[Read or Write SBUF]
    M --> N[End]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: ECU UART Link in an Automotive Network

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Engine ECU 8051] -->|TXD P3.1| B[RS232 Line Driver MAX232]
    B -->|DB9 Connector| C[OBD II Port]
    C -->|K Line or CAN| D[Diagnostic Tool Scanner]
    D -->|Response| C
    C -->|RXD| B
    B -->|RXD P3.0| A
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the function of the SCON register in 8051 with the help of its bit format.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
SCON (Serial Control, address 98H) is the bit-addressable register that controls the 8051's serial port. Its 8 bits are:
- **SM0, SM1:** Select mode 0, 1, 2, or 3.
- **SM2:** Multiprocessor communication enable.
- **REN:** Receiver enable (1 = receive allowed).
- **TB8, RB8:** 9th transmitted / received bit (Modes 2 & 3).
- **TI:** Transmit interrupt flag (set by hardware at end of byte, must be cleared by software).
- **RI:** Receive interrupt flag (set by hardware at end of byte, must be cleared by software).
*(1 mark for listing bits with full names, 1 mark for the modes 0–3 purpose, 1 mark for the TI/RI software-clear distinction — a favourite valuation point).*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is a 11.0592 MHz crystal preferred over 12 MHz in 8051 systems that use serial communication?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The 11.0592 MHz crystal is preferred because the timer reload value for standard baud rates (especially 9600) comes out as an exact integer (TH1 = 0xFD), producing **0% baud-rate error**.
With a 12 MHz crystal, the closest integer TH1 produces an actual baud rate of 10416.67 bps instead of 9600 bps, giving an **8.51% error** — which exceeds the UART receiver's tolerance of ±2.2% and causes framing errors in automotive ECUs.
*(1 mark for integer TH1 concept, 1 mark for 0% error advantage, 1 mark for tolerance value and framing error consequence.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the different operating modes of the 8051 serial port with appropriate diagrams. State the baud rate for each mode. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 UART can operate in four modes selected by SM0 and SM1 bits of the SCON register.

**Mode 0 (SM0=0, SM1=0) — 8-bit Shift Register Mode (Synchronous):**
- Data is transmitted and received through **RXD pin (P3.0)**, while **TXD pin (P3.1)** carries the shift clock.
- Frame: 8 data bits, **no start, no stop bit**.
- Baud rate is **fixed = F_OSC / 12**.
- Used to interface with serial shift registers like 74HC595.

**Mode 1 (SM0=0, SM1=1) — 8-bit UART (Asynchronous, Variable Baud):**
- Frame: 1 start bit + 8 data bits (LSB first) + 1 stop bit = **10 bits/frame**.
- Baud rate is **variable = 2^SMOD × F_OSC / (384 × (256 − TH1))**.
- Most widely used in PC-to-8051 and OBD-II communication.

**Mode 2 (SM0=1, SM1=0) — 9-bit UART (Fixed Baud):**
- Frame: 1 start + 9 data + 1 stop = 11 bits/frame.
- Baud rate = **F_OSC / 64 (SMOD=0) or F_OSC / 32 (SMOD=1)**.
- 9th bit stored in **TB8/RB8**; used in multiprocessor schemes.

**Mode 3 (SM0=1, SM1=1) — 9-bit UART (Variable Baud):**
- Frame identical to Mode 2, but baud rate is **variable (Timer 1 controlled)**.
- Same formula as Mode 1, but with 9 data bits.

| Mode | SM0 | SM1 | Frame | Baud Rate Source | Use |
|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 8-bit sync | F_OSC/12 | Shift register |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 8-bit + start/stop | Timer 1 | **PC comms / OBD** |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | 9-bit | F_OSC/32 or /64 | Multiprocessor |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 9-bit | Timer 1 | Multiprocessor |

*(1 mark per mode description + 1 mark consolidated baud-rate table + 1 mark for the frame bit breakdown — examiners expect the 10-bit Mode 1 frame to be explicitly drawn.)*

---

**(b)** An 8051 system uses an 11.0592 MHz crystal. Write an 8051 assembly language program to transfer the ASCII letter 'A' serially at 9600 baud, 8-bit data, 1 stop bit, continuously. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0023H          ; Serial Port ISR (not used here)
        RETI

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #20H    ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
        MOV     TH1,  #0FDH   ; Reload value for 9600 baud
        MOV     SCON, #50H    ; Mode 1, REN enabled, TI/RI cleared
                               ; 0101 0000 = 50H
        SETB    TR1           ; Start Timer 1

AGAIN:  MOV     SBUF, #'A'    ; Load 'A' into transmit buffer
HERE:   JNB     TI, HERE      ; Wait until TI = 1 (byte transmitted)
        CLR     TI            ; Clear TI for next byte
        SJMP    AGAIN         ; Loop forever

        END
```

**Explanation of instructions (for full marks):**
- `TMOD = 20H` → Timer 1 in Mode 2, software controlled.
- `TH1 = 0xFD` → 253, derived from $X = 256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600} = 253$.
- `SCON = 50H` → SM0=0, SM1=1, REN=1, TI=RI=0.
- `SBUF = 'A'` → Writing to SBUF triggers transmission.
- `JNB TI, HERE` → Polling TI flag (essential polling technique).
- `CLR TI` → TI must be cleared by software (this is a KTU high-yield point).

*(1 mark for timer init, 1 mark for baud rate derivation, 1 mark for SCON, 1 mark for SBUF write, 1 mark for TI polling, 1 mark for TI clear, 1 mark for loop structure.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the serial port programming structure of the 8051. List the registers involved and their functions. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 serial port is built around three registers: **SBUF, SCON, and PCON (SMOD bit)**, supported by **Timer 1** for baud-rate generation.

**Registers and their functions:**

| Register | Address | Function |
|:----|:----:|:----|
| SBUF | 99H | Dual buffer — write transmits, read receives. |
| SCON | 98H | Mode select, REN, TI, RI, SM2, TB8, RB8. |
| PCON | 87H | SMOD bit — doubles baud rate when set. |
| TMOD | 89H | Configures Timer 1 in Mode 2 for baud-rate clock. |
| TCON | 88H | TR1 control bit to start/stop Timer 1. |
| IE | A8H | ES bit — serial interrupt enable. |

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
   Timer 1 Overflow (TH1 auto-reload)
              |
              v
   +-------------------+
   | Baud Rate Clock   |  <--- SMOD (PCON.7)
   +-------------------+
              |
       +------+------+
       v             v
  +---------+   +---------+
  | TX Side |   | RX Side |
  | SBUF(w) |   | SBUF(r) |
  |  SCON   |   |  SCON   |
  +---------+   +---------+
       |             |
       v             v
    TXD P3.1       RXD P3.0
```

The **TX side** loads a byte into SBUF, shifts it out serially via TXD, then sets the TI flag.
The **RX side** samples RXD, when a complete byte is received it stores in SBUF and sets the RI flag.
**Timer 1** provides the baud-rate clock; **SMOD** doubles it. The **interrupt** line from TI/RI can be routed to the CPU via the **ES bit in IE** register.

*(1 mark for block diagram, 1 mark per register row × 5, 1 mark for TX/RX symmetry — examiner's favourite for full marks.)*

---

**(b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to receive a byte of data serially in Mode 1 at 9600 baud, save it in internal RAM location 30H, and echo it back to the sender. Assume F_OSC = 11.0592 MHz. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0023H
        LJMP    SERIAL_ISR

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #20H    ; Timer 1, Mode 2
        MOV     TH1,  #0FDH   ; 9600 baud reload
        MOV     SCON, #50H    ; Mode 1, REN=1
        SETB    TR1           ; Start Timer 1
        SETB    ES            ; Enable serial interrupt
        SETB    EA            ; Enable global interrupt
HERE:   SJMP    HERE          ; Wait for interrupt

;----------- Serial ISR -----------
SERIAL_ISR:
        JB      TI, ECHO      ; If TI=1, branch to ECHO
        MOV     A, SBUF       ; Read received byte
        MOV     30H, A        ; Save in internal RAM
        CLR     RI            ; Clear RI flag
        RETI

ECHO:   CLR     TI            ; Clear TI flag
        RETI

        END
```

**Polling Version (alternative model for 7 marks):**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #20H
        MOV     TH1,  #0FDH
        MOV     SCON, #50H
        SETB    TR1

WAIT:   JNB     RI, WAIT      ; Wait for receive
        MOV     A,   SBUF     ; Read byte
        MOV     30H, A        ; Save to RAM
        CLR     RI            ; Clear RI
        MOV     SBUF, A       ; Echo byte back
LOOP:   JNB     TI, LOOP      ; Wait for echo to finish
        CLR     TI            ; Clear TI
        SJMP    WAIT          ; Get next byte

        END
```

**Key KTU Validation Points:**
- TH1 = 0xFD because $\frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600} = 3$, so $X = 256 - 3 = 253$.
- Both **TI and RI must be cleared by software** (this is a top-1 pitfall).
- `MOV SBUF, A` initiates transmission automatically — the hardware does the rest.

*(2 marks for correct TH1 derivation, 2 marks for SCON + Timer init, 2 marks for RI/TI polling+clear, 1 mark for correct echo logic.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Serial Port Programming questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to clear TI/RI by software.** Unlike many other flags, the 8051 hardware *sets* TI/RI but *cannot clear them* — students who rely on hardware clearing lose 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> 2. **Wrong TH1 formula usage.** Many students use $X = \frac{F_{OSC}}{384 \cdot Baud}$ instead of $X = 256 - \frac{F_{OSC}}{384 \cdot Baud}$. KTU expects the *reload value* X, not the timer count — read the question twice.
> 3. **Confusing SMOD effect.** SMOD=1 *doubles* the baud rate; it does not halve it. In SMOD=0, Mode 2 baud = F_OSC/64; in SMOD=1, it is F_OSC/32.
> 4. **Not setting REN = 1.** Without REN=1, no data can be received — the RX side will appear "dead" even with a perfectly configured baud rate.
> 5. **Using 12 MHz crystal and silently using TH1 = 0xFD** — this is mathematically wrong; with 12 MHz, TH1 = 0xFD yields ~10416 baud with 8.5% error.
> 6. **Missing `SETB TR1`** to start the baud-rate generator — a one-bit omission that breaks everything.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Three mandatory steps** to initialize 8051 serial port: *(i)* Program Timer 1 in Mode 2, *(ii)* Configure SCON, *(iii)* Start TR1.
- **Master formula:** $Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \cdot F_{OSC}}{384 \cdot (256 - X)}$ for Modes 1 and 3.
- **Master formula (TH1):** $X = 256 - \dfrac{F_{OSC}}{384 \cdot Baud}$ — give answer in **hex** for KTU.
- **11.0592 MHz** is the automotive UART crystal of choice — yields **0% error** at 9600 baud.
- **SBUF address = 99H**, SCON address = 98H, PCON address = 87H (memorize these).
- **Mode 1** is the most commonly used mode in automotive OBD-II and infotainment.
- **SCON = 50H** is the canonical "Mode 1, receiver enabled" initialization byte.
- **TI/RI must be cleared by software** — common KTU pitfall worth 1–2 marks.
- **REN must be set to 1** to enable reception.
- **SMOD bit** in PCON.7 doubles the baud rate.
- **Frame = Start (1) + Data (8) + Stop (1) = 10 bits** in Mode 1 — 9600 baud ⇒ 960 frames/sec.
- **9th-bit modes (2 & 3)** are used for multiprocessor communication; 9th bit stored in TB8/RB8.
- **Mode 0** is synchronous (shift register mode) — used to expand outputs via 74HC595.
- **ES bit in IE register** enables serial interrupt; **EA** must also be set.
- **SBUF write = transmit**, **SBUF read = receive** (single address, two registers).
- **Real-time automotive use:** OBD-II at 10.4 kbps (K-Line), GPS modules at 9600 baud, Bluetooth HC-05 at 9600/38400 baud.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupts Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT746 - MICROPROCESSORS AND CONTROLLERS IN AUTOMOTIVES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interrupts Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interrupt Programming** in the context of automotive microcontrollers is a structured, event-driven firmware technique where the **CPU** is forced to suspend the execution of its current sequential program flow, branch to a dedicated **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** in response to a hardware or software trigger, execute the ISR, and then return precisely to the interrupted instruction using a context-restore sequence.

In automotive embedded systems (typically built around the **8051 core**, **ARM Cortex-M3/M4**, or **Infineon TriCore** families), interrupts are the **primary mechanism** for handling time-critical events such as:
- **Crankshaft position sensor pulses** (engine timing)
- **Wheel speed sensor interrupts** (ABS / ESP)
- **CAN bus message arrival** (inter-vehicle communication)
- **ADC end-of-conversion** (battery management)
- **Timer overflow** (ECU scheduling)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Doorbell vs. The Watchman"

> [!NOTE]
> **Polling Method (Without Interrupts):**
> Imagine a watchman who must walk to the front door, check if anyone is there, then return, every few seconds. This wastes enormous time and energy even when nobody arrives. The CPU "polls" the flag bit in a loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interrupt Method (With Interrupts):**
> Imagine a watchman doing his main duty, while a **doorbell** is connected to his ear. The instant a visitor presses the bell, the watchman is **interrupted**, drops everything, attends to the door (the **ISR**), and then resumes his original task from exactly where he left off.

This is precisely how an **ECU (Electronic Control Unit)** in a modern car handles the **crank position sensor** — it cannot afford to poll the sensor, because missing even one tooth pulse could cause the engine to misfire. The interrupt guarantees **deterministic, microsecond-level response**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded Standard KTU Definitions)

- **Interrupt Source**: The peripheral or external pin that generates the interrupt request (e.g., `INT0`, `T0`, `ADC`, `CAN`).
- **Interrupt Request (IRQ)**: The hardware signal flag raised by the source.
- **Interrupt Enable Register (IE)**: The SFR (Special Function Register) that globally and individually allows interrupts.
- **Interrupt Priority Register (IP)**: The SFR that decides which interrupt wins when two fire simultaneously.
- **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**: The dedicated function executed in response to the IRQ.
- **Vector Address**: The fixed memory location (e.g., `0003H` for `INT0` in 8051) where the ISR begins.
- **Context Saving**: Saving `ACC`, `PSW`, registers, etc., on the stack before executing the ISR.
- **Reti (Return from Interrupt)**: Special instruction that signals end of ISR and triggers automatic register restoration.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the 8051 Interrupt System

The classic 8051 (still widely taught in KTU 2024 automotive syllabus) supports **5 interrupt sources**:

| S.No | Source | Vector Address (8051) | Flag Bit | Enable Bit |
|:----:|:------:|:--------------------:|:--------:|:----------:|
| 1 | Reset | `0000H` | — | Always on |
| 2 | External Interrupt 0 (`INT0`) | `0003H` | `IE0` (TCON.1) | `EX0` (IE.0) |
| 3 | Timer 0 Overflow | `000BH` | `TF0` (TCON.5) | `ET0` (IE.1) |
| 4 | External Interrupt 1 (`INT1`) | `0013H` | `IE1` (TCON.3) | `EX1` (IE.2) |
| 5 | Timer 1 Overflow | `001BH` | `TF1` (TCON.7) | `ET1` (IE.3) |
| 6 | Serial Port (UART) | `0023H` | `TI/RI` (SCON.0/1) | `ES` (IE.4) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **total vector space** reserved for all 6 sources is only **8 bytes × 6 = 48 bytes**, starting from `0003H` to `002BH`. In real automotive code, a **LJMP (Long Jump)** instruction is placed at each vector to redirect to the actual ISR located elsewhere in code memory.

## 2.2 The Two Master Control Bits (KTU High-Yield)

| Bit | Name | Register | Address (SFR) | Function |
|:---:|:----:|:--------:|:-------------:|:--------:|
| `EA` | Enable All | `IE.7` | `0A8H` | Global master switch — must be `1` for any interrupt to fire |
| `—` | Reserved | `IE.6` | — | Not used |
| `—` | Reserved | `IE.5` | — | Not used |
| `ES` | Enable Serial | `IE.4` | — | UART interrupt |
| `ET1` | Enable Timer 1 | `IE.3` | — | Timer 1 overflow |
| `EX1` | Enable INT1 | `IE.2` | — | External pin P3.3 |
| `ET0` | Enable Timer 0 | `IE.1` | — | Timer 0 overflow |
| `EX0` | Enable INT0 | `IE.0` | — | External pin P3.2 |

**The Golden Rule of 8051 Interrupts:**
$$\text{IRQ Fires} \iff (EA = 1) \;\land\; (Individual\;Enable = 1) \;\land\; (Trigger\;Condition\;Met)$$

## 2.3 Edge-Triggered vs. Level-Triggered Interrupts

| Mode | Control Bit | Behavior | Automotive Use Case |
|:----:|:-----------:|:---------|:--------------------|
| **Level-Triggered (Low)** | `IT0 = 0` (TCON.0) | Interrupt fires as long as pin is `LOW` | Used for **active-low sensors** like some Hall sensors |
| **Edge-Triggered (Falling)** | `IT0 = 1` (TCON.0) | Interrupt fires ONLY on `1→0` transition | Used for **crank position teeth**, **wheel speed sensors** (preferred — avoids multiple triggering) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In an **edge-triggered external interrupt**, the flag `IE0/IE1` is **automatically cleared** by the hardware when the CPU vectors to the ISR. In **level-triggered mode**, the flag is **not** cleared — the external source must release the pin before `RETI` executes, otherwise the ISR re-enters infinitely.

## 2.4 Interrupt Priority Logic (Two-Tier System)

| Priority Level | Bit Value (IP register) | Outcome |
|:--------------:|:-----------------------:|:--------|
| **High** | `1` | Wins over a simultaneous Low-priority interrupt |
| **Low** | `0` | Default; yields to High-priority |

The **natural priority order** (when IP bits are equal) is: `INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial`.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Interrupt Latency and Timing

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|:----------|:--------|:------------|
| **Interrupt Latency** | $t_{latency} = t_{detect} + t_{stack\;save} + t_{vector}$ | Time from IRQ to first ISR instruction |
| **Cycle Count for 8051 ISR Entry** | 2 machine cycles (`LCALL`) + 2 cycles (push PC) = **4 cycles** | Fixed hardware overhead |
| **Maximum Blockable Time** | $t_{max} = 1 / f_{OSC} \times 12$ per cycle | One 8051 machine cycle |
| **Critical Section Disable** | $EA = 0$ → atomic block → $EA = 1$ | Prevents nested interrupts during register update |
| **Timer Tick Rate** | $f_{tick} = f_{OSC} / (12 \times (2^{16} - T_{initial}))$ | Used when timer interrupt fires at known interval |

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER use the vertical pipe `|` inside the table above — the markdown parser breaks.** The above uses `\vert` notation only.

## 2.6 Real-World Automotive Utility

In a **Bosch ECU** or **delphi MT80** engine controller, the crank position sensor triggers `INT0` on every falling edge of a 60-2 tooth wheel (60 teeth minus 2 missing for position reference). The ISR must:
1. Capture timer value (`TH0:TL0`)
2. Compute engine RPM
3. Calculate ignition dwell and spark advance
4. Trigger the ignition coil via GPIO
5. Return — all within **~500 µs** to maintain timing accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Generic Interrupt Programming Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical 5-step sequence** that KTU expects for any 8051 interrupt programming question.

**Step 1 — Choose the trigger mode**
Set the bit `ITx` in `TCON`:
- `SETB IT0` → falling edge on `INT0`
- `CLR IT0` → low-level on `INT0`

**Step 2 — Enable the specific interrupt**
Set the corresponding bit in the `IE` register:
- `SETB EX0` (for external interrupt 0)

**Step 3 — Enable the global interrupt**
- `SETB EA`

**Step 4 — Write the ISR at the correct vector address**
For an ISR at vector `0003H`:
- Use the `ORG` directive
- Place a `LJMP` to the actual ISR (because the 8-byte space is too small)
- Save context with `PUSH ACC` and `PUSH PSW`

**Step 5 — Restore context and return**
- `POP PSW` and `POP ACC`
- Execute `RETI`

## 3.2 Worked Example — Engine RPM Counter Using `INT0` and `T0`

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
> An automotive ECU uses an 8051 running at **11.0592 MHz**. A 60-tooth reluctor wheel is connected to `INT0` (edge-triggered). Timer 0 counts clock cycles between teeth. Calculate the engine RPM from the captured timer value, and write the complete interrupt program.

### 3.2.1 Derivation of the RPM Formula

Let $N_{ticks}$ = Timer 0 value captured between two consecutive teeth.

Step 1 — One 8051 machine cycle duration:
$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\,\mu s
$$

Step 2 — Time elapsed between two teeth (in seconds):
$$
T_{tooth} = N_{ticks} \times T_{cycle}
$$

Step 3 — Time for one full revolution (60 teeth, so multiply by 60):
$$
T_{rev} = 60 \times T_{tooth} = 60 \times N_{ticks} \times T_{cycle}
$$

Step 4 — Revolutions per second:
$$
f_{rev} = \frac{1}{T_{rev}} = \frac{1}{60 \times N_{ticks} \times T_{cycle}}
$$

Step 5 — Convert to RPM (Revolutions Per Minute):
$$
\text{RPM} = f_{rev} \times 60 = \frac{60}{60 \times N_{ticks} \times T_{cycle}} = \frac{1}{N_{ticks} \times T_{cycle}}
$$

Final clean formula:
$$
\boxed{\;\text{RPM} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times N_{ticks} \times 60} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{720 \times N_{ticks}}\;}
$$

Step 6 — Substituting $f_{OSC} = 11.0592 \times 10^{6}$ Hz:
$$
\text{RPM} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{720 \times N_{ticks}} = \frac{15360}{N_{ticks}}
$$

> **Verification:** If $N_{ticks} = 15360$ → RPM = 1 rev/sec = **60 RPM** ✓ (very slow idle). If $N_{ticks} = 1920$ → RPM = **4800 RPM** (typical cruising).

## 3.3 Complete 8051 Assembly Program (KTU Exam Standard)

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN            ; Reset vector jumps to main

        ORG     0003H           ; INT0 vector address
        LJMP    EXT0_ISR        ; Redirect to actual ISR

        ORG     000BH           ; Timer 0 vector address
        LJMP    T0_ISR          ; Redirect to Timer 0 ISR

        ORG     0030H           ; Main program starts here
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #09H      ; T0 in 16-bit counter mode (gate=0, C/T=1)
        MOV     TH0, #00H       ; Reset counter high byte
        MOV     TL0, #00H       ; Reset counter low byte
        SETB    IT0             ; Edge-triggered on INT0 (P3.2)
        SETB    EX0             ; Enable INT0
        SETB    ET0             ; Enable Timer 0 overflow
        SETB    EA              ; Global interrupt enable
        SETB    TR0             ; Start Timer 0 as counter
HERE:   SJMP    HERE            ; Idle main loop — CPU waits for interrupts

;--------------------------------------------------------
; ISR for INT0: tooth detected, latch timer value
;--------------------------------------------------------
EXT0_ISR:
        PUSH    ACC
        PUSH    PSW
        MOV     A, TL0          ; Low byte of count
        MOV     R0, A           ; Save low byte in R0
        MOV     A, TH0          ; High byte of count
        MOV     R1, A           ; Save high byte in R1
        MOV     TH0, #00H       ; Reset counter for next period
        MOV     TL0, #00H
        SETB    20H             ; Set a flag for main to process
        POP     PSW
        POP     ACC
        RETI                    ; Return from interrupt

;--------------------------------------------------------
; ISR for Timer 0 overflow: count missing teeth (timeout)
;--------------------------------------------------------
T0_ISR:
        PUSH    ACC
        SETB    21H             ; Indicate timeout — engine stopped
        POP     ACC
        RETI

        END
```

## 3.4 C Language Equivalent (Modern Automotive Embedded Style)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned int tooth_count = 0;
bit tooth_flag = 0;
bit timeout_flag = 0;

void ext0_isr(void) interrupt 0   // Vector 0 = INT0 at 0003H
{
    unsigned int current = (TH0 << 8) | TL0;
    tooth_count = current;
    TH0 = 0x00;
    TL0 = 0x00;
    tooth_flag = 1;
}

void t0_isr(void) interrupt 1      // Vector 1 = Timer 0 at 000BH
{
    timeout_flag = 1;              // Engine has stopped or stalled
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x09;                   // T0: 16-bit counter mode
    TH0 = 0;  TL0 = 0;
    IT0  = 1;                      // Edge-triggered
    EX0  = 1;                      // Enable INT0
    ET0  = 1;                      // Enable T0 interrupt
    EA   = 1;                      // Global enable
    TR0  = 1;                      // Start counter

    while (1)
    {
        if (tooth_flag)
        {
            // RPM = 15360 / tooth_count  (for 11.0592 MHz)
            tooth_flag = 0;
        }
        if (timeout_flag)
        {
            // Engine stopped — disable fuel injection
            timeout_flag = 0;
        }
    }
}
```

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Interrupt Programming Patterns

| Application | Interrupt Source | Mode | Critical Action Inside ISR |
|:------------|:-----------------|:-----|:--------------------------|
| Engine RPM sensing | `INT0` (crank sensor) | Edge-triggered | Latch `TH0:TL0` |
| Knock window timing | `INT1` (knock sensor) | Edge-triggered | Start ADC, capture window |
| Wheel speed (ABS) | `INT0` or `INT1` | Edge-triggered | Latch period for slip calc |
| UART diagnostic (OBD-II) | Serial `RI/TI` | Flag-based | Read `SBUF`, push to ring buffer |
| Battery monitor (BMS) | ADC `EOC` (via polling/int) | Polled or event | Read ADC, check thresholds |
| CAN message arrival | `CAN` peripheral IRQ | Level/edge | Read CAN mailbox, signal RTOS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — 8051 Interrupt Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Program Loop] --> B{Check Next Instruction}
    B --> C[Execute Normal Code]
    C --> D{Hardware IRQ Detected}
    D -- No --> B
    D -- Yes --> E[Finish Current Instruction]
    E --> F[Save PC on Stack]
    F --> G[Load ISR Vector Address]
    G --> H[Clear IRQ Flag Auto]
    H --> I[Execute ISR Body]
    I --> J{Priority Higher IRQ}
    J -- Yes --> K[Nest Interrupt OR Hold]
    J -- No --> L[Execute RETI]
    K --> I
    L --> M[Pop PC from Stack]
    M --> N[Resume Main Program]
    N --> B
```

## 4.2 Interrupt Vector Memory Map (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[0000H Reset] --> B[0003H INT0]
    B --> C[000BH Timer 0]
    C --> D[0013H INT1]
    D --> E[001BH Timer 1]
    E --> F[0023H Serial UART]
    F --> G[002BH End of Vector Space]
    G --> H[0030H Onwards: User Code]
```

## 4.3 Interrupt Enable Register (IE) Bit Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bit 7 EA Global] --> B[Bit 6 Reserved]
    B --> C[Bit 5 Reserved]
    C --> D[Bit 4 ES Serial]
    D --> E[Bit 3 ET1 Timer1]
    E --> F[Bit 2 EX1 INT1]
    F --> G[Bit 1 ET0 Timer0]
    G --> H[Bit 0 EX0 INT0]
```

## 4.4 Nested Interrupt Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ISR Currently Running] --> B{Current Priority Level}
    B --> C{High Priority Running}
    B --> D{Low Priority Running}
    C --> E[Block New Low Priority IRQ]
    C --> F[Allow New High Priority IRQ]
    D --> G[Allow Any New IRQ to Preempt]
    E --> H[Continue Current ISR]
    F --> I[Save State, Execute New ISR]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Return via RETI Chain]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** What is an Interrupt Service Routine? Mention any two registers used in 8051 interrupt programming.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** is a special function that the CPU executes in response to a hardware or software interrupt. It begins at a fixed **vector address** in code memory and ends with the `RETI` instruction, which restores the program counter and returns to the main program.
Two registers involved are:
1. **IE (Interrupt Enable register, address `0A8H`)** — controls global (`EA`) and individual interrupt enables (`EX0`, `ET0`, `EX1`, `ET1`, `ES`).
2. **TCON (Timer/Counter Control register, address `88H`)** — holds interrupt flags (`IE0`, `TF0`, `IE1`, `TF1`) and the trigger-type bits (`IT0`, `IT1`).
*(1 mark for ISR definition, 1 mark each for the two registers = 3 marks)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Compare edge-triggered and level-triggered external interrupts in 8051.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Edge-Triggered (`ITx = 1`) | Level-Triggered (`ITx = 0`) |
|:-------|:---------------------------|:-----------------------------|
| Activation | Only on `1→0` transition | Whenever pin is held LOW |
| Flag handling | Hardware clears flag automatically | Flag NOT cleared — external source must release the pin |
| Risk | No re-triggering | Risk of repeated entry if pin stays LOW |
| Automotive use | Crankshaft, wheel speed sensors | Some active-low Hall sensors |

*(1 mark per row × 3 = 3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> ### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model 2024]
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the interrupt structure of the 8051 microcontroller. List all interrupt sources with their vector addresses. (7 marks)
> **(b)** An 8051 system with crystal frequency **12 MHz** is used to count external pulses on `T0` (P3.4). The Timer 0 overflow interrupt is used to update a `SECONDS` counter every time 50000 pulses are received. Write the complete 8051 assembly program to initialize the timer, the interrupt, and the ISR. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks
- **Diagram**: Use the architecture from Section 4.1 (mermaid flow). **(2 marks)**
- **5 Sources + Reset** listed with vector addresses: `0000H` Reset, `0003H` `INT0`, `000BH` `T0`, `0013H` `INT1`, `001BH` `T1`, `0023H` Serial. **(2 marks)**
- **Role of IE register** — global `EA` and individual enables. **(1 mark)**
- **Role of IP register** — two-level priority (high/low). **(1 mark)**
- **Edge vs. level trigger** — `IT0`/`IT1` in TCON. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute the timer reload value.**
Timer is in **Mode 1 (16-bit)**. With $f_{OSC} = 12\,\text{MHz}$, one machine cycle $= 1\,\mu s$.

We need an overflow every 50000 counts:
$$
\text{Timer initial value} = 2^{16} - 50000 = 65536 - 50000 = 15536
$$
Converting $15536_{10}$ to hex: $15536 = 0x3CB0$ → `TH0 = 3CH`, `TL0 = 0B0H`.

**Step 2 — Complete Program:**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     000BH           ; T0 overflow vector
        LJMP    T0_ISR

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #05H      ; T0 in Mode 1, counter mode
        MOV     TH0, #3CH
        MOV     TL0, #0B0H
        MOV     R7, #00H        ; SECONDS counter = 0
        SETB    ET0             ; Enable T0 interrupt
        SETB    EA              ; Global enable
        SETB    TR0             ; Start counter
LOOP:   SJMP    LOOP            ; Main does nothing — interrupt-driven

T0_ISR: PUSH    ACC
        PUSH    PSW
        MOV     TH0, #3CH       ; Reload for next 50000 counts
        MOV     TL0, #0B0H
        INC     R7              ; SECONDS = SECONDS + 1
        POP     PSW
        POP     ACC
        RETI
        END
```

**Mark split for (b):** Timer math (2 marks) + Initialization (2 marks) + ISR (2 marks) + Reload logic (1 mark) = 7 marks.

> ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Model 2024]
> **(a)** Explain the role of the `IE` and `IP` registers in 8051 interrupt programming with their bit-level format. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Design an interrupt-driven automotive dashboard warning system. An over-temperature sensor is connected to `INT1` (edge-triggered) and a buzzer is connected to `P1.7`. Write the C program using Keil-style interrupt syntax. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks
- **IE register** (`0A8H`) — full bit diagram (Section 4.3) **(3 marks)**
- **IP register** (`0B8H`) — bits `PX0`, `PT0`, `PX1`, `PT1`, `PS` to set high/low priority **(2 marks)**
- **Global enable rule** $EA \wedge E_x$ **(1 mark)**
- **Priority tie-breaking** — natural order `INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial` **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit BUZZER = P1^7;          // Buzzer pin

void over_temp_isr(void) interrupt 2   // Vector 2 = INT1 at 0013H
{
    BUZZER = 1;              // Sound the buzzer
    // Optional: store error code in EEPROM / flag register
}

void main(void)
{
    BUZZER = 0;              // Initially OFF
    IT1   = 1;               // Edge-triggered on INT1
    EX1   = 1;               // Enable INT1
    EA    = 1;               // Global enable
    while (1)
    {
        // Main loop can perform other dashboard tasks
        // (e.g., RPM display, fuel level check)
    }
}
```

**Mark split for (b):** Pin declaration (1 mark) + ISR setup (2 marks) + Trigger config (2 marks) + Main loop logic (2 marks) = 7 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls
> 1. **Forgetting `SETB EA`** — Most common mistake. Without `EA = 1`, *no* interrupt fires, even if individual bits are set. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Wrong vector address** — Writing ISR at `0000H` instead of `000BH` for Timer 0. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Missing `RETI`** — Using `RET` instead of `RETI`. The `RETI` instruction is what tells the 8051 interrupt controller that the ISR has ended; using `RET` will leave interrupts disabled. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 4. **Failing to reload Timer** — In Mode 1, the timer does NOT auto-reload. Forgetting to reset `TH0:TL0` inside the ISR causes the next interval to be wildly wrong. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Long ISRs** — If the ISR takes longer than the inter-tooth time, teeth are missed. Examiners deduct marks if no comment about ISR brevity is mentioned. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Confusing `IE0` flag with `EX0` enable** — `IE0` is the *flag* (set by hardware), `EX0` is the *enable* (set by software). Mixing them up is a classic 2-mark deduction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ An **interrupt** is a hardware or software event that temporarily halts the CPU to execute a high-priority task (the **ISR**), then resumes.
- ✅ 8051 supports **5 interrupt sources** + Reset, each with a **fixed 8-byte vector slot** starting at `0003H`.
- ✅ **Two master controls**: individual enable bit (e.g., `EX0`) + global `EA`.
- ✅ **Edge-triggered** (`ITx = 1`) is preferred for crank/wheel sensors — auto-clears flag.
- ✅ **Level-triggered** (`ITx = 0`) requires the external source to release the pin before `RETI`.
- ✅ **Priority** is two-tier: high (`1`) and low (`0`), set via the **IP register**.
- ✅ **Natural priority order** (when IP bits equal): `INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial`.
- ✅ ISR must be **short, fast, and re-entrant-safe**; save `ACC` and `PSW` on stack.
- ✅ Always end ISR with `RETI`, never `RET`.
- ✅ In **Mode 1 (16-bit)**, reload `TH0:TL0` inside the ISR.
- ✅ RPM formula for 8051: $\text{RPM} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{720 \times N_{ticks}}$.
- ✅ In **C (Keil)**: use `void name(void) interrupt <vector_number>` — vector numbers: `0=INT0, 1=T0, 2=INT1, 3=T1, 4=Serial`.
- ✅ Automotive ISRs must respect **deadline constraints** (e.g., 500 µs for crank sensing).
- ✅ Use **critical sections** (`EA = 0` … `EA = 1`) when modifying shared variables between ISR and main.
- ✅ Modern automotive MCUs (ARM Cortex-M) use **NVIC** with up to 256 interrupts and 16 priority levels — concept is identical, hardware is enhanced.
